- **References:** `<9toine$lbj$1@scavenger.euro.net> <3c0596db.936980074@192.168.2.2> <3c02c7c6.0@newsman.viper.net> <3c0702ea_2@news1.prserv.net> <3c07aae2.0@newsman.viper.net>`

As a Windows developer since Win 3.1, I read this with great interest.

Ken's sincerity cannot be doubted and he makes some very good points about
the use of Win API. (And the smokescreens that various interested parties
may put up to persuade you NOT to use them...<G>)

I would agree that once you code a few API calls the process loses much of
its mystique and terror.

Ken is absolutely right in his examples of simple window and control
building and it IS simple. (But so is using a third party tool to do it...)

However, there ARE pitfalls in the use of  more complex APIs which are
tiresome in the extreme; the main ones I have  experienced are with getting
data in the right format from COBOL, to be recognized by the API. There are
no diagnostics or clues, it simply doesn't work if you get it wrong. At
worst it can hang your system. I have spent many hours of trial and error
before getting some calls to work properly and I have been glad of
assistance from programmers who are more experienced in this particular area
to get certain complex calls to work. Once it works, of course, you forget
all about the sweat...

Now, if a third party tool can shield you from this, then, personally, I
would be in favour of using that tool (and I do...). However, in most cases,
as Ken points out, the third party tools don't adddress these areas and you
HAVE to use the API.

For quick building of Windows applications I use Fujitsu PowerCOBOL. It is
OO based, easy to learn, and gets you to a GUI in no time flat. Yes, I could
develop a library of Objects as Ken suggests, to provide the GUI controls,
but Fujitsu have already done it and it works fine. None of the specific
shortcomings he mentions with combo boxes, resizing, etc., are manifested by
the PowerCOBOL objects.(I was shocked at the idea of having to predefine the
number of items to be displayed by a ListBox, Ken <G>...)

On the contrary, the controls are packaged complete with Events, Methods,
and Properties which make using them a doddle, and provide in many cases
enhanced functionality beyond what the raw API call would provide. (No, I am
NOT on commission for Fujitsu...in fact, I won't be using their products in
future (reserving judgement on .Net COBOL until we get to see it), but that
is more about support, licensing, and responsiveness than inherent failures
in the product.)

I think one point that was missed in Ken's post is that using a third party
tool or using the Win API are NOT mutually exclusive. I have Windows
applications which utilise both and they work very well.

BOTTOM LINE:

1. What tools you will need depends very much on what you want to do.

The kind of development Ken is doing demands much more than simply
interacting through a window. (I wouldn't mind betting that your
applications are pretty to watch, Ken...<G>)

2. There is an argument that says investing a large amount of time in
learning the details of a low level interface may be unjustifiable, given
that technology moves at the rate it does. Anyone who has been in this game
more than 15 years can probably tell you about knowledge that was painfully
gained, then rendered redundant within a few years by the march of
technology. (Fortunately, sometimes it comes back...Look at CICS <G>).

This is particularly pertinent when the knowledge applies to a specific
product... (Do you start over if you move to UNIX, Ken...? Nope, just don't
move, right?)

3. It is irresponsible for Vendors to promote their products with sweeping
statements that are not balanced or fair. (But we will NEVER stop them doing
it <G>; all we can do is exercise our judgement and measure their claims
against experience. Of course, the guys who couch their pitch in simple
plain examples and statements of fact, will acquire much more credibility
than the guys who don't...<G> )

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol and screens

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <**Ken**Mullins**@**mindspring.com** remove **'s>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<9toine$lbj$1@scavenger.euro.net> <3c0596db.936980074@192.168.2.2> <3c02c7c6.0@newsman.viper.net> <3c0702ea_2@news1.prserv.net> <3c07aae2.0@newsman.viper.net> <3c081d5a_2@Usenet.com>`

```

"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote

> I think one point that was missed in Ken's post is that using a third
party
> tool or using the Win API are NOT mutually exclusive. I have Windows
> applications which utilise both and they work very well.

For most 3rd party tools (at least the ones I am familiar with), this
statement is 100% correct...But in the case of ScreenIO, the approach taken
actually hides the windows message loop from the developer...You could not
get to certain windows messages and notifications...(Unless this has changed
in the last year)...

To me, this is a very serious flaw in their product design...They have
attempted to over simplify true windows development...Without access to the
windows message loop, there are indeed many APIs and even standard windows
functions that could never be used in a ScreenIO application...As a result,
using ScreenIO and "some" native APIs IS indeed mutually exclusive...

A really simple example...

When I looked at ScreenIO, you could not even use a simple windows timer
API...Lets say you wanted to display the clock in the corner of your GUI
screen...The only way to do that was to code a cpu loop...Not something I
would want to do...Windows provides simple APIs to initiate timers...You
could set a timer to expire every second and update your screen clock...But
without access to the windows message queue, you would never know the one
second timer expired...The API works by notifying you that it is time to
update your clock via a simple message sent to you via the application's
message loop...If you can't access the message loop to process the message,
you can't use the API function...

This was just one of many examples of APIs and/or standard windows functions
that could never be used within a ScreenIO application...Windows is a
message and event based operating system...That will never change...If you
do not have access to the application's message loop, you are restricted to
using only the functions and event notifications that ScreenIO passes to the
application...

Windows applications usually are much more than just a pretty user
interface...No 3rd party user interface product can provide all of the
functionality of the windows APIs...

Again, my comments are based on an evaluation of the ScreenIO product one
year ago...Maybe things have changed since then...

kenmullins
newnan, ga USA
```

- **References:** `<9toine$lbj$1@scavenger.euro.net> <3c0596db.936980074@192.168.2.2> <3c02c7c6.0@newsman.viper.net> <3c0702ea_2@news1.prserv.net> <3c07aae2.0@newsman.viper.net> <3c081d5a_2@Usenet.com> <3c08fe4a.0@newsman.viper.net> <3C0919A5.A7F9EB37@shaw.ca>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3C0919A5.A7F9EB37@shaw.ca...
> However, are you using Dialog Editor to create Dialogs or are you doing it
all
> with APIs ?

I use the Dialog Editor to establish the initial client area for a program,
as well as menus, etc...I use the windows API to create a standard
window...Then I use the CreateDialog API to create a modeless dialog using
the resource file from the dialog editor...I then size and scale that
modeless dialog to fit the window...Using this approach, you do not need to
programmatically determine where the control is located, how big it is,
etc...I rarely actually create controls directly via the API...The dialog
editor gives you a much easier way to create controls and design
screens...Remember, that a modeless dialog window procedure is nothing more
than a standard window procedure...It works great...The modeless dialog is
the child of the parent window...

kenmullins
newnan, ga  USA
```

Jan Swaelens wrote:
>> 
> EXEPT : I get Runtime Error 170
>             ->Load failure on file c:\cobol\binr\Adis
> 
> I am using adis, the config program works fine, ... what the hell is wrong ?

You need to link ADIS, ADISINIT and ADISKEY into ADIS.EXE that can be
found in COBDIR or link these into your own execuatable.

For MS 5.0 see page 324 of System Reference.

#### ↳ Re: Cobol: Structured vs. "Drop-Down" Performance

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-12-01T05:41:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C086D67.EDE1C69D@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<e87297f1.0111301911.1e4a08f4@posting.google.com>`

```
Kevin Arthur wrote:

> Without getting into a major philosophical debate on the use of "goto"
> vs. structured programming, can someone just quantify for me the
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
>

*****
Who sez. ? Just a gut feeling - if so, not good enough. Are they measuring
elapsed time between different versions of the same program.
*****

>
> So, down on the bare metal, performance-wise:
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
> Kevin Arthur

Oh my Gawd ! There should be a law banning people like you., (i.e. DARING
to mention yet again the wretched GO TO). It appears to me that your
co-workers have got entrenched into a particular style - and no way Jose,
do they want to give up on that.

The simple answer is BS to the performance - Maintenance, (i.e., clarity
of the coding and segregation into meaningful paragraphs) should be the
first criteria and performance will flow from that. Maintenance wise even
one-time programs can go wrong, or even worse, they finish up as a regular
feature program. I know, I've been there ! Given the ever increasing
speed  of computers, why worry about the odd nano-seconds. (20 years ago
on a Radio Shack a one program COBOL compile could absorb 30 minutes, now
we are down to seconds - and the seconds I'm talking about are Micro
Focus).

Mainframe wise, agreed there will be instances where a program handling
considerably large volumes should be tweaked for performance, but as a
generalization for peace of mind, consider maintenance first.

Consider other aspects of performance, like IFs or EVALUATES - strictly
speaking, we should follow Pareto (20% of conditions account for 80% of
the action), so we should arrange the tests in descending value - but how
many of us actually do that - we think Red, White and Blue and rarely does
it occur to us to arrange the code to test Blue first, because that gets
the most hits in a given situation.

Even, as in your example where you start at the top, your mainline can
still be a set of PERFORMS - and to keep your boss and co-worker happy,
list those PERFORMS in a downwards sequence <G>

Jimmy
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol: Structured vs. "Drop-Down" Performance

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 2001-12-01T17:53:05+11:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C087E51.9BF2DED0@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<e87297f1.0111301911.1e4a08f4@posting.google.com> <3C086D67.EDE1C69D@shaw.ca>`

```
I did tuning work on mainframe COBOL programs for years and never found an
instance where performs made a discernable difference. The instructions per
straight perform are about 4. A typical move will burn up 5-15. It is a
non-issue unless you are really in the very inner CPU intensive loop. In such
a case you probably have to recode in assembler to get worthwhile
improvements in my experience.

The slowest instruction these days is IF, because it causes pipeline breaks.
And CALL. CALL seems to burn up 120-150 instructions and can really hurt you
if overused. Especially dynamic calls. And there are extra instructions per
argument.

If a perform is a hidden IF, it will cost like an IF eg

PERFORM x-para until flag-y.

The 'until' is like an IF.

You have to look at the circumstances. Computer history is full of people
spending $100,000's to save $1 worth of CPU time. How often is the
job/transaction run? How much CPU does it use? At 5c per CPU second, does
anyone care? Sometimes someone does. We had a table access routine that was
called 5,000,000,000 times per night during the critical batch window. That
justified assembler code built on the fly to eliminate pipeline breaks and
keep it all in cache.

Tim Josling

"James J. Gavan" wrote:

> Kevin Arthur wrote:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Kevin Arthur
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol: Structured vs. "Drop-Down" Performance

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-12-01T10:10:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C08AC56.5A56FC1A@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<e87297f1.0111301911.1e4a08f4@posting.google.com>`

```
Kevin Arthur wrote:
> (snip)
> A "PERFORM" causes registers to be pushed onto a stack, a jump
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> can "see" what the code is doing, no?

This is not true for IBM mainframe COBOL.  First of all, the system
has no hardware stack, although it is true that registers may need to
be saved for subroutine calls, and certainly for program CALL's.

Secondly, IBM's COBOL optimizer will move performed code in-line when
it perceives that it will result in a performance improvement.  So the
code you PERFORM may not be located away from the mainline.

Using GO TO does not automatically result in a faster running
program.  Using PERFORM does not automatically result in a slower
running program.  Tim Josling points out that IF and CALL are the most
expensive instuctions.  How can you improve those by using GO TO?

The typical causes of poor runtime performance are bad I/O planning
and poor algorithm design.  You might find some useful tips on
improving COBOL performance here:
http://home.att.net/~arnold.trembley/cob-rte.htm
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol: Structured vs. "Drop-Down" Performance

- **Date:** 2001-12-01T10:00:24-08:00

- **Message-ID:** `<3C091AB8.26A9@paralynx.com>`
- **References:** `<e87297f1.0111301911.1e4a08f4@posting.google.com> <3C08AC56.5A56FC1A@worldnet.att.net>`

```
Arnold Trembley wrote:

> The typical causes of poor runtime performance are bad I/O planning
> and poor algorithm design.  

In the 1970s I worked in Calgary with a man who would bet that he could double 
performance touching nothing but JCL.  He rarely lost the bet.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol: Structured vs. "Drop-Down" Performance

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-12-02T10:20:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C0A006D.11221740@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<e87297f1.0111301911.1e4a08f4@posting.google.com>`

```
Kevin Arthur wrote:
> 
> Without getting into a major philosophical debate on the use of "goto"
> vs. structured programming, can someone just quantify for me the
> (possible) PERFORMANCE difference between the two methodologies?

This was argued about 30 years ago when CPU performance and memory
limitations actually mattered.  The result was that there was no real
difference in performance, even then.
 
> Here's why: The legacy of Cobol coding at the company where I work has
> been that "drop down" programming is faster than structured
> programming (i.e. using "perform" to execute modular paragraphs).

Obviously they learnt something 30 years ago and have had their minds
clamped shut ever since (eg they became managers).

> I recently re-wrote in structured format a program like the above.
> Over time it had simply become an un-maintainable abomination.

Exactly.  Not only that but there is usually no way that an unstructured
mess can be analysed for performance issues and improved.
 
> My boss and a co-worker insist however, that the new program simply
> must be suffering from a performance hit as a result.  

Your boss learnt something 30 years ago and since then has had his mind
clamped shut by becoming a manager.  The lackey co-worker knows which
side bread is buttered.

> My boss is not
> concerned with maintenance; these are programs used for conversions,
> so he considers them one-time programs.  (Major changes are still done
> for new conversions, however, hence my re-write)

Why would _anyone_ care about performance of a one-off conversion ?  The
_only_ issue is that it run correctly first time.  Having to rerun it
once because it wasn't perfect will negate any trivial 'performance
issues' in a thousand runs.

> So, down on the bare metal, performance-wise:

If it is a conversion program, or indeed anything that has file I/O,
then CPU performance is irrelevant to the program run-time, the program
will be spending 99% of the time waiting for data transfers.  In any
computer built in the last quarter century (except possibly the
Commodore 64), the I/O will be overlapped with the CPU using disk
caching or buffering and so the CPU run-time of the program is not even
additive to the file I/O time.

Arguing about GO TO vs PERFORM as a performance issue is a complete
waste of time anyway - it is the algorithms that are important.

The slackest coded quick-sort in interpreted Basic will outperform the
best hand coded assembler bubble sort (given appropriate data).  The
best way to reduce run time is to code so it works first time, every
time.  Small easily understood, separately testable modules, performed
from higher level logic will be more likely to do this (for you).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol: Structured vs. "Drop-Down" Performance

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2001-12-02T00:31:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20011201193106.29762.00001442@nso-cl.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3C0A006D.11221740@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
In article <3C0A006D.11221740@Azonic.co.nz>, Richard Plinston
<riplin@Azonic.co.nz> writes:

>
>Why would _anyone_ care about performance of a one-off conversion ?  The
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Performance in a one-of conversion is still important when the 
base program is used as a shell for every future conversion process
on other files of similar format.
Performance is performance.  Not many people are happy to wait around 
all day for a million record extract/conversion/build process to complete.
Most other post conversion processes are dependent upon verification 
that the converted files balance and are accurate before stepping forward
with the new production files.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol: Structured vs. "Drop-Down" Performance

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@paralynx.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-02T07:13:17-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C0A450D.5FEF@paralynx.com>`
- **References:** `<3C0A006D.11221740@Azonic.co.nz> <20011201193106.29762.00001442@nso-cl.aol.com>`

```
Sff5ky wrote:
> 
> In article <3C0A006D.11221740@Azonic.co.nz>, Richard Plinston
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> all day for a million record extract/conversion/build process to complete.

You'll still be I/O bound in a big conversion, since there is no way that the incoming 
data will be cached.  You can do a hell of a lot of crunching while the next record is 
being fetched.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol: Structured vs. "Drop-Down" Performance

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2001-12-03T04:04:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20011202230426.27633.00001088@nso-cq.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3C0A450D.5FEF@paralynx.com>`

```
In article <3C0A450D.5FEF@paralynx.com>, Ed Guy <ed_guy@paralynx.com> writes:

>
>You'll still be I/O bound in a big conversion, since there is no way that the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I am not sure what you mean by I/O bound and cacheing.  In my experience with 
sequential flat file conversion, I/O performance bottleneck can be mitigated by
proper use of blocking factors and alternate areas which in effect can grab 
multiple records in a single physical access.  Your I/O delay is reduced even
further with the write-behind caching provided by the system resources.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol: Structured vs. "Drop-Down" Performance

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@paralynx.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-02T22:38:45-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C0B1DF5.4F6F@paralynx.com>`
- **References:** `<3C0A450D.5FEF@paralynx.com> <20011202230426.27633.00001088@nso-cq.aol.com>`

```
Sff5ky wrote:
> 
> In article <3C0A450D.5FEF@paralynx.com>, Ed Guy <ed_guy@paralynx.com> writes:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> further with the write-behind caching provided by the system resources.

You can do all sorts of things to mitigate it, BUT you have to physically read it and 
the CPU can be doing your crunching while the read is happening.  This is always 
assuming it's a big file so that you can't grab it all in one gob, but if it was small 
enough that you could, you wouldn't be concerned about performance.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol: Structured vs. "Drop-Down" Performance

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-12-03T18:06:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ugepr$q3l$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<e87297f1.0111301911.1e4a08f4@posting.google.com> <3C0A006D.11221740@Azonic.co.nz>`

```

On  2-Dec-2001, Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

> Why would _anyone_ care about performance of a one-off conversion ?  The
> _only_ issue is that it run correctly first time.  Having to rerun it
> once because it wasn't perfect will negate any trivial 'performance
> issues' in a thousand runs.

Sometimes you have only a small window to run these.  Sort of like tearing
down a bridge over a freeway, you want to do it as fast as possible to not
disrupt traffic...   As fast as possible and still do the job right!

But the speed gains won't be from replacing PERFORM with GO TO, it will be
by understanding your data, understanding your data structure, using such
tools as sorting effectively, and ordering your work optimally.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol: Structured vs. "Drop-Down" Performance

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-12-04T08:06:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C0C83FE.BAC6C3C4@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<e87297f1.0111301911.1e4a08f4@posting.google.com> <3C0A006D.11221740@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
> >Why would _anyone_ care about performance of a one-off conversion ?  The
> >_only_ issue is that it run correctly first time.  Having to rerun it
> >once because it wasn't perfect will negate any trivial 'performance
> >issues' in a thousand runs.
> >

Sff5ky wrote:

> Performance in a one-of conversion is still important when the 
> base program is used as a shell for every future conversion process
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> with the new production files.

You are quite right, even though the context was obvious I should have
qualified it as:

> Why would _anyone_ care about _CPU_ performance of a one-off conversion ?

For reasons already explained the difference in performance between
PERFORM and GO TO is completely insignificant, to the point of being
irrelevant.  This is not just re-arranging the deck chairs on the
Titanic, it is being obsessive about aligning the legs with the joins in
the decking.

Everything in the code should be organised to ensure that the program
runs correctly first time up and will handle every possible exception
cleanly, a rerun will cost more time than even the slackest code could
possibly manage to do.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol: Structured vs. "Drop-Down" Performance

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompsonNOSPAM@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-02T10:53:05-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C0A4E61.E47BDC3E@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<e87297f1.0111301911.1e4a08f4@posting.google.com>`

```
Kevin Arthur wrote:
> 
> Without getting into a major philosophical debate on the use of "goto"
> vs. structured programming, can someone just quantify for me the
> (possible) PERFORMANCE difference between the two methodologies?

[Any one who posts an answer to this, don't do it in an
"interpreted" code gen fashion. Read the full argument
before injecting answers that are meaningless once you get
to the end of the whole answer.]

We must start with a little history and memory theory, so
let's begin with the S/370 machines.

The performance difference is not about cache misses
(although that is a detriment to performance). The problem
is one of locality of reference with regards to Virtual
Storage (VS). So, the bigger the program, the more likely
that code you need to execute is paged out of the system (PC
or Mainframe, you still get swap/trim). It makes no
difference on GOTO or PERFORM with what I'm discussing. And,
we are not going to take into consideration the DATA
DIVISION part of a program as we will assume it would be the
same size regardless of coding style (fall through v.
structured).

That said, let us look at reality. S/370 up to 1990, central
storage was expensive (it was getting progressively
cheaper). Mainframes using VS made 4MB appear to be 16MB
(VSE & VS1 for the whole memory scheme, while MVS did this
per address space). Therefore, at the slower machine speeds
than we have now, a COBOL program would exist partly in a
page data set rather than all of it in memory (assuming a
moderately or greater loaded machine). The perform logic
could not be optimized completely to negate this, so CICS
programmers (We are going back to 1.5 and earlier) generally
found that fall through logic was the fastest. Please
remember that I/O xfer at that point was at best 3MB/sec for
any DASD I/O.

The locality of reference problem here was that, even though
the DAT Buffers (i.e., Dynamic Address Translation
Tables/Cache store) were loaded with all the addresses for
all pages of your program, the moment you lost the dispatch
slice, a new locality of reference took place. NOTICE:
Locality of reference here is with regards to page
boundaries. The next time your program gets executed, DAT
had to rebuild its cache (even though the segment and page
tables were still pointing to page frames and not DASD).
This is a time consuming thing which causes your dispatch
slice to spend time recovering. Now let us make this worse
and assume that you did not get dispatched again until after
two memory cleanup cycles (I'm sorry, I've forgotten what
this is called in S/370-z/Architecture terms). This then
causes page trim (your pages haven't been touched for x
seconds, so they get scheduled to a page data set).
Therefore, a fall through program would perform better than
a program with performs of paragraphs that are on/in
different pages.

Now we have a new locality of reference problem. Every time
we attempt to resolve a Virtual Address, we find that the
page table entry is "invalid" and so we have to schedule a
page to be brought back into storage. While this is going
on, you have lost your dispatch cycle. Now do you see why a
structured program that did not use GOTO would cause CICS
some serious performance hits? If you don't, I can continue
with this and show you how a program that is large enough to
take over 12 page frames can cause a CICS region (or any
other for that matter) to cause something called thrashing.
Particularly if it is written top down structured with
performs all over the place. 

That was YESTERDAY's problems. We need to look at TODAY and
the machines we have TODAY.

Today you can't find a mainframe with less than 64MB of
C-Store (RAM to the rest of you). So the VS locality of
reference problems gets more focus on the cache misses. GOTO
or GOTO-less programming will generally suffer the same.
[There are some problems where a COBOL program written in a
fall through fashion will see certain performance boots, but
there aren't that many that are worth the attendant
headaches.]

Now let us add the idea of cached DASD controllers and
faster I/O channels and you negate some of the VS
performance issues.

Next take a machine and give it more than 128MB of RAM
(C-Store for the mainframe folks) and the paging issues
actually become moot (especially when you consider E-store).
Add to this the data xfer rates that are now above 16MB/sec
and reduced latency, etc. etc. and paging is much much
faster (assuming you even have a paging problem). And the
advent of larger DAT Buffers (which was promoted by MDF and
now PR/SM).

Now let look back to about 1995 and mainframes with 2GB
C-store and 2GB E-store. Your program is how large compared
to everything else that is running? The processor speed is
what compared to a S370/168MP?

About 1998 we have mainframes with over 300MIPS, PCs with
greater than 500MHz clocks and most machines with more RAM
than good sense (OK, micros**t platforms do suffer from
serious code bloat).

At this point in time, one is much better off to write any
program for maintainability than for raw CPU speed
(exception, the kind of code that I sometimes get to write
-- SVCs & PC routines that become part of the O/S, but then
I do this in ALC). With optimization done by the compiler
and memory management that has been greatly enhanced within
OS/390 (I won't even speak to micros**t in this area),
structured programming only makes sense today (this from a
die hard GOTO user).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol: Structured vs. "Drop-Down" Performance

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-12-03T12:45:57+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c0abe65_8@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<e87297f1.0111301911.1e4a08f4@posting.google.com> <3C0A4E61.E47BDC3E@ix.netcom.com>`

```
Good exposition there, Steve.

It took me back... to the days when Virtual storage was invented.

As a young programmer imbued with zeal and idealism I made it my business to
completely understand as much as possible about the system side of 360 (and
, later, of course, 370)

When the first VS OSes appeared many of us were just dismayed...let me
explain why.

Computer Programming in those days was an art...There were 2 primary
constraints:

1. Space.

Ths was expensive and limited (as you described). The idea of "paging" code,
even with a very fast (ha, ha!) drum device was anathema. Overlaying was
something we did very carefully and organised our "segment limits" (old
COBOL) with great regard to the processing flow of our programs. How could
the system software possibly do as good a job as we did?

2. Time.

Even the fastest processors available were slower than a PC-AT. I remember
when I first found out that core storage took 1.5 microseconds to access (on
a 360-30 in 1966) how impressed I was and how long it took to get my head
round the fact that disk IO was thousands of time slower. Today the memory
in the Notebook I am writing this on is around 2000 times faster than
that...

The "art" was in balancing the two constraints. You couldn't teach it like a
science. It required imagination and sensitivity and skill and judgement.

If you gave a program more "space" (took more memory) it usually ran faster.
Just like today, we were always trying to get things to run as quickly as
possible, so we spent many hours optimising our code within the available
constraints. (No optimizing compilers to do it for us; "good" COBOL
programmers understood the Assembler code generated and experimented with
ways to get the compiler to generate better code...organise WS to avoid data
conversion, use MVI instructions to set flags and CLC to test them because
it was cheaper than arithmetic, and all that stuff (to this day my flags are
always "pic x", on ANY platform...<G>). I was very interested to see exactly
 this kind of exchange being posted here in CLC under the "unsigned packed
numeric" topic...It is good to see that there are still people around who
care about this stuff...<G>)

But, as with most things, there was a necessity to "dumb it down". So they
invented Virtual Storage which removed the space constraint (well, it did in
theory...<G>) and the natural evolution of technology took care of providing
more power. No need for "art" any more, just teach the language and let them
loose... the system will cope with the inefficiency.

As you can imagine, those of us who took our work seriously (the intensity
of the young is kind of frightening, isn't it <G>) were totally dismayed by
this new proud release from IBM. We fought a tooth and nail rearguard action
to discredit the new technology (doesn't that sound familiar?...nothing
really changes <G>).

The first thing was to understand how it worked and then write programs that
would give it a real hard time...

A couple of us revamped a certain production suite to ensure that the
locality of reference which Steve described was as inefficient as
possible... The run time went from 14 minutes to over an hour. Much wailing
and gnashing of teeth from Management and we just looked smug and said
"Well, that's the new IBM technology you insist on buying..." Of course we
couldn't get away with it indefinitely, and I later made quite a lot of
money optimising systems that were suffering due to improper use of locality
of reference (and bad I/O)... Make the Poacher a Gamekeeper <G>...Today, I
understand that the optimizing compilers do all this for you... I have mixed
feelings about that.

Thanks for the post Steven.

Brought back some happy memories of working at the coalface with other
"miners" who were characters... I guess today the coalface has moved and the
challenge is in better application understanding, but there is still a small
place for the kind of skills we acquired then.

I have deliberately refrained from entering the "structured" vs "drop down"
debate because I believe it is pointless. It is pointless for the reasons
you explained. Today's systems will handle either model with many times the
efficiency that was available in the middle decades of the last century.

So the primary concern HAS to be maintainability of code.

(That's one reason why I use OO...<G>)

I believe this thread started because the original poster was faced with
Management stuck in the 70s.

My advice: Don't worry about it.

It is their money, let them call the shots. You've explained your case and
given it your best shot. Respect their right to be wrong. During the course
of your IT career you will encounter MANY cases where Management get it
wrong.

Deal with it.

As long as you are taking their money, give them the best advice you can,
but don't lose sleep over it...

It is only computer programming  <G>.

Pete.

Steve Thompson <sthompsonNOSPAM@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:3C0A4E61.E47BDC3E@ix.netcom.com...

#### ↳ Re: Cobol: Structured vs. "Drop-Down" Performance

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-12-03T18:00:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ugeej$pqe$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<e87297f1.0111301911.1e4a08f4@posting.google.com>`

```

On 30-Nov-2001, j6vflbl6vy6g8o001@sneakemail.com (Kevin Arthur) wrote:

> Without getting into a major philosophical debate on the use of "goto"
> vs. structured programming, can someone just quantify for me the
> (possible) PERFORMANCE difference between the two methodologies?

When I started, IBM recommended that we use ALTER GO TO statements to
improve performance.

Now I measure performance differently.  My costs are related to improving
performance of the most expensive elements of programming:

Opportunity cost
Reliability
Maintenance cost


When there is a conflict between these and machine cycles, machine cycles
loses the battle.

However, these days optimizing compilers have improved to the extent that
most of my machine optimization is done by having a good understanding of
I/O, especially database structure.  Even simple efficiencies such as
initializing a variable before doing the loop 1000 times can be taken care
of by the optimizer (but I do them anyway).

One of the more interesting coding efficiencies I have done was with
non-compiled Basic.  The first statement was a goto, followed by commonly
executed paragraphs then the main code after.  This was because finding that
common paragraph took time back then.

At any rate - concentrate on improving your I/O and your execution
efficiency will receive its greatest gains.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol: Structured vs. "Drop-Down" Performance

- **From:** "Scott Dickinson" <sddickinson@attbi.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-04T04:52:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YDYO7.4459$TZ1.8848@rwcrnsc51>`
- **References:** `<e87297f1.0111301911.1e4a08f4@posting.google.com>`

```
Kevin,

I can't give you specifics on the number of microseconds of cpu time a goto
versus perform statement takes.  I do know from experience that a goto
statement is more efficient than a perform statement.  We use the goto
extensively in online COBOL programs where efficiency is the main concern.

For a onetime conversion program that runs in a batch environment, where
maintainability is not an issue, it probably does not matter how you code
the program.

My only warning is that you can develop bad coding habits if you fail to use
structured coding techniques.  It does not take that much extra time to
structure your code and the time you save testing and debugging a program,
even a onetime program, will be worth the effort.

"Kevin Arthur" <j6vflbl6vy6g8o001@sneakemail.com> wrote in message

> vs. structured programming, can someone just quantify for me the
…[79 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol: Structured vs. "Drop-Down" Performance (THANK YOU!!)

- **From:** Ed_Stevens@nospam.noway.nohow (Ed Stevens)
- **Date:** 2001-12-06T14:09:15+00:00

  ueber  Re: Hi, Jackson Structured Programming??

>> But is there anyone that can enlighten me about 'Jackson structured
>> programming techniques'.

  Jackson explained how to do "structured programming", i.e. proper  
loops, with the old COBOL before 1985, just using GOTO commands. An  
absolut horror in my view. With todays COBOL compilers this is  
absolutely not necessary.

  Interesting might be how Jackson explained BACKTRACKING, sort of Stack  
handling in COBOL


Yours,

## Thread: search vs. sequential file — 18 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### search vs. sequential file

- **From:** "Full Rms" <nope@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-01T17:34:15+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c090679$0$27940$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be>`

```
I would like to search in a sequential file. I've created a database called
'spelers'. In this db there are a number of people with different data.
Now I want to search after the name.  (I know this isn't quite good in a
sequential file, but nevermind that!! :-) )
My problem now is that COBOL is going to read the database, line after line.

        IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
      *************************
        PROGRAM-ID.  zoek_speler.
        date-written. 2001-11-26.
      *Author                FullRms.

        ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
      **********************
        CONFIGURATION SECTION.
        SOURCE-COMPUTER. pc.
        OBJECT-COMPUTER. pc.

        INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
        FILE-CONTROL.
        SELECT OPTIONAL spelerbest
                 ASSIGN TO "spelers"
                 ORGANIZATION is line sequential
                 ACCESS MODE sequential
                 FILE STATUS IS spelerstat.

        DATA DIVISION.
      ***************
        FILE SECTION.
        FD spelerbest.
        01 speler-rec.
           03 snaam                pic x(16).
                   88 speler-ok value "/".
           03 svnaam               pic x(15).
           03 snr                  pic 99.
                   88 nr-ok value 1 thru 18.
           03 straat       pic x(15).
           03 nr           pic x(4).
           03 postcode     pic 9(4).
           03 gemeente     pic x(20).
           03 telefoonnr   pic x(12).
           03 geboortedat.
                   05 dag          pic 9(2).
                           88 dag-ok value 1 thru 31.
                   05 maand        pic 9(2).
                           88 maand-ok value 1 thru 12.
                   05 jaar         pic 9(4).
           03 ploeg.
                   05 categorie    pic x(4).
                           88 cat-ok value "DUIV" "PREM" "MIN" "KAD"
                           "SCHO" "JUN" "RES" "EERS".
                   05 elftal       pic x.
                           88 elf-ok value "A" "B" "C".
           03 aantdoelpnt          pic 9(3).
           03 aantwedstrd          pic 9(3).
           03 aantseiz             pic 99.

       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       77 naamploeg   pic x(15) value "F.C. Katastroof".
       77 spelerstat pic xx.
       77 sw-eof     pic 9.
       77 wacht      pic x.
       01 datum.
           03 jaar    pic 9(4).
           03 maand   pic 99.
           03 dag     pic 99.
       01 datum-e.
           03 dag     pic 99/.
           03 maand   pic 99/.
           03 jaar    pic 9(4).
       77 zoeknaam    pic x(16).

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
      ********************
       0-overzicht.
           accept datum from century-date
           move corresponding datum to datum-e.
           open input spelerbest
           initialize sw-eof
           perform 1-scherm
           read spelerbest
               at end
                   display "leeg bestand, kan niets afdrukken"
                           at 2040
                   accept wacht at 2480
                   end-accept
               not at end
                   perform until sw-eof = 1
                       read spelerbest
                           at end move 1 to sw-eof
**************************************************************
Here above occures my problem.  COBOL reads the whole database and then
performs paragraph 'check'
How can I make that COBOL reads, verify if zoeknaam = snaam and continues if
this isn't so?
It is obvious that now only the last record is present in snaam.
When I search for the first record, COBOL isn't going to find it... and I
really don't know how to solve this problem...
**************************************************************
                          perform check
                       end-read
                   end-perform
           end-read
           close spelerbest
           Exit Program.
           Stop Run.

       1-scherm.
           display spaces erase screen
                   naamploeg                               at 0101
                   "Zoeken op naam van de spelers /scherm" at 0127
                   datum-e                                 at 0170
                   all "-" size 80                         at 0201
                   "Spelernaam         :"                  at 0505.
           accept zoeknaam at 0525.

       2-gevonden.
           display spaces erase screen
                   naamploeg                               at 0101
                   "Zoeken op naam van de spelers /scherm" at 0127
                   datum-e                                 at 0170
                   all "-" size 80                         at 0201
                   "Spelersnaam        :"                  at 0505
                   "Spelersvoornaam    :"                  at 0605
                   "Spelersnummer      :"                  at 0705
                   "Adres              :"                  at 0805
                   "Straat  :"                             at 0827
                   "Nr      :"                             at 0927
                   "Postcode:"                             at 1027
                   "Gemeente:"                             at 1044
                   "Telefoon           :"                  at 1105
                   "Geboortedatum      :"                  at 1205
                   "Ploeg :"                               at 1305
                   "Categorie  :"                          at 1313
                   "Elftal     :"                          at 1413
                   "Aantal doelpunten  :"                  at 1505
                   "Aantal wedstijden  :"                  at 1605
                   "Aantal seizoenen   :"                  at 1705.

       2-toon.
           display snaam           at 0526
                   svnaam          at 0626
                   snr             at 0726
                   straat          at 0838
                   nr              at 0938
                   postcode        at 1038
                   gemeente        at 1054
                   telefoonnr      at 1126
                   geboortedat     at 1226
                   categorie       at 1326
                   elftal          at 1426
                   aantdoelpnt     at 1526
                   aantwedstrd     at 1626
                   aantseiz        at 1726.
           accept wacht            at 2455.
           move spaces to zoeknaam.

       2-ntgevonden.
           Display "De naam die je ingaf, kan niet worden teruggevonden
      -            "in de database.  Probeer het opnieuw!" at 2005
           accept wacht at 2480.
           perform 1-scherm.

       check.
           if snaam = zoeknaam
                           perform 2-gevonden
                           perform 2-toon
                       else perform 2-ntgevonden
                       end-if.

Thx for helping

Full Rms
```

#### ↳ Re: search vs. sequential file

- **From:** "Willem Clements" <willem@horizontes-informatica.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-01T18:46:58+01:00

```
On Sat, 1 Dec 2001 17:34:15 +0100, Full Rms wrote:

>I would like to search in a sequential file. I've created a database called
>'spelers'. In this db there are a number of people with different data.
…[171 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Hi,

You have included the "perform check" statement in the "at end" clause of the
read statement, and so only performs this check at the end of the file.
Move the perform out of the read (just below the "end-read") and look what
happens.

You might want to change one more little thing. If you have found your
record, you don't have to read the rest of the file I suppose

Veel succes met het testen


Willem Clements
Brainbench MVP for COBOL II
http://www.brainbench.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: search vs. sequential file

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-12-01T21:08:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C09473E.C2D8B398@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3c090679$0$27940$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gnovea2.pminews@News.CIS.DFN.DE>`

```


Willem Clements wrote:

> On Sat, 1 Dec 2001 17:34:15 +0100, Full Rms wrote:
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I see Willem has already solved your problem - but he understands double-Dutch
better than I do <G>. You were SOoooo close !

I take it you are experimenting, not writing a 'live' program ? Couple of points
worth noting :-

Your record layout indicates a Vendor/Customer Name and Address record,  i.e. it
is not a transaction file, but rather a Masterfile - traditionally handled as an
ISAM not Sequential or Line Sequential. Imagine if American Express used your
technique the zillions of records they would have to read before they got to
Mols, Swaans, van den Ackker or van Heusen. (You have got your file stored on
disk but think of a sequential in terms of reel(s) of magnetic tape).

Firstly use an ISAM file with a Customer Number PrimeKey, then use snaam (Last
Name or Family Name ?) as an Alternate Key WITH DUPLICATES. Pseudo code :-

        Open I-O ISAM File
        set Program NotFinis to true
        perform until ProgramFinis
            display Empty Screen Record
            accept snaam

            if   snaam <> spaces
                read ISAM File key is snaam
                       invalid key - display "not in file message"
                       not invalid key perform display CustomerRecord
                 End-read

            else set Program Finis to true
                    (OR if you have the feature - EXIT  PERFORM)
            end-if
    end-perform
    close ISAM File
    Stop Run

Note the above lets you do more than one query and the program terminates when
you enter an snaam of spaces.

PS: If you have a compiler with Screen Section - you could make it easier than
your two paragraphs 2-gevonden and 2-toon.

PPS: Curious which compiler you are using, Micro Focus Net Express didn't like
the following from your code :-

        PROGRAM-ID.  zoek_speler.  *** didn't like the underscore
        accept datum from century-date
        display spaces erase screen

"Veel succes met het testen" - I agree, the program was nicely put together.
Verstaant U <G>

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: search vs. sequential file

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2001-12-01T16:17:14-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0112011617.144e51c4@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3c090679$0$27940$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gnovea2.pminews@News.CIS.DFN.DE>`

```
"Willem Clements" <willem@horizontes-informatica.com> wrote in message news:<jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gnovea2.pminews@News.CIS.DFN.DE>...
> On Sat, 1 Dec 2001 17:34:15 +0100, Full Rms wrote:
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

> http://www.brainbench.com

rest of post can be seen in original message

Hello Full, I largely agree with Willem's suggestion, but doing
exactly as he says will still mean that you always miss the first
record, so do the perform check just before the second read instead of
after it.  If the test is successful in check, you can set sw-eof to
'1' there so that no further records are read as suggested by willem.

Regards Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: search vs. sequential file

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-12-03T18:58:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C0BCBCF.DAF4DC86@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3c090679$0$27940$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gnovea2.pminews@News.CIS.DFN.DE> <fcd09c56.0112011617.144e51c4@posting.google.com> <3c0a0550$0$24140$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be> <vZvO7.69929$XM4.5808@afrodite.telenet-ops.be> <3C0ABB0C.E29D8D6C@shaw.ca> <AQGO7.70159$XM4.5950@afrodite.telenet-ops.be>`

```


Full Rms wrote:

> > Create a test file with the records in EXACTLY the following sequence :-
> > Now search, (i.e. accept zoeknaam), in the following sequence :-
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Could I use the perform statement for this?

Full,

First an answer to your question - Yes do a PERFORM. Try and avoid doing the
same thing in different places, particularly EXITs or STOP RUNs - for clarity
make programs PERFORM those routines. I'm glad to see you have included
copyfiles - that makes it so much easier for you to adapt to other programs.
Also you have under 'check' Paragraph, isolated your file handling routine -
that's a good way to go.

Now I have your complete program and have compiled it - I see it works, and
does it just fine. BUT - you are only doing one enquiry. Assume you had some
three or four teams of soccer players and there are some 60 people involved -
that means 60 runs of the program to search for each individual.

Note also that if I enter a blank zoeknaam - you still search all the records
in the file.
You can overcome that one by :-

        accept zoeknaam
        if zoeknaam = spaces
           EXIT PROGRAM

To have more than one enquiry against your sequential file you would have to do
the
following :-

    accept zoeknaam

    if zoeknaam <> spaces
       perform SEARCH-ROUTINE

   else EXIT PROGRAM
   end-if.

SEARCH-ROUTINE.

    open input spelerbest
    do search
    close spelerbest

That way each "Open" gets you to the beginning of the file, which was one of
your queries. But it isn't a particularly successful solution - and in the real
world, you would be put against a wall and shot <G>.

Seeing that you mentioned this was part of a project to edit, query and print -
then the only satisfactory solution is an ISAM file. I'm not going to take up
bandwidth here on how to do that, but if you would like more information, write
to me privately. BUT first check with your COBOL teacher so that he/she doesn't
get into a snit <G>.

I think your teacher has done a good job putting the coding concepts across and
you seem to have a good handle on what you have learned so far - plus I think
your written English is EXCELLENT !

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

Howard Brazee wrote:

> On  3-Dec-2001, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> There are situations where I would consider a table instead of a keyed file.

Sorry - not this one Howard. He wants to develop the concept of a traditional
database, whether we were talking COBOL files or a DB. No doubt this was a first
stab, (and he's done a bloody good job !),  and the teacher is going to take
them through to ISAMs - softly, softly, catchee monkee. Given a traditional ISAM
approach he could totally ignore this search as a separate entity :-

        PrimeKey = initials e.g. (Howard Brazee = "HB" and yours truly "JJG")

and using a four-character key the initials can be unique by introducing 'Middle
Initial" where necessary. Take following :-

    Gus van Heusen                    GVH
    Jerry van Heusen                    JVH
    Kick van den Accker            KVDA
    Tom van den Ackker            TVDA

Not too difficult to remember, and makes more sense than keying in "van den
Ackker", plus of course problem where there are two or more van den Ackkers.

    display EmptyScreenRecord
    accept PrimeKey

     if Prmekey <> spaces
        read using PrimeKey

        if RecFound
           set RecordFound to true
            set RecordNotChanged to true
            display fullScreenRecord

        else Accept FullScreenRecord

                if LastName = spaces
                   end input routine

               else  validate input - and spit out error messages
        End-if

    On a screen equivalent of the Windows OKButton :-

    if Record Changed

        if RecordFound
            rewrite

        else write
    End-if


Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: search vs. sequential file

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2001-12-02T12:27:11-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0112021227.52f1da39@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3c090679$0$27940$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gnovea2.pminews@News.CIS.DFN.DE> <fcd09c56.0112011617.144e51c4@posting.google.com> <3c0a0550$0$24140$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be>`

```
Hello Full, I don't know why the program just stops, but assuming that
2-ntgevonded is producing an error message to say that the name hasn't
been found, the check procedure should not perform 2-ntgevonden, this
must be done after all records have been read.  Thinking about the
problem some more, your program should have an additional switch such
as sw-found, which will also need initialising.  The procedure check
would set this switch instead, then you would have a conditional test
just before closing spelerbest such as

if sw-found not = '1'
   perform 2-ntgevonden
end-if

Also rather than just testing sw-eof, your perform until should
instead be

perform until sw-eof = '1' or sw-found = '1'.

Hope it helps, regards Robert

"Full Rms" <nope@hotmail.com> wrote in message news:<3c0a0550$0$24140$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be>...
> > Hello Full, I largely agree with Willem's suggestion, but doing
> > exactly as he says will still mean that you always miss the first
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…

Full, the problem is your first time routine.  You might change it to
this:

         perform until sw-eof = 1
             read spelerbest
                   at end
                       move 1 to sw-eof
                       display "leeg bestand, kan niets afdrukken"
                               at 2040
                       accept wacht at 2480
                       end-accept
               not at end
                       perform check
               end-read
               end-perform
           
*  Delete second end-read


Regards,

##### ↳ ↳ Re: search vs. sequential file

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-12-03T18:19:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ugfho$qjh$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3c090679$0$27940$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be> <jvyyrzubevmbagrfvasbezngvpnpbz.gnovea2.pminews@News.CIS.DFN.DE>`

```
A common way of reading a file is as follows:


Read first record.
Perform read-loop until EOF.
...


Read-loop.
    Test data.
     process data.
     Read next record.
>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>><<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<

There obviously has to be some AT-END  or status code checking to set EOF. 
(if you have multiple files, use multiple status codes or EOF switches. 
Never use the same one for multiple purposes if you want to keep maintenance
programmers from messing up in the future.

Some people say having two reads isn't "structured", but changing to two
performs is not a real gain.

One advantage to this style is that there are no real switches to be set and
checked from within code.  EOF is set only immediately before it is checked.
```

---

<a id="t-f15577cec9"></a>
## Thread: Screen Scraping Works, BUT... — 1 msgs

### Screen Scraping Works, BUT...

- **From:** Greg Ferro <gferro@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-01T18:50:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C092773.E6F113ED@sprynet.com>`

```
There was a screen scraping debacle at a major credit card company
several years ago.

A user department was tired of using 3270 emulation on their PC's, so
they simply hired some consultants to perform screen scraping and
produce a GUI interface to the legacy system.

Unfortunately, the user department didn't inform the Information Systems
department that they were now screen scraping.

When the IS department made cosmetic changes to the mainframe screen
formats, screen scraping stopped working and so did the user
department!  When the users complained to Quality Assurance, QA checked
the screens using 3270 terminal emulation.  Finding no problems, QA
closed out the trouble tickets as "problem not reproducible".

If you are going to use screen scraping, make sure the mainframe people
know what you are doing!
```

## Thread: FREE inline date-math subr - only TWO LINES long. Wow! — 23 this month / 24 total

_Topics: [`Date and calendar processing`](topics.md#dates)_

### FREE inline date-math subr - only TWO LINES long. Wow!

- **From:** bszyszko@rochester.rr.com (Blaine Zee)
- **Date:** 2001-12-02T08:30:46-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.pop,comp.lang.rexx
- **Message-ID:** `<7789cf15.0112020830.2e5db942@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello, world.

Use my program below to calculate datevals INLINE without all the overhead
of calling a system API, function or subr... Easily written in any language. 

--------

REM Inline pseudocode example for date math from 12/30/1923 to 10/11/2197.
REM Output is 0-99999 where 0=Sunday, 1=Monday, etc. Short int to 6/3/2103.

REM To get Excel, Lotus or other dateval, add 8765:


INT INLINE FUNCTION dateval(INT m,INT d,INT y)

DIM ar(12) AS INT {2,33,61,92,122,153,183,214,245,275,306,336}


10   dateval = INT((y - 1924) * 1461 \4) + ar(m) + d

20   IF m < 3 THEN dateval = dateval - (yr MOD 4)

END FUNCTION

REM How long is YOUR date math routine??
--------

*
A photon travels from me to the mirror. Another one travels from the
mirror to me. Relative to one another, they are moving at TWICE the speed
of light. So much for Einstein's relativity theory then.

Happy Holidays. If you are in the 146xx Zip Code, look me up and send
a card - if you want to share more tips, or if you play chess,
or if you are a S36/AS400/VB tinkerer like me. Include a lottery ticket
just for fun. Hey, you never know. Don't e-mail though, my inbox is clogged.
*
```

#### ↳ Re: FREE inline date-math subr - only TWO LINES long. Wow!

- **From:** "David Frank" <dave_frank@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-03T16:59:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.pop,comp.lang.rexx
- **Message-ID:** `<PbOO7.187158$zK1.49893816@typhoon.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<7789cf15.0112020830.2e5db942@posting.google.com>`

```
program test ! Fliegel and VanFlandern's julian date algorithms
implicit none
interface
  function jday2date (jday)  result (date)
    integer :: jday, date(3)
  end function
end interface

integer :: jday, date(3)
integer :: yyyy=2001, mm=12, dd=03   ! date of this test

! 1-line statement (date to julian day)
jday = dd-32075+1461*(yyyy+4800+(mm-14)/12)/4
+367*(mm-2-(mm-14)/12*12)/12 -3*((yyyy+4900+(mm-14)/12)/100)/4

! julian day to date
write (*,91) jday2date(jday)   ! outputs 2001/12/03

91 format (i4,2('/',i2.2))
end program

function jday2date (jday)  result (date)
implicit none
integer :: jday, date(3), i,j,m,n, yyyy, mm, dd

m = jday+68569
n = 4*m/146097
m = m-(146097*n+3)/4
i = 4000*(m+1)/1461001
m = m-1461*i/4+31
j = 80*m/2447
dd = m-2447*j/80
m = j/11
mm = j+2-12*m
yyyy = 100*n+i+m-4900
date(1) = yyyy ; date(2) = mm ; date(3) = dd
end function
```

#### ↳ Re: FREE inline date-math subr - only TWO LINES long. Wow!

Blaine Zee wrote:
> 
> Hello, world.
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> of light. So much for Einstein's relativity theory then.

Go back and re-read what Einstein said. He said that the speed of light
appears constant to all observers. You are the observer and both
photons' velocities will appear to be 'c' to you. If you are one of the
photons traveling at 'c' then time dilation takes over and the other
photon appears to be approaching you at 'c' also. 

Don't confuse closure rate with 'appearance' of velocity.


> 
> Happy Holidays. If you are in the 146xx Zip Code, look me up and send
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

##### ↳ ↳ Re: FREE inline date-math subr - only TWO LINES long. Wow!

- **From:** "Tony T. Warnock" <u091889@cic-mail.lanl.gov>
- **Date:** 2001-12-04T07:45:24-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.pop,comp.lang.rexx
- **Message-ID:** `<3C0CE184.4F32CE4E@cic-mail.lanl.gov>`
- **References:** `<7789cf15.0112020830.2e5db942@posting.google.com> <9uhrv1$419$1@suaar1ac.prod.compuserve.com>`

```
      FUNCTION juldate (year,month,day)
c
c   Converts DAY/MONTH/YEAR to a Julian date transformed to 2000
c
       IMPLICIT NONE
c
      INTEGER juldate, day, month, year
c
      juldate=367*year-7*(year+(month+9)/12)/4-3*((year+(month-9)/7)/100

     1 +1)/4+275*month/9+day-730516
      END
```

#### ↳ Re: FREE inline date-math subr - only TWO LINES long. Wow!

- **From:** bszyszko@rochester.rr.com (Blaine Zee)
- **Date:** 2001-12-19T21:41:45-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.pop,comp.lang.rexx
- **Message-ID:** `<7789cf15.0112192141.6057698d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<7789cf15.0112020830.2e5db942@posting.google.com>`

```
bszyszko@rochester.rr.com (Blaine  Zee) wrote in message news:<7789cf15.0112020830.2e5db942@posting.google.com>...
> 
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> END FUNCTION

Thousands of queries and re-posts on this routine wrongly claim that
it does not handle leap years, leap centuries, etc. correctly.

It does, it is just not apparent in the code. The purpose of this subr
was to not code for such conditions, while still keeping true to them.
Thus the reader cannot see such exception handling yet the datevals
compute properly.

More notes/feedback:

1: yr mod 4 was meant to be "y mod 4". Most figured this out.

2. If you have some static memory lying around, you can replace the
code of:

   INT((y - 1924) * 1461 \ 4)

with an array lookup (in FORTRAN or other HL languages) or a
based-index pointer or XLAT function in low-levels, thereby dropping
the MULT and the IDIV!

   such as ARRAY(Y) + ar(m) + d. You'd need to precalculate the array
values and store them as static vals as DATA:

DIM ARRAY(1274) AS INTEGER {1923*0, v1, v2...vn} STATIC
COMMON INTEGER*4 ARRAY(1274) /1923*0, v1, v2...vn/
  and so on depending on the language used.

3. Element Zero: Some compiler models choose array base "0" and "1"
depending upon configuration. Use DIMENSION (1 to nnnn) in Fortran or
(OPTION BASE 1) in basic-compatibles.

Or define the (ar) array subscript as:

0,33,61,92,122,153,183,214,245,275,306,336

with ar[0] being zero (in cases such as C++)

- Blaine Z
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: FREE inline date-math subr - only TWO LINES long. Wow!

- **From:** "James Giles" <jamesgiles@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-12-20T09:55:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.pop,comp.lang.rexx
- **Message-ID:** `<xAiU7.233391$3d2.11173656@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<7789cf15.0112020830.2e5db942@posting.google.com> <7789cf15.0112192141.6057698d@posting.google.com>`

```

"Blaine Zee" <bszyszko@rochester.rr.com> wrote in message
news:7789cf15.0112192141.6057698d@posting.google.com...
...
> > INT INLINE FUNCTION dateval(INT m,INT d,INT y)
> >
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> it does not handle leap years, leap centuries, etc. correctly.

By direct test it can be unambiguously stated that it
*doesn't* handle these things correctly.

First: assume that \4 really means divide by 4 (I've
never seen \ used as divide before in any language)
and that yr was really supposed to be y.

If you give it today's date: Dec. 20, 2001 the program
gives 28480.  Now give it a date 4 centuries from now:
Dec. 20, 2401.  The program gives 174580.  The difference
between these two numbers should be the number of days
between the two dates.  The difference is 146100.  But
the number of days between those two dates is actually
146097.

This is because the program assumes that all years
divisible by 4 are leap years, but the years 2100,
2200, and 2300 are *not* leap years.

Further tests reveal additional problems.

Dec. 31, 2001 gives day number 28491.  Jan. 01, 2002
gives day number 28490.  They are consecutive days,
but your program claims they are in reverse order!

Jan. 01, 1924 gives day number 3.  Jan. 01, 1925 gives
367.  But, the two days are 366 days apart (1924 was a leap
year).  Your program claims they were 364 days apart.
Where are the two other days?

Take a specific, very simple, example to analyze more
carefully:  Feb. 29, 1924 and Mar. 01, 1924.  Since
the year is 1924, the first term in line 10 is zero, and
the year mod 4 is also zero which means that line 20
does nothing.  So, your day numbers are simply
ar[m] + d.  Well, for Feb. 29, ar(m) is 33 and d is 29,
so the day number is 62.  For Mar. 01, ar(m) is 61 and
d is 1, so the day number is 62.  So, your program claims
they are the same day.  But, 1924 was a leap year, so Feb. 29
was a legitimate date.

By direct observation of the answers your program
generates we can all see that it has serious problems.
Before cross-posting to 5 different newsgroups that
all the people that have responded to your program
are wrong, why not actually test it?

A Fortran version that's similar to yours in concept,
but should give correct answers is:

      integer function dateval(m, d, y)
      integer, intent(in):: m, d, y
      integer, parameter:: ar(12) = (/365, 396, 59, 90, 120, 151, 181, &
         212, 243, 273, 304, 334/)
      integer :: yr

      yr = y
      if (m < 3) yr = y-1
      dateval = yr*365 + yr/4 - yr/100 + yr/400 +ar(m) + d + 1721060

      return
      end

Remember that in Fortran, the quotient of two integers
is an integer, so truncation with an INT function is not
necessary here.  With so many language's newsgroups
cross-posted, it's best to point these things out.

This function obviously doesn't test for the validity
of the input, so you can feed it nonsense (like
15/45/-2002 or something).  A month that's out
of range though might trigger an error depending
on whether the program is compiled with array
bounds checking turned on.

The answer is correct for any date in the Gregorian calendar
(so, dates after Oct. 15, 1582 are the only valid ones).
Obviously this is only true if the calculation doesn't
overflow your integers.  The return value is the Julian
Date (which begins at noon), and is based on the
epoch of Jan. 01, 4713 BC as day 1.

You can count from any epoch you like by changing that
large constant.  For example, to use Jan. 01, 1924 as day
one, replace 1721060 with -702725.

There are several ways to do this same calculation.  Many
are very clever and dispense with the need for an auxilliary
array and/or use fewer operations.  But, with memory so cheap
these days (and CPU cycles cheap too), there seems to be
no reason to adopt anything other than a clear statement
of the actual rules of the calendar.  At least it's easy to
verify that it's right.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: FREE inline date-math subr - only TWO LINES long. Wow!

- **From:** "Postman" <Nobody@st-abbs.fsnet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-12-30T13:29:16
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.pop,comp.lang.rexx
- **Message-ID:** `<a0n56p$6og$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7789cf15.0112020830.2e5db942@posting.google.com> <7789cf15.0112192141.6057698d@posting.google.com> <xAiU7.233391$3d2.11173656@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
| First: assume that \4 really means divide by 4 (I've
| never seen \ used as divide before in any language)
| and that yr was really supposed to be y.


Try VB ... \ 4 is integer divide

9 \ 4 = 2  !!!!!


Does VB count as a language?


BoT
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: FREE inline date-math subr - only TWO LINES long. Wow!

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-12-20T16:24:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.pop,comp.lang.rexx
- **Message-ID:** `<9vt361$dh9$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<7789cf15.0112020830.2e5db942@posting.google.com> <7789cf15.0112192141.6057698d@posting.google.com>`

```

On 19-Dec-2001, bszyszko@rochester.rr.com (Blaine  Zee) wrote:

> Thousands of queries and re-posts on this routine wrongly claim that
> it does not handle leap years, leap centuries, etc. correctly.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> compute properly.

If nobody ever sees the subroutine, that's OK.   But I have to have a real
good reason - and real good documentation - to use code where how it
functions is not apparent to all.

Is this routine significantly faster than more obvious code?  Does that
matter?

Eschew obfuscation.
```

#### ↳ Re: FREE inline date-math subr - only TWO LINES long. Wow!

- **From:** bszyszko@rochester.rr.com (Blaine Zee)
- **Date:** 2001-12-19T22:06:08-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.pop,comp.lang.rexx
- **Message-ID:** `<7789cf15.0112192206.389c09c2@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<7789cf15.0112020830.2e5db942@posting.google.com>`

```
bszyszko@rochester.rr.com (Blaine  Zee) wrote in message news:<7789cf15.0112020830.2e5db942@posting.google.com>...
> Hello, world.
> 
> Use my program below to calculate datevals INLINE without all the overhead
> of calling a system API, function or subr... Easily written in any language. 
> 
1. Those that posted that that Mar 1 of a non-leap year returns the
same dateval of Feb 29 of a non-leap year are correct. Feb 29 is not a
valid input for non-leap years. In a non-leap year, Feb 29 IS mar 1,
thus the dateval returned by this subr IS correct.

2. In the optimization to remove division and multiplication
instructions from
 the code (posted on 12/20/01), the array initialization should read:
{0,2,33..etc} whereas in the 12/20 post it incorrectly read {0,33...}

- blaine "z"
```

#### ↳ Re: FREE inline date-math subr - only TWO LINES long. Wow!

- **From:** bszyszko@rochester.rr.com (Blaine Zee)
- **Date:** 2001-12-19T22:14:31-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.pop,comp.lang.rexx
- **Message-ID:** `<7789cf15.0112192214.26c0f5b3@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<7789cf15.0112020830.2e5db942@posting.google.com>`

```
bszyszko@rochester.rr.com (Blaine  Zee) wrote in message news:<7789cf15.0112020830.2e5db942@posting.google.com>...
> INT INLINE FUNCTION dateval(INT m,INT d,INT y)
> DIM ar(12) AS INT {2,33,61,92,122,153,183,214,245,275,306,336}
> 10   dateval = INT((y - 1924) * 1461 \4) + ar(m) + d
> 20   IF m < 3 THEN dateval = dateval - (yr MOD 4)
> END FUNCTION

See new no-mult and no-div posting on lang.fortran.
```

#### ↳ Re: FREE inline date-math subr - only TWO LINES long. Wow!

- **From:** bszyszko@rochester.rr.com (Blaine Zee)
- **Date:** 2001-12-19T22:21:53-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.pop,comp.lang.rexx
- **Message-ID:** `<7789cf15.0112192221.5fdfbc66@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<7789cf15.0112020830.2e5db942@posting.google.com>`

```
bszyszko@rochester.rr.com (Blaine  Zee) wrote in message news:<7789cf15.0112020830.2e5db942@posting.google.com>...

> 20   IF m < 3 THEN dateval = dateval - (y MOD 4)

This statement can be replaced by:
   dateval = dateval + yadj(m - 1923, y)

   where the pre-calculated, static array "yadj" contains the values
of the statement 20's CPU-intensive "if" and "Mod" functions.
```

You get around the 16MB limit by compiling with 'RENT' - even for non-CICS
batch
programs.

"Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote in message
news:3C058F49.765D27A2@istar.ca...
> If the AIX and AS400 COBOL products support 64 bit addressing and there
> are shops taking advantage of the capability, then lack of that
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> >  Does anyone in IBMMAIN know of 'real-world' (no retirees please)
reqirements
> > for 64-bit addressing in COBOL?  We DO know that people want to be able
to
> > code COBOL data items that are bigger than 16M (our current limit) but
we
> > could go much larger and still be AMODE 31.  Larger data items is in the
> > list of things to do, but we are looking for input on 64 bit
requirements.
> > Specifically, any COBOL programs that address 2MB of data and need more?
> >
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> >  Does anyone in IBMMAIN know of 'real-world' (no retirees please)
reqirements
> > for 64-bit addressing in COBOL?  We DO know that people want to be able
to
> > code COBOL data items that are bigger than 16M (our current limit) but
we
> > could go much larger and still be AMODE 31.  Larger data items is in the

requirements.
> > Specifically, any COBOL programs that address 2MB of data and need more?

## Thread: COBOL FAQ — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2001-12-03T11:40:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers,alt.answers
- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_1007379574@rtfm.mit.edu>`

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2000/07/02
Version: 3.01
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Sunday, July 2, 2000

The COBOL FAQ has moved.  To view the most recent version, please check out the
web version at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send private
email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

The following is Appendix B.5 - Changes to create Version 3.01

 o Recorded new location of the FAQ
 o Updated some old (bad) web links
 o Added some new COBOL books (particularly those written or contributed to - by
frequent comp.lang.cobol submitters).
 o Added information about LegacyJ (PERCobol) and changed the company name from
"Synkronix" to "LegacyJ".
 o Updated information for changes in product and company names and addresses
where available
 o Updated the URLs for downloading the draft of the next COBOL Standard and
CobCy
 o Started the Samples/Example Section
 o Tried to get rid of most specific references to Windows/NT, Windows/95,
Windows/98, and Windows/2000.
 o Added links to Xinotech
 o Added information on Norcom and GUI ScreenIO
 o Added (back) information on Wang
 o Added (back) information on the Tiny-COBOL ('74 Standard) project
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Micro Focus job site?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-12-03T19:56:37+00:00

- **References:** `<bnkN7.201516$3d2.8687664@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <EnQO7.122$kY4.3222@dfw-service2.ext.raytheon.com>`

```


Donald Steier wrote:

> -----Original Message-----
> From: JobSeek_msn@careerbuilder.com  [mailto:JobSeek_msn@careerbuilder.com]
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Support, Server support,

I like that one "TOP SECRET Clearance". When I was in the RAF I was, plus some
other hush-hush categories. Did I ever relate this one to you guys ?

I was at HQ MEAF (Egypt) back whennnnnnn....., working in Personnel. The job
structure :-

                    SPSO (Senior Personnel Staff Officer)
                    Group Captain (1 star)

P1 - Discipline, Courts of          P2 - Officers                P3 - non-coms
Inquiry, Courts martial etc.            Wg. Cdr P2                Wg. Cdr P3
Wing Commander (Lt Col)

Mail/telegrams(signals) come in - distribution in sequence, SPSO, P1, P2 and P3.
(Wg.Cdr. P1 is an Aussie (RAAF) seconded - Jim Lombard).

In comes a piece of correspondence from the Air Ministry in London, introducing
"UK Eyes Only" - in summary for 'Brits' only - not to be shown to the other
riff-raff, (we also had Kiwis). SPSO sees, initials and passes on. Gets to Jim
Lombard. He sees and initials :-

    "Thanks very much. J.A. Lombard, R. AUSTRALIAN A.F !".

Jimmy
```
