<a id="t-ce5905cb20"></a>
## Thread: Cross-platform call from C++/Solaris to COBOL/Mainframe?? — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Cross-platform call from C++/Solaris to COBOL/Mainframe??

- **From:** dusan.chromy@tiscali.cz (Dusan Chromy)
- **Date:** 2001-12-03T07:50:23-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<12e4f4ff.0112030750.26f4093f@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi all,

we need to call a legacy COBOL program on an IBM/390 mainframe from a
C++ code on Solaris. The COBOL program takes two string parameters,
the first one is an input parameter, the second one is an output
parameter - the C/C++ signature would be: void unxxx(const char* in,
char* out)

I wonder what is the standard way to do this? Are there some
recommended products to do this? I think that every solution will
involve some programming on both sides (mainframe, Solaris), the ideal
solution for us would be one with minimal programming on the mainframe
side.

Thanks for your suggestions!

Dusan
```

#### ↳ Re: Cross-platform call from C++/Solaris to COBOL/Mainframe??

- **From:** Thane Hubbell <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-03T21:31:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WaSO7.47364$xS6.78099@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<12e4f4ff.0112030750.26f4093f@posting.google.com>`

```
You need CORBA or something similar to broker the communications between the two
platforms.  You can't simply "call" it.

In article <12e4f4ff.0112030750.26f4093f@posting.google.com>, Dusan Chromy
says...
>
>Hi all,
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>Dusan
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cross-platform call from C++/Solaris to COBOL/Mainframe??

- **From:** dusan.chromy@tiscali.cz (Dusan Chromy)
- **Date:** 2001-12-04T01:00:36-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<12e4f4ff.0112040100.4ba41182@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<12e4f4ff.0112030750.26f4093f@posting.google.com> <WaSO7.47364$xS6.78099@www.newsranger.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell <nospam@newsranger.com> wrote in message news:<WaSO7.47364$xS6.78099@www.newsranger.com>...
> You need CORBA or something similar to broker the communications between the two
> platforms.  You can't simply "call" it.
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> >Dusan

Thanks for the response. This sounds good to me (CORBA) except for the
lack of overall experience with mainframe, which makes the task of
setting up CORBA on the mainframe side at least difficult.

How about registering the needed COBOL program as an external function
in DB2 (yes, the mainframe has DB2 on it) and calling it via DB2 CLI
from anywhere. Would that be feasible?

Dusan
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cross-platform call from C++/Solaris to COBOL/Mainframe??

- **From:** Daniel Jacot<daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch>
- **Date:** 2001-12-04T12:47:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sB3P7.48070$xS6.78678@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<12e4f4ff.0112030750.26f4093f@posting.google.com> <WaSO7.47364$xS6.78099@www.newsranger.com> <12e4f4ff.0112040100.4ba41182@posting.google.com>`

```
Stored Procedure is fine if your DB2 is configured to accept foreign calls.
CICS-to-CICS is OK, too. MQ is another possibility. CORBA is not as difficult to
install since it can be done is USS. It is rather a problem of cost. The only
vendor of mainframe CORBA is IONA (afaik). 

On 4 Dec 2001 01:00:36 -0800, in article
<12e4f4ff.0112040100.4ba41182@posting.google.com>, Dusan Chromy stated...
>
>Thane Hubbell <nospam@newsranger.com> wrote in message news:<WaSO7.47364$xS6.78099@www.newsranger.com>...
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>Dusan

-------------------------------------------------
With kind regards
Daniel Jacot
-------------------------------------------------
visit us at: http://www.winterthur.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Cross-platform call from C++/Solaris to COBOL/Mainframe??

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <Todd.Lucas@C-Cubed.net>
- **Date:** 2001-12-04T00:33:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BRUO7.153436$kf1.49604449@news1.rdc1.ne.home.com>`
- **References:** `<12e4f4ff.0112030750.26f4093f@posting.google.com>`

```
C-Cubed supports this functionality (as well as file access) via a Windows
platform - nothing for Solaris though.  Check out www.c-cubed.net if you're
interested ...


"Dusan Chromy" <dusan.chromy@tiscali.cz> wrote in message
news:12e4f4ff.0112030750.26f4093f@posting.google.com...
> Hi all,
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Dusan

<a id="t-c630a74447"></a>
## Thread: MF runtime license fees — 12 msgs

### MF runtime license fees

- **From:** cgoodman@imi.mb.ca (Charles Goodman)
- **Date:** 2001-12-03T13:50:48-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e8e74e8.0112031350.4f10077c@posting.google.com>`

```
1.  We have three MF NetExpress licenses with full support for our
three programmers. (yes ungodly expensive)

2. We are using netExpress 3.0 but have CD's to load 3.1

3. When using Windows 2000 we find that REBUILD fails, there is
limited support of full file names, encounter buffer overruns when
loading ISAM file.

4. MF support says that we must go to NetExpress 3.1 to get support
for Windows 2000

5.  We use MF internal ISAM files without fileshare.

6.  All applications are for in-house use.

7.  We expect 250 users of the system within 12 months, with each user
having several COBOL applications open simultaneously.


A couple of concerns/questions:

What end user licenses do we need if we stay with 3.0?
What end user licenses do we need if we migrate to 3.1?
We could use system administration advice from others with Win2000
experience.

Regards,  Charlie Goodman
```

#### ↳ Re: MF runtime license fees

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-12-03T23:02:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C0C0525.D8D29C03@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<e8e74e8.0112031350.4f10077c@posting.google.com>`

```


Charles Goodman wrote:

> 1.  We have three MF NetExpress licenses with full support for our
> three programmers. (yes ungodly expensive)
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> Regards,  Charlie Goodman

I really do wish they would get their act together !  A month or so back,
I got one of those reassuring blurbs, (from the California office),  about
their commitment to COBOL and did I want a freebie of University Edition -
why not, I said "Yes" Plus they also sent me a Mickey Mouse book on using
it - although I've been using Visoc and Net Express, since God knows when.

Ironically, ONLY this morning, as I'm sipping my cuppa - a phone call from
some bright eyed bushy-tailed new salesman in Toronto. "Hi ! Just
following up on your query, and how can we help". Why doesn't the right
hand know what the left hand is doing. I hadn't queried bugger all !

But I did ask him, so what does this "run time" cost - $3,300 Canadian -
J-E-S-U-S ! Then he softens it by saying that it's a ten-deal package, and
of course for 'loners' like you it depends upon your software. From a
message posted here, I think, the 'good words' were, "If your software
package is as low as $1,000, then of course, nothing to pay". I could have
asked, how many bits of software at $1K a crack have I got to sell to make
a living - I'm not bloody Microsoft !

Now of course that's not the complete story. Although he originally sang
the praises of Fujitsu, I see now that Pete Dashwood is 'less than
enchanted' with Fujitsu, for similar reasons - he aint going to F/J V. 6.

In the case of Micro Focus they did a snow job on us - selling VISOC for
about $CDN 800, then they slipped in a new price for Net Express, (real
decent of them - we got that at a discount if we were already VISOC
users), but having killed off the Micro Focus Forum they substituted, at
least for N. America, the Answerlab, where you can get fairly generalized
answers - absolutely bloody zippo for OO, now that Steve Biggs has left
Newbury - and to my mind a hefty annual fee for precious little help.
Haven't got enough support - ahhh we can offer a graded  table of
technical support features, plus an incremental value of greenbacks.

They are all charging a 'hefty' price ( at least from a loner's
perspective), for the basic product. I seem to recall a posting a couple
of days ago, can't remember whether it was CA Relia or Acu - but they
wanted about $3K plus for starters.

OK guys you do respectively produce excellent tools, so it's arguable as
to what the base price should be - but for God's sake stop soaking us on
runtime and technical support fees, or at least come up with some grading
system, based on the individual developer's size - I know for certain some
COBOL users who have said "Piss on it!" and have switched to Visual Basic
or Java.

To Charles, the original enquirer - my deepest sympathies, 3 lots of cost
for the basic tool, no doubt three lots of cost for technical support -
they've got you by the balls !.

You know what I would do on reflection, stick with V 3.0 and rename the
bloody files to get them through the Rebuilds - worst case, write out
sequentials and create fresh ISAMs..

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MF runtime license fees

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <Todd.Lucas@C-Cubed.net>
- **Date:** 2001-12-04T00:31:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8PUO7.153434$kf1.49602575@news1.rdc1.ne.home.com>`
- **References:** `<e8e74e8.0112031350.4f10077c@posting.google.com> <3C0C0525.D8D29C03@shaw.ca>`

- **From:** chris@group3developments.com (chris)
- **Date:** 2001-12-04T12:36:22-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a0e5a88.0112041236.7349ebc9@posting.google.com>`

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ My stance on Fujitsu, was: Re: MF runtime license fees

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-12-05T12:19:56+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c0d5d8b_3@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<e8e74e8.0112031350.4f10077c@posting.google.com> <3C0C0525.D8D29C03@shaw.ca> <bE5P7.110598$YD.9083642@news2.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
To avoid any further speculation or spin from users of this group I will
make the following statement:

I stress that the following is personal opinion, based on my own experience.
It can (and probably will) be hotly argued by others.These comments may also
not apply if you are a Corporate Licenser with many seats and the resulting
leverage this gives you with Fujitsu Marketing.

1. The Fujitsu products are excellent. Technically superb.

2. Unfortunately, as the success of their products has grown, the company,
at least in the United States, has failed to maintain the early
responsiveness which was a hallmark of their support.

Four years ago it was possible to communicate with people in the
organization on technical issues. Now you register on a database and
eventually get assigned someone who will write to Japan on your behalf...

There ARE (or WERE) some extremely good technical people retained by Fujitsu
in the US, but the SYSTEM has changed and this has resulted in deteriorating
service to the customer base.

For example, around four years ago I was very impressed when a senior
manager there actually phoned me (I was in the U.K., he was in the US) to
resolve a problem. The same manager today doesn't even acknowledge my e-mail
(even when it is in response to his request). His workload has increased so
much he simply doesn't have the time that he did when the enterprise was
getting off the ground.

The overall standard of administration has visibly declined as the volume
has increased. In the US, this is not such a problem because you can pick up
a phone and get it sorted. For those of us outside the US, this is much more
problematic. Notwithstanding the cost of being put on hold or waiting for
IVR on an International call, there are the time differences...

E-mails frequently get bounced by the Fujitsu server but they won't
acknowledge that this happens, even when presented with the mail daemon
output. Maybe it only happens to mail OUTSIDE the US...(To be fair, I have
not communicated with them for some time now and this problem may have been
handled)

3. Jimmy is wrong when he says I will not implement Version 6. I haven't
done so to date but I reserve the right to do so (especially as I have paid
for it!).

My main problem with V6 is that it REQUIRES contact with Fujitsu if you want
to install or re-install it. Given the reservations expressed above, and the
fact that I am unlikely to be living and working in the USA in the
foreseeable future, would you take the risk? Install a product that is key
to your business and HOPE that the communication with the vendor will
improve?

Scenario: My hard disk crashes and I need to rebuild my system at 2 in the
afternoon (local time) on a working day...  With Version 5, no problem. With
Version 6, I need to contact Fujitsu and it is 5 in the morning in
California...

I don't think so...

My other problems with V6 are:

3.1. It cannot be installed with V5 so the systems can be compared and
benchmark tests can be run. If you install it and find there are problems,
you are then stuck with having to uninstall it and all the rigmarole that
that entails. What used to be a simple straightforward process is now
unwieldy and high risk.

3.2. The Professional Edition offers little improved functionality over V5.
(For Marketing reasons the exciting stuff has been moved up into the
Enterprise Edition.) The main one I would want is Java/COBOL but it is
limited in Fujitsu V6 anyway, and I can write my own interface in V5, using
Java NI...(there are some implications with this when it comes to remote
method invocation, so I will probably just drop COBOL altogether in this
environment and go to Java Servlets. Any wonder people are moving away from
COBOL...?)

4. I am not perturbed by ANY of the Licensing for Fujitsu products, which I
consider to be fair. It is the MECHANICS of implementing this, and the
difficulty of efficient and timely communication with the organization that
bothers me.

5. The lack of Run Time fee is to be applauded and other vendors could take
note.

SUMMARY:

1. For me, Fujitsu COBOL products have run their course. I no longer need
support (in fact, I have provided it to others (also disillusioned with
Fujitsu support) for free) on Version 5. If I go to V6, I will probably need
some support and that is not an attractive prospect. The gains are not worth
the sweat.

2. NetCOBOL is a whole different ball game (and a different division of the
Company) and I await with interest its arrival.

3. I cannot (and will not) recommend the use of Fujitsu COBOL products to
Corporates outside the US, not because there is anything wrong with the
products, but for the reasons outlined above. It is just too high a risk.

Pete.

PS I will not be posting publicly on this subject again, unless the
situation changes and I can be more positive.

JerryMouse <news@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:bE5P7.110598$YD.9083642@news2.aus1.giganews.com...
>
> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > enchanted' with Fujitsu, for similar reasons - he aint going to F/J V.
6.
> >
> There's still no runtime fees for Fujitsu. I think Pete is perturbed over
> the development license (use on multiple machines, pay multiple fees).
>
>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: My stance on Fujitsu, was: Re: MF runtime license fees

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-12-05T03:01:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C0D8E68.C569C4FC@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<e8e74e8.0112031350.4f10077c@posting.google.com> <3C0C0525.D8D29C03@shaw.ca> <bE5P7.110598$YD.9083642@news2.aus1.giganews.com> <3c0d5d8b_3@Usenet.com>`

```


"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

> My other problems with V6 are:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Unfortunately that's a problem which probably applies to most of them. In
theory, I could say "To hell with it", and go back to VISOC, (and I'm fairly
confident that what I have written in N/E Versions 3.0 and  3.1. would work with
VISOC - with fewer support goodies in the IDE - BUT still that same excellent
Animator !). Unfortunately, like you,  I can't just load different versions of
the 'same' - because they share recognizable files (dlls etc.); in the case of
VISOC, the appropriate dll would be an 'older' version. I haven't tried it - but
no doubt there are conflicts when the software updates Registry entries. Closer
to the current situation, I'm fairly certain I can't  have V3.0 alongside V 3.1.
(Similarly as I said, I have a copy of University Edition - but I DAREN'T load
it for a looksee - just in case).

In a sense this is not unreasonable from the vendor's perspective, "Well, you
are upgrading, aren't you ?". But I also take your point about a belt and braces
approach for comparison before you let go of the older version.

'Support' will always be a bugaboo, and it really doesn't matter whether your
are talking computer software or that spanking new vehicle the slippery salesman
sold you a year ago. So called 'support' does tend to slide off. As you've noted
Fujitsu's  'success' has tended to overwhelm them - pinched too many customers
from Micro Focus (including you !). Don't forget though, the initial freebie
carrot to attract their audience. (I had a copy, but necessitated borrowing
Caxton's printing machine to print out the manuals <G>).

Tech Support, specific to a vendor, was more effective, I feel, when we had the
style of the Compuserve Micro Focus Forum and you and Michael M. amongst others
used to jump in. It got 'killed' because the PR coming out on VISOC was lousy -
but note I said above, I'm still of the opinion I could use that product.

It would tend to reduce the general interest traffic here in CLC, but perhaps
the approach each vendor should consider is that old CompuServe approach - and
no fees please.

I'll give you an indication about Technical Support, and where other users with
advice come in. They are both associated with Dopdown Lists :-

Developer asks. "How can I have a Dopdown list of names but hide the Codes ?".
The tech guy responded, "Why would you WANT to do that ?". The difference
between vendor and developer perspectives. Hopefully most here will latch on
immediately. I responded, "Because he wants to show his end-user the names, not
confuse them with  codes, but he needs to access that code from the Dopdown to
include in his file record".

I asked, some while much later, "How can I just show Dopdown descriptions and
tab to keep the code out of view ?'. The answer didn't come from Tech Support,
it came from Ken Mullins, "Use Monspaced Font". That did the trick for me.
(Ken is your byline still the "Customer is always right "  <G>)


>
> 3.2. The Professional Edition offers little improved functionality over V5.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

That's a pity - and probably based on pricing. I know from Thane it contains a
powerful Sort and in addition to the Java links that you are talking about, I
understand it also contains the finishing touches to Collections/Dictionaries,
perhaps the most important side weapon to be carrying when playing with OO. I
would feel completely 'naked' without this feature. So folks are not attracted
to Enterprise and sadly miss out on Collections/Dictionaries, plus any Java
goodies, although as you say you can get around the latter.

>
> 1. For me, Fujitsu COBOL products have run their course. I no longer need
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Inevitably we reach the situation, where having sweated it, we resolve queries
by ourselves and on a day-to-day basis just don't need Tech Support. I can't be
certain, but I think my annual support fee would be about $CDN 900 - why should
I shell that out, 'just in case I want to ask a question'. I'm very content that
my approach to Dialogs for instance is easier than anything in the N/E demos -
and you've seen some of their demos <G>.

*********

What really triggered it for me, was the useless call from the salesman that
morning and then I see some poor sod confronted with the runtime fee problem -
it hit a raw nerve. As we all know, what is uppermost in the salesman's mind is
not our well being, but the commission cheque he takes home ! (Cynical - You
betcha ! I've been on this Earth too long to be otherwise).

Jimmy
```

diffierence between the two companies is striking.  In a big
production environment, corporations who pay a support fee, albeit a
small one, sleep better, and  'look' better.

I can tell you that one of these vendors had a habit of NOT returning
my phone calls, while the other was outstanding.  This played a key
role in my choices.

A second bone of contention, Fujitsu went to bed with Microsoft.
Microfocus continues to play in the middle of the road.  I have seen
the choice (an expensive one) made on this flexibility issue alone.

EF

someone has ten applications open at once is that one user or ten?

<a id="t-9445788d67"></a>
## Thread: FREE inline date-math subr - only TWO LINES long. Wow! — 10 msgs

_Topics: [`Date and calendar processing`](topics.md#dates)_

### Re: FREE inline date-math subr - only TWO LINES long. Wow!

- **From:** R.A. Vowels <robin_v@bigpond.nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-04T05:10:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.pop,comp.lang.rexx
- **Message-ID:** `<jVYO7.131867$e5.54508@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
bszyszko@rochester.rr.com (Blaine  Zee) writes: > Hello, world.
> 
> Use my program below to calculate datevals INLINE without all the overhead
> of calling a system API, function or subr... Easily written in any language. 

Don't bother.
It doesn't do what it claims.
The days are wrong for most if not all Januaries and Februaries,
and are wrong for the year 2100 (I didn't bother beyond).
Even when the formula is corrected, 30 and 31 December 1923 are wrong,
and the (corrected) formula is valid from 1924 to 1999 only.

[Apart from this problem, the variable 'yr' should be 'y'.]

TRY_DATE:
   PROCEDURE OPTIONS (MAIN, REORDER);
      DECLARE Origin FIXED BINARY (31);
      DECLARE 1 somedate,
                  2 d CHARACTER (2),
                  2 m CHARACTER (2),
                  2 y CHARACTER (4);
      DECLARE J FIXED BINARY (31);

      Origin = DAYS ('30121923', 'DDMMYYYY');
      DO J = DAYS ('30121923', 'DDMMYYYY') DAYS ('31122100', 'DDMMYYYY');
         STRING(somedate) = DAYSTODATE (J, 'DDMMYYYY');
         IF dateval(m, d, y)+origin ^= J THEN
            PUT SKIP EDIT (somedate, dateval (m, d, y)+origin, J) (A, X(1));
      END;
   END TRY_DATE;

[The "Origin" is the adjustment between Lilian days and this
formula.]

> --------
> 
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> END FUNCTION
```

#### ↳ Re: FREE inline date-math subr - only TWO LINES long. Wow!

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-12-05T07:48:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.pop,comp.lang.rexx
- **Message-ID:** `<3C0DD130.C2E898C1@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<jVYO7.131867$e5.54508@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
R.A. Vowels wrote:

> >
> > Use my program below to calculate datevals INLINE without all the overhead
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> The days are wrong for most if not all Januaries and Februaries,

> > 10   dateval = INT((y - 1924) * 1461 \4) + ar(m) + d

The routine relies on the precision of the intermediate result of 1461 /
4.  This makes it completely unreliable and the results will vary
depending on language, compiler, platform and implementation.

It may well have given the correct results in whatever version of some
Basic language that it is written in, but the poster is clueless about
why it works, or not.

Stages in learning programming:

      First you think you know nothing,
      Then you think you know everything,
      Then you know you know nothing.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: FREE inline date-math subr - only TWO LINES long. Wow!

- **From:** Paul van Delst <paul.vandelst@noaa.gov>
- **Date:** 2001-12-04T14:39:26-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.pop,comp.lang.rexx
- **Message-ID:** `<3C0D266E.3DBBFF62@noaa.gov>`
- **References:** `<jVYO7.131867$e5.54508@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <3C0DD130.C2E898C1@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote:
> 
> R.A. Vowels wrote:
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>       Then you know you know nothing.

I would reverse the first two. I've heard similar but expressed thusly (from clueless to
exalted leader):

Stage 1: Inconscious incompetence - when you really have no clue and don't even know it.
Stage 2: Conscious incompetence - when you realise you have no clue, but you're working on it.
Stage 3: Conscious competence - clueness achieved, but you still have to keep working at it.
Stage 4: Inconscious competence - all the tribes of Gondwanaland bow down before thee.

cheers,

paulv
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: FREE inline date-math subr - only TWO LINES long. Wow!

- **From:** mcalhoun@ksu.edu (Myron A Calhoun)
- **Date:** 2001-12-04T16:10:47-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.pop,comp.lang.rexx
- **Message-ID:** `<9ujhl7$2dm@unix1.cc.ksu.edu>`
- **References:** `<jVYO7.131867$e5.54508@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <3C0DD130.C2E898C1@Azonic.co.nz> <3C0D266E.3DBBFF62@noaa.gov>`

```
>Stage 1: Inconscious incompetence - when you really have no
          clue and don't even know it.
>Stage 2: Conscious incompetence - when you realise you have
          no clue, but you're working on it.
>Stage 3: Conscious competence - clueness achieved, but you
          still have to keep working at it.
>Stage 4: Inconscious competence - all the tribes of Gondwanaland
          bow down before thee.

As I heard it long ago:

He who knows not, and knows not he knows not, is a fool; shun him.
He who knows not, and knows he knows not, is a child; teach him.
He who knows, and knows not he knows, is asleep; awaken him.
He who knows, and knows he knows, is wise; follow him.

Can also substitute "she" and "her" for "he" and "him".

--Myron.
```

<a id="t-d3f3673b60"></a>
## Thread: Dynamic linking — 1 msgs

### Dynamic linking

- **From:** "Reijo Koivisto" <reijo.koivisto@datamar.fi>
- **Date:** 2001-12-04T10:15:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2n1P7.5441$zZ5.111718@news.kpnqwest.fi>`

```
Our working environment is AIX 4.3.3, Tuxedo 7.1, DB2 7.1 and Micro Focus
Object COBOL V4.1 for UNIX. The problem is the Tuxedo, whitch has changed
it's library format since version 6.5, from statically linked librarys to a
dynamically linked libries. We haven't been able to link these dynamic
libraries. Does Micro Focus Object COBOL V4.1 support dynamic linking? There
is no LIBCOBOL.SO whitch manual says is the mark of support for dynamic
linking on Unix systems but the manual specifically excludes AIX when saying
that but fails to say anything about AIX.

Reijo Koivisto
```

## Thread: EXTFH TRACE - MF netx — 5 msgs

### EXTFH TRACE - MF netx

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-12-04T16:02:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ws6P7.249251$W8.9026648@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
    Does anybody know how to view the trace file that Net Express 3.1
creates
when you add the line "TRACE=ON" to the extfh.cfg file.  It has the
extension
.XFH, and is a binary file, not a text file.

    Windows version.
```

#### ↳ Re: EXTFH TRACE - MF netx

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-12-04T17:48:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C0D0CFD.A7E3F89C@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<ws6P7.249251$W8.9026648@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```


Russell Styles wrote:

>     Does anybody know how to view the trace file that Net Express 3.1
> creates
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>     Windows version.

I have used it, but forget how. So I'll dabble and get back to you in due
course.

To be perfectly truthful, although the feature is there it really doesn't
help you greatly, unless you are delving into bits and bytes. Given that
you follow your own set pattern for coding programs, (e.g. you handle
Masterfile edits in a standard fashion), doing an Animate against the
source is probably a much easier way to follow the problem.
Familiarization with your own programming style tends to point you at
your problem, given the information from the Animator.

Particularly with OO, the trace givers you a HUGE trace list when you are
into something like handling a string (CharacterArray) as it jumps to and
fro between many, many methods.

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: EXTFH TRACE - MF netx

- **From:** "Robert Sales" <Robert.Sales@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-05T09:35:43
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ukq9b$eri$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com>`
- **References:** `<ws6P7.249251$W8.9026648@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
There's currently no facility available to our users to do anything with the
trace information.  This was really developed so that, in the case of any
problems, the user could send this stuff to us to analyse and hopefully to
get to the root of the problem.
Internally we do have a 'trace replay' facility but this has so far not been
made available externally - a probable product enhancement at some future
date.
```

#### ↳ Re: EXTFH TRACE - MF netx

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-12-06T03:22:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C0EE4F6.3A48E613@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<ws6P7.249251$W8.9026648@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```


Russell Styles wrote:

>     Does anybody know how to view the trace file that Net Express 3.1
> creates
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>     Windows version.

Russell,

My apologies for previous message - Should have read your Subject matter
more closely. I started to read up on Trace and then saw the reference to
EXTFH - and that rang a bell about your subject matter. I have no
knowledge of EXTFH so any contribution I would make would be irrelevant.
I've only used Trace directly from the IDE and solely with OO. Plus I see
EXTFH Trace is handled slightly differently.

Nevertheless, it still holds true. Unless you've got a real 'weirdo', a
consistency of your own programming style and stepping through Animator
will probably assist you better. It's a little like taking on the old
challenge of using 'D' in column 7.

The only person I am aware of who uses EXTFH is Ken Mullins. Ken, have
you ever used TRACE with the external file handler ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

version of RM/COBOL, version 7.00.03 for 32-bit Windows.  You could take
advantage of COBOL-85 language features along with all of the RM/COBOL
extensions to the language and you would be on a supported product.  Visit
www.liant.com for more details.

Robert Heady

##### ↳ ↳ Re: rm/cobol Runtime <ver 1.5e>

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-05T14:50:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zuqP7.2254$Xj.1405996203@newssvr11.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<j69P7.106393$z8.14415972@news02.optonline.net> <scdP7.11$RF1.68@castor.casema.net>`

```
I can offer a bit of history but, unfortunately, no technical advice on
Windows ME or 2000.

RM/COBOL version 1.5e is a version of the extremely old (in 'software
years') 1974 standard based product.  Development on the 1974 product ceased
15+ years ago, when the 1985 standard based product (called RM/COBOL-85 at
the time) was introduced.  Support for the 1974 product was terminated a few
years later, and RM/COBOL-85 was then renamed to RM/COBOL.

Recompilation and file conversion are necessary when moving from the '74
product (i.e. ver 1.5e) to the '85 product.  The conversion tools are still
available.  Once on the current product, Ronald's assertion is true -- all
that is needed is the proper runtime.  The benefit of doing this, however,
is that Scott should observe about a 5x performance improvement.

BTW, version 1.5e was not even the last version of the '74 product, so Scott
is using software that is almost 20 years old!

Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: rm/cobol Runtime <ver 1.5e>

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "ronald leenheer" <r.leenheer@wanadoo.nl>
- **Date:** 2001-12-06T01:09:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<czzP7.7$UN.178@pollux.casema.net>`
- **References:** `<j69P7.106393$z8.14415972@news02.optonline.net> <scdP7.11$RF1.68@castor.casema.net> <zuqP7.2254$Xj.1405996203@newssvr11.news.prodigy.com> <9uljbs$gjb$1@bob.news.rcn.net>`

```
That's why the version did sound familiar to me (I'm using rm now for about
17 years)
I don't think you'll need to convert anything when you keep on using 74 mode
(compile with -7)
The only thing you'll NEED to do is to read every file with comp-3 fields
move the fields to a normal field and back.
like:
       01  HULP                PIC S9(11)V9(4).

           MOVE SOS-OMZET-DP    TO HULP.
           MOVE HULP TO SOS-OMZET-DP.
           MOVE SOS-AANTAL-DP   TO HULP.
           MOVE HULP TO SOS-AANTAL-DP.
           MOVE SOS-OMZET-TM    TO HULP.

The op-code for a signed 0 field is changed. So it can happen that a field
with value 0
Doesn't respond on a zero like:
         IF    SOS-OMZET-DP > 0 DISPLAY ">0" GO TO EINDE.
         IF    SOS-OMZET-DP < 0 DISPLAY "<0" GO TO EINDE.
         IF    SOS-OMZET-DP = 0 DISPLAY "=0" GO TO EINDE.
         DISPLAY "shit".
EINDE.
It'll display 'shit'.

Another thing to look after is the value of the function key's in the new
environment.
I've never seen any conversion tools, but I've always written them my self.
Ther still was something else about the conversion but I can't remember
what.

<a id="t-0c445cad2d"></a>
## Thread: New CICS removes Translator support for VS COBOL II (and OS/VS COBOL) — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### New CICS removes Translator support for VS COBOL II (and OS/VS COBOL)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-04T19:11:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ld9P7.1945$714.179188@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
As expected (and stated as the "direction" in the last CICS release), the
latest release of CICS - as announced at:
  http://www.ibmlink.ibm.com/usalets&parms=H_201-354

removes translator support for OS/VS COBOL and VS COBOL II.  The exact
statement (including what to expect NEXT) is:

"Translator support for the earlier (pre-Language Environment) compilers
OS/VS COBOL (5740-CB1, 5740-LM1, and 5734-CB4), VS COBOL II (5668-958, also
5688-023), OS PL/I Version 1 (5734-PL1), OS PL/I Version 2 (5668-910, also
5668-909), and SAA� AD/Cycle� C/370T (5688-216) is withdrawn and is not
available in this release. It is planned that run-time support for OS/VS
COBOL programs (regardless of run-time library used) and for any other
programs running under pre-Language Environment run-time libraries will be
withdrawn from the next release."
```

## Thread: I have a problem with a Data File — 7 msgs

### I have a problem with a Data File

- **From:** "Ambrosio" <AMBROS@nettaxi.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-05T10:03:19+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9uknq6$sj3$1@diana.bcn.ttd.net>`

```
Hi all.

    I�m gonna make a new Visual Basic program to a dentist. He has right now
a cobol based program but he wants to upgrade.
    This cobol program has a huge data files, but I haven�t got any FD of
them.
    Well, I�m Searching a tool that allow me to read this file and export it
as a raw text, or Access Table or SQL table or anything else. So, from that
intermediate format I could attack it with  Visual Basic.

Thanks in advance.
```

#### ↳ Re: I have a problem with a Data File

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-12-05T11:56:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nXnP7.35$Ko.15291@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<9uknq6$sj3$1@diana.bcn.ttd.net>`

```
Ambrosio <AMBROS@nettaxi.com> wrote in message
news:9uknq6$sj3$1@diana.bcn.ttd.net...
>
>     I�m gonna make a new Visual Basic program to a dentist. He has right
now
> a cobol based program but he wants to upgrade.
>     This cobol program has a huge data files, but I haven�t got any FD of
> them.
>     Well, I�m Searching a tool that allow me to read this file and export
it
> as a raw text, or Access Table or SQL table or anything else. So, from
that
> intermediate format I could attack it with  Visual Basic.

(I also posted reply to alt.cobol, where you asked in a separate post).

See, for sure: http://www.flexus.com/ebd2asc.html

That deals with the subject of "Using COBOL-created data across platforms or
with non-COBOL language products."

Unfortunately for you, the one requirement for using your data is you need
the FD.  Without it, you are reduced to "deducing" the FD by inspecting
bytes and guessing record lengths, PICTURE clauses and the like.

There are some software tools available which can make the guesswork a lot
easier and/or "guess" for you  and I'm sure those vendors will chime in, but
without an FD you are in deep doo-doo.

Contact the vendor of the other software. They have got to have the FD; and
if they are any form of life higher  than pond scum they will make it
available to you. They may not make available all the detailed meanings of
each field, but those are not always important when doing a data conversion.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: I have a problem with a Data File

- **From:** "Lansdale" <lansdale@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2001-12-07T02:44:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol

- **References:** `<9uknq6$sj3$1@diana.bcn.ttd.net> <nXnP7.35$Ko.15291@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
One way interpret what the fields mean if you do not have source code is to
do some controled data entry.
Have the user enter some data of each type into form they use. Then scan the
text file for same data.

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <news@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-05T13:56:20+00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<8IpP7.96210$ox2.5195907@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<9uknq6$sj3$1@diana.bcn.ttd.net>`

```
Depending on the file type, your quest may well be impossible, even with the
FD. For example, all ISAM files have proprietary formats that depend on the
vendor. While some have been translators, many have not. Two solutions we've
used are:

1. The existing system MAY have a method of exporting the data into a text
format.

2. The original vendor can be of immense help. Assuming your mutual client
is in good standing with the original vendor, they should willing (although
not pleased) to assist in the conversion.

Of course you know you are undertaking a life-time job. Medicare and
insurance requirements change almost daily (even more often than payroll tax
tables).

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@paralynx.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

Ambrosio wrote:
> 
> Hi all.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> intermediate format I could attack it with  Visual Basic.

Take a look at ParseRat (http://www.parserat.com/).  Many people have used it in similar 
circumstances.  It's relatively easy if you know the file layout and will help you to 
deduce it if you don't - particularly if you know what the numeric data is SUPPOSED to 
be for a few records (text fields are easy, it's recognising which binary format, 
endianism etc. was used for numeric data that takes the time).
```

## Thread: Query on HP 3000 COBOL — 6 this month / 13 total

_Thread started [2001-11](2001-11.md#t-650a888cc8)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Query on HP 3000 COBOL

- **From:** "Gazaloo" <gaz@a.loo>
- **Date:** 2001-12-12T02:29:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QhzR7.35675$ER5.403740@rwcrnsc52>`
- **References:** `<3C003CB8.64534554@ix.netcom.com> <648b862c.0111291047.78d21e10@posting.google.com> <3c079a0d$1@giga.realtime.net>`

```
"Ira D. Baxter" <idbaxter@semdesigns.com> wrote in message:
Are you willing to sketch in a few sentences the primary differences, if you
know them, between  HP3000 COBOL and IBM's versions?

The main thing to twig is that the HP3000 COBOL was built by HP using
technology from Micro Focus.

Gazaloo.
```

#### ↳ Re: Query on HP 3000 COBOL

- **From:** none@none.com (wildman)
- **Date:** 2001-12-05T16:51:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c0e49ce.1271357@news.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<3C003CB8.64534554@ix.netcom.com>`

```
Hi, steve, I support all three, so here are my thoughts...

HP3000s run the MPE operating system.  'final' release is 7.0
HP recently anounced their support for HP3000 class minis will
terminate in 2006.  The 3000's database is turbo IMAGE -
a hierarchical databse.  ODBC connectivity to IMAGE is available
through ALLBASE.   

HP9000 run HPUX having unix file structure.  They typically use an
RDBMS, like Sybase with SQL.

The 3000's file structure is 3-tiered where unix is the more familiar
root tree structure.

The HP3000 is more of a mini computer than a server.  So its more like
an IBM mainframe in some respects.  Its job handling facilities are
solid.  Trying to use IMAGE in the client server world will demand
some thought.  For example, using ODBC SQL against a hierarchical
database structure requires understanding turbo IMAGE rather than just
relational databases. 

Your biggest challenge is utilities.  The 3000 has a set of third
party tools and editors very different from the 9000. 

On Sat, 24 Nov 2001 19:35:04 -0500, Steve Thompson
<sthompsonNOSPAM@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>Anyone have experience with the HP 3000 (and 9000) machines?
>Do you also have experience on an IBM system?
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>330/335-9907 office
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Query on HP 3000 COBOL

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompsonNOSPAM@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-05T15:37:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C0E3F23.135C91E9@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3C003CB8.64534554@ix.netcom.com> <3c0e49ce.1271357@news.uswest.net>`

```

>

move it to another system (probably Linux based at this
point) should not be that difficult. Our team will just need
to take some time to build the tools to make it happen.

We've been approached because of that "death" annoucement
and the fear that some have concerning HP given the merger
situation.
```

- **From:** praul@isot.com (Paul Raulerson)
- **Date:** 2001-12-13T10:59:56-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40f8f32c.0112131059.62cc85e3@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3C003CB8.64534554@ix.netcom.com> <3c0e49ce.1271357@news.uswest.net> <3C0E3F23.135C91E9@ix.netcom.com>`

```
Whoo boy - don't bet on that. A closer fit to the HP3000 is probably
an AS/400, which has the added advantage of the toolsets and the file
system being more like a 3000 than any UNIX filesystem.  (And I love
UNIX, so don't get me wrong here...)

The COBOL will also be a closer match, though screen handling is
somewhat different as well; much more like a mainframe than the Micro
Focus based 3000 view system.

Database issues, well under UNIX, you are going to need to have
someone who really knows any RDBMS you use to maintain, tune, and
configure it. On the AS/400, this isn't an issue since DB/2 is a
built-in integral part of the system.

Finally, the development tools available under Linux, while very good,
are about as different as they can be from MPE/ix.  SDE and PDM under
an AS/400 will be a lot more comfortable.

Just my $0.02.

-Paul

<a id="t-a75fea4186"></a>
## Thread: What's the difference? — 41 msgs

### What's the difference?

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <news@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-06T00:45:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<JczP7.256347$dk.17394792@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
Between:

MOVE SPACE TO GOMER-REC.
OPEN OUPUT GOMER-FILE.

and

OPEN OUTPUT GOMER-FILE.
MOVE SPACE TO GOMER-REC.

Execution of the 1st rendition generated ghastly execution-time errors (via
the Fujitsu compiler, who shall remain nameless) that took literally days to
root out.

Admittedly, the original code did not follow best-practice conventions, but,
frankly, I had never heard of a restriction against making use of storage
assigned to an FD before opening the affiliated file.

Well, we learned something new (or maybe old) and just wanted to share. Now
I'm off to get very, very drunk.
```

#### ↳ Re: What's the difference?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-06T01:12:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uCzP7.5728$714.467178@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<JczP7.256347$dk.17394792@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
The difference is that one has "defined" results and one doesn't.  The
"record area" (often known as the "file buffer") is UNDEFINED before an OPEN
or after a CLOSE.  Therefore, the MOVE of spaces to the 01-level under the
FD does NOT necessarily move anything to the actual area from which data is
written. The error (that you are probably getting) relates to trying to move
SPACEs to some "undefined" (unallocated?) place in storage.  You only
OFFICIALLY get that area when you do the OPEN.

P.S.  There are some OBSCURE exceptions to this rule - having to do with the
use of the SAME AREA phrase - and some "possible" differences if the file is
defined with the EXTERNAL Clause, but in GENERAL, it is simply a "results
are unpredictable" to ever "reference" the record area before an OPEN or
after a CLOSE.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: What's the difference?

- **From:** "Robert Sales" <Robert.Sales@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-06T09:00:31
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9uncjv$omn$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com>`
- **References:** `<JczP7.256347$dk.17394792@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <uCzP7.5728$714.467178@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
May be worth pointing out that in Micro Focus products (and probably
others), the file area is allocated statically so that initializing the
record area before the open works ok.  This is true except when the product
is running in Mainframe emulation mode (Mainframe Express), in which case
the record is allocated on the open to ensure 'correct' Mainframe behaviour.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What's the difference?

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-10T09:58:43-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9v2t4i$spq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<JczP7.256347$dk.17394792@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <uCzP7.5728$714.467178@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <9uncjv$omn$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com>`

```
Just as a point of interest:  Unisys MCP/AS COBOL compilers typically
describe their data spaces using "untouched" data descriptors.  The act of
referencing such an "untouched" space results in a hardware interrupt,
fielded and recognized by the system software ("initial presencebit
interrupt") , which in turn allocates the memory space, adjusts the
descriptor to point to it, and then returns control to the program.  Thus,
record areas (and even data base structures) may be used in MCP/AS COBOL
programs with considerable impunity regardless of whether the files (or data
bases) in question have been opened or not.

    -Chuck Stevens

#### ↳ Re: What's the difference?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-12-06T01:50:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C0ECF69.C06D9BFE@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<JczP7.256347$dk.17394792@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```


JerryMouse wrote:

> Between:
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> I'm off to get very, very drunk.

Before you get too drunk, or when you are sobre again, and je sais ne rien about
Fujitsu. But I think your problem is :-

    MOVE SPACES TO GOMER-REC
    OPEN OUTPUT GOMER-REC

You *think* you've moved spaces to the record, when in truth nothing relating to
the file really happens until you Open Output, establishing a handle to the file
with the ability to return a file status code, and it's at that stage that the
record area becomes significant. (Go back to earlier usage of COBOL, where to
conserve memory, you could use the syntax SAME FILE or SAME RECORD AREA, for
several files/records - your moving spaces would be irrelevant).

Consider the following for an ISAM opened I/O  :-

    move MyKey to Gomer-PrimeKey
    Read Gomer key is Gomer-Primeky
      invalid key set RecordNotFound to true
      not invalid key set RecordFound to true
    End-read

    if    RecordFound
          start displaying record or whatever
           you could be changing fields and if so set RecordChanged to true and
            eventually do a REWRITE

    else initialize Gomer-Rec    <----- It's HERE your spaces are significant
           move MyKey to PrimeKey
           and move other stuff to Gomer-Rec
           now WRITE Gomer-Rec
    End-if

I'm no expert, but above would be my logical take on the situation you describe.

Parallel your query with SQL -

    move spaces to my SQL fields
    now CONNECT  to the Database or 'invoke' a particular table
    - that move spaces isn't worth tiddly-squat. - the Sql fields are only
    significant when you get into the Table

Jimmy, Calgary AB

PS: On being drunk. Early part of last century, Winston Churchill was a couple
of sheets to the wind, in the House of Commons. Nancy Astor, (I believe),
prodded him from the benches behind. "Winston. You are drunk !". He leered at
her and said, "And Madam, you are beautiful". Then added, "But tomorrow, I
shalll be sobre !".

And for the gent who had the by-line "Mary had a little Lamb", this from an
Irish joke book :-

    Did you hear about the Irish red setter that was lying down chewing a bone.
    When it got up, it only had three legs.
```

#### ↳ Re: What's the difference?

- **From:** Bill <wfsent@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2001-12-06T03:17:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C0EE32E.D2F83CEE@optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<JczP7.256347$dk.17394792@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
Until you open a file you don't have any storage assigned to the FD. So
who knows what part of memory you're writting to

Also at end of file, you can't access the record area. So often it's
easiest to write file matching programs (mainfile vs transaction file
etc) using keys that are defined in WS.

read a record, if not eof move key to w-s, else move high-values to w-s
etc

Bill

#### ↳ Re: What's the difference?

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)
- **Date:** 2001-12-06T04:05:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<18CP7.1671$0g.46208@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<JczP7.256347$dk.17394792@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
In article <JczP7.256347$dk.17394792@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>,
JerryMouse <news@bisusa.com> wrote:
>Between:
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>root out.

The difference is that one teaches you the value of the dictum 'Thou Shalt
Not *Touch* File Buffers'.

In the FD copybook the DATA RECORD should read:

01  FILEREC PIC X(nnn).

(where nnn has been coded in the RECORD CONTAINS clause).  After this one
should *always* READ INTO, WRITE FROM and do everything else to
WS-FILEREC-LAYOUT.

DD
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: What's the difference?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-12-06T05:22:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C0F0122.8D3E2E08@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<JczP7.256347$dk.17394792@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <18CP7.1671$0g.46208@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```


NA wrote:

> The difference is that one teaches you the value of the dictum 'Thou Shalt
> Not *Touch* File Buffers'.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I know this one has come up before, but care to elaborate. Is it perchance a
mainframe thing associated with how the O/S does file handling ? I understand
what you are getting at - to avoid corruption, but essentially I don't do it on a
PC. So a recap on the dangers - and "my style" below :-

sequentials -

invariably I'll kick-off with an initialize seq-rec or print-rec
move different bits of info to seq-rec or print-rec
write seq-rec or print-rec

ISAMS (or Relatives for that matter):

set RecordNotChanged to true
move ws-key to PrimeKey
Read File key is Primekey
 invalid key set RecordNotFound to true
 not invalid key set RecordFound to true
end-read

say we are into displaying on a screen

if    RecordFound
      display using the File-Rec
      and if user changes a field set RecordChanged to true

else initialize File-Rec
       set RecordChanged to true
       move ws-key to Primekey
       start accepting new record
        (say it's a Customer Record - if they leave the first field = Name =
blank, then
        obviously they don't want to continue, so exit input routine)
       when complete start accepting changes
End-if

When OK - do validity checks and if errors 'display" and prompt for fresh input
on 'offending' fields - then return to 'When OK'

When OK is "A-OK", i.e. no validity errors

if    RecordChanged

      if RecordFound REWRITE File-Rec

     else WRITE File-Rec
     end-if
End-if

(Obviously above doesn't cover deletes - if RecordFound and they select "Delete"
-
then delete it when they give a positive to, "Are you sure you want to delete
this record ?").

Now currently of course, (using OO),  I've got my file info split between
Business Logic and separate File Handling classes. Even so, the above is the
technique I used with dear old RM/COBOL '74.

Maybe I've just been damn lucky but I've never had problems with writing or
rewriting from File-Rec. Reflecting back, the background to that was that I was
trying to conserve memory when restricted to 64K on a Tandy(Radio Shack), hence
no ws-Rec ???????

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What's the difference?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-06T07:49:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rqFP7.32620$WC1.3323434@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<JczP7.256347$dk.17394792@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <18CP7.1671$0g.46208@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3C0F0122.8D3E2E08@shaw.ca>`

```
Jimmy,
  In your note, you don't show when you do the OPENs and CLOSEs.  I think
(but am not certain) that you agree that it is a "no-no" to "touch" the
record-area before an OPEN or after a CLOSE.  As this is "explicitly
undefined", the behavior DEFINITELY does vary from compiler-to-compiler,
release-to-release, and OS to OS.

As far as the READ INTO/WRITE FROM, this is (yet another) religious battle.
As long as you don't "touch" the record area (defined under the FD) before
an OPEN or after a CLOSE, you are OK, either way.

There are some "historical" (and/or hysterical) reasons that IBM mainframe
people "tend" (not always - not all programmers - not all shops) to use READ
INTO and WRITE FROM (for *all* file types).

A) At one time, IBM allowed you to access the "buffer" after a CLOSE.  Lots
of programs used the "move high-values to record-under-fd" approach to
indicate EOF.  Then IBM *stopped* allowing references to this area and all
those programs DIED when programs checked the fields for high-values AFTER
the file was closed.
  Once burnt, then NEVER let anything be "moved to" or to be "tested from"
those fields again.

B) Given some (primitive) debugging tools, it was MUCH easier to "read" the
data in WS copies of records (when an ABEND occurred) than it was to find
the FD (which might have "lots" of records "buffered" in them - at time of
"dump").

C) Many mainframe COBOL programmers came from master-file/transaction-file
"update" environments where you (often? usually? almost always?) needed to
"keep" the last record to compare it with the next record.  Therefore, you
ended up "moving" it to WS anyway, so why not START with a READ INTO - and
then do your WRITE FROM when you had finished doing all the "updates" in WS.

  ***

I believe that many (most?) IBM mainframe COBOL people still think it is
"good" programming to always do READ INTO and WRITE FROM - even if they
don't know WHY they need to do this.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What's the difference?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-06T09:20:02-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9uo9c3$1e92$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<JczP7.256347$dk.17394792@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <18CP7.1671$0g.46208@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3C0F0122.8D3E2E08@shaw.ca> <rqFP7.32620$WC1.3323434@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Bill Klein wrote:

<<I think ... that it is a "no-no" to "touch" the record-area before an OPEN
or after a CLOSE.  As this is "explicitly undefined", the behavior
DEFINITELY does vary from compiler-to-compiler, release-to-release, and OS
to OS. >>

Just as a terminology nit:  Offhand (and I might well have missed it) I
don't see the contents of the record area being *explicitly undefined*; the
'74 and '85 standards and the FCD seem to emphasize the *unavailability* of
the record area itself to the runtime element.

I agree that one of the consequences of a storage area being unavailable to
the program is that from the standpoint of that program the contents of that
unavailable storage area are perforce *implicitly* undefined, but that's a
consequence, not an underlying rule.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What's the difference?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-12-06T18:32:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C0FBA6D.9B49CBBD@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<JczP7.256347$dk.17394792@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <18CP7.1671$0g.46208@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3C0F0122.8D3E2E08@shaw.ca> <rqFP7.32620$WC1.3323434@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:

> Jimmy,
>   In your note, you don't show when you do the OPENs and CLOSEs.  I think
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Bill,

Most certainly I don't attempt to "touch" the record area before an OPEN or
after a CLOSE.

For clarity -

RM/COBOL - perform X000-OPEN-FILES and PERFORM X999-CLOSE FILES - separate
housekeeping routines at start-up and close of program. (One ugly thing, can't
remember file count - I think maybe 8 or 10. You could get a file-status 98
indicating a crashed index. Wasn't so - it, to the best of my knowledge, was an
undocumented limit in RM/COBOL both on the Tandy and PC compilers - so nothing
to do with either the Tandy TRSDOS (nicknamed "TrashDOS") or MSDOS. Given that
you needed more than the limit, necessitated both opening and closing currently
required files, plus a use of SAME FILE/RECORD AREA).

There was one "nasty" with the TRSDOS, regardless of programming language. Tom
or Volker may recall. The O/S only allowed you to make 12 (?) extensions, (add
more records). You had to be aware of this and put your files through a TRSDOS
rebuild.

OO approach - Essentially the same thing, OPEN and CLOSE methods within the
Business Logic class, messaging  from Business logic to the respective File
classes.

Even if I ONLY open output an ErrorFile when needed - still invoke the OPEN in
the File Class. CLOSE procedure - "If ErrorFile (object reference ) <> null then
invoke ErrorFile class "close").

As DD and you have both confirmed - it was a style introduced to mainframing,
justified at the time of its original introduction (Even now, doesn't do any
harm if you actually want to code that way)..

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What's the difference?

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)
- **Date:** 2001-12-06T14:55:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kFLP7.1975$0g.47749@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<JczP7.256347$dk.17394792@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <18CP7.1671$0g.46208@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3C0F0122.8D3E2E08@shaw.ca>`

```
In article <3C0F0122.8D3E2E08@shaw.ca>, James J. Gavan <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:
>
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>I know this one has come up before, but care to elaborate.

Eh?  There is little elaboration needed for a Commandment; consider:

'Thou Shalt Not Commit Adultery'

'Could you elaborate on that?'

>Is it perchance a
>mainframe thing associated with how the O/S does file handling ?

I believe so... my training was in that newfangled COBOL '74 - I think the
textbook was McCracken - for IBM mainframes.  In addition to the problems
associated with addressing a buffer before OPENs, after CLOSES and after
bad IO attempts there was also something funky (how's *that* for Technical
Talk?) which could result from attempts at arithmetic operations on
buffered data... I don't remember the specifics on this last one but I
recall that it bit a buddy on the butt when we were doing some work on a
WANG VS.

[snippage]

>Maybe I've just been damn lucky but I've never had problems with writing or
>rewriting from File-Rec. Reflecting back, the background to that was that I was
>trying to conserve memory when restricted to 64K on a Tandy(Radio Shack), hence
>no ws-Rec ???????

My first COBOL instructor told us about getting fired for asking that his
shop upgrade from 8K to 16K (Manager: '16K?!?  *No* programmer worth his
salary needs 16K!')... I wonder how the compiler you used on the Tandy
dealt with RESERVE NO ALTERNATE AREAS.

(in these days of cheap memory this wondering is, of course, purely
academic)

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What's the difference?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-12-06T17:49:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C0FB038.993B640@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<JczP7.256347$dk.17394792@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <18CP7.1671$0g.46208@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3C0F0122.8D3E2E08@shaw.ca> <kFLP7.1975$0g.47749@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```


NA wrote:

>
> My first COBOL instructor told us about getting fired for asking that his
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Can't answer you on that one about the Tandy, 'cos I dumped the books, long,  long
ago - but this from RM/COBOL V2 for PCs :-

    SELECT file-name
        ASSIGN .....
        RESERVE integer {AREA} {AREAS}
        ORGANIZATION etc......

"The RESERVE clause may be used but it is inactive in RM/COBOL. It is supported for
the sake of compatibility with other systems".

So, it never raised its ugly head......

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What's the difference?

- **From:** "warren.simmons" <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-12-06T18:39:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hXOP7.146347$WW.9355326@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<JczP7.256347$dk.17394792@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <18CP7.1671$0g.46208@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3C0F0122.8D3E2E08@shaw.ca>`

```
Hi James,

Nice reply. However, my reading of it did not break the rules NA gave.

I/O has historically been a separate and vendor supplied code.  Vendors
"try" to optimize for a wide variety of user types.  Since I'm way out of it
by now, I can't say that there are new types, but
Not invented here usually rears it's head.  Otherwise, how do you lock in
the user?

Warren Simmons


James J. Gavan <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3C0F0122.8D3E2E08@shaw.ca...
>
>
> NA wrote:
>
> > The difference is that one teaches you the value of the dictum 'Thou
Shalt
> > Not *Touch* File Buffers'.
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > (where nnn has been coded in the RECORD CONTAINS clause).  After this
one
> > should *always* READ INTO, WRITE FROM and do everything else to
> > WS-FILEREC-LAYOUT.
> >
>
> I know this one has come up before, but care to elaborate. Is it perchance
a
> mainframe thing associated with how the O/S does file handling ? I
understand
> what you are getting at - to avoid corruption, but essentially I don't do
it on a
> PC. So a recap on the dangers - and "my style" below :-
>
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>         (say it's a Customer Record - if they leave the first field = Name
=
> blank, then
>         obviously they don't want to continue, so exit input routine)
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> When OK - do validity checks and if errors 'display" and prompt for fresh
input
> on 'offending' fields - then return to 'When OK'
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> (Obviously above doesn't cover deletes - if RecordFound and they select
"Delete"
> -
> then delete it when they give a positive to, "Are you sure you want to
delete
> this record ?").
>
> Now currently of course, (using OO),  I've got my file info split between
> Business Logic and separate File Handling classes. Even so, the above is
the
> technique I used with dear old RM/COBOL '74.
>
> Maybe I've just been damn lucky but I've never had problems with writing
or
> rewriting from File-Rec. Reflecting back, the background to that was that
I was
> trying to conserve memory when restricted to 64K on a Tandy(Radio Shack),
hence
> no ws-Rec ???????
>
> Jimmy
>
>
```

>

Or:

#### ↳ Re: What's the difference?

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-12-06T14:48:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9uo0b1$2a2$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<JczP7.256347$dk.17394792@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
Fujitsu is technically correct in allowing execution time errors here, as
that space was undefined.

That said, that is IMHO, unacceptable behavior for a compiler.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: What's the difference?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-12-06T17:46:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<faOP7.524$hE.265746@paloalto-snr2.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<JczP7.256347$dk.17394792@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <9uo0b1$2a2$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:9uo0b1$2a2$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
> Fujitsu is technically correct in allowing execution time errors here
[MOVE to file buffer with file not open], as
> that space was undefined.
>
> That said, that is IMHO, unacceptable behavior for a compiler.

Um, what do you want the compiler to do, trace all possible execution paths
of the program to determine if a "read" or "move" could 'possibly' be
executed with the file unopened and issue a compile-time warning?

I mean, I really don't need a compile time warning about this: I did RTFM.
Besides, I'll betcha the typical program could have so many 'syntactically
possible' ways to get to a READ or MOVE with the file not open that you'd
have so many warning messages they'd be useless anyway.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What's the difference?

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <news@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-06T21:56:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9QRP7.161720$8q.17257410@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<JczP7.256347$dk.17394792@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <9uo0b1$2a2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <faOP7.524$hE.265746@paloalto-snr2.gtei.net>`

```

"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>

> > Fujitsu is technically correct in allowing execution time errors here
> [MOVE to file buffer with file not open], as
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Um, what do you want the compiler to do, trace all possible execution
paths
> of the program to determine if a "read" or "move" could 'possibly' be
> executed with the file unopened and issue a compile-time warning?
>
No, of course not. But the runtime could set a switch when the file is
opened and any access to the record area before the switch was set could
generate an error message more meaningful than "The Mayor's horse has
Chastic Fibrosis (a disease usually found in quartz)."

On the other hand, your suggestion about the compiler testing all possible
paths is a good one.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What's the difference?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-06T22:47:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PzSP7.33811$WC1.3452480@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<JczP7.256347$dk.17394792@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <9uo0b1$2a2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <faOP7.524$hE.265746@paloalto-snr2.gtei.net> <9QRP7.161720$8q.17257410@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
I think that the "confusion" in this thread had to do with the statement,

 "That said, that is IMHO, unacceptable behavior for a compiler."

Many people use the phrase "a compiler" to refer to BOTH the compiler and
the run-time support (usually? always? almost always?) provided by the same
vendor.

I read the original note as an indication that when Fujitsu gave a user
UN-friendly message at run-time, that this was viewed as unacceptable.  I
would tend to agree with this, although what one user/programmer finds
"useful" gibberish to others.

P.S.  This is time for my usual "suggestion" that anyone finding the current
run-time message incomprehensible should CONTACT THE VENDOR for an
"enhancement request" to improve the message.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What's the difference?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-12-07T14:40:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hx4Q7.684$ih2.30428@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<JczP7.256347$dk.17394792@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <9uo0b1$2a2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <faOP7.524$hE.265746@paloalto-snr2.gtei.net> <9QRP7.161720$8q.17257410@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
FILE STATUS will work with READ ( but not MOVE).

Can't think of anything which will work with MOVE except in a Windows
Environment (IsBadReadPtr, IsBadWritePtr).

But isn't the easiest "fix" to just avoid accessing the buffer until the
file is open?


MCM

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-07T10:03:09-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rsp11u83fc2rk5rs0k0rngpm0hdeh7dt8p@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<JczP7.256347$dk.17394792@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <9uo0b1$2a2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <faOP7.524$hE.265746@paloalto-snr2.gtei.net> <9QRP7.161720$8q.17257410@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
On Thu, 06 Dec 2001 21:56:21 GMT "JerryMouse" <news@bisusa.com> wrote:

:>"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>

:>> > Fujitsu is technically correct in allowing execution time errors here
:>> [MOVE to file buffer with file not open], as
:>> > that space was undefined.

:>> > That said, that is IMHO, unacceptable behavior for a compiler.

:>> Um, what do you want the compiler to do, trace all possible execution
:>paths
:>> of the program to determine if a "read" or "move" could 'possibly' be
:>> executed with the file unopened and issue a compile-time warning?

:>No, of course not. But the runtime could set a switch when the file is
:>opened and any access to the record area before the switch was set could
:>generate an error message more meaningful than "The Mayor's horse has
:>Chastic Fibrosis (a disease usually found in quartz)."

Ask your compiler provider to implement something equivalent to SSRANGE (an
IBM compiler option which will insert additional object code to check all
subscripts at the time they are used). That way if one is willing to suffer
the performance hit they can shoot themselves in the foot.

:>On the other hand, your suggestion about the compiler testing all possible
:>paths is a good one.

Snicker.
```

##### ↳ ↳ READ INTO/WRITE FROM wars was Re: What's the difference?

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-12-07T00:32:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C100E3E.239DE3AA@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<JczP7.256347$dk.17394792@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <9uo0b1$2a2$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Since I am fairly certain IBM COBOL for this and that and now just this
will give you a S0C4 protection exception trapped by the LE routines for
MOVE SPACES TO UNOPENED-FILE-REC, I think that Fujitsu is taking the
correct action.  Since COBOL II kept addressability to the buffer after
an AT END condition (addressability was dropped in earlier COBOL's at
END OF FILE rather than CLOSE) and the LE dump (or earlier Abend-aid
dumps) give you the record that was available at time of abend, most of
the necessity for READ INTO/WRITE FROM has gone away.  The one area that
remains on many systems is if you have arithmetic data that must be
aligned.  If the record isn't a multiple of the alignment factor (word,
half word, etc.), the arithmetic fields in the second and many of the
subsequent records will not be aligned properly if the records are
buffered.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services, Inc.

Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> Fujitsu is technically correct in allowing execution time errors here, as
> that space was undefined.
> 
> That said, that is IMHO, unacceptable behavior for a compiler.
```

In the IBM cobol environment open acquires the buffers and makes the ad-
dress available to the pgm and the access method. As to why the system doesn't
react more graciously to the attempt to move data to the rec area before open, I
guess it doesn't know what the hell to do with it. As to the compiler flagging
it as an error, I don't know. Guess it's a question of "not my yob". Bill Kline
and I danced around a bit on a similar isssue involving the cobol compiler's
refusal to get involved in someone coding an IF stmt that could never be true.

Jack

> Bill Kline and I danced around a bit on a similar isssue involving the
cobol compiler's
> refusal to get involved in someone coding an IF stmt that could never be
true.

The IBM Mainframe COBOL compiler's optimizer detects this condition,
discards the 'unreachable' code from the load module and reports it. It is
up to the user to 'do something' but the the tool is certainly there.

MCM

Jack,

It's not a matter of whose job it is. There are many technical reasons that
have existed from the
beginning that control  allocation of devices including buffers. If you have
read the messages
discussing the method used in Main Frames (a primary IBM forcus) you will
see that until the program starts Nothing or very little is allocated in the
way of resources. These systems are to
"large" and "fast" that in pico seconds a new resource requirement must be
met. After the
program starts, and resources are allocated, the operating system must see
what the program is to do. Then resources may be allocated to a program..

We program with the idea that everything is related to OUR program, and that
when the program starts, the computer knows everything it needs to know. Our
declaration of I/O and File specs before we step the computer through the
logic of the needed operation that OUR job is designed to do.  It is not
magic.

If you did not order soup would you expect a soup spoon? Well, I would
because my hand trembels too much to hit my mouth with a fork lots of the
time.  Anyway, I hope this silly description of the way things operaate will
make a difference in how you think about the steps you write in a program.
You need to order soup before the waiter will bring the spoon.  That calls
for the OPEN
before the Read or Write. It also calls for the CLOSE if you wish to save
the results.  Like calling
for the bill, then paying and leaving.

Regards,

Warren Simmons
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What's the difference?

- **From:** stephenjspiro@mail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-12-10T00:51:53-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<928495c6.0112100051.13161798@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<JczP7.256347$dk.17394792@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <9uo58n$9idjv$1@ID-101435.news.dfncis.de> <6GEQ7.54862$xS6.88932@www.newsranger.com>`

```
John H Sleight Jr <nospam@newsranger.com> wrote in message news:<6GEQ7.54862$xS6.88932@www.newsranger.com>...
> In the IBM cobol environment open acquires the buffers and makes the ad-
> dress available to the pgm and the access method. As to why the system doesn't
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jack   


Depends on the operating system. In IBM Mainframe DOS (used to be,
anyway), you could access the FD record area before the open.  In
OS/VS2 and succeeding releases, if a file is opened for INPUT, you
cannot access the FD record area UNTIL THE FIRST READ, because the
buffers are not allocated until a record is actually read. In VS1 and
earlier releases, you could access after the OPEN even on OPEN INPUT. 
As someone (Bill Klein) said, buffer access is an operating system (or
I/O subsystem) consideration, not a compiler feature.

---------
Regarding the religious issue, I very rarely use READ INTO or WRITE
FROM. For very large files, that can be a huge amount of overhead. And
I usually find it completely unnecessary.
Similarly, in CICS, I create my COMMAREA with a GETMAIN, and always
work with it in LINKAGE, not WORKING-STORAGE.  Similarly with maps and
other data acquired from I/O.
My experience with people who always use WORKING-STORAGE is that most
of them do not really understand buffers and/or LINKAGE adequately; or
they learned from such people, and prefer the style with which they
were trained.   For some, I suppose, in non-MVS environments, there
may also be error-message or debugging considerations.
In MVS, WORKING-STORAGE for I/O is inefficient.  

Stephen J Spiro
```

the FD information cannot be accessed until after the first read is simply not
true.

How it the world do you think a record key is filled in for a START or READ on
an indexed file?  I've done this a lot with this release and beyond -- you can't
access it before the open, but you certainly CAN before a read.
