###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What's the difference?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2001-12-10T14:16:16-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f6c8a076.0112101416.c7cf20f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<JczP7.256347$dk.17394792@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <9uo58n$9idjv$1@ID-101435.news.dfncis.de> <6GEQ7.54862$xS6.88932@www.newsranger.com> <928495c6.0112100051.13161798@posting.google.com> <3C15B3E0.A7737601@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message news:<3C15B3E0.A7737601@Azonic.co.nz>...
> Stephen J Spiro wrote:
> > 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> would you set the key ?

The draft COBOL standard, and I think all previous standards, specify
that the record area is not available until a successful OPEN
statement has been executed.  The exception is when another OPEN
statement has already opened a file and the SAME clause of the
I-O-CONTROL paragraph has been specified such that both files use the
same record areaa.  Similarly, the record area is no longer available
after a CLOSE statement, unless another file is still open for which
the SAME clause rules also apply.

The benefits of not allowing such access is that it allows direct
access to the file buffers for reading and writing, i.e. in IBM
mainframe assembler terms one can then use locate mode for the I-O
instead of using GET and PUT mode.  This is arguably more efficient in
the general case, preventing the program using what would otherwise be
an implicit form of working-storage for the record areas even when
still in the FD definition and to which the object code would have had
to move the record from the real file buffer.

Whether or not I use READ INTO and WRITE FROM depends on the
circumstances, I tend to use them when doing traditional sequential
updates.

Another point to watch is that when writing or updating variable
length files, the use of working-storage for the record area can be
beneficial.  This is because changing the length of a record when
updating a file in situ necessitates a DELETE and WRITE instead of a
REWRITE.  And, because when using WRITE FROM, the I-O subsystem can
wait until the record is written to determine whether to write the
block before the current record or after it, depending on whether the
size of the current record would cause the file buffer to overflow,
which would not be known until the length is specified.  This allows
the I-O subsystem to prevent the creation of unneccessarily short
blocks for the file.

For those unaware of the idea of record blocking in files, on
mainframes (well the IBM ones that I know about) records are grouped
in blocks as physical rather than logical records to enable tape and
disk storage to be used more efficiently by reducing the the number of
inter-block gaps that are necessary to separate physical records.
```

#### ↳ Re: What's the difference?

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-12-06T19:48:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C0FCB91.7AB2E375@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<JczP7.256347$dk.17394792@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
JerryMouse wrote:
> 
> MOVE SPACE TO GOMER-REC.
> OPEN OUPUT GOMER-FILE.

Some compilers, not all, create the memory area for the file record when
the OPEN statement is successful.  This means that you may not access
any of the record area until a successful OPEN of the file has been
completed. Nor may you access this area after a CLOSE because the memory
may (will) be freed.
```

---

<a id="t-c92c5af522"></a>
## Thread: "TABLE-SIZE" a reserved word ? — 4 msgs

### "TABLE-SIZE" a reserved word ?

- **From:** tmartin9@caramail.com (Timothy Martin)
- **Date:** 2001-12-05T18:54:50-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bf43e12d.0112051854.554f3499@posting.google.com>`

```
Earlier today I almost had a question about sorting tables,
but then I found out the sub-program that I am getting
data from can sort the data it sends me.  So I have no
question now about sorting tables, but when I was wondering
about how to sort a table I did a search on groups.google
and I found a very good article posted by a guy named
"Leif Svalgaard".  In June of 1998 Mr. Svalgaard posted
the following:

In all  examples of  code below,  we assume  that the data
being sorted is defined as follows:

01  TABLE-TO-SORT.
    02  TABLE-SIZE              PIC S9(5)  COMP.
    02  TABLE-MAX               PIC S9(5)  COMP  VALUE +1600.
    02  TABLE-ITEM                         OCCURS 1600 TIMES
        03  TABLE-KEY           PIC X(9).
        03  TABLE-DATA          PIC X(11).

The size  of the key and data simply reflect the data used
in the test programs.  The table size, 1600 items, makes the
table 32000  bytes large.    COBOL  does  not  allow  tables
greater than 32767 bytes (except as a non-portable extension
on some  systems).   A useful device for working with larger
tables (memory permitting) is to split the table:

01  LARGE-TABLE-ITEM1.
    02  TABLE-KEY               PIC X(9)  OCCURS 2900 TIMES.

01  LARGE-TABLE-ITEM2.
    02  TABLE-DATA              PIC X(11) OCCURS 2900 TIMES.

1.                    Bubble Sort

As the  name suggests,  bubble sort moves items up a table
like bubbles  in a  tube.  The algorithm can be explained as
follows: pass  over the data, comparing and exchanging items
so that  the largest  item ends  up at the end of the table.
Repeat for  the remaining  items until  the table is sorted,
that is, no exchanges were made during the latest pass.

The following code does a bubble sort:

01  VARIOUS-INDICES.
    02  ITEM-NBR                PIC S9(5)  COMP.
    02  SWAP-NBR                PIC S9(5)  COMP.
    02  JUMP-SIZE               PIC S9(5)  COMP.
    02  UPPER-LIMIT             PIC S9(5)  COMP.
01  VARIOUS-VALUES.
    02  SWAP-ITEM               PIC X(20).
    02  SWAP-INDICATOR          PIC X(1).
       88  NO-MORE-SWAPS           VALUE IS SPACE.

BUBBLE-SORT-THE-ARRAY.
    MOVE "SWAP" TO SWAP-INDICATOR
    MOVE   1    TO JUMP-SIZE
    PERFORM BUBBLE-SORT
         UNTIL NO-MORE-SWAPS
    .

BUBBLE-SORT.
    MOVE SPACE TO SWAP-INDICATOR
    COMPUTE UPPER-LIMIT = TABLE-SIZE - JUMP-SIZE
    PERFORM COMPARE-AND-SWAP-KEYS
         VARYING ITEM-NBR FROM 1 BY 1
            UNTIL ITEM-NBR > UPPER-LIMIT
    .

COMPARE-AND-SWAP-KEYS.
    COMPUTE SWAP-NBR = ITEM-NBR + JUMP-SIZE
    IF TABLE-KEY (ITEM-NBR) > TABLE-KEY (SWAP-NBR)
          MOVE TABLE-ITEM (ITEM-NBR) TO SWAP-ITEM
          MOVE TABLE-ITEM (SWAP-NBR) TO TABLE-ITEM (ITEM-NBR)
          MOVE  SWAP-ITEM TO TABLE-ITEM (SWAP-NBR)
          MOVE "SWAP" TO SWAP-INDICATOR
     .
====================================================
My question now is all that above okay ?  I keep looking
at and thinking it has a tiny error in it.  Won't the
program bomb at "COMPUTE UPPER-LIMIT = TABLE-SIZE - JUMP-SIZE",
because TABLE-SIZE has no defined value ?  There's no VALUE
clause for TABLE-SIZE, and I don't see it getting initialized
anywhere else. 

I am not being a stickler for details, but just simply wondering 
if there is something about "TABLE-SIZE" that I don't know
about.  Thanks for any info. 

I could just test this code out, but I don't have a lot of
time, and I figure it is easier to ask here.  I hope nobody
minds.
```

#### ↳ Sequenced tables

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2001-12-06T08:41:00-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C0F9F9C.1FA7FA90@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<bf43e12d.0112051854.554f3499@posting.google.com>`

```
In my opinion, the example you found has some problems.  In addition to
omitting the part of the code that loads the table, as another post
mentions, it omits the DEPENDING, ASCENDING / DESCENDING (KEY), and
INDEXED clauses, so SEARCH ALL could not be used, and all 1600 items
will participate in a search, because COBOL cannot tell how many actual
items are in the table.  In such a case, it is necessary to set unused
items to HIGH-VALUE, so that the compares work, and this increases run
time.  It mentions limits that existed in IBM's OS/VS COBOL compiler,
but have been relaxed (by a factor of 128) in newer compilers.  I don't
know too much about other vendors' compiler limits.  Finally, it does a
bubble sort, which is space efficient but time inefficient, and to crown
my complaints, it uses subscripts in place of indexes (probably not too
bad in the bubble sort case, if the compiler has a good optimizer).

Why not just insert the table items in the proper sequence?  This is
faster than waiting until the table is loaded to sort the entire set of
items (it is essentially an insertion sort, if I remember my sorting
definitions), and if the data is received in the proper sequence, it is
as fast as a load that doesn't check sequence, except for the one IF
statement.

Here's an example:
First, here is an example of how I like to define a table:
01  SOURCE-CODE-LIMIT PIC S9(4) BINARY VALUE +4096.
01  SOURCE-CODE-SIZE  PIC S9(4) BINARY VALUE ZERO.
01  SOURCE-CODE-TABLE.
 05 SOURCE-CODE-ENTRY
               OCCURS 1 TO 4096
            DEPENDING SOURCE-CODE-SIZE
            ASCENDING SOURCE-CODE
              INDEXED SC-X.
 10 SOURCE-CODE       PIC X(06).
This definition tells COBOL all it needs to do an efficient binary
search of the table, and provides the necessary variables to allow the
programmer to test for overflow while loading.

Here is generic code for adding an item to a table in the proper
sequence:
      ADD 1            TO 'object'
      IF  'object'     >  'table-limit'
          PERFORM TABLE-OVERFLOW
      END-IF
      SET 'index'      TO 'object'
      SET 'index' DOWN BY 1
      IF  'object'     >  1
          PERFORM MAKE-ROOM-IN-TABLE
              VARYING 'index' FROM 'index' BY -1
              UNTIL   'index'   <  1
                 OR   'new-entry-key' >  'entry-key(index)'
      END-IF
      MOVE 'new-entry' TO 'entry(index + 1)'
      .
MAKE-ROOM-IN-TABLE procedure reads:
  MAKE-ROOM-IN-TABLE                             SECTION.
      MOVE 'entry(index)' TO 'entry(index + 1)'
      .

The TABLE-OVERFLOW procedure would do something useful such as reading
the rest of the input file and adding to the counter, so that it could
display a message that says the table capacity is "n", and the number of
input records is "m".  Then, the programmer wouldn't have to do much
investigation to fix the problem.

Obviously, the MAKE-ROOM-IN-TABLE procedure could be eliminated in favor
of using an in line PERFORM, but I wrote this before our shop was fully
off of 1974 ANSI COBOL compilers.
```

#### ↳ Re: "TABLE-SIZE" a reserved word ?

- **From:** jraben@cascinc.com (Jeff Raben)
- **Date:** 2001-12-06T17:54:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c0fb049.8100585@news.bullseyetelecom.net>`
- **References:** `<bf43e12d.0112051854.554f3499@posting.google.com>`

```
tmartin9@caramail.com (Timothy Martin) wrote:

snip
>     .
>====================================================
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>minds.

if you notice... there are no data elements filled.
You are looking at a code fragment.
The MAIN(?) part fills the table and counts the items filling
TABLE-SIZE.

Jeff


and stir with a Runcible spoon...
```

#### ↳ Re: "TABLE-SIZE" a reserved word ?

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-12-06T19:56:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C0FCD78.6961952@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<bf43e12d.0112051854.554f3499@posting.google.com>`

```
Timothy Martin wrote:
> 
> BUBBLE-SORT-THE-ARRAY.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> anywhere else.

There is also nothing that sets Table-Key and Table-Data for the items
to be sorted.  These
values and Table-Size will be set before the program does a PERFORM
Bubble-Sort-The-Array.  The Table-Size will be a count of the number of
items that the program puts into the array, which must be less or equal,
and may be much less, than Table-Occurs.
```

## Thread: XML for COBOL programmers — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### XML for COBOL programmers

- **From:** coboltoxml@yahoo.com (Yakov)
- **Date:** 2001-12-06T08:03:57-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<72cb811c.0112060803.200bb859@posting.google.com>`

```
As a group of highly qualified developers and program analysts with
large experience in COBOL, CICS, DB2, Web Sphere, MQSeries, Java etc.
we were vary excited to hear about your interest in getting XML output
from COBOL and other MainFrame Legacy applications.
It seems that many organizations all over the world are facing the
same challenge.
Recently we&#8217;ve developed an overall independent solution to this
problem, based on the following principals:
1. Dialog with web server via MQ Series 
2. Receive URL & XML buffers to a COBOL structure by using our COBOL
API
3. Produce output XML files from genuine COBOL structures (including
occurs, redefines, copy and value options). In addition we give more
powerful meaning to the COBOL structure by defining dynamic arrays,
optional elements and independent processes (as standalone procedures
etc.).
The API is vary easy and user friendly and supports user exit
processing. (It was build explicitly for MainFrame environment.) One
of the most important features of which is high performance. It does
not require any special installations in CICS/COBOL environment.
The output XML Document is a well-formed XML with attributes and CDATA
features. It can contain DTD or XSL assigns.
We are interested in engaging in partnership with a serious
organization that is ready to back us up in our continuous effort to
get our solution to the status of a complete product.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: R: reading a microfocus data file

- **From:** none@none.com (wildman)
- **Date:** 2001-12-07T16:06:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c10da88.2062549@news.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<3c0fc581.27893262@news.libero.it> <lMQP7.253107$sq5.12267685@news.infostrada.it>`

```
On Thu, 06 Dec 2001 20:44:01 GMT, "matrix"
<andateacagaretutti@porcaputtana.al> wrote:

>Ciao, mi chiamo Fabrizio e sono un programmatore Cobol.
>i vari comp-3 comp-6 eccetera sono formati di compressione
…[103 more quoted lines elided]…
>
COMP is integer.  COMP-3 is like a floating point number written to
disk as binary data which means it does not write ASCII digits (like
PIC 99 would).  Nor is it integer.  It uses the binary number system,
so a large number like S9(10)V99 takes 6 bytes of storage.  If you're
new to COBOL, try thinking of COMP-3 fields as numbers that are
individually zipped - or as we say in COBOL - packed decimal.

I think the formula for COMP-3 was to divide the number by 2 to arrive
at the storage size.  If the length was odd, then round upwards.   For
example, PIC S9(9) COMP-3  is 5 bytes of packed decimal storage.  
The decimal point is always implied by the program picture and is not
written to disk.  

You might write a COBOL program to make things easier.  The MOVE
statement converts for you ...

MOVE  S(9)V99 [COMP-3]  TO  PIC S9(9)V99

You can also specify SIGN LEADING, TRAILING or strip it off with an
'edited' picture clause 9(9).(2) which will include the decimal point
when you write, if you need it.  Importing numbers to the likes of
MS-Access or Sequel Server usually expect the physical decimal point.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: reading a microfocus data file

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2001-12-07T14:37:04-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0112071437.25c4570@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3c0fc581.27893262@news.libero.it> <9uonaf$a1hd5$1@ID-101435.news.dfncis.de>`

```
"Jeff Farkas" <yo_dog40@hotmail.com> wrote in message news:<9uonaf$a1hd5$1@ID-101435.news.dfncis.de>...
> "Duilio Foschi" <dedalus@yifan.net> wrote in message
> news:3c0fc581.27893262@news.libero.it...
…[44 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Duilio Foschi

Jeff has taken you part of the way, but you also need to preserve the
signs, where present, of the numeric COMP-3 fields.  Signs are
specified where the S picture character is used, for example, as in
"PIC S999v99  COMP-3".

To do this you need to specify the new numeric fields as "PIC 9(n)
SIGN IS LEADING SEPARATE CHARACTER", where 'n' is the appropriate
equivalent definition for the particular numeric field, including
decimal places where necessary.  Then, when your conversion program
moves the numeric data from the old field to the new fields, the sign
will be stored in the leading separate character.  You could instead
specify the sign as a trailing character, but it doesn't really matter
which way you do it.  Remember that as a result of the conversion the
new data record will occupy more space than the original, so you will
have to specify JCL appropriately.
```

```
Ciao Duilio

Mi stai chiedendo la cosa piu' "stupida" e semplice che normalmente
si fa come primo programma (Hello World!) in qualsiasi linguaggio.

Il tutto dipende da che tipo di compilatore cobol usi (Testuale o GUI).

Per far apparire la scritta "ciao" alla riga 12 position 45 dello schermo 
in DOS, prendendo a base l'Acucobol si puo' scrivere:

IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID.  TEST.
ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
DATA DIVISION.
PROCEDURE DIVISION.
INIZIO.
    DISPLAY "CIAO" AT 1245 ERASE.
(oppure DISPLAY "CIAO" LINE 12 POSITION 45.)
    STOP RUN.

Per il problema della conversione, se mi fornisci il tracciato record
originale del file, posso provare a crearti un programma in cobol, che
poi dovrai adattare al compilatore dos in tuo possesso.
Cerchero' di mantenere i limiti imposti dagli standard che usano quasi 
tutti i compilatori.

La mia email: softline2000@tin.it

Ciao
Gianni
```

```
after that? All lines are the same length, so basically it's a fixed-size

JV
```

```
SD  SORT-FILE.
01  SORT-REC.
   02  SORT-KEY   PIC X(30).
```

```
Jacques,

What version of MFSORT are you using ?    My experience is that there are
changes between releases and even fix packs that may cause the 007 error(s).

If you have upgraded MFSORT, make sure when you applied the fix pack
from MF, that you also applied the documentation fix pack as well - if you're
trying to use syntax from the MF doco and it's not working, double check
this.

If all elase still fails, try posting your MFSORT statements and or JCL here.


Ethan Schultz


Jacques Vidal wrote:

> How do I sort a a line sequential file on the 1st 30 characters - these are
> guaranteed to be alphanumeric, although there my be packed decimal values
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> JV
```

#### ↳ Re: Microfocus sort utility

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-12-07T12:41:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C10B90E.88AF4F18@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<9uoi2l$i6m$1@s1.read.news.oleane.net>`

```
Jacques Vidal wrote:
> 
> How do I sort a a line sequential file on the 1st 30 characters - these are
> guaranteed to be alphanumeric, although there my be packed decimal values
> after that? 

You cannot sensibly have packed decimal (or COMP-anything) fields in a
LINE SEQUENTIAL file.  This is because in an ASCII file a packed field
could have a value that can be interpreted as a tab or line feed
character that would disrupt the format.

> All lines are the same length, so basically it's a fixed-size
> record sequential file with an EOL field separator.

Then just specify is as sequential and include the EOL in the record
layout.

> I've tried numerous syntaxes but always get an error 007 (syntax error). Is
> it because a LS file is assumed to contain only plain text?

To sort any data: table, line sequential, or any other just use INPUT
and OUTPUT PROCEDUREs and get the data from whereevr necessary and put
it out as required.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Microfocus sort utility

- **From:** "Jacques Vidal" <jacquesvidal@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-07T12:13:10
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9uqb82$8vm$1@s1.read.news.oleane.net>`
- **References:** `<9uoi2l$i6m$1@s1.read.news.oleane.net> <3C10B90E.88AF4F18@Azonic.co.nz>`

```

Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> a �crit dans le message :
3C10B90E.88AF4F18@Azonic.co.nz...

(Replying to several posts in one place)

> Jacques Vidal wrote:
> >
> > How do I sort a a line sequential file on the 1st 30 characters - these
are
> > guaranteed to be alphanumeric, although there my be packed decimal
values
> > after that?
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> > I've tried numerous syntaxes but always get an error 007 (syntax error).
Is
> > it because a LS file is assumed to contain only plain text?
>
> To sort any data: table, line sequential, or any other just use INPUT
> and OUTPUT PROCEDUREs and get the data from whereevr necessary and put
> it out as required.

The MFSORT I'm using is the one that comes packaged with OCDS 4.1.10 for
AIX. No MF fixes were applied on this system after the initial install.

My problem is these files data already exists, there are several of them
using several record layouts (the 1-30 was just an exemple), I have no
access to the COBOL sources that created these files, and no COBOL compiler
to write a sort program.

I just have a huge set a data files which I know were all created as line
sequential, with fixed-length fixed-layout lines that may contain COMP-3
data.

I've tried:

$ mfsort sort fields 1,30,CH,1 use datafile1 org LS record F give sortedfile

and also several others but always get these 007 error messages


TIA,

Jacques Vidal
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Microfocus sort utility

- **From:** Eden Server Support <TechSupport@RosebudUSA.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-07T08:07:05-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C10BEF9.14FD8F0F@RosebudUSA.com>`
- **References:** `<9uoi2l$i6m$1@s1.read.news.oleane.net> <3C10B90E.88AF4F18@Azonic.co.nz> <9uqb82$8vm$1@s1.read.news.oleane.net>`

```
Jacques,

I have no OCDS experience, but that shouldn't matter - MFSORT is
supposed to run everywhere, right ?

I use the following statements on Windows to sort a sequential file:


USE   T:\BCMD\TDRELDEV\BSDATA\TDTO050.DAT
RECORD,F,1455 ORG,SQ
GIVE   T:\BCMD\TDRELDEV\BSDATA\TDTO050.DAT
SORT FIELDS=(3,3,CH,A,19,9,CH,A)

Of course you'll have to change record length from 1455 to your length,
file names to your names and substitiue your FIELDS= info - but this
should work for you.

Note:  I use these statements in a TAKE file - see the MFSORT doco
on using TAKE...


Ethan Schultz


Jacques Vidal wrote:

> Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> a ï¿½crit dans le message :
> 3C10B90E.88AF4F18@Azonic.co.nz...
…[50 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jacques Vidal
```

```
CSC stands for Cablevision Systems Corporation.  But CSC Holdings may
offer ISP services over a huge area.

(Plagiarized directly from a press release) Cablevision Systems
Corporation is one of the nation's leading entertainment and

metropolitan area.
```

```
Just a little help from Network Solution's whois server.

http://www.netsol.com/cgi-bin/whois/whois

Although it is certainly possible for people to attempt to cloak their
identities using the Internet, e-mail and news headers usually carry a
bit of detail on the server from which the posting or message
originated.

million households in NYC.
```

```
2)  Once in class, pay attention, take notes

3)  Read the textbook

is free.  Next time, however, you'll have to pay my going rate of $100
USD an hour.
```

## Thread: SOMEONE PLEASE HELP ME COBOL PROGRAM — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### SOMEONE PLEASE HELP ME COBOL PROGRAM

- **From:** Serial # 19781010 <amar80@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2001-12-07T05:35:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<25l01uoct4ai5kdcfmmg9aqh81dvouv4bs@4ax.com>`

```
THIS IS THE DOCUMENT I HAVE ATTACHED


Create a student management system for the following tasks:
-Add/insert new students records
-Update student information
-Delete student record
-Add/update/delete student grade records

All these options should be invoked using a menu of choices.This
project can be done on powerCOBOL with a windows GUI or on a DOS based
interface.Data should be either stored on flat file (ordered files) or
in database(You should be able to manipulate the database with this
program).

The student record will be as follows:
Stuid 9(9)
Lname X(20)
Fname X(20)
Address1 X(20)
City X(10)
State X(10) default sahould be NJ
Email X(20)

Student grade record should be as follows:
Stuid	 9(9)
CourseName	X(30)
Grade 		X(3)
Date			mm/dd/yyyy format

This record structure can be modified as you wish.
Moreover more credit will be given to programs which actually execute
and have some innovation like use of database with powerCOBOL.
```

#### ↳ Re: SOMEONE PLEASE HELP ME COBOL PROGRAM

- **From:** Charles <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-10T19:00:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eD7R7.56391$xS6.91078@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<25l01uoct4ai5kdcfmmg9aqh81dvouv4bs@4ax.com>`

```
On Fri, 07 Dec 2001 05:35:09 GMT, in article
<25l01uoct4ai5kdcfmmg9aqh81dvouv4bs@4ax.com>, Serial # 19781010 stated 
>
>THIS IS THE DOCUMENT I HAVE ATTACHED
>
Hope you have written a GUI Cobol Program before.
If you havent you better look for a hello world example.

Entering a class is easy.  You just collect the infomation put it in a
record and write the record.  You would have to have a student record before you
can enroll a student in a class.  In the real world you do not delete a record
for a class.  What you do instead is use a grade code for withdrawn or some
other code.  It is possible to delete a student, but only if all the other
records with their student ID are deleted first.

To change a record you have to read a record and then rewrite it.
To delete a record you have to read a record and then delete it.
Better use records with a record key/index.

On an interactive program you do not end the program when you reach the end of
the file you have to make it end when you press a F3 key or a given command like
END or a Button.

In our school we have a file record for each student with their address and
other information, then we have a file for each course with the Course code,
course number, course section (CIS 135 01).  This has just the course data like
type name hours closed/open/cancelled, and number of students enrolled and the
limit that can enroll. Then we have 2 ways to look up the class file with the
grade in it; (course, stud-ID) or (stud-ID, course). You have to do both to make
class rosters and a list of classes for each student.  We use an occurs for the
times days the course meets which isn't normal form in a database, so we could
actually put all that in a separate file.  Courses can meet up to 7 days a week,
and at different times or locations, on different days.  

Of course each course file and class file also has a key for the year and
semester.  You have to know what semester the course is counted as.  Then we
have a semester file to indicate if each student has enrolled for the semester.
You can go crazy on a program like this because the possibilities are endless.

We never delete a student record because if a student drops out then the state
requires a record of their academic status.  The only time a Student record
could be deleted using a flat file is when all the associated class records with
the student identifier are deleted too to insure data integrity.  This would
only be done if the student had given us a wrong ssn.  Even if a student does
drop all classes before they start, we still need to have a record of the
activity because we probably already created a fee record and a bill, that we
need to change also.  Then if a student doesnt pay on time, we automatically
drop them from the course also.
>
I think we use about 100 online programs to manipulate our Student and
accounting information system, and most of them are about 1,000 lines each, give
or take a thousand.
>
>Create a student management system for the following tasks:
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
I believe he said he was working in some kind of PC COBOL environment...
so here's my contribution:


IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID.  FINLPROG.  
ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.   
DATA DIVISION.          
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01  SUMMARY-GRAND-TOTAL-LINE.
    05  FILLER PIC X(38) VALUE
        X'4940616D4041406641696C7572654077686F40'.
    05  FILLER PIC X(38) VALUE
        X'61656773406F746865727340746F40646F4074'.
    05  FILLER PIC X(38) VALUE
        X'6865697240776F726B40666F72407468656D2E'.
PROCEDURE DIVISION.
    DISPLAY SPACES AT 0101.
    DISPLAY SUMMARY-GRAND-TOTAL-LINE AT 1015.
    GOBACK.

DD
```

```
On Sun, 9 Dec 2001 00:40:52 +0800, "ecbaby" <ecbaby@hongkong.com>
wrote:

>Dear all,
>    I want to have a CICS simulator. Where can I get one ?
>
It depends how much you want to pay <s>  

If you want it for free - no chance (at least I don't know of a free
CICS simulator).

If you buy IBM's Visual Age for COBOL product - Enterprise Edition,
(or IBM's Visual Age for PLI, Enterprise Edition), it will set you
back for about USD 3000.  Lot's of money, but it includes an IBM
Mainframe compatible COBOL (or PLI) compiler, and a CICS Emulator
(which supports the same API as CICS/ESA Version 4.1.  It does not
support the CICS Web enablement features of CICS Transaction server,
though)
```

## Thread: Loading Tables — 8 msgs

### Loading Tables

- **From:** igal2000@hotmail.com (Igal)

```
Hey Guys! I have two problems....
1. could anyone give me an example of loading tables from data-files
and explain to me what is the basic logic behind it?
2. I had this problem in one of my tests and I could not get it...
 an input record consist of 15 group items, each with a 3-digit part
number and an associated 3-digit quantity on hand. Assuming there are
50 input records:
 a. write a working-storage entry to store the data
 b. write a procedure division code to load the records into the
working storage entry you created
 c. write procedure division code to print the quantity on hand for
part number 126
 d. write proc-division code to find the average quantity for all the
parts.

I hope someone can guide me through the process of doing this, because
my book is really bad...so is my teacher....
Thanks!!!
```

#### ↳ Re: Loading Tables

- **From:** Charles <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-11T15:15:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<DqpR7.57443$xS6.92895@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<c124eb5e.0112080927.5a5c0938@posting.google.com>`

```
On 8 Dec 2001 09:27:01 -0800, in article
<c124eb5e.0112080927.5a5c0938@posting.google.com>, Igal stated 
>

Oops! hit the wrong button.

This goes back to physics!!!

1.  It is faster to read a table than a file, since it is already in memory.
Reading a file takes longer, but it can be done that way in certain cases.  If
you have to search the table for every record of the main file it even takes
longer.

2.  A table is usually loaded using a sub-script.  If you have a table it
usually has an occurs (table occurs 200 times).  It is normal to read a table by
either using the varying verb or by incrementing an index or a variable you
described in working storage, as you read the file.    

3.  You can add the phrase "indexed by <variable>" to create an index for the
table.  It might be needed later to search the table.

>Hey Guys! I have two problems....
>1. could anyone give me an example of loading tables from data-files
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thanks!!!

01  MY-TABLE OCCURS 500 TIMES INDEXED BY PART-IDX.
05  T-PART-NO     PIC X(5).
05  T-PART-DESC   PIC X(30).
05  T-QTY-ON-HAND PIC 9(7).




You have a file you have to describe as input.  You just read it in order to
load the data into the file layout you have for the file. You move the data you
want into the table using a subscript.  
EXAMPLE:  "MOVE IN-PART-NO TO T-PART-NO (PART-IDX)".
The logic is the same as making a simple report.  The only difference is you are
moving the input fields to the table fields using PART-IDX as a sub-script.
When you get to the end of the file, you go to the next paragraph in the program
instead of the end of program processing.  You close the file because you no
longer need it.  You can use the same variable for end of file in the next part
of the program by setting it back to its starting value.   

This is really simple stuff.  If you know how to make a table, read a file, and
move a variable into a table you know all you need to know.  You just do this in
the initialization section of your program like setting printlines to zero.  You
can use the varying verb to actually increment the index or subscript, or just
add one to a variable for the subscript.
```

#### ↳ Re: Loading Tables

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompsonNOSPAM@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-13T17:11:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C18E12D.96B9317B@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<c124eb5e.0112080927.5a5c0938@posting.google.com>`

```
Igal wrote:
> 
> Hey Guys! I have two problems....
> 1. could anyone give me an example of loading tables from data-files
> and explain to me what is the basic logic behind it?
<snip>
1) You will need to write all of the logic for SELECT, FD,
01, OPEN INPUT and READ for the table. You will want to do
this as part of your house-keeping routines, because it must
be done prior to any other programming that will need to
reference the table.

2) The logic behind this is, the table may be common to many
different programs and/or it may be updated on a fairly
frequent basis. This would then cause you to have to
recompile a program every time the table changed.
<snip>
> 2. I had this problem in one of my tests and I could not get it...
>  an input record consist of 15 group items, each with a 3-digit part
> number and an associated 3-digit quantity on hand. Assuming there are
> 50 input records:
<snip>
The input record would look similar to this:

01 INPUT-TABLE-RECORD.
   02 INV-TABLE-ELEMENT      OCCURS 15 TIMES.
      03 INV-DETAIL.
         05 INV-PART-NO      PIC 9(3).
         05 INV-QUANTITY     PIC 9(3).

It is your job to set this up for indexing or subscripting.

<SNIP>
>  a. write a working-storage entry to store the data
<SNIP>

The table would look something like this:

01 INVENTORY-TABLE.
   03 INVENTORY-RECORDS OCCURS 1000 TIMES.
      05 INVENTORY-PART-NO  PIC 9(3) COMP-3.
      05 INVENTORY-QUANTITY PIC 9(3) COMP-3.

It is your job to set this table up to use index or
subscript.
<snip>
>  b. write a procedure division code to load the records into the
> working storage entry you created
<snip>
As I said before, you must determine the logic for this. It
will require an OPEN INPUT and a READ. Your job is to put
the code in there to handle the end of file, and to
implement subscript or index of the table and input record
table.
<snip>
>  c. write procedure division code to print the quantity on hand for
> part number 126
<snip>
Now, you will have to write the search logic or some
specific verb to do this (depending on how you ultimately
set up the table, indexed or subscripted, and if you paid
attention you would arrange for this to be in ascending
order by part number -- or it would already come to you in
ascending part number order).
<snip>
>  d. write proc-division code to find the average quantity for all the
> parts.
<snip>
Well, if you know math, then you will have to sequentially
read your table and capture the quantities. Then you will
also have to pay attention and capture the number of REAL
table entries (hint - the number 1000 that I used is well
beyond the number of entries you will get from 50 records
each having 15 entries). Only after this will you be able to
write the correct statements to determine the averages.
<snip>
> 
> I hope someone can guide me through the process of doing this, because
> my book is really bad...so is my teacher....
> Thanks!!!

You would have been in really tough shape had you been in my
COBOL class. You would have been expected to write this and
turn it in. And then for the next assignment, you would have
had to exchange this program with someone else in the class
(randomly selected by me), and then you would have been
given new specs that have to be applied to that program.
Your grade would be determined by how well you got your
"new" program to work, and how well your original program
could be modified (YES! It meant for me, having to grade 50
of these to spend about a week just doing the grading).
```

#### ↳ Re: Loading Tables

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-12-16T09:19:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C1C6722.D2ECB9@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<c124eb5e.0112080927.5a5c0938@posting.google.com>`

```
Igal wrote:
> 
>  an input record consist of 15 group items, each with a 3-digit part
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> parts.

If I were to be marking the results of this test then I would give the
highest marks to the solution that had c. similar to:

        MOVE 126                       TO Printer-Part-No
                                          Part-No
        MOVE Table-Quantity(Part-No)   TO Printer-Quantity
        WRITE Printer-Line

and d. as:

        MOVE ZERO         TO Total-Quantity
        MOVE ZERO         TO Total-Parts
        PERFORM VARYING Part-No FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL Part-No > 999
            IF ( Table-Part(I) > ZERO )
                ADD 1     TO Total-Parts
                ADD Table-Quantity(Part-No) TO Total-Quantity
            END-IF
        END-PERFORM
        COMPUTE Average-Quantity = Total-Quantity / Total-Parts

This would show that the programmer had read through the whole question
before deciding on a design that will produce the actual required
results of parts c) and d).

It would show that the programmer used the information supplied (that
part-numbers are 3 digit) and had been able to conceptualise the
transformation of arrays from one form on input to another form to solve
the problems that exist in produced the required results.

It would be becessary to increment each subscript by 1 if part number
'000' was valid.

If part c. had been coded similar to:

     MOVE 126      TO Part-Code
     MOVE "N"      TO Found-Flag
     PERFORM VARYING Record-No FROM 1 BY 1 
               UNTIL Record-No > 50
                  OR Found-Flag = "Y"
        PERFORM VARYING Group-No FROM 1 BY 1 
                  UNTIL Group-No > 15
                     OR Found-Flag = "Y"
            IF ( Group-Code(Record-No Group-No) = Part-Code  )
                MOVE Group-Quantity(Record-No Group-No)
                            TO Part-Quantity
                MOVE "Y"    TO Found-Flag
            END-IF
         END-PERFORM
     END-PERFORM

then I would give it very low marks.  This indicates that the solution
had been decided before the problem had been fully read.  The structure
is based on the input and _not_ on the requirements of the problem.  The
indications are that the design of the program had been decided when
reading part b) leading to a part c) (above) that is overly complex and
failing to actually solve the required problem.

An additional point would be given if the found-flag had not been coded
and the 'Part-Quantity' had been added to for each occurance of part-no
126.  However this would still make it impossible to determine the
average quantities of the _parts_, as against the average quantity of
the _groups_, without a further transformation into an array that was
either sorted by part number or used the part number as an index. There
is nothing to say that the part-number won't repeat in the input data.

A solution that only used a single array and stored the data
sequentially would get an intermediate score from me as: the 50 is only
to be assumed, there may be more; and the data would need to be sorted
or accumulated by part number before the average quantity can be
determined as it will be necessary to know not only the total quantity
but the number of _different_ part numbers.

As with _all_ programming, it is necessary to design to solve part d)
not just to solve part c) or (worse) just part b) and then hope that the
rest can be fixed later.

I hope that no one will accuse me of 'missing' something here.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Loading Tables

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <nospam@newsranger.com>

<a id="t-e49e5c4b05"></a>
## Thread: COBOL Migration Alternatives? — 30 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### COBOL Migration Alternatives?

- **From:** broom@voicenet.com (broom)
- **Date:** 2001-12-08T17:16:30-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c948eb61.0112081716.3700dfde@posting.google.com>`

```
Yea though I walk through the valley of death.

I am a clueless COBOL newbie.  I am a C and Perl programmer, 
an Oracle SQL geek, a mid-range Sun hardware geek, who has 
been thrust in a very dangerous situation.

My input can destroy the careers of many COBOL programmers
in my company, and I wish to avoid that.  A failed implementation
could destroy the company.  I like that even less.

The corporate MIS direction seems to be throw away COBOL,
move from the MVS on the mainframe, and redeploy the world 
on the Linux partitition of the mainframe, and possibly
move to Linux or Solaris elsewhere.

Since I know many of what they consider the target 
technologies, I've been put in a research and recommend
position.

We have 30 years of flat file and VSAM COBOL code that has
been twisted and tortured into place.  We use CICS.  Our 
actual data quantity is pretty small, ie: few hundred GB.

We've got about 100 users, customer service types, plus
inventory tracking and specialized accounting code. 

The buzzwords of the moment are Linux on the Mainframe,
IBM Websphere, and Java.

What are my alternatives?

My goals are:

1) Don't kill the company.

2) Keep as many current COBOL programmers as possible.  
Their institutional knowledge value is much higher than
the cost of their lack of skills in any new technologies.
As long as they attempt to learn the new stuff, we don't want
to lose them.

3) Move forward in limiting use of the mainframe and COBOL.
The cost of running an mainframe far exceeds the cost of any
alternative technologies, and the alternatives are far faster,
which means we can accomplish more.  Same with COBOL compared
to something like PERL.  

The problem is, it took years of discipline before "structured" 
COBOL took off, and the concept of "structured" PERL doesn't 
exist, so most PERL written by newbies is hideous.

The problem with COBOL within my company currently is that
it might take a month to do something in COBOL that would take
2 days in PERL.  So the COBOL is looking pretty bad.  But it
seems that people who coded in COBOL for years do not easily
move to PERL.  So I'd like some alternatives to research and pitch.

4) Move flat file data to a SQL based repository system.  DB/2 or
Oracle are most likely.  We have too many copies of the same data
in too many places.  Some layouts are only defined in the COBOL as 
opposed to copy books.  Different programs accessing the same
files have different layouts, due to them not agreeing on
the filler.  It is OK for the programs, but they can't be trusted
to document all the data.

Since you people know COBOL, what would you prefer to happen
in your company if this directive came down?  What language
would you prefer, and why?  What environment, tools, etc?

Note:  Please don't shoot the messenger.  I told them if they
wanted to get off the mainframe, go to a big Sun box, using 
UniKix, and keep the COBOL.  They didn't want to hear it.
Said it was getting harder and harder to find competent COBOL
programmers, and needed an alternative.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Migration Alternatives?

- **From:** Thane Hubbell <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-09T03:21:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IMAQ7.54673$xS6.88774@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<c948eb61.0112081716.3700dfde@posting.google.com>`

```
Two messages here - Kill the mainframe and get rid of COBOL too.  At first it
appears that the mainframe is the expense  - then it appears that it's lack of
COBOL talent.

Recomendation:  Determine the problem.  If it's unclear to management what the
problem is - then the solution will never be accepted.

I'm confused by these facts:

1.  The mainframe is too expenseive.
2.  Move everything to the Unix side of the mainframe.
3.  There's no COBOL programmers
4.  We have to get rid of the extra programmers if they can't learn the new
languages.

Why not keep COBOL - move to Unix anyway to save the cost of the mainframe (or
to NT) using one of the NT CICS products - and train the other programmers in
COBOL -  You save the expense of the mainframe, you have plenty of programming
talent, and you save the company.  If the goal is to just get rid of COBOL
because - I Can tell you this - the company is in danger.  I've seen this
attempted numerous times - with no success.  


In article <c948eb61.0112081716.3700dfde@posting.google.com>, broom says...
>
>Yea though I walk through the valley of death.
…[72 more quoted lines elided]…
>programmers, and needed an alternative.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Migration Alternatives?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-12-09T03:38:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C12DCF1.343FF8E7@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<c948eb61.0112081716.3700dfde@posting.google.com>`

```


broom wrote:

> Yea though I walk through the valley of death.
>

Yep ! And it could finish up with your head on the chopping block <G>

I would be reticent at this stage to comment in detail because I'm into
PC Desktop and OO COBOL - no savvy on mainframes or Unix etc.

One thing for sure your evaluation set up is DEAD WRONG. As you are a
newbie to COBOL it is an absolute MUST that you have one of those COBOL
guys as your LIEUTENANT. Go back to your boss and get him to think on
that one.

It might also help if you indicated the following :-

- Time scale for evaluation - must be completed by ?
- Time scale - transfer of applications to new machine/languages - is
somebody setting a target for this ?

Absolutely important that you are given a realistic time scale for
above, with some latitude.

So that we know where you are at, your own evaluation of yourself on the
following, i.e. are you (a) Beginner (b) Intermediate (c) Advanced,  for
:-

    - C
    - Perl
    - SQL - "geek" indicates 'Advanced' ?
    - OO
    - Sun hardware - "geek" indicates 'Advanced' ?

You'll appreciate your answers to above obviously affect your
evaluation.

You've indicated some 'nasties' about a lax attitude, (obviously a
build-up over the years) to COBOL file formats. Given that you stayed
with COBOL, two alternatives :(1) 'Spruce up' the file formats into an
installation standard and stay with flat files, (and this could be done
way before you make a switch), or, (2) still using COBOL, convert to DB
tables using ESQL - BUT - not an overnight job.

One small point, when referring to PERL you take a 'knock' at COBOL - OO
COBOL can be blistering fast ! (In case you are not aware there is IBM
mainframe OO, plus OO COBOL is also available from Fujitsu and Micro
Focus). And there are flavours available for Unix/Linux.

Incidentally one motivation for including COBOL in your solution is that
30 years development represents a HUGE amount of legacy code - if much
of that could be built into a new environment (?????)

Let's see what others have to say.

Jimmy, Calgary AB

>
> I am a clueless COBOL newbie.  I am a C and Perl programmer,
…[70 more quoted lines elided]…
> programmers, and needed an alternative.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Migration Alternatives?

- **Date:** 2001-12-09T09:02:04+00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<3C132846.6F1F901B@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<c948eb61.0112081716.3700dfde@posting.google.com>`

```
broom wrote:
> 
> Yea though I walk through the valley of death.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> could destroy the company.  I like that even less.

And there are a lot of examples of failed implementations:
http://www.actscorp.com/Re-boot_Hill/re-boot_hill.htm

> 
> The corporate MIS direction seems to be throw away COBOL,
> move from the MVS on the mainframe, and redeploy the world
> on the Linux partitition of the mainframe, and possibly
> move to Linux or Solaris elsewhere.

In my opinion, your company may not be able to survive its management.

> 
> Since I know many of what they consider the target
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> inventory tracking and specialized accounting code.

So, how much will it cost to completely re-build those applications
for another platform, in different languages, with acceptable service
levels?  How much time will it take?  And how do you keep the company
running while you're doing this complete rewrite?

> 
> The buzzwords of the moment are Linux on the Mainframe,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 1) Don't kill the company.

You may not be able to save it.  Take another look at Re-Boot Hill.

> 
> 2) Keep as many current COBOL programmers as possible.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> to something like PERL.

I think you will find that the statement above is false.  In many
cases the alternatives to the mainframe are more expensive by an order
of magnitude or more, and service levels are much lower.  I work in a
large data center, with large IBM mainframes and large Sun computers
(E10000).  The mainframe is the single most cost-effective asset, and
some systems are so large they cannot be replicated on Sun.

> 
> The problem is, it took years of discipline before "structured"
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> move to PERL.  So I'd like some alternatives to research and pitch.

If you can do it in PERL in two days, then do a pilot system.  If it's
that much better, what's holding you back?  Correct me if I am wrong,
but I thought PERL was normally an interpretive language.  Can you
really get acceptable runtime performance from PERL?

> 
> 4) Move flat file data to a SQL based repository system.  DB/2 or
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> programmers, and needed an alternative.

This final item does not make sense to me.  I know several COBOL
contractors who have been laid off since 9/11, and cannot find work. 
They say it's the worst they've even seen.  If you're located in a
reasonably large city, it should be easy to find COBOL programmers
right now, and probably at lower rates than a year ago.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Migration Alternatives?

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-12-10T02:41:54+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c136c56_9@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<c948eb61.0112081716.3700dfde@posting.google.com>`

```
Mainly out of admiration for your courage <G>, I offer the following
comments..

broom <broom@voicenet.com> wrote in message
news:c948eb61.0112081716.3700dfde@posting.google.com...
> Yea though I walk through the valley of death.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
At least you are taking the job seriously... These are considerations which
would not even occur to many people. I don't believe you are the "geek" you
say you are...<G>


> The corporate MIS direction seems to be throw away COBOL,
> move from the MVS on the mainframe, and redeploy the world
> on the Linux partitition of the mainframe, and possibly
> move to Linux or Solaris elsewhere.
>
A Management style which tends to follow fashion rather than evaluate
cost/benefits in an unbiased way, by the sound of it.

Many companies share the concern at the decline of COBOL programming
support, but it is not the bogey it is being made out to be. Despite the
general feeling, there are many places still teaching COBOL and elsewhere in
this thread it has been noted that there are still COBOL contractors
available. Still, if they have their hearts set on it, you are right to see
what the best path would be.

What follows is my opinion. There are a number of "right" answers to the
dilemma you describe. As this is free advice (and worth the price <G>) I'll
describe ONE of them...

They have to do SOMETHING with the existing code. If they are adamant that
they don't want to maintain it, then it must be converted to something they
feel more comfortable with.

PERL does not fit this category.

While the modern incarnation of Perl (Perl5?) has come a long way from its
roots as a "Practical Extraction and Report Tool", best suited to tricky
little code generation within the CGI or ASAPI on a Server, and despite its
OO support, to a COBOL programmer, it smacks of RPG <G>...

Besides, there is little correlation between Perl and COBOL so I am not
surprised that the COBOL Programmers have difficulty adapting to it. They
will have difficulty adapting to anything remotely Object Oriented (with the
possible exception of Java, see below) and that's why OO COBOL has not
enjoyed the user acceptance it deserves...yet.

Java on the other hand can be relatively easily generated from COBOL (I'm
sure LegacyJ systems will be in here shortly...making their pitch. I stress
I have no connection with them and have never used their product, but, based
on feedback from reliable sources, I WOULD use it if I was in the situation
you describe...) and it is easily fitted to the Web and IntraNet. It also
deploys nicely on the mainframe and the Client/Server systems.

No Management in their right mind would reject your suggestion to convert
the existing code to the Language of the Moment. (Especially when there are
very powerful tools available to do so...). The COBOL Programmers get to
keep their jobs (and the company gets to keep their application knowledge)
while they are learning Java, as they convert using the LegacyJ (or
whatever) tools.

Learning OO in a Java context is certainly the best way I have found (easier
than Smalltalk and MUCH easier than with C++) for dyed-in-the-wool COBOL
Programmers. (This is based on personal, first-hand experience; others will
have different stories. Nevertheless, the good COBOL programmers I know
personally, who have embraced OO, came by way of Java.)

So the "conversion to Java" strategy looks like a game where everybody wins,
and the Management can be reassured that all the Legacy code is being
leveraged into a universal, cross platform language, with in-house expertise
remaining on-site.

This means a huge exercise in deciding priorities for conversion and
planning, but you will have that with any strategy you decide on. (Apart
from the status quo, of course, but we have determined that that is not an
option for Management.)


> Since I know many of what they consider the target
> technologies, I've been put in a research and recommend
> position.
>
Well, they can't be all bad Management. At least you were selected on
credentials other than having married the Boss's daughter...<G>

> We have 30 years of flat file and VSAM COBOL code that has
> been twisted and tortured into place.  We use CICS.  Our
> actual data quantity is pretty small, ie: few hundred GB.
>
LegacyJ is strong on CICS conversion.

VSAM files should also be no problem, although you will obviously convert to
RDB as part of this exercise. Oracle or DB/2 (UDB in preference?) should be
fine and it will do the existing staff no harm to learn SQL while they are
at it...<G>

Personally, I'd do the Data conversion first, and THEN the code. I recently
consulted to a software house in Melbourne where we did such a conversion.
60 PC ISAM files to RDB. The COBOL team had no experience with SQL or RDB
but the data conversion went like a dream. (I wrote some tools to assist it
and one of these takes a COBOL FD and SELECT and generates a 3NF RDB,
removing the repeating groups to attached tables, maintaining referential
integrity, and implementing the same primary and alternate keys as were
defined in the original COBOL, without intervention by the user.) Having
built the RDB, I then wrote some templates to load it, and the local team
modified the templates as required and loaded the RDB directly from the ISAM
files. With defining and loading the new database almost fully automated,
data conversion was achieved in 5 working days. The real taskof modifying
the existing COBOL programs to access the new database has proceeded in the
background and I believe is now complete.

I mention the above, not just to publicise the fact that I have tools to do
this (although that doesn't hurt...<G>), but really to assure you that the
conversion can be simple.

> We've got about 100 users, customer service types, plus
> inventory tracking and specialized accounting code.
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
This shows wisdom far beyond a "geek"...<G>

> 3) Move forward in limiting use of the mainframe and COBOL.
> The cost of running an mainframe far exceeds the cost of any
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yes, that is probably true. But what about reliability, security, and sheer
dogged continuity? Mainframes excel in these areas...


> The problem is, it took years of discipline before "structured"
> COBOL took off, and the concept of "structured" PERL doesn't
> exist, so most PERL written by newbies is hideous.
>
Stop writing it. Move to Java. Sooner rather than later... I know you know
and love it, I know you can do nearly anything with it, but it has no
credibility against Java (any more than COBOL does...). We all have our
favourite Languages (mine is JavaScript - no relation to Java - at the
moment...<G>), but our personal biases cut no ice with Senior Management.
They believe what they read in the papers. What they read in the papers is
the ever onward and upward rise of Java...

> The problem with COBOL within my company currently is that
> it might take a month to do something in COBOL that would take
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I understand this problem and touched on it above. The future of Programming
belongs to OO. OO COBOL may be a little while coming, and many COBOL users
will (and have) resist(ed) it tooth and nail (that is one of the very
reasons that COBOL is dying...). Now your application COBOLlers have a
chance to get a foot in the door with OO through Java.

I believe that standard COBOL will be relegated to sequential Batch
Processing  (which it has always been unsurpassed at). If you keep the
existing programmers they will have dual expertise (Java and COBOL) and can
do the Batch stuff standing on their heads.

If I'm wrong, and by some miracle, mainframe OO COBOL becomes established in
say, 5 years, you will have some very valuable people schooled in both
camps. Either way, you (the Company) can't lose.

> 4) Move flat file data to a SQL based repository system.  DB/2 or
> Oracle are most likely.  We have too many copies of the same data
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
If you go for the Java conversion, the generated Java Classes will simplify
the maintenance because of their OO nature. Many of these COBOL definitions
can be consolidated into base classes and extended Classes. These would be
Java application Classes with the appropriate Methods for the data they are
attached to, and deployable across the Corporate Network.

As described above, conversion to RDB is desirable and inevitable.


> Since you people know COBOL, what would you prefer to happen
> in your company if this directive came down?  What language
> would you prefer, and why?  What environment, tools, etc?
>
OK, I've covered what I would do, and why, if I was in the situation you
find yourself in. Some of it will be unpalatable to some people here, but,
as always, I call 'em like I see 'em <G>. There are other equally viable
strategies, but the above would be my preferred one.

My backround is 35 years writing COBOL.

(Please visit: www.aboutlegacycoding.com  and check out the information
there from LegacyJ systems on PerCOBOL (you might enjoy the "Tales from the
Bit Bucket" while you're there...or have a look in the Archives for an
article in the April 2001 issue called "Meet the Future", I believe it has
some bearing on why you should go Java, and you should certainly get your
COBOL people to read it.)

> Note:  Please don't shoot the messenger.  I told them if they
> wanted to get off the mainframe, go to a big Sun box, using
> UniKix, and keep the COBOL.  They didn't want to hear it.
> Said it was getting harder and harder to find competent COBOL
> programmers, and needed an alternative.

It is hard to find something if you really don't want to find it...<G> This
argument alone is not a valid one for the move they are requiring. But the
argument that they REALLY want out and to "secure the future" is valid
(albeit, unpalatable for COBOL guys...)

In closing, I  think you  are exhibiting the right qualities for a good
Manager. (You have realised the value of existing staff and many Managers
NEVER do that <G>).  I have no doubt that whatever you go with, you will do
it right.

Good Luck!

Pete.
```


###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Migration Alternatives?

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-12-12T05:35:51+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c163b7b_11@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<c948eb61.0112081716.3700dfde@posting.google.com> <3c136c56_9@Usenet.com> <bF3R7.317470$dk.21054290@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```

JerryMouse <news@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:bF3R7.317470$dk.21054290@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com...
>
> "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
> news:3c136c56_9@Usenet.com...
>
> > Java on the other hand can be relatively easily generated from COBOL
(I'm
> > sure LegacyJ systems will be in here shortly...making their pitch. I
> stress
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > you describe...) and it is easily fitted to the Web and IntraNet. It
also
> > deploys nicely on the mainframe and the Client/Server systems.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Nope, I don't agree that.

Java may well evolve into something else and that something may be a
MicroSoft product, but even that is by no means certain.

Netscape Javascript is alive and well. I have been frequenting
comp.lang.javascript and am amazed at the volume of posts, the hugely wide
ranging areas of the language, and the vitalityof the kids who are writing
it. (in fact, I come here for a rest ...<G>). Seriously, the volume of
traffic on it is astounding.

I know from my own experience that Java is an excellent OO language and it
is reaching the point where the customer base is large enough (and
influential enough) to ensure the survival and growth of the language. It
has gone way beyond Browser wars and "ownership" of the Language. The User
base have taken control of it and it is thriving.

MS have already published JScript, their own implementation of JavaScript,
but it has had little impact on JavaScript.

Nobody I know uses VBScript (except Malaysian and Philipino Computer Science
students who want to write viruses after school...<G>). It must be very
disappointing for Bill Gates' Company to find that even the VB Programmers
are writing dynamic web pages in JavaScript, rather than VBScript as you
might expect. There are reasons for this which I won't go into here.

I believe the World can tolerate more than one King, and more than one
Kingdom.

Meantime, I am very happy to use Java Applets, and JavaScript for Client
side DHTML, and COBOL CGI or Java Servlets with SSI where practicable, for
the server side stuff.

It may all change with the advent of .NET. Time will tell...

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Migration Alternatives?

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <news@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-09T13:52:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<n0KQ7.185274$uB.22108712@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<c948eb61.0112081716.3700dfde@posting.google.com>`

```

"broom" <broom@voicenet.com> wrote in message
news:c948eb61.0112081716.3700dfde@posting.google.com...

>
> The corporate MIS direction seems to be throw away COBOL,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> position.

It is almost imperative you learn enough (about) COBOL to know where you're
coming from before you can adequately assess the path to your destination.

>
> We have 30 years of flat file and VSAM COBOL code that has
> been twisted and tortured into place.  We use CICS.  Our
> actual data quantity is pretty small, ie: few hundred GB.

"Twisted" code implies poor programmers (and/or poor supervision) - OR -
many urgent responses to problems or customer requests. Is your company one
where each day there is a fire to put out? It's possible to write really bad
code in COBOL, but it's far, far easier to write ghastly code in most other
languages. So, if you have many emergencies, the resulting code (as bad as
it might be) is more easily maintained in COBOL than in Perl or, God Forbid,
C++.

>
> We've got about 100 users, customer service types, plus
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> IBM Websphere, and Java.

Companies that chase the buzz words never catch them.

>
> What are my alternatives?
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> to lose them.

You can keep all your COBOL programmers if you stick with COBOL. Sounds like
you're committed to the 'new stuff.'

>
> 3) Move forward in limiting use of the mainframe and COBOL.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> to something like PERL.

You can also accomplish more by buying a faster computer. Really cheap
compared to a re-write. Suppose you get, say, two additional programmers to
do the re-write (assuming your current staff is fully occupied) and, after a
year, they've converted one of your systems such that it runs in half the
time. Further, say that one system currently accounts for 20% of your
processing. You net a 10% saving on your overall processing expense. How
long to recover the $150,000 you spent on those two programmers? Now suppose
you spend $150,000 for a faster computer and reduce your operating expense
for ALL systems by 50%. No brainer.


>
> The problem is, it took years of discipline before "structured"
> COBOL took off, and the concept of "structured" PERL doesn't
> exist, so most PERL written by newbies is hideous.

Virtually ALL Perl programmers are newbies. Try finding one with five years
experience.

>
> The problem with COBOL within my company currently is that
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> move to PERL.  So I'd like some alternatives to research and pitch.

And it might take five minutes in FORTRAN. What's wrong with having a full
tool-kit? Use the language best suited to the task at hand (Hint: crunching
massive databases is not something Perl does very well - nor FORTRAN for
that matter).

>
> 4) Move flat file data to a SQL based repository system.  DB/2 or
> Oracle are most likely.

DB2 and Oracle are hardly the latest-and-the-greatest, 'buzz words,' in your
company's vernacular. Apparantly money is not a problem?

>We have too many copies of the same data
> in too many places.  Some layouts are only defined in the COBOL as
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> to document all the data.

Ah ha! Poor standards, poor communication, poor management. If these
characteristics haven't changed, you're doomed. Data dictionaries,
documentation, talking to the programmer in the next cubicle are CHEAP; it
only takes interest on the programming supervisor's part and a smidgen of
discipline.

>
> Since you people know COBOL, what would you prefer to happen
> in your company if this directive came down?  What language
> would you prefer, and why?  What environment, tools, etc?

I have no idea.

>
> Note:  Please don't shoot the messenger.  I told them if they
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> programmers, and needed an alternative.

Is it possible your statement could be more accurately phrased: "Said it was
getting harder and harder to find competent COBOL programmers THAT WANTED TO
WORK HERE FOR WHAT WE WERE WILLING TO PAY, and needed an alternative
(emphasis added)." Teen-agers who geek-out on Perl, Visual Basic, HTML, 3-D
rendering, CGI, MP3, etc. work cheap and are plentiful.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Migration Alternatives?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-12-09T14:06:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ndKQ7.147$a65.183665@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<c948eb61.0112081716.3700dfde@posting.google.com>`

```
broom <broom@voicenet.com> wrote in message
news:c948eb61.0112081716.3700dfde@posting.google.com...
>
> I am a clueless COBOL newbie...
> The problem with COBOL within my company currently is that
> it might take a month to do something in COBOL that would take
> 2 days in PERL.  So the COBOL is looking pretty bad....

Here is your problem. There is no way in hell a competent COBOL programmer
should take eleven times as long (22 business days versus two business days)
as a competent PERL (or anything) programmer to do the same job. If the
'month' includes delays caused by other work which must be completed first,
that's a management problem.

COBOL does not look bad here; some of your COBOL programmers and/or
management does, however. That, or your time estimates are pure BS, in which
case no recommendation will address your problem, because you don't know
what your problem is.

With my (righteous?) indignation out of the way, why REPLACE your COBOL code
with anything? No reason your system cannot use "mixed source language"
applications. Use PERL or C or whatever where appropriate based on available
programming/institutional skills, and COBOL for other parts of the system.

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Migration Alternatives?

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-12-10T00:09:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C13FD71.1D96C9BF@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<c948eb61.0112081716.3700dfde@posting.google.com>`

```
First, I would recommend that if you go from COBOL, you find an
alternative that does decimal arithmetic.  In terms of the mainframe
being more expensive, are you on a way old box?  Research current costs
and be sure that you add up like costs for each of the selected
environments, the security system, the performance monitor, etc.  It
sounds like you have lousy COBOL discipline and need an application
overhaul anyway.   Whatever route you take, make sure you have end up
with documented applications that you can test.  Also while you can be
obscure in COBOL (I have to deal with that daily), I understand that it
can be worse in most of the alternatives.

Your company should be aware that most of us probably learned COBOL on
the job (in my case, I got training in one the predecessors, FACT from
Honeywell and learned COBOL by reading a Burroughs manual on the coach
section of the Super Chief during a 39.5 hour trip from LA to Chicago).
In a few years you may not be able to get C/C++ and Java programmers
because the vogue will have moved on to languages Y and Z.

The best way to replace COBOL probably is to get business packages where
your company DOES NOT have access to the source and you can only make
changes allowed by the package.  Of course the company will have to
change the way it does business to match the packages philosophy and
constraints so that may not fly.  

It took Dalhousie University 8 years to kill the mainframe.  There may
be more productive ways to spend your company's time and money.  You
bleed during conversions.  On the other hand, you may not have much
choice if yours is a relatively small shop because IBM's z series folks
have forgotten the original promise of the IBM 360 to handle everything
from the smallest to the largest.  If you don't need at least 60 MIPS of
capacity, IBM would rather sell x (Intel), i or p (AS400 or AIX, or vice
versa, I can't keep them straight).  
   
I know this was a ramble but I hope it gives you food for thought.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services, Inc. cfmtech@istar.ca
or 	                        clark.morris@ns.sympatico.ca
broom wrote:
> 
> Yea though I walk through the valley of death.
…[72 more quoted lines elided]…
> programmers, and needed an alternative.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Migration Alternatives?

- **From:** broom@voicenet.com (broom)
- **Date:** 2001-12-18T21:21:39-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c948eb61.0112182121.5378560b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<c948eb61.0112081716.3700dfde@posting.google.com> <3C13FD71.1D96C9BF@istar.ca>`

```
OK, a little more gasoline to the fire.

And NO, this is not a troll or my language is
better than yours war.

Further investigation leads me to UniKix, again.

I'd rather fight for a slowly rehosted migration,
than a traumatic rewrite.  I previously proposed
UniKix, but as an off-the-cuff reaction, as opposed
to a well researched alternative.  They shot me down,
but this might be worth fighting for.

UniKix is now owned by Sun, so there is a single 
support point, which is a plus.

It includes JCL->Script interpreters, an initiator
based job environment, a CICS emulation, and a buncha
goodies such as VSAM EBCDIC->ASCII conversion. 

They only support the use of MicroFocus, though, claiming
it is the only non-IBM compiler that is actually truly
compatible, including whatever libraries people on the
mainframe expect to see.  And they claim that depending
on the compiling options, it WILL generate truly native
code, which should be just as fast as the IBM compiler on
the MF. Does anyone have any experience to back this up, or
are they "mispeaking"?

Has anyone here ever gone through a true mainframe to UniKix
migration?


Thanks.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Migration Alternatives?

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@NOSPAM-hot.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-20T05:21:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LzeU7.42309$Zr.1605882@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<c948eb61.0112081716.3700dfde@posting.google.com> <3C13FD71.1D96C9BF@istar.ca> <c948eb61.0112182121.5378560b@posting.google.com>`

```
Let me ask, since UniKix is essentially a CICS environment, what are you
using JCL for? <grin>

Second, I don't see how it can compare in price or performance, or
capability with true CICS (i.e. Transaction Server) hosted on an comparable
open platform, including Sun. Under AIX for example, the COBOL compiler is a
mere $800, with no runtime and no additional license fees. And TX Server
integrates seamlessly with both DB/2 and WebSphere (as in exec-cics read
html-form end-exec type of stuff...)  You still get the little goodies like
ASCII <->EBCDIC
conversions and all thatstuff. Heck, the IBM compiler will let you compile
and operate in EBCDIC on an open systems platform, all by itself.

At one point, UniKix may have been competitive, but I sure don't see it any
longer.

-Paul

"broom" <broom@voicenet.com> wrote in message
news:c948eb61.0112182121.5378560b@posting.google.com...
> OK, a little more gasoline to the fire.
>
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Migration Alternatives?

- **From:** Eden Server Support <TechSupport@RosebudUSA.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-20T05:18:02-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C21BADA.45002167@RosebudUSA.com>`
