```
Hi,

I've been watching this thread and thought I'd throw in my 2 cents...

broom wrote:

> OK, a little more gasoline to the fire.
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> but this might be worth fighting for.

Yes, fight - but be careful...

> UniKix is now owned by Sun, so there is a single
> support point, which is a plus.

Yes, it is owned by Sun, which is unfortunate for many Unikix
users - rather soon after thier acquisition of Unikix they announced
no further support unless Unikix is runing on Sun hardware.

Ouch !   This has quite a few people looking for alternatives to
Unikix.


> It includes JCL->Script interpreters, an initiator
> based job environment, a CICS emulation, and a buncha
> goodies such as VSAM EBCDIC->ASCII conversion.

Yes, this is true, however look closely at their PPF batch facility.
PPF is supposedly a magic wand that you wave over your batch
jobs that let's some steps operate simulatneously.   The idea sure
sounds great, but the Sun literature says the whole process is very
complex & requires Sun consultants to institute.   Sounds to me
like it's not even software - just consulting.

Does anyone have experience with PPF ?


> They only support the use of MicroFocus, though, claiming
> it is the only non-IBM compiler that is actually truly
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> are they "mispeaking"?

Yes, MicroFocus *does* really do these things !  And more !
The code is native, compiles & links to exe/dll, is fully multi-threaded

and is compatible (calling to and from) most PC languages.

I can attest to all this first-hand.   My company, and I personally,
have
migrated clients from mainframes to Unikix, (old) MicroFocus MTS and
from on VSE to MVS and vice versa.   All the non-ainframe based
clients use MicroFocus NetExpress.

As for Unikix, well, (shameless plug...) my companys product, Eden
Server
( http://www.rosebudusa.com ) has all the features of Unikix NT,
including batch,
CICS, BMS, Job submission, report archiving and much more, including
lower
prices than Sun !

Check it out !

Ethan Schultz
Rosebud Management Systems, LLC

EthanS@RosebudUSA.com

>
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Migration Alternatives?

- **From:** broom@voicenet.com (broom)
- **Date:** 2001-12-09T17:06:19-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c948eb61.0112091706.7495f72f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<c948eb61.0112081716.3700dfde@posting.google.com>`

```
I'll attempt to reply to as many points as possible
in a single message, as opposed repeated responses.

We have a love/hate relationship with the mainframe.  Our
data quantity is too small to justify it for MIS work,
but more than enough for the services portion of our business.
But we are evenly split between using Unix and the mainframe
for our services portion, and it is a reality that we are moving
to Unix for a large portion of that.  Which leaves less and
less MIS utilization costing more and more as a percentage.

I am a critical reason for the large batch processing moving 
off, since I am one of the few Unix/SQL/Perl geeks, and I 
am a bit competitive.  So when I say 4 weeks to 2 days 
comparison, this is VALID.  For me. And for the majority
of COBOL people who code in my company.  I am talking large systems, 
with typical data quantity in the realm of 100GB per process,
multipled by 5 as steps are taken.  I purchase isolated boxes
for these tasks, which allow be to dedicate 10GB of memory and
10 CPUs to processes, but I go cost effective by only having
a single Sun 450 for main CPU and IO, and Intel Linux for
external CPU tasks.  

Due to the cost differential of the systems, I can dedicate
far more power to the task, which in turn drives my coding
style.  I have no problem loading up a hash of 1,000 MB worth
of data to churn throught it.  This would require my counterpart
on the MF to deal with a few MB in memory, and BILLIONS of 
competing disk accesses.

We are almost fully sequential batch style processing for every
thing we do on the MF.  We are 50/50, between batch and database
style on Unix (Oracle).

And I have compared sequential large file grind throughs, both
simple and complex data manipulation, etc.  Our mainframe is
about the same speed (maybe 10% FASTER) than a Sparc 450 for
regular COBOL VS Unix Microfocus COBOL, yet the Unix COBOL is
still interpreted, which costs it.  If I moved that same program
to an Intel or AMD system, it gets 4 times faster.  But my IO
isn't nearly as good on those systems, so I juggle, which adds
human overhead I want to avoid.  When we compare pure binary
programs, such as sort, my 3 CPU Sun 450 is 13 times faster than
the mainframe.

Now let me argue the both sides (if possible).  

YOU: Every single one of your tests were run in "isolation", they can't 
compare with the many things running at the same time on the 
mainframe.  

ME: Yes they can.  If I had 1/4 the budget the mainframe 
had, I'd run these tasks 5 times faster simultaneously.  I measure 
IO, CPU, network response, and watch the scale.  I know when it 
tops, and where I have breathing room.  But:  Give me one crappy 
coder who is given wide range on his coding style, and I'm screwed.  
The mainframe has far better control over the poor programmers, and 
can allocate resources at a much finer level.  Sun claims to have a 
workload manager but since I have no experience, I can't pitch it 
until I do.  The best I can hope for is management to toss out the
crappy coders, and that never happens because the worse coder you 
are, the better your personality seems to be, at least to non-coders.
Our current mainframe IO is horrible, and IBM blames EMC while EMC
blames IBM.  Either way, a 3 CPU Sun 450 is about even in CPU tasks
to our 3 CPU mainframe, while the Sun IO is about 10 time faster.

YOU: Your mainframe is OLD!  How can you compare?  You are such an
asshole for setting up a straw man and blowing him away.  Get 
a new goddamn mainframe and everything will be better.

ME: Might.  Won't matter.  Even if we were willing to, our software expense 
will kill us.  The ONLY reason we would upgrade the CPUs would be to 
give more power to Linux partition where the new CPU would not trigger 
a cost increase on the other side.  But all my tests so far show REALLY
bad performance, so I'm not looking forward to it.

YOU: The mainframe is SO much more reliable than any other technology, it 
only takes a few days of failure to see how stupid any type of movement
off it would be.  You are an idiot for even thinking it could work.

ME: So far my experience has been mixed.  I've got boxes with uptimes
of YEARS, and boxes that bounced like a ping pong ball for a while. I 
don't trust ANYTHING.  Even the mainframe.  They rebooted it yesterday, but
didn't tell me why.  I'd cluster/mirror anything that needed to be 24*7.
I'd prefer 2 6800s to 1 15K.  My disk is all external RAID 5, multi-pathed,
etc.  But EMC is pitching me, and I LIKE hardware snapshots over my current
Veritas.  Either way, I'm sure I can reasonbly guarantee 99.999% uptime
compared to current MF, simply by mirroring EVERYTHING, for 1/2 the of cost 
of the mainframe.  But it would be complex initially, and a full time job 
to set it up and maintain it.  But we already have MF system programmers,
and they seem to do little more than act as disk resource managers.

YOU: How DARE you argue for you prefered language here!  This is a COBOL
group, dammit.  You are here just to start a flame war for you own 
entertainment.  You will burn in hell.  Matta fact, you are just like
the NAZ* @%#$^#$^.  I almost triggered a Godwin, there, ehh?

ME: Actually, NO, I am not arguing.  Perl for MIS systems is probably
insane.  Perl for isolated service jobs is great.  But service jobs
are typically single client focused, and can be highly complex for
that task, but will not trigger the downfall of the entire company
if there are hidden bugs.  Hidden bugs in MIS systems can get you 
arrested and thrown in jail for financial manipulation.  So that 
is why I ask YOU, the COBOL programmers, what my alternatives are.
I am not qualified to list them.  So far the highest marks go to Java.
I've done a SMALL (about 1 month, solid, but only the last week 
was truly productive) bit of coding in Java, and liked it, but 
that was for an interactive network utility, not a huge MIS sysem.
And I have 'C' background, so I was pre-disposed to liking Java.

YOU: If you have crappy COBOL now, you will crappy "next language" in the
future.  Your company spent too much time fighting fires, and not
enough time designing/coding, so nothing you do can help unless you
fix your corporate culture, and we KNOW that will never happen, so
you might as well quit now.

ME: Well, previously our compay was a mix of companies with competing 
interests.  We seem a bit consolidated now.  I don't know if it has
changed enough, but it is something to keep in mind.  We are aware
of the need to centralize the data into a SQL based repository, which
is a good 1st step in the process.

YOU: You just want to kill off any high cost high quality programmers.
Go ahead, hire some VB/Perl/Python/Script kiddie.  You'll come BEGGING
to us in a few years when the project tanks and you need an emergency
rescue of your system.  And if you are lucky, we will only double our rates.

ME: You might be right.  But  doubt it.  If we actually do go forward,
any initial new hires will be people with experience in both COBOL
and whatever new language is chosen, which won't be cheap.  And new 
COBOL programmers got into it for Y2K consulting dollars.  They usually
SUCK, because they have such a limited scope of experience, and they
expect BIG $$, for almost no productivity.  So you gurus rightly charge 
well, because there is a limited pool of you, and the work you do is so 
important.  But it is a downward spiral because very few new systems are 
written in it.  We do pay experienced programmers far more than new
ones, and we'd prefer that, because we KNOW the experienced are far more
productive.  But there is a limit.  And when that hits, they start
looking for alternatives.

YOU: If you think your language is so hot, whip up a pilot project and try
it out. (snicker, snicker).

ME: Did my own with certain types of things with Perl, and DID move many 
things.  But those were easily identifiable isolated projects.  And my
success rate in Perl hires is 50/50, with MANY wannabes being thrown
out of the interview.  So while I can vouch for my own abilities, and 4 others
in my company, I would never propose a large complex multicoder, multiyear 
project based on the assumption of finding mature, experienced Perl coders.

YOU:  There is no such thing as a Perl/Java/other coder with any experience.
They are all internet/CGI/script kiddies, with no real experience, no ROI,
and no business sense.  Give it up.

ME:  Actually, we fondly refer to them as ".COM Idiots."  Right now we have
5 people who I consider good Perl coders, with varying experience, minimum 
of 5 years Perl, preferably with some other such as 'C', SQL, etc.  It takes
me about 2 years to beat in large file manipulation before they are ready
for the real world of data warehousing.  

YOU: And where can you find someone with 5 years Perl?  

ME: Sitting at this keyboard. 9 years actually, with 6 years of 'C' before 
that.  And I still try to write new code, with newly learned techniques,
whenever appropriate.  Other than that, head hunters usually.

Whew.  I hope I covered the majority of the issues.

Thanks for reading this far.

Barry
```

```
files on the Unix box and keep the COBOL and likely be FASTER.
```

```
Alternatively you could keep your database and use Micro Focus  COBOL. Yes
flat files are usually faster but there are other things to consider when
migrating, such as the architecture of the application.

See www.microfocus.com and www.cobolportal.com
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Migration Alternatives?

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompsonNOSPAM@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-10T04:11:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C1435C6.544625C@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<c948eb61.0112081716.3700dfde@posting.google.com>`

```
Broom, 

I've already read your response to the others at this point.
So don't think your answers apply to me. In fact, I'd almost
say you were a troll, except that what you've mentioned is
something that I've seen rather recently at a certain former
client (LARGE electric utility). They made this big jump off
the mainframe for some of the reasons you've given. But then
they realized that their declaring the conversion being a
success didn't make it so. Particularly reports that they
used to run on demand, now have to be scheduled a week in
advance...

My responses are embedded:

broom wrote:
<snip>
> 
> The corporate MIS direction seems to be throw away COBOL,
> move from the MVS on the mainframe, and redeploy the world
> on the Linux partitition of the mainframe, and possibly
> move to Linux or Solaris elsewhere.
<snip>
What is the age of the management making this decision, and
what is their actual experience with computer systems in
general? Has there been some turn-over in the IT/MIS
department of late?
<snip>
> 
> Since I know many of what they consider the target
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> What are my alternatives?
<snip>
Let me start off with my opening salvo (some of which is
based on data not in your original post):

First, has anyone done anything to actually tune MVS? IBM's
DFSMS and WLM (Data Facilities/System Managed Storage & Work
Load Manager) hardly ever match against someone that
actually knows the data and fixes the JCL streams correctly.
Also, VSAM can be a real b***h to tune. Then there is the
CICS tuning issue(s). Let us not forget VTAM and needing to
tune it.

Sometimes you have to get a real senior systems programmer,
not one that has been around 5 years and only knows SMP/E.
So the question is, has there been any serious capacity
planning and tuning done with this mainframe?

I can't tell you how many managers do not know anything
about a mainframe, and listen to borkers about the skill
sets of the sysprogs they are pushing (all the right
keywords on their resume, but no actual application of
them!).

Then you have IBM and EMC doing the finger pointing routine,
and you really need a PSR type (one of the old guys who
knows hardware and O/S internals) to get them to knock it
off.

I suspect that IBM wants the company to buy an ESS Shark.
So, I'd look out for some serious politics here. I've seen
companies bring in a serious consultant that knows hardware
and internals of IBM's MVS to figure out what is going on.
Then give the actual causing party the bill for the third
party (and it got paid!). However, see one of my later
comments -- the client company caught in the middle better
have real clean hands.
<snip>
> My goals are:
> 
> 1) Don't kill the company.
<snip>
Next salvo. 

Many years ago, the big panacea was to migrate from some
flavor of DOS (mainframe) to MVS. Many of those conversions
ended up with the MIS director (the one that made the
decision to go for it) being fired. The MIS director that
inherited the mess either quit or was let go and the third
(sometimes it was the Fifth(!)) director was the one where
the conversion completed (designated completed because the
DOS system was decommissioned and DUO/360 was being used!).

There was another panacea and that was to convert to an IBM
mainframe system.

For some companies, this decision very nearly put them out
of business.

A group of people (various actually) got together and
figured out how to do these things in an automated fashion.
Each of these groups learned a few things after getting
burned once or twice:

A) Software Platform to a different Software Platform is
risky business (e.g., DOS to MVS)
B) Changing programming languages during the conversion is
asking for disaster (e.g., RPG to COBOL or COBOL to RPG)
C) Hardware architecture change to another Hardware
architecture is a serious business issue (big-endian,
little-endian, incompatible data formats, data xfer speed
differences, etc.) -- (e.g., UNIVAC to IBM)
D) Changing I/O methods is asking for disaster (e.g., ISAM
to IDMS)
E) Temp-work miscalculations result in ordering more DASD or
having excess -- Not a shop stopper (yes, shop not show) --
(e.g., DOS to MVS, HONEYWELL to IBM).

Now, what you have really been asked to do is consider doing
points A-E with one or two more twists thrown in (e.g., CICS
to ???).

What are the odds that things will go wrong? What disaster
planning, for recovery purposes, are you going to do?

Your goal is a very serious one. If I were you, I'd be
laying out some very critical plans and looking for the $$$
exposure this conversion will have. I'd be looking at every
way possible to make this happen in an as automated fashion
as is possible (and have the back out plan ready to run at
every migration point).
<snip>
> 2) Keep as many current COBOL programmers as possible.
> Their institutional knowledge value is much higher than
> the cost of their lack of skills in any new technologies.
> As long as they attempt to learn the new stuff, we don't want
> to lose them.
<snip>
It sounds like you might want to make these COBOL
programmers into analysts and have others do the programming
until the COBOL programmers can come up to speed (or, the
company decides that there is a newer sexier language to
use).

Broom, this is an old problem. When 4GLs came out, COBOL was
announced as dead. When IBM started working on AI languages
such as Prolog and TIRS along with Repository Manager, we
were told that COBOL was dead. Then the big rush to C & C++
came and we were told that COBOL was going to die.

In the background I hear some music... "Where have all the
young girls gone, long time passing, where have all the
young girls gone..." Wait, I hear another oldie, "The drums
keep-a pounding rhythm to the brain....and the beat goes on,
and the beat goes on..."
<snip> 
> 3) Move forward in limiting use of the mainframe and COBOL.
> The cost of running an mainframe far exceeds the cost of any
> alternative technologies, and the alternatives are far faster,
> which means we can accomplish more.  Same with COBOL compared
> to something like PERL.
<snip>
If there are many studies that show that the total cost of
operating a mainframe are less than the costs of other
environments, then I'd start wondering why management is
making these noises. I'd be very tempted to make some body
prove this.
<snip> 
> The problem is, it took years of discipline before "structured"
> COBOL took off, and the concept of "structured" PERL doesn't
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> move to PERL.  So I'd like some alternatives to research and pitch.
<snip>
I'd start asking myself some questions with your point 3.

A) You mentioned an IPL (in another post) but no one told
you why. I'd find out. Did someone manage a deadly embrace?
Did someone do something foolish that caused the console
buffers to fill up and MVS only had one choice -- croak?

>>> What does an IPL or a reboot of any machine actually cost? How many people are idled for how much time when this happens? What data has to be restored? What manual operations are actually required in each case and how long does it take, who has to do it, and what does it actually cost when any system crashes for any reason? <<<

B)Structured here seems like a straw-man. Why even mention
it? 

C) Why does it take the COBOL programmer a month to do
something that takes 2 days in PERL? What is it that they
are having to do that you don't have to do? Once you know
the answer to this productivity issue, you now have the
ability to deal with the real issue.
 
> 4) Move flat file data to a SQL based repository system.  DB/2 or
> Oracle are most likely.  We have too many copies of the same data
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> to document all the data.
<snip>
You have just hit on one of the reasons for an automated
migration/conversion. The tools for this are supposed to
detect these issues and fix 'em with a standard canned
answer.

What you have on your hands is, a requirement to clean up
all the data. And you can't migrate this system until the
data is clean. If you try, the PERL programs you write will
have problems.

And that [COBOL to PERL] brings up another problem. One
can't do a line for line migration. It takes too bloody
long, it is too incredibly expensive! You have to have some
one who knows COBOL and PERL (preferably about 20 people
like this). And they would have to convert those programs by
hand. Converting a COBOL program to RPG would take me about
a week to do (and I've worked on RPG compilers!). 

Broom, can you write a PERL (or any other language) program
that will parse/scan COBOL and produce PERL? Will it do it
at better than a 95% rate? Because if it doesn't, then every
program must be verified by hand. [My experience doing COBOL
to COBOL, Honeywell to IBM and DOS to MVS migrations].
> 
> Since you people know COBOL, what would you prefer to happen
> in your company if this directive came down?  What language
> would you prefer, and why?  What environment, tools, etc?
<snip>
I'd start asking management to provide me with the financial
impact analysis that has been done, and I'd explain why I
want it: I need to see what the costs are that I've got to
beat. I've got to see if I can make this happen in a time
frame that will result in a good ROI (return on investment).
I've got to figure out if I can reduce the TCO (total cost
of ownership).

I'd also ask them why the mainframe is not able to keep up
with my SUN since the mainframe is MEMORY centric (I'm
assuming that the SUN is bus centric like a PC). 
<snip>
> Note:  Please don't shoot the messenger.  I told them if they
> wanted to get off the mainframe, go to a big Sun box, using
> UniKix, and keep the COBOL.  They didn't want to hear it.
> Said it was getting harder and harder to find competent COBOL
> programmers, and needed an alternative.
<snip>
I'd ask management what a COBOL programmer makes per year v.
what a PERL programmer makes (I think you get the point of
this question).

I'd ask management where they are checking for COBOL
programmers. Ask them if they have tried running an ad for a
COBOL programmer on this news group or any other.

And just for fun, go to your HR department (or the prefered
headhunter) and ask them to tell you how they would make up
an AD or req with the following information:

n COBOL programmers needed
Must have 5+ years experience with COBOL, CICS & VSAM
Our environment:
OS/390 V2R10
CICS/TS
DFSMS
JCL

And then when you get back:

COBOL programmer applications being accepted for programmers
with the followinq qualifications:
Must have 5+ years experience in the following:
COBOL
CICS/TS
DFSMS
OS/390 V2R10
VSAM
JCL
And must have a 4yr degree.
Salary $30-45K/yr (or some such).

You will start to understand your problem. You see, OS/390
V2R10 hasn't been around for 5 years. No COBOL programmer
has DFSMS experience, that is a system level application
that makes JCL coding easier (and tuning for mindless
systems programmers that only know SMP/E). I'm seeing this
all the time, and this, I'm afraid, is where much of the
problem lies.

Lastly, the Linux on the mainframe is an interesting thing
(I've installed it on two so far). Will they get more bang
for their buck from the mainframe by going to it? As long as
their workload is not floating point based (intensive), they
very well might. But Linux for the mainframe is not a mature
system yet. At this point Linux can't handle more than 4
CPUs before it has problems dispatching (seems to have quite
a bit of internal MP overhead). OS/390 or z/OS seems to have
this problem around 14 CPUs, and z/VM seems to be able to
handle 24 from what I recall. And how good is the kernel
with I/O at this point? I don't know, and I don't think IBM
does either.

Back to Linux -- how many users can telnet into the S/390
based Linux and run the Xwindows environment from there? I
really don't know, but I'm sure it can easily handle 100
even with a 20 MIP machine (a Multiprise 3000-H30 is 60 MIPs
and is the smallest of the Multiprise machines today). Can
you seamlessly migrate the data and applications to Linux
and keep the GUI the users see the same?

Just how much money does this company want to spend on this
conversion/migration?

I hope you don't feel shot. But there are many questions
that need to be addressed. And I think you can see that some
of them are quite political.

If I knew the company, and knew what they do, etc., etc., I
could tell you what language I'd want to use and the
platform I'd want to use it on. But I don't have all that
information, so I honestly can't answer you in this area.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Migration Alternatives?

- **From:** praul@isot.com (Paul Raulerson)
- **Date:** 2001-12-10T10:57:49-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40f8f32c.0112101057.798401d6@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<c948eb61.0112081716.3700dfde@posting.google.com>`

```
Well, I sympathize. Were it I in your position, I would explore three
possibilities:

(1) How much of the back end data processing can I separate from the
web based display processing? This is the critical key, in my view.
All that 30 years of back end dataprocessing is near impossible to
immediately replace, and if your managmeent demands that you do so,
attempt to get them to buy off on an evolotionary process, rather than
a revolutionary process.

(2) Use JSP's exclusively on the web designs - avoid Applets like the
plauge.
This sounds slightly counter intuitive, but in truth, allowing Applets
into the picture introduces many compatibility issues, slows doesn the
displays, and will get your inexperienced Java programmmers tied up in
knows worrying about things like producing sorted columns. *Do that on
the mainframe, where you already understand the technology. There is
plenty of time to move to other technologies after the dang thing is
working!* You will have to have your systems guys figure a way to have
the Java call your backend data manipulation processes . If you have
DB/2 on site, this is trivial, but VSAM is a bit more of a nusiance.

(3) As an alternative to #2 above, use the CICS interfaces to allow
you to call the backend processing directly from the Web Pages in
COBOL. This is slightly more complex, and has less to do with
WebSphere and Java than you might like though, since the majority of
development resides on the Mainframe. It is also more difficult to
separate out display and processing logic.

And a couple other little gotcha's to watch out for - 
  Stay away from VisualAge Generator - it looks nice, but unless you
are willing to encompass and learn SmallTalk, you ain't gonna be happy
with it.

  Do look at VisualAge Java and Visual Age COBOL. Both can be used to
pretty easily interface into the backend data systems.

  If you can sell them on it at all, go with an intial "web"
implementation using CGI, rather than a full blown WebSphere system.
It isn't as scalable, but the technology is much easier to implement
and understand for managmeent types,
and with only a 100 users, you are quite unlikely to ever exceed the
CGI capabilities and need that kind of scalability.

  If someone suggests duplicating the database on an "open systems"
platform, just kill them before they have a chance to spread that
contaigion around... Much easier to do jail time than suffer through
that kind of agony.

  It doens't matter what technology you choose to implement, get
*something* working and grow from there. Even if you have to throw it
away a half dozen times before you are happy with it and managment
buys off on it, get *something* working as quickly as possible.
Believe me, the whole area is so complex that you are going to spend
years getting a good solid handle on it. And the *only* way to get any
kind of a handle on that slippery stuff is to get something working.
Call in favors, do whatever it takes, but a working example using 'out
of date' technology like CGI processing will sell every time over
pie-in-the-sky superslow incorrect processing using the hippest and
most up to date technology available.

Best 
-Paul



broom@voicenet.com (broom) wrote in message news:<c948eb61.0112081716.3700dfde@posting.google.com>...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Migration Alternatives?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@NOSPAM-hot.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-12T15:55:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<y5LR7.38589$zp5.2345829@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<c948eb61.0112081716.3700dfde@posting.google.com> <c948eb61.0112101946.30c7d54a@posting.google.com> <P4BR7.52255$f75.2437460@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <9v7nib$uas$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com>`

```
Not really, at least, that has not been my experience at all.

Mainframe DB/2 is usually well managed and highly optimized. Stored
procedures
on the mainframe are very likely to be COBOL programs, and for the most
part,
the people managing the thing don't do much else besides manage the
database.

Oracle installations on open systems are often - well - less well managed.
The people
writing the applications do not go through code reviews or any kind of
database
approval process, and often you find nothing more than flat files copied
into
tables. You even find people that resist or totally fail to understand why
PL/SQL
programs should be packaged. (*sigh*)

Success is much more likely if the same people (DBAs, Programmers, etc.) are
doing the port,
but usually companies hire people in off the street or worse -
'consultants'. These guys have
zero comprehension of the business practices and issues that need to be
satisfied by the
open systems programs, and the end result is nothing less than a
catastrophic mess. (Usually -
there are exceptions to every rule.)

This is especially true of wet behind the ears 'Web Application Designers'
who are really good at
making little people dance around on the screen, but have no idea of what
say, relational integrity is.
They often propose "replicating" the database on the mainframe to our copy
of Oracle
'once or twice a day.'  I have *dozens* of instances of this type of thing
recorded.

It is a disease I tell you! :)

Going the other way is not much better sometimes either - the kids that have
had some success
with a little web application fail to comprehend that they cannot just
change this or that table structure.
They get tunnel vision on their application, because it is a LOT of work to
dig in and understand
why something is the way it is - perhaps it requires documenting or at least
understanding dozens of
other processes. Mainframers tend to do this as part of their job, it isn't
quite so easy to put an application
on a machine of it's own in that world. ;)

Don't even get me started on distributed database management in the open
systems world. (grrr...)

But a free piece of advice, avoid Oracle in any mixed database situation; if
you have DB/2 on the mainframe,
put DB/2 on your open systems as well. Besides being cheaper, you stand a
much greater chance of success.

-Paul
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Migration Alternatives?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-12-18T13:28:25+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c1e8e27_5@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<c948eb61.0112081716.3700dfde@posting.google.com> <c948eb61.0112101946.30c7d54a@posting.google.com> <P4BR7.52255$f75.2437460@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <9v7nib$uas$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com> <3c187d08$1_4@Usenet.com> <g55T7.81320$f75.3637679@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```

Paul Raulerson <praulerson@NOSPAM-hot.rr.com> wrote in message
news:g55T7.81320$f75.3637679@typhoon.austin.rr.com...
> Well, more power to you. I bet however, that a good DBA would not agree
with
> you on that.
> Especially if you are talking about a 24X7 production environment. Unless
> you are talking about
> some mind of PC database with light use, such as Access.
>
It's a hypothetical argument, Paul. I was a DBA for many years (hopefully, a
good one <G>) on both mainframe and PC systems. I have administered
Hierarchic, Network, and Relational Databases, and even designed and built
VSAM databases (using both KSDS and RRDS) for major international
corporates, BEFORE DB2 and the Relational Model were developed.

There is no reason why ANY database should not be reliable 24x7, whether it
is ACCESS on a PC or UDB on a mainframe. Your "light use" is irrelevant to
the argument of converting flat files to RDB.

I use ACCESS as a tool for conversion because it is ubiquitous and it
implements the Relational model very well. Once the system has been
converted (and the functionality checked out) it can be moved to ANY
platform (mainframe or PC) which implements the Relational model. Volume
testing and consequent tuning can then be carried out.

I am NOT advocating that people should design Relational Databases based on
Flat files (I'm including randomly accessible flat files in this), but I am
saying there is no need for conversion of existing data into an RDB to be a
"disaster".

Most DBAs would object to "converting" flat files to RDB. You are quite
correct about that. I also would prefer NOT to do this. But in the real
world we don't always (actually, we don't ever...) have the luxury of being
able to say: "Let's go back to square one and take our existing data (which
has grown like Topsy over many years) and redesign the whole lot for
RDB...". Given that we MUST bite the bullet, what I am saying is that it
doesn't HAVE to be bad. I've done a number of conversions on various
platforms (mainframe and Client/Server) and learned a thing or two...

In fact I have developed tools which automate most of this task and do it
very well... the result is a Relational Database in Third Normal Form,
Repeating groups removed to associated relations, referential integrity
implemented, and the same indexes as were available in the original system
propagated to the new RDB.(So you can use the same access pathways as
always, but now they can be easily optimized with standard RDB utilities.)

This is all achievable by automated analysis of COBOL source FDs and
SELECTs. Doing it in this way eliminates the possibility of human error.

While the new database will probably never perform as well as it would have
if it had been designed from scratch, it can allow the company to move away
from being tied to the COBOL file system and provide an architecture that is
"open" and accessible by many standard tools and software. It is also
scalable and can be used to build better new systems.

It sounds to me as though you have had one or more bad experiences when
attempting this exercise.

All I'm saying is "Don't learn the wrong lesson." It doesn't HAVE to be a
disaster.

In fact, it CAN be a major success story.

Pete.

                http://www.usenet.com
```

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@NOSPAM-hot.rr.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<c948eb61.0112081716.3700dfde@posting.google.com> <c948eb61.0112101946.30c7d54a@posting.google.com> <P4BR7.52255$f75.2437460@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <9v7nib$uas$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com> <3c187d08$1_4@Usenet.com> <g55T7.81320$f75.3637679@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3c1e8e27_5@Usenet.com>`

```
Ah - there is difference - you *are* a DBA. :)

Now if your average programmers, or web designers, or Java-only programmers
had done that
conversion... <grin>

Actually, in a lot of cases, the move to a RDBMS is the opportunity to
redesign data and implement
some structure. It is also the first time most COBOL programmers get
introducted to normalized data.
I take some small measure of delight in watching that event happen. They
always come around sooner
or later, but it is fun watching them stuggle.

And yes, you are right, it doesn't have to be a disaster. I have products
that are file based that run
very well, and have for years. They are also ported to both DB/2 and Oracle
on various platforms.
If I hace to code in anything but COBOL or assembler (or perhaps, PL/1 and
RPG), I'll take a database
everytime.

-Paul
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Migration Alternatives?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-12-19T02:20:40+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c1fbedf_7@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<c948eb61.0112081716.3700dfde@posting.google.com> <c948eb61.0112101946.30c7d54a@posting.google.com> <P4BR7.52255$f75.2437460@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <9v7nib$uas$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com> <3c187d08$1_4@Usenet.com> <g55T7.81320$f75.3637679@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <3c1e8e27_5@Usenet.com> <2_xT7.36361$Zr.1255096@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
Paul Raulerson <praulerson@NOSPAM-hot.rr.com> wrote in message
news:2_xT7.36361$Zr.1255096@typhoon.austin.rr.com...
> Ah - there is difference - you *are* a DBA. :)
>
Well, no. Not nowadays, but it is a hat I have worn many times in the past
so it is fair to say I have that knowledge.

> Now if your average programmers, or web designers, or Java-only
programmers
> had done that
> conversion... <grin>
>
The Java Database model stresses the use of multi-tier architecture,
normalization, and exclusive use of the relational model. Java programmers
are likely to be much better trained in this regard than their COBOL
counterparts who were raised on VSAM/ISAM.

But you are correct insofar as I don't know of any Java shops who have
attempted this conversion, so it is mere speculation whether they would make
a better or worse job of it than anybody else.

> Actually, in a lot of cases, the move to a RDBMS is the opportunity to
> redesign data and implement
> some structure.

Absolutely. Endorse this 100%.

>It is also the first time most COBOL programmers get
> introducted to normalized data.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Ah, I see you have a mean streak...<G>

> And yes, you are right, it doesn't have to be a disaster.

OK, that was the main point in your post that I took issue with. As you have
now withdrawn your remark, we can drop this and be friends <G>...


> I have products
> that are file based that run
> very well, and have for years. They are also ported to both DB/2 and
Oracle
> on various platforms.

Excellent! I concur with the approach of using what works...

> If I hace to code in anything but COBOL or assembler (or perhaps, PL/1 and
> RPG), I'll take a database
> everytime.
>

Now that one bothers me a little because I would (and do) use a database
even when I AM coding in COBOL. In fact, I find using the database purely as
a repository and controlling it through ESQL from COBOL (usually via ODBC)
is an extremely flexible and efficient way to build systems. It opens up all
the advantages I mentioned in my previous post and means that a COBOL
program doesn't have to be written every time you need to enquire or report
on it. (Just run any of the many tools which allow on-line SQL...Crystal
Reports, SPUFI or whatever, for ad hoc reporting/inquiry).

We've debated this one in this forum before and I know there is a huge base
of COBOLlers who can't see past VSAM/ISAM. I have no problem with that (I
strongly advocate the right of programmers to use whatever they believe to
be best, as long as they can support their choice with a reasoned argument).
In fact I once felt the same way... but you grow <G>.

My personal opinion is that the COBOL file system has served us well for
over 30 years, but it is time to drop it... (I'm not advocating it should be
removed from the language; that would be stupid. I'm simply saying it should
NOT be the repository of first choice when we come to design new systems.)

Anchoring COBOL in this file system isolates COBOL from the other tools
which exist in the market place, prevents COBOL systems from exchanging and
sharing data with systems developed in other Languages, and consequently,
makes Management suspicious that if they go COBOL they will be faced with a
major data conversion within a few years. All of these perceptions are
contributing directly to the decline of COBOL.

Fortunately, the converse is also true. Showing that "standard", "open" data
can be manipulated by the Language best suited to manipulating data (COBOL),
that other tools and systems can interact with the database directly (even
concurrently, if required), that ad hoc reports/queries can be called off in
minutes, then there is much less anxiety on the part of Management.

Whatever their perception of COBOL, at least they know their data won't be
locked in to a system that REQUIRES a language over which there is a
question mark, in order to be accessed. If the COBOL system does the job
well, they can be confident in using it for more projects, secure in the
knowledge that there is no risk to the Company's data resource.

Personally, I'd like to see COBOL programmers being trained in RDB design as
part of their course, and the file system only mentioned in passing, with
maybe one or two exercises to access an indexed file randomly and
sequentially (dynamically) just so they know how to maintain the old stuff
<G>.

Before we are deluged with posts screaming about the efficiency of the file
system and the horrific inefficiency of databases let me just say: "Give it
up."

Modern, properly designed RDBs, implemented and administered by DBAs who
know what they're doing, will not suffer in performance comparisons with
equivalent flat file systems. Not even on huge volumes. If you find
differently, the RDB needs attention. Simple as that.

The only possible exception to this would be large volume sequential access
on primary key, where the COBOL files can employ a huge blocking factor. The
RDB must necessarily compromise its "blocking" (paging, actually) to support
both random and sequential access.

I don't intend to debate all this again. Use whatever you like, but realise
that continually isolating COBOL is not helping its survival.

Pete.

                http://www.usenet.com
```

```
quite well with C structures
or Pascal Records or just about any language.

formats in, or that I want to minimize
data transfer for. I also prefer files for small environments where putting
in a database is sometimes more
trouble than it is worth, or where I want to reinitialize the database on a
periodic basis.

in

Now I tend to disagree with that in part, mostly because I am NOT an
advocate of
ad-hoc reports. Reports need to *tell* you something, and often, ad-hoc
reporting
is *not* telling the user what they think it is. There is no substitute for

analysis of the data and a well thought out report. There is most especially
no ad-hoc
reporting solution - doubly so in data warehousing. ;) At least, no IMNSHO.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Migration Alternatives?

- **From:** Charles <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-19T15:06:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e22U7.4094$XC5.4240@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<c948eb61.0112081716.3700dfde@posting.google.com>`

```
On 8 Dec 2001 17:16:30 -0800, in article
<c948eb61.0112081716.3700dfde@posting.google.com>, broom stated 
>
>Yea though I walk through the valley of death.
>
The best suggestion I have is to list out the possible alternatives and then
pick the top 5 to 3 and work up some cost estimates.  These have to be
realistic.  Asking for cost estimates from outside resources is a good idea
also.  They tend to be more realistic.

For the  cost estimates insure you have the operating cost of hiring whatever
kind of database admin and programmers at a reasonable salary range.  Today you
can not hire an experienced programmer unless you are ready to pay for the
experience.  Don't hesitate to call up some outside help.  A firm that does this
kind of sytems configuration, conversion, and design can come in handy and save
months of wasted time.

Another option is to buy a canned package.  If a package already exists that is
designed for a business like yours it might be worth it to pay the 1-5 million
it will cost.  I expect a real conversion costing about 1 million dollars or
more.  You still have to have data backup and recovery worked into the system.
A database is a lot more expensive than you think, and it takes up more space
than you think.  It takes a team of design professionals to set one up that will
be able to provide you the same results you have today.  Then of course you
probably want to gain new abilities with your new system.

A Database seems like a good idea.  Just keep in mind that you still have to
support 100 users and a database takes up a lot more storage space.  Ideally you
need a system that mirrors the database and has a complete backup copy.  A good
backup tape system is nice too.  If you use a database what will the package
cost, what are the development costs, and can your current system handle it?  It
is almost impossible to duplicate the dependablility of a well tuned mainframe.

There are advantages to having a complete system redesign.  All of the files
will at least have the same file description in the beginning.  We manage a
school with 8000 students on a mainframe with only 3 COBOL programmers.  Only 2
programmers actually know CICS.  The difference is that we purchased our current
package as one complete unit.  When it was designed about 15 years ago it was
pretty good, but we keep tweaking it because of the redesign costs to go to a
database.  We added a report generating tool called Decision Analyzer that can
link files and make reports on the fly from any PC with a windows interface.  It
also makes nice download files.

We implemented a central project manager to develop new projects.  The project
manager schedules the meetings and then evaluates a project to see if it is
worth doing and assigns priorities.  Someone has to be willing to say no to
stupid ideas.  If you can't agree on a copybook format that is a management
issue.  Who is running the company?  Are the programmers running it, or is
management running it?  You probably need an IT Manager that can make decisions
and force people to get along.  IT without a strong manager is a bad idea.  You
are asking for trouble.


>I am a clueless COBOL newbie.  I am a C and Perl programmer, 
>an Oracle SQL geek, a mid-range Sun hardware geek, who has 
…[69 more quoted lines elided]…
>programmers, and needed an alternative.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Migration Alternatives?

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-12-19T16:14:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9vqe75$ep5$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<c948eb61.0112081716.3700dfde@posting.google.com> <e22U7.4094$XC5.4240@www.newsranger.com>`

```
If you do it yourself, it will cost twice as much and take twice as long,
and the users will blame you.

If you have others do it, these costs will double again, and the product
won't be quite what you want - but all the users will be on your side.
```

<a id="t-d4b2ae752f"></a>
## Thread: Parser to parse a COBOL file — 9 this month / 38 total

### Parser to parse a COBOL file

- **From:** mshetty@mail.com (mshetty)
- **Date:** 2001-12-09T05:14:30-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfbb8fd4.0112090514.4fb0f2ec@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,

I have to design a parser to compare function calls between COBOL and
C.
Something like
PROGRAM_ID. abc.
LINKAGE SECTION.
01 A_IN S9(4) COMP.

PROCEDURE DIVISION USING BY VALUE A_IN.

is equal to 

void abc(short a_in);

This what I plan to do to achieve the above. Eliminate multiple spaces
(between words, before words) using "tr". Then parse for PROGRAM-ID
(would give me the function name), WORKING-STORAGE SECTION(would give
me variables used in CALL statements), LINKAGE SECTION (would give me
variables used in PROCEDURE DIVISION statement), PROCEDURE DIVISION
(to get function arguments) and CALL (to get details of functions
called by this function / procedure).

Since, the position is fixed I can look for WORKING-STORAGE SECTION
then go on parsing till I get a LINKAGE SECTION.

Would help if I could get some comments/ views on this.

Thanks and Regards,
M Shetty
```

#### ↳ Re: Parser to parse a COBOL file

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-12-10T03:06:11+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c137367_10@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<bfbb8fd4.0112090514.4fb0f2ec@posting.google.com>`

```
Looks fine to me...

Remember to check for upper/lower case or convert each source line to upper
before trimming. (you may have intrinsic "TO-UPPER" depending on your
COBOL).

You are trimming spaces then checking for the reserved words you want. I
wouldn't do this; I'd forget the trimming and simply use transition
diagramming to detect immediately what is required with fewer byte compares.
The transition code can flush the lines that fail, quicker than your compare
for the length of the reserved word. This could be a sledgehammer to crack a
nut (when I first did this I was working on a COBOL compiler for
Burroughs...<G>) Tell you what though...the syntax scanning flew!

I wrote a paper on this around 10 years ago but I can't find it now. It was
published by Ed Arranga in one of the early paper copies of COBOL Report. He
might still have it somewhere.

If you are really interested, mail me and I'll write some code samples to
give you the idea.

Pete.
mshetty <mshetty@mail.com> wrote in message
news:bfbb8fd4.0112090514.4fb0f2ec@posting.google.com...
> Hi,
>
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> M Shetty
```

```
Notes on transition diagramming despatched by private mail.

I think David's suggestion is a good one too, but your needs are much less
than a full blown parser.

Pete.
mshetty <mshetty@mail.com> wrote in message
news:bfbb8fd4.0112091853.491f01ed@posting.google.com...

news:<3c137367_10@Usenet.com>...
> > Looks fine to me...
> >
> > Remember to check for upper/lower case or convert each source line to
upper
> > before trimming. (you may have intrinsic "TO-UPPER" depending on your
> > COBOL).
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > diagramming to detect immediately what is required with fewer byte
compares.
> > The transition code can flush the lines that fail, quicker than your
compare
> > for the length of the reserved word. This could be a sledgehammer to
crack a
> > nut (when I first did this I was working on a COBOL compiler for
> > Burroughs...<G>) Tell you what though...the syntax scanning flew!
> >
> > I wrote a paper on this around 10 years ago but I can't find it now. It
was
> > published by Ed Arranga in one of the early paper copies of COBOL
Report. He
> > might still have it somewhere.
> >
> > If you are really interested, mail me and I'll write some code samples
to
> > give you the idea.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
```

#### ↳ Re: Parser to parse a COBOL file

- **From:** "Ira D. Baxter" <idbaxter@semdesigns.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-18T11:02:24-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c1f74b5$1@giga.realtime.net>`
- **References:** `<bfbb8fd4.0112090514.4fb0f2ec@posting.google.com>`

```
If you are doing serious comparisons of function signatures in both
languages,
it seems to me that you have to be able to parse both function signatures
(with all their complexities) in both languages..
Then you have to define carefully what you mean by "equal"
(does "char *" mean "zero terminated"?  How do you zero terminate a COBOL
string?),
and then implement the comparision routine itself.

For that, you need a single tool that can parse both at the same time,
and provide you with structured access to the parsed results.
Our DMS Software Reengineering Toolkit can do that;
you can put tested C and COBOL parsers into the same
custom analyzer, get parse trees, and implement
your equality check.

Maybe your problem is constrained (can be constrained?) enough
so that an ad hoc string hacking solution is sufficient,
but if you can really do that, I'd guess your situation
doesn't really warrant such an automated check at all.

How many function calls to C (count'em) exist
in your COBOL code total?
```

```
Ira Baxter writes ...

[snip]

> How do you zero terminate a COBOL string?),

Use a 'z' data type (at least in IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM or later).

[snip]

<ad>
This is covered in detail (with lots more, of course) in our course "Beyond
COBOL II" which discusses all the changes to the IBM mainframe COBOL compilers
from COBOL II on, up through COBOL for OS/390 & VM V2.2.1. The new "IBM
Enterprise COBOL for z/OS and OS/390" changes will be in a new course out first
quarter 2002.

</ad>

Kind regards,


Steve Comstock
```

```


S Comstock wrote:

> Ira Baxter writes ...
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Similarly in Net Express using OO :-

    01 myTitle pic x(13) value z"Dialog Title".

Note - one extra character for the Null terminator. OR :-

 move z"ReturnCustomerPrimeKey "  to ls-MethodName - again note "extra space"
before closing quote, denoting the x'00'.

Have no idea whether the IBM or Micro Focus approaches are part of the future
Standard. Still according to Bill's latest, we'll only know when ISO consent to let
us take a look see at the final document. (If it isn't part of the Standard - you
have to wonder, considering two 'major players' introduced it to their compilers.)

You have to despair at the bureaucracy !

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Parser to parse a COBOL file

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-18T22:37:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QyPT7.17$PO5.2862@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3c1f74b5$1@giga.realtime.net> <20011218123257.14901.00000663@mb-fp.aol.com>`

```
Or (NOT currently available with any IBM COBOL compiler - but available with
many others), use the syntax that will become ANSI/ISO conforming some time
at the end of next year and use

   "literal " & X"00"

type structures.
```

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@NOSPAM-hot.rr.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YULQ7.37115$f75.1397304@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
Boy, am I getting rusty on workstation COBOL. Does anyone remember how to
right the correct
EOL sequence (In IBM Workstation COBOL) for any platform?

In other words, under AIX I need it to right a Line Feed, and under Windows
I need it to
write a Carraige-Return/Line Feed pair, basically from a DISPLAY statement.
I thought I used to
know how to do this, but if so, the memory has totally escaped me.

Thanks in advance. :)
-Paul
```

#### ↳ Re: Writing a platform specific EOL character set

- **From:** praul@isot.com (Paul Raulerson)
- **Date:** 2001-12-10T11:04:03-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40f8f32c.0112101104.2e72002a@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<YULQ7.37115$f75.1397304@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
Duh- I figured it out again. use figurative constants in the
special-names paragraph.  If anyone else is interested, here a short
little guy that acts liek a CGI program in most COBOLs. (A CGI program
was eaierted to test in.)

       identification division.
       program-id. test1.
       environment division.
       configuration section.
       special-names.
           symbolic characters CR is 14, LF is 11.
       data division.
       
       procedure division.
       program-start.
           display 'Content-type: text/html' CR LF CR LF
           display '<html>This is a test</html>'
           goback.






"Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@NOSPAM-hot.rr.com> wrote in message news:<YULQ7.37115$f75.1397304@typhoon.austin.rr.com>...
> Boy, am I getting rusty on workstation COBOL. Does anyone remember how to
> right the correct
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> -Paul
```

### Do you understand this question?

- **From:** igal2000@hotmail.com (Igal)
- **Date:** 2001-12-09T19:03:48-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c124eb5e.0112091903.3890db79@posting.google.com>`

```
An input record consists of 15 group items, each with a 3 digit part
number and an associated 3 digit quantity on hand. Assuming there are
50 input records...write a working storage entry to store the data..
ok, so this is what I would do
01 WS-TABLE.
     05 TABLE-ENTRY OCCURS 50 TIMES.
        10 PART-NO   PIC 9(3).
        10 QUANTITY  PIC 9(3).

Now...what's the deal with the "15 group items", am I not doing
something?
Thank you guys, excuse my ignorance....IGAL
```

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>

#### ↳ Re: Do you understand this question?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>

```
As Thane has already stated, the question sure could have been written more
clearly.  (NOT your problem - but the teachers - In the real world, one
thing you learn to do is intelligently "question" your users to find out
what they REALLY want - not what they say they want).

I read this to mean that
 A) There are 50 possible input records
   B) Each input record includes 15 separate "items" - including a part
number and quantity on hand

This sounds (to me) like a "two-level" table (if you have covered those in
class already), e.g.

01  Full-Table.
   05  One-Per-Record occurs 50 times indexed by Record-index.
         10  Each-Item  Occurs 15 Times  indexed by Item-index.
                15  PART-NO   PIC 9(3).
                15  QUANTITY  PIC 9(3).

(Sorry for "don't do their homework people" - I don't know how to "describe"
this without showing it).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Do you understand this question?

- **From:** stephenjspiro@mail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-12-15T22:46:40-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<928495c6.0112152246.68810fad@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<c124eb5e.0112091903.3890db79@posting.google.com> <tGWQ7.43389$WC1.4284473@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message news:<tGWQ7.43389$WC1.4284473@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>...
> As Thane has already stated, the question sure could have been written more
> clearly.  (NOT your problem - but the teachers - In the real world, one
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>                 15  QUANTITY  PIC 9(3).

This is OK for loading the table, but awkward for accessing.
I would code it

 01  Full-Table.
    05  One-Per-Record occurs 50 times indexed by Record-index  PIC
X(90).
 01  Filler REDEFINES Full-Table.  
          10  Each-Item  Occurs 750 Times  indexed by Item-index.
                 15  PART-NO   PIC 9(3).
                 15  QUANTITY  PIC 9(3).

Since PART-NO and QUANTITY are the only meaningful terms for
processing, accessing them with a single index is much easier.  You
can use a one-level SEARCH.

Stephen J Spiro
```

#### ↳ Re: Do you understand this question?

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-12-10T20:30:22+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c146546_2@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<c124eb5e.0112091903.3890db79@posting.google.com>`

```
Yes, no problem <G>...

Despite protestations to the contrary, the question is worded clearly.

You got it right, the only bit you missed was the fact that 50 records need
to be stored and each record is "grouped" as two fields.

Here's the code:

01 ws-table.
     12 the-record-level occurs 50
                                    indexed by trl-x1.
          15 the-record-group occurs 15
                                    indexed by trg-x1.
               18 part-no   pic 999.
               18 quantity  pic 999.

You can see how close you were...<G>.

Now, if you want to reference a whole input record (all 15 groups) you can
say:

set trl-x1 to <whatever number between 1 and 50 represents the record you
want>
move the-record-level (trl-x1) to ...<wherever you want to put it>

OR

you can use a fixed subscript (which gets resolved at compile time, instead
of at run time, so it is more efficient) thus...

move the-record-level (14) to ...<whatever>

(Obviously, you can only use a fixed subscript if it will always have that
value...efficient, but not very flexible...)

If you want the part number and quantity together, of the 13th group in the
23rd record, you can say:

move the-record-group (23, 13) to... <whatever>

(or set the two indexes like this...)
set trl-x1 to 23
set trg-x1 to 13
move the-record-group (trl-x1, trg-x1) to...<whatever>

The real advantage of being able to reference your table with variable
indexes comes when you have to "put stuff away"...

HINT:(some code has been omitted for clarity, and to ensure that you do your
own homework <G>)
...
working-storage.
01  EOF-flag    pic x value space.
      88 no-more-input   value '1'.
      88 theres-still-input value '0'.
...
procedure division.
...
perform
     varying trl-x1
         from 1
            by 1
        until trl-x1 > 50 or no-more-input
            read in-file
                 at end
                          set no-more-input to TRUE
                          exit perform
            end-read
            move in-record to the-record-level (trl-x1)
 end-perform
...

Your compiler MAY not support "exit perform"... If it doesn't you will need
to use the "not at end" clause as well as "at end" and you will need to move
the "move in-record to the-record-level (trl-x1)" statement into the "not at
end" scope.

I'm sure you will realise that you can reference the individual part number
or quantity by providing a pointer to the two levels ofthe table, exactly as
we just did for the "Group"...

move part-no (trl-x1, trg-x1) to ...
move part-no (39, trg-x1) to...
move part-no  (47, 1) to...
move quantity (trl-x1, 3) to ...

This is manipulation of a two dimensional table. There is nothing mysterious
about it, once you realise the "cascading" nature of its hierarchic
structure.

Remember only to put the PICTURES at the "bottom", after all the levels of
the hierarchy have been defined...(see above.)

You should read about defining tables with OCCURS, and using indexes and
SEARCH to access them.

Also, realise this is a contrived exercise purely for yur education. In real
life it is VERY unlikely that you would read input records into a table.

Why do you think that is?

HTH,

Pete.

Igal <igal2000@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:c124eb5e.0112091903.3890db79@posting.google.com...

> number and an associated 3 digit quantity on hand. Assuming there are
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
```


###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Index vs Subscript (was: Do you understand this question?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-10T14:49:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jY3R7.468$5K4.72642@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<c124eb5e.0112091903.3890db79@posting.google.com> <3c146546_2@Usenet.com> <Pw3R7.208457$8q.20217448@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
With some compilers (certainly not all) on some operating systems (certainly
not all) indexes are still SUBSTANTIALLY faster/better performers than
subscripts (no matter how defined).

Long before OO came along, it seemed to me (and I know that others feel
exactly the opposite) that an "index" more closely "modeled" the real world
of tables than a subscript did/does.  On the other hand, when the "entry
into a table element" is a "real world object itself" - then I expect it to
be a subscript (rather than using lots of SET statements).

I may be wrong, but it seems to me that it NEVER hurts to define an index
for a table (when first creating it).  You may or may not ever actually
reference it in your procedure division.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Index vs Subscript (was: Do you understand this question?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Ron" <Ron@NoSpamForMe.Com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-10T15:08:15-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9v3882$p6c$1@suaar1ab.prod.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<c124eb5e.0112091903.3890db79@posting.google.com> <3c146546_2@Usenet.com> <Pw3R7.208457$8q.20217448@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <jY3R7.468$5K4.72642@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
If you're using the SEARCH verb, use an index.
Otherwise, my humble opinion is, use a subscript.
It is much easier to see and control the value in
a subscript than an index. Particularly on the
mainframe when you're looking at a dump.
```

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Index vs Subscript (was: Do you understand this question?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-12-10T22:09:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<voaR7.166477$YD.13117995@news2.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<c124eb5e.0112091903.3890db79@posting.google.com> <3c146546_2@Usenet.com> <Pw3R7.208457$8q.20217448@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <jY3R7.468$5K4.72642@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
I always preferred subscripts.

Subscripts are easier to find in dumps.
Subscripts are closer to my education as a physics major.
We never have to set a subscript to zero and then up by 1.   Ugly, ugly
code.
We're used to using the MOVE command instead of the SET command.

That said -

Indices used to be more efficient.
Bad programmers won't use the same index for several tables.
We have to learn indices anyway if we want to use SEARCH tables.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Index vs Subscript (was: Do you understand this question?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-12-11T15:05:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C1620CF.1D322B92@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<c124eb5e.0112091903.3890db79@posting.google.com> <3c146546_2@Usenet.com> <Pw3R7.208457$8q.20217448@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <jY3R7.468$5K4.72642@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <voaR7.166477$YD.13117995@news2.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> We never have to set a subscript to zero and then up by 1.   Ugly, ugly
> code.

How strange, I often set a subscript to zero and then increment by 1. 
Usually this is, say, 'Item-Limit', I increment by 1 before adding an
item to the table and use it as the subscript when adding the item.  

         MOVE ZERO    TO Item-Limit
         ..

         ADD 1        TO Item-Limit
         MOVE Data    TO Item-Data(Item-Limit)

This provides a count of the numbers of items so that processing is:

         PERFORM
             VARYING Item-Index FROM 1 BY 1
               UNTIL Item-Index > Item-Limit

It doesn't look ugly to me.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Index vs Subscript (was: Do you understand this question?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-10T23:15:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<UlbR7.1322$5K4.180086@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<c124eb5e.0112091903.3890db79@posting.google.com> <3c146546_2@Usenet.com> <Pw3R7.208457$8q.20217448@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <jY3R7.468$5K4.72642@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <3C153D5D.2EFE6179@shaw.ca>`

```
One of the philosophical "things" behind OO - is that what you program is
"objects that model the real world".  (Definitely MY wording, not those of
the language theorists."

Therefore, there is an issue (question?) about whether an index or a
subscript actually "models" the real world.  That was the topic that I was
raising (poorly).  To me, an "index" more closely resembles the "access"
into a table/array - while a "subscript" is appropriate if it actually
reflects some "real world" object, e.g. department-number, age, etc.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Index vs Subscript (was: Do you understand this question?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-12-11T00:48:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C155858.5481C1D6@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<c124eb5e.0112091903.3890db79@posting.google.com> <3c146546_2@Usenet.com> <Pw3R7.208457$8q.20217448@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <jY3R7.468$5K4.72642@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <3C153D5D.2EFE6179@shaw.ca> <UlbR7.1322$5K4.180086@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:

> One of the philosophical "things" behind OO - is that what you program is
> "objects that model the real world".  (Definitely MY wording, not those of
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

OK, see what you are getting at. Back to the 'real world' - ummh. But frankly
non-OO COBOL is about the 'real world' isn't it, no less than OO.

Surprisingly I think indexing, as illustratedt, could, depending upon the
particular problem (task), have the edge over OO, specifically when you go to
more than one-dimensional.

In a one dimensional array an OO Dictionary probably wins. You don't have to
have your collection elements (table entries),  ordered, plus your elements are
'unlimited'.. By use of method "includesKey" gets you direct to the element. For
the uninitiated, think of a Dictionary as an "in-memory" ISAM file - key and
data, added to the table, next element added at bottom.

Not so sure when you go two-dimensional etc. I've never tried it but to achieve
the same as a conventional Table Search I think you would need the ability to
have a 'Dictionary within a Dictionary", i.e., each element (row) in the
Dictionary is itself a Dictionary. (AND - I don't even know if it is possible).
If we have any Smalltalk expertise here, they could tell us.

I was mulling this one over, having read your message. One of those traditional
mileage tables, showing distance between Chicago and Los Angeles,  Chicago and
New York etc. Off the top of my head, I think I would do it this way :-

   01 DepartureCityName occurs n pic x(20).    *> both these 'ascending'
   01 ArrivalCityName      occurs n pic x(20).    *> alphabetically

   01 MileageTable.
        05     occurs     n.
            10  Mileage occurs n pic 9(05).

Do a Name 'search all' for the two 01 CityNames, then use the indexing (x, y) to
get
the actual mileage from the Mileage Table.

OO-wise the two Name 01's could be as above to search or treat them as
dictionaries and use method "includesKey". Either approach still giving you the
indexing (x, y) into the MileageTable which again could be a non-OO Table or an
Ordered Collection of elements, each element having an occurs 'n'.

Be curious how others would tackle this, both  non-OO, and anybody into C++,
Java etc., how they would do it.

BTW - your suggestion that a subscript perhaps mimicks more closely the 'real
world', you've still essentially got to to do this :-

    perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > ls-size

        if MyObject = TableObject(n)
           then we've got a winner

    End-perform

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
'real
> world', you've still essentially got to to do this :-

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

You can code this way whether n is defined as a separate subscript or as
INDEXED BY on TableObject.  The compiler will generate code with slightly
better performance in the latter case (avoiding multiply).

Gunnar.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Do you understand this question?

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-12-11T13:45:35+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c155afa_7@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<c124eb5e.0112091903.3890db79@posting.google.com> <3c146546_2@Usenet.com> <Pw3R7.208457$8q.20217448@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```

JerryMouse <news@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:Pw3R7.208457$8q.20217448@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com...
>
> "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 2. Why indexes ever?

Well, Jerry, having tutored "beginning students" for the past 18 years I
find they are not as stupid as you might think.

The use of an index or a subscript is carried out in the same logical
manner. They both act as a "pointer" to an array. I would not cloud the
issue by discussing the relative (platform dependent) performance or going
into long discussions regarding offsets and operand multiplication with a
beginnng student, but I see no harm in getting them used to the habit of
automatically defining an index when they use OCCURS.

There is no more difficulty in using indexes than there is in using
subscripts, from the point of view of the programmer.

If you personally have a difficulty with this and find them abhorrent, then
I suggest that that is a problem for you and not for the beginning
student.<G>

( I can provide some therapy to help you overcome this mental
block...remedial tutoring is something I undertake gladly, for free...<G>)

Besides it opens up the world of SEARCH and SEARCH ALL which are very
powerful COBOL constructs.

Now, please don't let your personal preferences interfere or bias the
learning process for others...

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Do you understand this question?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <news@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-11T16:29:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tvqR7.176444$YD.13547400@news2.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<c124eb5e.0112091903.3890db79@posting.google.com> <3c146546_2@Usenet.com> <Pw3R7.208457$8q.20217448@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3c155afa_7@Usenet.com>`

```

"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
> >
> > 1. Why indexes (instead of subscripts) for a beginning student? Seems
(to
> > me) indexes unnecessarily complicate the assignment.
> > 2. Why indexes ever?
>
> Well, Jerry, having tutored "beginning students" for the past 18 years I
> find they are not as stupid as you might think.

I teach computing science at the university level. They ARE as stupid as I
think.

>
> The use of an index or a subscript is carried out in the same logical
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> automatically defining an index when they use OCCURS.

There is no 'great harm' - just confusion. Indexes and subscripts both act
the same way, but have very different operational differences. To the
student, "If they're the same, why can't I COMPUTE an index or SET a
subscript?"

>
> There is no more difficulty in using indexes than there is in using
> subscripts, from the point of view of the programmer.

Agreed. But to the student ...

>
> If you personally have a difficulty with this and find them abhorrent,
then
> I suggest that that is a problem for you and not for the beginning
> student.<G>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> powerful COBOL constructs.

SEARCH is Chapter 12. We are in Chapter 3. Even later chapters cover Report
Writer, OO, DBF, etc.

>
> Now, please don't let your personal preferences interfere or bias the
> learning process for others...

Why not? Putting a stumbling-block in front of the sighted isn't very
profitable. Stumbling blocks only work on the blind.
```

```
LOL!

I believe you get what you deserve. Try treating them as though they are
smart, but simply uninformed. People will often stop when they have reached
your expectation...

> >
> > The use of an index or a subscript is carried out in the same logical
> > manner. They both act as a "pointer" to an array. I would not cloud the
> > issue by discussing the relative (platform dependent) performance or
going
> > into long discussions regarding offsets and operand multiplication with
a
> > beginnng student, but I see no harm in getting them used to the habit of
> > automatically defining an index when they use OCCURS.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
For the same reason that you READ a FILE and WRITE a RECORD. It is required
by the Language syntax. There is no need to give confusing low level
explanations until the concepts have been thoroughly grasped.

> > There is no more difficulty in using indexes than there is in using
> > subscripts, from the point of view of the programmer.
>
> Agreed. But to the student ...

My students ARE programmers. Just not fully experienced yet.
>
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> > block...remedial tutoring is something I undertake gladly, for
free...<G>)
> >
> > Besides it opens up the world of SEARCH and SEARCH ALL which are very
> > powerful COBOL constructs.
>
> SEARCH is Chapter 12. We are in Chapter 3. Even later chapters cover
Report
> Writer, OO, DBF, etc.
>
Well maybe your curriculum needs review? I would bring SEARCH in at the time
we covered arrays. Others will proceed through data definition and then onto
procedure code. Use what works... Either way, an understaning of the use of
indexes is a prerequisite to understanding SEARCH. And it is simple.

> >
> > Now, please don't let your personal preferences interfere or bias the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Ah, that's way I keep falling over <G>...

Pete.
```

```
Yes, all of this is OK and demonstrates that you have grasped the concept
correctly (and there were those here who thought it was beyond you...tsk!
tsk!)

You CAN use REC-SUB but I would advise you to get in the habit of defining
indexes with your tables. You can see the religious war this simple advice
has caused, by reading the rest of the thread...<G> Nevertheless, I'm
sticking to my guns...trust me.


> And is that ok that i don't use the at end clause after the loading ?

Yes, but it is less elegant than the posted sample. Review and compare the
solutions. (yours and mine).

Also in passing, learn to write your code in a clearly laid out manner. You
don't need to put complex constructs on one line. Later, when you have
thousands of lines of code and you need to amend something, you'll be glad
you built good habits from the start...

> It's my understanding that since there are no more instructions, the
> control is going to go back to the main-mod anyways...ok...thanks you
> all!

A correct assumption in this case.

Why would you NOT read input records from a file into a COBOL table?

You avoided answering the question. I don't really care whether you answer
it or not, but thinking about it will do you some good...<G>

If you can't get it, mail me privately.

Pete.
```
