###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Subscriping and Indexing

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "warren.simmons" <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-12-11T06:52:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<C2iR7.290841$W8.9800984@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<c124eb5e.0112091903.3890db79@posting.google.com> <3c146546_2@Usenet.com> <c124eb5e.0112100800.3a20291@posting.google.com> <3c15600f_9@Usenet.com>`

```
To all interested or not in the subject. COBOL started with just one. The
group
debated for years on the addition of the other.  I recall when the decision
was
made to add the other, the NCR representative volunteered to do the work for
the
proposed change. It still took a year or so for him to get around to doing
the
proposal, and for the group to pass it into the spec.

Warren Simmons
warren@tinycobol.org
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Subscriping and Indexing

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-11T11:44:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hkmR7.2999$vK1.304431@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<c124eb5e.0112091903.3890db79@posting.google.com> <3c146546_2@Usenet.com> <c124eb5e.0112100800.3a20291@posting.google.com> <3c15600f_9@Usenet.com> <C2iR7.290841$W8.9800984@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
And one of the "interesting" enhancements in the draft of the next COBOL
Standard is that you may (finally) use arithmetic expressions as subscripts.
(This is WELL beyond "relative subscripting/indexing" that has been in the
Standard already.)  There are some "fun" rules about what happens when one
of the operands to the arithmetic expression used as a subscript is, itself,
subscripted (or reference modified, or a function-identifier).

You can come up with some pretty "mind boggling" theoretical examples, but
the real world uses will be "nice to have" (IMHO).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Subscriping and Indexing

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-12-11T13:13:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zDnR7.3$A5.573@paloalto-snr2.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<c124eb5e.0112091903.3890db79@posting.google.com> <3c146546_2@Usenet.com> <c124eb5e.0112100800.3a20291@posting.google.com> <3c15600f_9@Usenet.com> <C2iR7.290841$W8.9800984@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <hkmR7.2999$vK1.304431@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
William M. Klein <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:hkmR7.2999$vK1.304431@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net...
> And one of the "interesting" enhancements in the draft of the next COBOL
> Standard is that you may (finally) use arithmetic expressions as
subscripts.
>..... There are some "fun" rules about what happens when one
> of the operands to the arithmetic expression used as a subscript is,
itself,
> subscripted (or reference modified, or a function-identifier).
>
> You can come up with some pretty "mind boggling" theoretical examples, but
> the real world uses will be "nice to have" (IMHO).

To go a step further, Bill, as someone who has been programming in BASIC for
about 15 years, I am very used to using arithmetic expressions as
subscripts, and subscripted variables as subscripts themselves, or having
reference-modified items included in these expressions.

Once you get the hang of using "subcripted subscripts" it really makes for a
powerful tool (linked lists, in-memory indices when you need to sort a
table) , and when I go out to do COBOL stuff I really miss it. Although, I
must admit that COBOL code without "subscripted subscripts" is probably
easier for the next guy to understand and maintain.

MCM
```

On 11-Dec-2001, "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:

> Once you get the hang of using "subcripted subscripts" it really makes for
> a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

It will be nice for cases where we wish for offset subscripts e.g. when
subcodes range from 00000 to 99999:

Move "Y" to subcode-switch (subcode + 1).

Isn't this clearer (and safer) than:
compute temp-field = subcode + 1
Move "Y" to subcode-switch (temp-field).
?

Still, I would like to have the compiler handle it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Subscriping and Indexing

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-12T00:05:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sbxR7.4229$vK1.408878@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<c124eb5e.0112091903.3890db79@posting.google.com> <3c146546_2@Usenet.com> <c124eb5e.0112100800.3a20291@posting.google.com> <3c15600f_9@Usenet.com> <C2iR7.290841$W8.9800984@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <hkmR7.2999$vK1.304431@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <zDnR7.3$A5.573@paloalto-snr2.gtei.net> <gIwR7.291425$W8.9865413@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
A "semi-extreme" example.

Let suppose you have a 7 occurrence table for the days of the week.
However, as "we all know" the remainder of the
    Function Integer-of-Date / 7
gives you values of 0 thru 6 (rather than "subscript" values of 1 thru 7).
Therefore, with the next COBOL Standard, you will be able to code something
like (and I *hope* I have matching left/right parenthesis):

 01 Tabl.
      05  Each-Item occurs 7 times.
            10 Todays-Item  Pic X(10).
                ...
  Move Todays-Item (((Function Integer-of-Date (Function NumVal (Function
Current-Date (1:8))))
                                      / 7 ) + 1)
           to Some-Output-Field


P.S.  IMHO, this particularly example would be CLEARER if you didn't use all
of these "evaluations" in setting up the subscript ... But I think it does
show the theory.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Do you understand this question?

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-12-10T21:20:03-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<AXdR7.6330$eF1.1223818@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<c124eb5e.0112091903.3890db79@posting.google.com> <3c146546_2@Usenet.com>`

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message

> Also, realise this is a contrived exercise purely for yur education. In
real
> life it is VERY unlikely that you would read input records into a table.
>
> Why do you think that is?
>

Pete, you have me curious, as I rather like keeping tables in line
sequential files, rather than using value statements or copybooks.  At least
tables that vary in composition, that is. I see several advantages.  You can
change them with a text editor and not recompile, you can later write user
update programs for them and call it a setup function, etc.  Aside from the
slight startup cost, I cannot see a real disadvantage.

Of course, I write all PC stuff.  Execution time seldom is important, and
when it is, it is always obvious at the design stage.

Here is a "real world" sample table that I store in a file.

       01 TAX-TABLE.
           04 ONE-TAX              OCCURS 4 TIMES.
               05 TAX-SHORT        PICTURE XXX.
* example ... GST, PST, VAT
               05 TAX-LONG         PICTURE X(25).
*example "Goods and services tax"
               05 TAX-DETAILS      OCCURS 6 TIMES.
                   06 TAX-LETTER   PICTURE X.
*letters for the customer to use as code, default "E" for example.
                   06 TAX-RATE     PICTURE 99V99.
*maybe anything from percent to dollars per tonne, depending on calculation
code
                   06 TAX-METHOD   PICTURE 99.
* a method --- ie 01, tax is a percentage of the amount
*                         02 tax is a percentage of the amount plus tax
number one
*                         03 tax is a dollar per tonne rate
*                         04 tax is percentage of material portion of price
*                         05 tax is a percentage of shipping potion of price
etc.
It handles up to six tax codes for each of four types of sales tax. There
are about 15 methods, that used in various combinations, currently work in
35-40 different tax jurisdictions.  We have sample files for each
jurisdiction, and I have not had to change any sales tax code in over 5
years. Instead of me having to maintain 35 copy books and the nighmare of
distribution, my customers upgrade their tax tabels and send me a copy. I
just update my distribution library with the new table.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Do you understand this question?

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-12-12T03:41:54+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c161d3e_7@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<c124eb5e.0112091903.3890db79@posting.google.com> <3c146546_2@Usenet.com> <AXdR7.6330$eF1.1223818@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:AXdR7.6330$eF1.1223818@news20.bellglobal.com...
> "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> sequential files, rather than using value statements or copybooks.

That isn't what he is doing Don, and it isn't what I said either.

How many of your Master files do you read into tables? He is reading what
purports to be an elementary Product Master and storing the values in a
table.

My comment was that IN REAL LIFE (my emphasis) this would be VERY UNLIKELY
(my emphasis again.) I make this statement based on a lifetime of experience
in the field. (Several different paddocks actually...<G>) I have never seen
this done in ANY installation I have worked on, and I can see why. (So can
you if you think about it...<G>)

The point was to stimulate Igal to think about it.

Yes, there ARE occasions when you would read a file into a table.

For static tables it makes sense.

But the occasions when you do this are when you want to save a table on an
external medium. The point I was trying to get Igal to consider is why you
would NOT do this as a general rule...say, read the Customer Master file
into a table, or, as in this example, a Product Master file into a table...

(Wanna bet that someone will point out now that if these "files" are stored
on an RDB they ARE stored in a table...<G>...It's no wonder students get
confused....<G>)

CLUE: COBOL does not have, and has never had, dynamic table allocation.
(And if any mainframers start talking about GETMAIN and FREEMAIN I'll gently
remind them that while these facilities can be CALLed from COBOL (and CICS),
they are NOT part of the COBOL Language...)

Catch my drift <G>?

Pete.



 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services

----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Do you understand this question?

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-12-11T14:55:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9v56jq$hd6$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<c124eb5e.0112091903.3890db79@posting.google.com> <3c146546_2@Usenet.com> <AXdR7.6330$eF1.1223818@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
One ugly thing I often consider with copy members is the following:
01  :TAG:-BILL-REC-WORK.
    05  :TAG:-BILL-SORT-KEY.
        10  FILLER                PIC X.
        10  :TAG:-BILL-SORT-ZIP   PIC X(5).
        10  :TAG:-BILL-SORT-NAME  PIC X(16).
        10  :TAG:-BILL-SORT-DORM  PIC X(4).
        10  :TAG:-BILL-SORT-ROOM  PIC X(6).


Then my code can have:
COPY BRBILLPT REPLACING ==:TAG:== BY ==BR==.
COPY BRBILLPT REPLACING ==:TAG:== BY ==SORT==.


This allows me to have the same structure with different data names in the
same program:
MOVE SMT-DORM        TO SORT-BILL-SORT-DORM
or
MOVE SMT-DORM        TO BR-BILL-SORT-DORM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Do you understand this question?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-12-11T17:58:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C16499F.A0C590DD@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<c124eb5e.0112091903.3890db79@posting.google.com> <3c146546_2@Usenet.com> <AXdR7.6330$eF1.1223818@news20.bellglobal.com> <9v56jq$hd6$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:

> One ugly thing I often consider with copy members is the following:
> 01  :TAG:-BILL-REC-WORK.
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> MOVE SMT-DORM        TO BR-BILL-SORT-DORM

Agree 100% with the concept, but why do you start off by saying "One ugly
thing......?.".  It's a 'trick' I use frequently with OO - such as, lnk-xxxx
appears as input to a Method and when appropriate I move lnk-xxxx to global
ws-xxxxx. Or  vice versa, when returning something from a method. ( My 'xxxx'
above is the copyfile with ==(tag)== replacing).

This is an additional feature I use with copyfiles. Inevitably when returning
from methods that have accessed either COBOL files or DB Tables (and the
non-OO parallel would be a CALL subprogram) I want to know if my 'access' was
successful. Originally picked up the idea from Will Price (objectz.com) and
have expanded on it.

Sometimes - might just want a 'result', other times a result and data. So two
copy files :-

        Sqlresult..cpy and either a datafile.cpy, ( or some extract.cpy)

 invoke FileObject "getSomething" returning ls-values :-

01 ls-values.
    05 copy "sqlresult.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==05 ls==.
    05 copy "datafile.cpy"   replacing ==(tag)== by ==05 ls==.

Partially, Sqlresult.cpy  looks like :-
*>----------------
          (tag)-SqlResult                  pic 9(03).

           88  ResultOK                   value 0.
           88  FileResultOK              value 0.

           88  RecNotFound             value 1.

           88  NoMoreRows             value 2.
           88  FileFinis                       value 2.
            etc....
*>--------------------

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Partial word replacing (was: Do you understand this question?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-11T18:46:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0wsR7.3291$5K4.373936@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<c124eb5e.0112091903.3890db79@posting.google.com> <3c146546_2@Usenet.com> <AXdR7.6330$eF1.1223818@news20.bellglobal.com> <9v56jq$hd6$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3C16499F.A0C590DD@shaw.ca>`

```
And of course <G>

The "leading" and "trailing" phrases are being added in the next COBOL
Standard for both COPY/REPLACING and REPLACE.  Therefore, you will be able
to do "partial word replacement" while STILL keeping your copy member so
that it will compile-cleanly.

Contact your "vendor of choice" to see when they plan on providing this
support (if they don't already).

P.S.  The REPLACE statement is also enhanced to allow for EITHER adding
additional replacing phrases in the 2nd thru Nth REPLACE statement *or* (as
currently specified) to turn off whatever is currently in effect.
```

On 11-Dec-2001, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:

> Agree 100% with the concept, but why do you start off by saying "One ugly
> thing......?.".  It's a 'trick' I use frequently with OO - such as,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> above is the copyfile with ==(tag)== replacing).

The next version of CoBOL will look a lot more like English with the Leading
and Trailing clauses.  It will mean that I don't have to anticipate ahead of
time that I will be using a copy member differently in a program.  I will be
able to use a 10 year old copy member that is used all over the place,
changing its prefix in my program.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Do you understand this question?

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)

```
I thing the group should look back at his original post under 'Loading Tables'
and note that as part of his assignment he's to print the 126th occurance.

A two dimensional table as discussed doesn't have a 126th occurance as
such.....we old timers can compute it.. but I don't think that's part of the
original assignment, so I would assume he needs a 1 dimensional table.

To load would require a subscript on the input record and one on the table,
each input record having an occurance of 15 and the table an occurance of 750.

Just a thought (and staying out of the subscript vs index discussion :D)

Eileen
```

Terry Heinze wrote:

>     I agree with Eileen.  It seems everyone is making the solution more
> complicated than it needs to be.  I see no need for a 2-dimensional table or
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Eileen

Not sure I agree Terry - and back to IGAL's original message :-

>An input record consists of 15 group items, each with a 3 digit part
>number and an associated 3 digit quantity on hand. Assuming there are
>50 input records...write a working storage entry to store the data..
>ok, so this is what I would do

>01 WS-TABLE.
>     05 TABLE-ENTRY OCCURS 50 TIMES.
>       10 PART-NO   PIC 9(3).
>        10 QUANTITY  PIC 9(3).

And in his earlier message he said :-

> c. write procedure division code to print the quantity on hand for
>part number 126
> d. write proc-division code to find the average quantity for all the
>parts.

That's the one where Bill said, "Show us yours, and we'll show you ours"
.
And what I thought was Pete's sensible answer :-

>Here's the code:

>01 ws-table.
>     12 the-record-level occurs 50
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
 >              18 quantity  pic 999.

Eileen refers to the 126th occurrence - but it is PART NUMBER 126. He hasn't
told us specifically, so it could be any one of the occurrences, plus it is
possible Part # 126 may (might) appear in more than one record..

And if he is reading 50 records in from a file, (occurs 15) to a table - having
'juggled' with the table data - what happens when his instructor says, "Well
done guy's. Now write the updated table back to a file".

BTW - I agree with Don about reading in from files when circumstances permit,
particularly 'static' tables. That later observation I made to Bill about
handling mileage tables - I would have an entry in a file per City (with an
occurs N), and read them into a table, (or as an alternative OO collections/and
or dictionaries).

Don talks about 35-40 jurisdictions, so I guess he's including some of the US
States.
And if you think your tax system is complicated, come up to the Great White
North :-

GST (Goods & Services Tax - Federal, equivalent to European VAT) currently 7%
but ADDED after retail price. Then PST (Provincial ST - equivalent to State (?)
taxes) - differs per Province. (Oddball Province - Alberta doesn't have any PST
- and when we mosey down to Montana/Seattle - they used to give us a discount
'cos we were 'different' <G>). The other is HST(Harmonized ST). This is a
Federal tax in lieu of GST where they collect for themselves and the 'have not'
provinces on the East Coast (the 'fishermen'); the feds then give the provinces
a percentage of what is collected. I don't currently do any accounting systems -
so I'm not immersed in the ntty-gritty.

Jimmy
```

Sorry, I posted my response to Eileen and Terry before I saw this. You are

problem statement. Some simply misread it, others said they didn't

Yes, there ARE occasions when you would read a file into a table.

For static tables it makes sense.

But the occasions when you do this are when you want to save a table on an
external medium. The point I was trying to get Igal to consider is why you
would NOT do this as a general rule...say, read the Customer Master file
into a table, or, as in this example, a Product Master file into a table...

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Do you understand this question?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-12-11T10:14:11-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gmpR7.14827$DO.1408360@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<3c146546_2@Usenet.com> <20011210234310.11705.00001490@mb-ce.aol.com> <sggR7.7312$vw1.556035120@newssvr16.news.prodigy.com> <3C15AAD2.308C9CCE@shaw.ca>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3C15AAD2.308C9CCE@shaw.ca...

> BTW - I agree with Don about reading in from files when circumstances
permit,
> particularly 'static' tables. That later observation I made to Bill about
> handling mileage tables - I would have an entry in a file per City (with
an
> occurs N), and read them into a table, (or as an alternative OO
collections/and
> or dictionaries).
>
> Don talks about 35-40 jurisdictions, so I guess he's including some of the
US
> States.

Interesting ... I tend to use OO at the architectural level rather than the
lower level. That entire routine is bundled into a "tax calcualtion method",
that I use across several systems. The tax object is used at the point of
entry (as a cash register) for cash sales, as well as in the head office
accounts reveivable package. BTW, on stuff like gravel pits, the tax
jurisdiction goes down to the municipal level, though you are correct in
that the code is used in both Canada and the States.

In fact, I think it a great example for Howard of where OOP is beneficial,
so I will describe how it works.

The "sales-tax-object" has several screens ... one allows them to enter
prices per tonne and valid tax codes from the table. The tax codes (the
letters assigned at head office) are set up as 1-6 item pulldown lists. That
is used to set up prices for contracts.  The same method is used on the cash
register, for cash sales.  The cash register screen has a price per tonne
box, a shipping rate per tonne box, and a pair of radio buttons that
indicate whether that is a price per tonne or a fixed amount.  It has a
weight property that is filled in by the scale object on a cash register,
from a shipping document object in the accounts receivable module, or from
the keyboard for manual entry of data. That screen has both a "OK" button,
and a "CHECK" button that places a 15 line (pic x(25, occurs 15 times) cash
register slip on the screen, as well as filling out the net price, gross
price, and four tax amounts that are available as properties after the
method is used.  The "cash register mini report" is also available as a
property, and is lifted intact to place on a bill of sale for cash sales. It
is then stored in the record of the transaction along with the amounts so
that the bill of lading can be re-created exactly if needed.

A second screen allows them to set up the table.  That is only used in the
setup/head office code.  The actual table can be stored locally, or on a
head office server, as the situation requires. That is contained in a
different setup module that the installer or systems person uses at
installation. The sales-tax-object uses a standardized "setup-misc-info"
object that stores all our "one of a kind" data, for all systems, under six
character data names (the equivalent of a registry).  Miscellaneous data has
a "location" property that can be local (this machine only) or global (on
the central server, and common to all machines in the company).

The tax table, as shown in the sample code, is entirely invisible to the
higher level application code ... that person does not need to know how it
works.  They place the data into the object, and execute the
"calculate-prices" method, then check an error-flag.  On errors, they use
the error-number property or the error-message property, or both.  Defaults
are defined as the first of the six codes for each tax, and the actual codes
are entirely invisible to the application, as are the various cacluation
methods.  Stuff like where the tax table is stored, and how it is loaded,
are also entirely invisible. Data stored in other systems (like tax codes in
a contract) is entered and edited within the tax object.

Testing a new calculation code or a table revision is *very* simple.  One
simply fires up the cash register, enters some dollar figures, and hits the
"CHECK" button.  You can then see, on the screen, what an invoice or bill of
lading will look like in the amounts column, and check it with a calculator.
That can be repeated over and over, testing for overflow, roundoff, etc. The
names of the taxes etc. are simply data.

Head office uses the exact same routine to check their tax setup at the
begining of any fiscal year.  They check it, enter a password, hit the write
button, and every system in the organization that uses sales tax is updated,
from cash registers to accounts receivable, to inter-company inventory
transfers (presuming the table is stored globally on a server).

There are a couple of points, I would like to make here.  This is COBOL.
While most people teach OOP as being a tool to be used within a programming
conundrum, it is also a tool that can be used within a business conumdrum.
Just as a pulldown box can be an isolated object on a screen that has
nothing to do with the application beyond being used, certain things in
bussiness apps can be viewed as isolated objects that have nothing to do
with the application system beyond being used.

Sales tax calculation is a perfect example.  Sales tax calculation is
depended on an industry, and on a jurisdiction.  The fact that it is being
used in a cash register vs an accounts receivable system is totally
irrelevant.  By making it a complete standalone system in itself, and simply
using that object in all systems accross a company, one ensures two things
... that sales tax changes get implimented *everywhere" they are used, and
that any bugs in sales tax calculations are isolated into a single place.
This dramatically reduces maintenance costs.

While the same thing could be done (sort of) with copies and stuff, it is
much harder, and much more error prone.  Think of it as designing a "sales
tax calculator" that can be run as a standalone object or used in any
system, complete with its own screens, its own methods, and its own source
code.  The registers within that caculator can then be made available to the
higher level programmer by declaring them to be properties.  The programmer
can then move stuff in and out of the registers with simple move statements,
and the programmer can push the buttons on the calculator by invoking
methods.  NEW registers and methods can be introduced at any time, and do
not affect existing systems. Cobol understands the object at the compiler
level, and will give you a compile time error if you try to move a PIC X(10)
field into a PIC 9(5)v9(3) register within the object.

It is really quite neat.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Do you understand this question?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-12-11T20:04:06+00:00

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Do you understand this question?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-12-12T03:12:40+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c16162c_9@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<3c146546_2@Usenet.com> <20011210234310.11705.00001490@mb-ce.aol.com> <sggR7.7312$vw1.556035120@newssvr16.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Well, I'm sorry Terry, but on this occasion both  you and Eileen have missed
the point.

It is PART NUMBER 126 that he has to report on, NOT ENTRY 126.

Also the problem statement, as described under  BOTH the topics, is VERY
clear that the file contains 50 records, each of which has a GROUP of 15
sets of part number and quantity.

It is quite obvious that a 2 dimensional table is the best solution, and it
would be unreasonable to expect a newcomer to have to do the necessary
calculations to convert this to single dimensional table. (I agree that for
us old hands this is a trivial calculation, but it isn't when you are still
learning about table structures and trying to grasp the concepts, without
yet having encountered the underlying offsets and lengths).

The important lesson for Igal here is to grasp the concept of table levels
(which he has done admirably). There is a lesson for some others too... Read
a problem statement CAREFULLY before assuming you have understood it <G>.

And I do NOT accept that multi-dimensional tables are necessarily more
complicated than they need to be. If the underlying data structure warrants
multi-dimensions (as in this case) the result is simple and elegant. Much
more so than an equivalent single dimensioned table would be. (If this were
not the case there would be little point in having a multi-dimensional
facility in the language, right <G>?)

Go, and sin no more...<G>

Pete.

Terry Heinze <terryheinze@prodigy.net> wrote in message
news:sggR7.7312$vw1.556035120@newssvr16.news.prodigy.com...
>     I agree with Eileen.  It seems everyone is making the solution more
> complicated than it needs to be.  I see no need for a 2-dimensional table
or
> more than 1 index (or subscript, if you prefer).
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> > and note that as part of his assignment he's to print the 126th
occurance.
> >
> > A two dimensional table as discussed doesn't have a 126th occurance as
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> > each input record having an occurance of 15 and the table an occurance
of
> 750.
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Do you understand this question?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-12-12T02:30:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<DjzR7.6839$L33.1340880965@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3c146546_2@Usenet.com> <20011210234310.11705.00001490@mb-ce.aol.com> <sggR7.7312$vw1.556035120@newssvr16.news.prodigy.com> <3c16162c_9@Usenet.com>`

```
    I fail to see the need for 2 dimensions.  If I understand the original
question correctly, I would read the input 'til end of file.  Record 1 would
load occurrences 1-15 of the 750-occurrence table, record 2 would load
16-30, record 3, 31-45, etc. until record 50 which would load occurrences
736-750.  To find part number 126, serially search the table until you find
it.  What am I missing here?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Do you understand this question?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-12-12T22:44:37+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c172843_4@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<3c146546_2@Usenet.com> <20011210234310.11705.00001490@mb-ce.aol.com> <sggR7.7312$vw1.556035120@newssvr16.news.prodigy.com> <3c16162c_9@Usenet.com> <DjzR7.6839$L33.1340880965@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com>`

```

Terry Heinze <terryheinze@prodigy.net> wrote in message
news:DjzR7.6839$L33.1340880965@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com...
>     I fail to see the need for 2 dimensions.  If I understand the original
> question correctly, I would read the input 'til end of file.  Record 1
would
> load occurrences 1-15 of the 750-occurrence table, record 2 would load
> 16-30, record 3, 31-45, etc. until record 50 which would load occurrences
> 736-750.  To find part number 126, serially search the table until you
find
> it.  What am I missing here?
>
You are missing the ability to pull out any given group (15 per record) with
a single access and without having to calculate where it is.

Given that you put them away two at a time (part number and quantity) you
COULD do as you suggest, but now you must calculate whenever you want to
access a given record.

If I want part number and quantity together, from the 40th record, 3rd
group, I would need to calculate ((40 - 1) * 15) + 3).

Are you seriously suggesting that that is clearer and more intuitive than
(40, 3)?

Given the part number in occurrence 588, how do I know what record it is in?
How do I know which group it is? Maybe the Users require a pointer to groups
within a record...

I have to calculate the record. ((588/15) + 1) and the group (588 mod 15).

Why?

If I define something which matches the data structure being input, COBOL
provides facilities to handle it easily and simply.

Terry, I really don't care how you do it and I'm satisfied that no argument
I can make will persuade you.

That's OK.

My concern is that new people get to see the whole story and not some
"censored" version served up to them because someone else decided (rightly
or wrongly) that multi-level tables are "complex" and beyond the capability
of newbies. (I know from experience working with them that it isn't,
provided you get the concept of cascading levels across. Most people have no
problem dealing with the idea of something being contained in something
else. It is the basis for all hierarchic strucure...(see "Zen and the Art of
Motorcycle Maintenance" <G>)

COBOL is (at least it used to be...) a powerful language. Despite this
power, it is still simple. Only when other considerations are introduced, do
the waters get muddied. I know COBOL programmers who NEVER use
PERFORM...VARYING (in fact I have worked on sites where it was banned...).
Ask them why, and they will say it is too complex. I spend 20 minutes with
them and they are using it like pros. The same with nested IFs... If these
facilities are too complex to use then why are they in the Language?

Learn the Language, revel in its power and write simple clear concise code.

Anyway, I don't need to make this argument; Igal made it for me. He DID
grasp the concepts explained in my mail and he has apparently obtained high
marks.

The defence rests.

Pete.

                http://www.usenet.com
```

```
Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote:
>
> You are missing the ability to pull out any given group (15 per record) with
> a single access and without having to calculate where it is.

What assumptions have you made about the data that would have this as
relevant in any way ?

> Given that you put them away two at a time (part number and quantity) you
> COULD do as you suggest, but now you must calculate whenever you want to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> group, I would need to calculate ((40 - 1) * 15) + 3).

What significance do you attach to a particular record or to the
positions within it ?  Is there a record for each warehouse and the 15
groups indicating storage positions ?

Given there is no such actual structure indicated there may be no such
relavence at all, nothing to link the groups to a specific record, just
up to 750 products packed into a poor design.

 
> Are you seriously suggesting that that is clearer and more intuitive than
> (40, 3)?

Given that only parts 001 to 999 are valid then using the part number as
a subscript is much 'clearer and intuitive' than assuming some articial
and unwarranted structure.


> Given the part number in occurrence 588, how do I know what record it is in?
> How do I know which group it is? Maybe the Users require a pointer to groups
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Why?

Who cares what record it is in ?  If that was a requirement then the
file design would be different.

> If I define something which matches the data structure being input, COBOL
> provides facilities to handle it easily and simply.

If the 'data being input' had been structured correctly in the first
place then then it wouldn't need to be transformed into a table. 
Reproducing the poor design into W-S is completely unnecessary.  If it
were required to access by record number and position (because there was
some unspecified reason for this) can be done by accessing the file
relatively without reading it all into memory.

No.  It is only given that the part numbers and quantities are to be
read into the table, and for access to be made to a specified part
number (no matter how many times it occurs!).  For this the _only_
calculation ever needed is:  ADD 1 TO someindex.


> Terry, I really don't care how you do it and I'm satisfied that no argument
> I can make will persuade you.

That's because your 'argument' is crap.

> My concern is that new people get to see the whole story and not some
> "censored" version served up to them because someone else decided (rightly
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> else. It is the basis for all hierarchic strucure...

You haven't established that there is any such _actual_ hierarchy and
thus a multi-level table is completely an _unwarraranted_ complexity.

Perhaps the 'input records' are actually 90 column punched cards filled
with as much data as possible to save card cost and handling time (in
which case it isn't such a poor data design, but is not a hierarchy).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Do you understand this question?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-12-15T09:04:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C1B1223.610B0907@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3c146546_2@Usenet.com> <20011210234310.11705.00001490@mb-ce.aol.com> <sggR7.7312$vw1.556035120@newssvr16.news.prodigy.com> <3c16162c_9@Usenet.com> <DjzR7.6839$L33.1340880965@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3c172843_4@Usenet.com>`

```
> > What assumptions have you made about the data that would have this as
> > relevant in any way ?
> 
> There is no ASSUMPTION. It is GIVEN. Read what it said. They are in 15
> groups per record.

You missed the keyword: _relevant_ in my question.  It is given that the
input data is packed into records to be part of the exercise, there is
no other _relevence_ to this in spite of your insistence that others are
'missing' this.
 
> > > Given that you put them away two at a time (part number and quantity) you
> > > COULD do as you suggest, but now you must calculate whenever you want to
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> So you don't disagree that this is true,.

I disagree entirely that it is a requirement to access the data in this
way.  The 'third group of the 40th record' is a meaningless reference. 
You are 'saving' calculations that never need be done.
 
> It doesn't matter. This is an academic exercise. The data is stated to be
> structured as 50 records consisting of 15 groups of 2 items.

No the data is not 'structured' in this way, it is packed into the input
records.  There is no 'structure' that requires particular groups to
reside in specific records or in particular slots, or at least not so
for this exercise.

> No, it's because Terry is a programmer, not a student. So are you Richard.
> You have totally missed the point of the exercise. 

> And perhaps you have your head so far up your arse you are unable to see
> anything beyond your belly button, or even read the statement of a problem
> carefully enough to understand what is intended.

At least being wrong has not caused you to lose your  sense of humour...
(now where did I see that before?).

> If you want to argue that key fields in data can be used to reference
> tables, I have no problem with that. But that is not what THIS exercise is
> about.

Then, maybe it is you that has completely missed the point of what the
exercise was about.  Perhaps the insistence in the spec that the codes
are 3 digit numeric was a clue to setting up a 1000 item table and using
the code as a subscript.  The '50 records' was only an assumption to be
made and was not a given (did you miss that?), there may be more or
fewer.

The whole point was the requirement to access 'code 126' (not item 126
as you pointed out), or indeed any other code.  This is best done using
the code as a subscript rather than scanning the whole table looking for
every occurance (did you only consider that there may be only one) of
the required code.

Your 2D table _may_ be the best if there was a requirement to process
each of the records, but there wasn't.  Yours may be the best if it was
required to write them out again, but it isn't. Yours may be the best if
the given _assumption_ of 50 records is true, but there may be more.

Yours is the best solution to some other problem entirely.

And then you complain about how everyone else has 'missed' this.

> And I don't believe that you never use multi-level tables because they are
> "complex". 

No, I use multi-dimensional when they are _appropriate_, which isn't
this case.  Even if the input records _do_ have some actual structure,
as you assume, this is outside the scope of the exercise and is not only
not part of the spec but is not part of the solution.

Now it is possible that the 'best' solution for reading the data might
be:

    01   Group-Records.
         03  Record-Store            PIC X(90) OCCURS 50.
    01   Group-Store REDEFINES Groups-Records.
         03  Group-Item              OCCURS 750.
          05 Group-Code              PIC 999.
          05 Group-Quantity          PIC 999.

Then the records can be stored and accessed with a single subscript and
the groups can be searched (for code 126) in a simple linear way without
having to step down a contrived 2D table.
      
However, I see no difficultly in stepping down each record in turn while
doing an ADD 1 to the table index as the most 'complex' calculation that
is actually required.
```

#### ↳ Re: Do you understand this question?

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <news@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-10T14:18:26+00:00

```
Let's try describing the problem in a real-world vocabulary:

You have 50 warehouses. Each warehouse can store a varying quantity (0 to
999) of at most 15 different items (out of 999 possible items), each item
has a three-digit part number. Describe the data layout.


"Igal" <igal2000@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:c124eb5e.0112091903.3890db79@posting.google.com...
> An input record consists of 15 group items, each with a 3 digit part
> number and an associated 3 digit quantity on hand. Assuming there are
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Do you understand this question?

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-12-11T07:44:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C15B953.E4475517@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<c124eb5e.0112091903.3890db79@posting.google.com> <Su3R7.317362$dk.21049431@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
JerryMouse wrote:
> 
> Let's try describing the problem in a real-world vocabulary:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> has a three-digit part number. Describe the data layout.

I think that it is a completely unwarranted assumption that the '50
input records' each have a different origin or a varying number of group
items.
 
> "Igal" <igal2000@hotmail.com> wrote in message
> news:c124eb5e.0112091903.3890db79@posting.google.com...
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> > something?

There are 50 records each with 15 group items, total 750 part numbers
with quantity.  You need to read 50 times, each time you will get data
that is like the above but with OCCURS 15 instead of 50.  The WS-Table
should be OCCURS 750 (at least).  Using two subscript variables set one
of them at 1 before the first read and the other at 1 upon each read and
move using these, incrementing both after each MOVE pair (of part number
and quantitity).

I would assume that a part-no of zero indicates no entry - ie there are
only valid part-no 001 - 999.  In this case I would check each input
entry part-no for being numeric and > zero before moving and only
increment the subscript for the WS-table appropriately.

Alternately set the WS-Table occurs to 999 and use the part-no as the
subscript to access this.  You will need to INITIALISE the table first
(or MOVE ALL ZERO TO WS-TABLE), and then ADD the input quantity to
QUANTITY(Part) in case of duplicates on input.

If 000 is a valid part number then you would need to add 1 to each part
number (in a _four_ digit field) to get the table subscript and ensue
that this was OCCURS 1000.

The spec is very much like typical user specs, more questions than
answers: Is 000 valid, how is an empty input group indicated, are there
duplicates in the input, are duplicates valid or errors, what sequence
are they in, ...
```

## Thread: COBOL Homework Help — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### COBOL Homework Help

- **From:** KellyTech@techie.com (Kelly Schroeder)
- **Date:** 2001-12-10T13:11:22-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<55021a70.0112101311.30bbe32d@posting.google.com>`

```
I'm using Microfocus Personal COBOL for Win 3.1 with Murach's
Structured COBOL as the textbook.  My assignment (Project 10-1 from
page 762 for any that actually have this book) is to copy a copy
member into a program that validates whether a user inputs a correct
state and corresponding zip code.  The copy member is formated like
this:

01  STATE-TABLE-VALUES.
    05   FILLER    PIC X(12)   VALUE 'AZ1234598765'.

(The VALUE is not exact.)

I'm having problems getting the program to read the .cpy into the
table.  When it compiles, it shows up properly into the code, but I
don't know how to get it from there into the form that I need.  I also
am unsure about what elements I need in everything above the Procedure
division to set up the table and corresponding index.  Generalities in
response are fine.  I don't want any one to do my homework, but I'm
having difficulty with the under-lying principles of tables, indexes
and copy members.  Any help is appreciated.

Kelly Schroeder
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Homework Help

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <news@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-10T21:37:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dW9R7.53032$C8.3279592@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<55021a70.0112101311.30bbe32d@posting.google.com>`

```

"Kelly Schroeder" <KellyTech@techie.com> wrote in message
news:55021a70.0112101311.30bbe32d@posting.google.com...
> I'm using Microfocus Personal COBOL for Win 3.1 with Murach's
> Structured COBOL as the textbook.  My assignment (Project 10-1 from
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You are aware, of course, that a copy book is of use only at compile time
and, when invoked by the compiler, the resulting situation is as if you had
hand-coded whatever was in the copy book. That is, the copy book itself is
not available at execution time, only what was sucked up by the compiler at
compile time.

That said, pretend you you hand-coded the values found in the copy book and
move them around as you would any other data.

As to what you'll need. You probably need a table. A table is anything with
an OCCURS clause. Like an Excel spreadsheet with one column. Each element in
the table (an Excel row) is referenced by a subscript. For example:

01  MYMONTHS.
   02  THE-MONTH OCCURS 12 PIC X(3).

Presto: a table of twelve entries, each three bytes long. To use:

MOVE 'JanFebMarAprMayJunJulAugSepOctNovDec' TO MYMONTHS.
...
MOVE THE-MONTH(TODAYS-DATE-MONTH) TO PRINT-MONTH

where TODAYS-DATE-MONTH is an integer from 1 through 12.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Homework Help

- **From:** "ThatGuy" <thatguy@thatguy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-12-14T22:57:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MtvS7.10850$Z24.2863673@typhoon.jacksonville.mediaone.net>`
- **References:** `<55021a70.0112101311.30bbe32d@posting.google.com> <dW9R7.53032$C8.3279592@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```

"JerryMouse" <news@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:dW9R7.53032$C8.3279592@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com...
>
> "Kelly Schroeder" <KellyTech@techie.com> wrote in message
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> and, when invoked by the compiler, the resulting situation is as if you
had
> hand-coded whatever was in the copy book. That is, the copy book itself is
> not available at execution time, only what was sucked up by the compiler
at
> compile time.
>
> That said, pretend you you hand-coded the values found in the copy book
and
> move them around as you would any other data.
>
> As to what you'll need. You probably need a table. A table is anything
with
> an OCCURS clause. Like an Excel spreadsheet with one column. Each element
in
> the table (an Excel row) is referenced by a subscript. For example:
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Sounds like the copy book is just a series of lits which have to be searched
to determine whether state matches zip.  What you need to do is create a
table that redefines it, and use a subscript.
You can then do a PERFORM VARYING to compare whether the zip you're checking
falls within the range provided by the table (coding below spoiler space so
you can try it yourself first).
.
.
.
.
.
.
.
.
.
.
.
.
.
.
.
.
.
.
.
.
.
.
.
.
.
.




01  WS-NUMERICS.
       05  STATE-SS                                          PIC 99.

01  WS-SWITCHES.
       05 WS-ZIP-FOUND-SW                           PIC X VALUE 'N'.
             88 ZIP-FOUND                                       VALUE 'Y'.

01  STATE-TABLE-VALUES.
      05 FILLER      PIC X(12) VALUE 'AK9012199999'
      05   FILLER    PIC X(12)   VALUE 'AZ1234512449'.
      05  Etc.

01  WT-STATE-TABLE        REDEFINES STATE-TABLE-VALUES.
      05 WT-STATE-RECS               OCCURS 50 TIMES.
            10 WT-STATE                            PIC XX.
            10 WT-LOW-ZIP                         PIC 9(5).
             10 WT-HIGH-ZIP                        PIC 9(5).

MOVE 'N' TO WS-ZIP-FOUND-SW.
PERFORM XXXX-CHECK-ZIPS
  VARYING STATE-SS FROM 1 BY 1
      UNTIL STATE-SS > 50
         OR ZIP-FOUND.

XXXX-CHECK-ZIPS.
           IF CHK-ZIP NOT < WT-LOW-ZIP(STATE-SS)
            AND CHK-ZIP NOT > WT-HIGH-ZIP(STATE-SS)
              (Do whatever)
               SET ZIP-FOUND TO TRUE.
```

Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq

Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Sunday, July 2, 2000

The COBOL FAQ has moved.  To view the most recent version, please check out the
web version at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send private
email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

The following is Appendix B.5 - Changes to create Version 3.01

 o Recorded new location of the FAQ
 o Updated some old (bad) web links
 o Added some new COBOL books (particularly those written or contributed to - by
frequent comp.lang.cobol submitters).
 o Added information about LegacyJ (PERCobol) and changed the company name from
"Synkronix" to "LegacyJ".
 o Updated information for changes in product and company names and addresses
where available
 o Updated the URLs for downloading the draft of the next COBOL Standard and
CobCy
 o Started the Samples/Example Section
 o Tried to get rid of most specific references to Windows/NT, Windows/95,
Windows/98, and Windows/2000.
 o Added links to Xinotech
 o Added information on Norcom and GUI ScreenIO
 o Added (back) information on Wang
 o Added (back) information on the Tiny-COBOL ('74 Standard) project

## Thread: Deleting File from COBOL — 17 msgs

### Deleting File from COBOL

- **From:** floydj@netins.net (Floyd Johnson)
- **Date:** 2001-12-11T21:18:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9v5t7l$c8p$1@ins22.netins.net>`

```
I am using Fujitsu COBOL v.6 - and Thane's text.  Is there a way to delete
a file from within COBOL?  The book covers how to delete a record, but I
see nothing (though I do occasionally miss things) about deleting a
file.  We have created a temporary work file - but would like to delete it
after running the associated sub program.

Thank you for any advice.

Floyd Johnson
```

#### ↳ Re: Deleting File from COBOL

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-12-11T20:22:46-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C16BF76.75560C3@mb.sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<9v5t7l$c8p$1@ins22.netins.net>`

```
Floyd Johnson wrote:

> I am using Fujitsu COBOL v.6 - and Thane's text.  Is there a way to delete
> a file from within COBOL?  The book covers how to delete a record, but I
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Most COBOL implementations allow calls on the operating system to do various
file functions, including deletes.  It varies from one implementation to the
next.  If all else fails, you can open the file as output and close it
immediately: that will clear it to zero size, which is almost as good as it
not being there.

PL
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Deleting File from COBOL

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2001-12-12T03:40:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20011211224027.03143.00000236@mb-fq.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3C16BF76.75560C3@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
In article <3C16BF76.75560C3@mb.sympatico.ca>, Peter Lacey
<lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> writes:

>
>Most COBOL implementations allow calls on the operating system to do various
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

from the CBL_ Subroutines manual you might want to try this

CBL_DELETE_FILE2

CBL_DELETE_FILE2 deletes a file.

The difference between the CBL_DELETE_FILE subroutine and
this subroutine is that this subroutine can support a file name
containing a space.

Specification
The following explains how to specify CBL_DELETE_FILE2.

Parameter data definition
01 file-name PIC X(n).
01 status-code PIC S9(4) COMP-5.

Chapter 4. File Routines 71
Calling format

CALL "CBL_DELETE_FILE2" USING file-name
         RETURNING status-code.

Interface

file-name

Specifies a file name ending with a null character. If no path
name is specified, the current directory is assumed.

Return code
The return code is set in the status-code specified in the
RETURNING clause.

status-code
0: Successful
Other than 0: Failure
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Deleting File from COBOL

```

> PL

Floyd,

I had hoped one of the Fujitsu folks would have stepped in by now. There is a

(but as it is coloured in BLUE means it's not standard, and don't know whether
it is an intended standard for COBOL 2002).

Bill keeps telling us there are CBL_FILE routines in Fujitsu that parallel the

in the version of Fujitsu that he is using ?

Only other alternative - Michael M and Ken M - are there Windows APIs that Floyd

Jimmy

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Deleting File from COBOL

- **From:** stefan.meyer@triumph-international.de (Stefan Meyer)
- **Date:** 2001-12-11T23:44:29-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1c3f59c4.0112112344.5339c3f2@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9v5t7l$c8p$1@ins22.netins.net> <3C16BF76.75560C3@mb.sympatico.ca> <3C16D48D.69452006@shaw.ca>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message 

> Only other alternative - Michael M and Ken M - are there Windows APIs that 
>Floyd could use ?
> 
> Jimmy

Even if I'm not Ken or Michael....

Try this one:
FUNCTION long SHFileOperationA(REF SHFILEOPSTRUCT lpFileOp ) LIBRARY "shell32.dll"

This API-call includes "delete to trash", so that files can be restored.

HTH - Stefan Meyer
```

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-12-12T13:51:52+00:00

- **References:** `<9v5t7l$c8p$1@ins22.netins.net> <3C16BF76.75560C3@mb.sympatico.ca> <3C16D48D.69452006@shaw.ca>`

```
James J. Gavan <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3C16D48D.69452006@shaw.ca...
>
> Only other alternative - Michael M and Ken M - are there Windows APIs that
Floyd
> could use [to delete a workfile]?

Hmm... this looks promising...

=== FROM MSDN ===
Platform SDK: Files and I/O
DeleteFile
The DeleteFile function deletes an existing file.

BOOL DeleteFile(
  LPCTSTR lpFileName   // file name
);
=== end cite===


For what it's worth, MSDN offers a ton of info at a pretty low cost. I have
a "Library" subscription for about $100.00 per year. You get an updated CD
every quarter.

Anyone who wants to use the Windows API (which IMNSHO is one of the most
complete sets of programmer tools ever) should take a look at a Library
Subscription to MSDN. Microsoft has documented the Windows API as well as
IBM documents mainframe stuff, and IBM mainframe documentation is my
personal "gold standard." . You can also get the SDK (Software Development
Kit) on-line, or get a CD for a low cost. The SDK is kinda techie, contains
a lot of stuff no applications programmer will ever use and examples are
mostly written using C or C++; it may be worth a look for some but it's
probably overkill and a waste of money  for "primarily COBOL" shops.

...But for anyone who is working in the Windows environment in any primary
source language, a Library subscription to MSDN is a hell of a good
investment. If you work for a firm with multiple developers more good news:
Last year Microsoft changed the licensing to allow firms to license *one*
copy and have all developers share it. For $100.00 a year or so, your
management can reap a nice return on developer productivity.

A subcription is not really necessary: all the data are available on-line if
you have Internet access. HOWEVER... I have found that searching and
navigating the MSDN web site is not a whole lot of fun, and installing the
CD makes more sense for me.

As always, YMMV, so check pricing/details etc at the Microsoft MDSN web
site. (No I don't have the url handy).

MCM
```

Michael Mattias wrote:

> James J. Gavan <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
> news:3C16D48D.69452006@shaw.ca...
…[46 more quoted lines elided]…
> MCM

Good advice Michael, and fortunately Net Express does come with an non-updated
copy of the SDK. As you say, it can be overkill if you don't frequently want to
get at the nuts and bolts.

The only real time I looked was that dratted <TAB> key that MS introduced for
Windows instead of <ENTER> when moving through fields of a Dialog box. Many
applications, this might be justified, but in a number intensive application it
is ergonomically more efficient to use the Number Pad and hit the right side
<ENTER> key after each entry field. (Also accepting that it leaves the left hand
free to turn over sheets of paper).

In effect MS acknowledge this deficiency by offering three solutions to
substitute <ENTER> for <TAB> - why the hell couldn't it have been a Windows
option ?
(I use Quickbooks for the small amount of bookkeeping I need and they give you
the option "Do you want to use 'TAB' or 'ENTER' ?". Absolutely painless).

Unfortunately Microsoft's three SDK solutions are C coded examples - and like it
or not that doesn't just translate line for line into COBOL. I got half way
there but never resolved it until Steve Biggs in Newbury provided the clue -
"Use the method 'checkCharacterCode" for OVK-RETURN" - then I was in business.

However, the real purpose of this message is Report Writer. I'd be willing to
give it a shot if I could capture a printer's control characters. At the current
time I use N/E PC_PRINT routines - and the fact that they introduced these
suggests that it may not be easy.

Let's backtrack into your BASIC world. With Radio Shack I had their Line Printer
6 - a real clunker, but it did the job. I used a batch file, and from memory
CHR$26 to set the printer to CONDENSED. Unfortunately no longer have the source,
but both for that LP 6 and subsequently an Epson dot matrix for a PC, I
established the control codes for a specific printer, (from memory these were
COMP-6 in RM/COBOL) - allowed me to do STANDARD, CONDENSED, EXPANDED etc., and I
MOVED the control characters into the beginning or end of print records.

Now Report Writer - one could use the same technique, MOVING control codes into
the line formats in the RD Section, plus if a particular phrase needed to be
ITALICS that could be 'surrounded' by start and finish control codes..

I'm now talking HP lasers with a PC. So Win API wise, are there routines,
without getting overly complicated, that will let one capture the 'properties',
(complete control set), for a specific printer, not just the default ?

Another possibility would be to adapt something Pete mentioned a while back. He
puts print details to a WORD file and controls from there. That suggests the
report lines would have to be entered into WORD templates,  (if that's the right
terminology). But I question would that be a viable approach for a 100 page plus
report ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

look at the
    CODE is identifier
clause in the RD for getting "printer control" output on every line.  (There
is nothing "new" in this. It has been available since at least ANSI '74)

"William M. Klein" wrote:

> Jimmy,
>   I am not saying that this will "meet your needs" - but you might want to
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Thanks for the tip Bill. The on-line help is a mess looking for this type of
thing - but the following from Book 2 of the LRM :-
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1.4.7 The CODE Clause

The CODE clause specifies a two-character literal that identifies each
print line as belonging to a specific report.

General Format

WITH  CODE {Literal-1                }
                        {mnemonic-name    }

Syntax Rules

   1.Literal-1 must be a two-character nonnumeric literal.

         Literal-1 may be replaced by a mnemonic-name specified in
     the special-names paragraph. See the OS/VS COBOL
     Language Reference for additional details.

   2.If the CODE clause is specified for any report in a file, it must be
     specified for all reports in that file.

General Rules

   1.When the CODE clause is specified, literal-1 is automatically
     placed in the first two character positions of each report writer
     logical record.

   2.The positions occupied by literal-1 are not included in the
     description of the print line, but are included in the logical record
     size.
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Kind of gets you there once you *know* what the codes are that you want to use
for a specific printer. Doesn't quite have the flexibility I was thinking of -
line start codes, plus 'interjection' elsewhere within the line to indicate say
BOLD or ITALIC.

But it's a start. Thank you.

Jimmy
```

- **References:** `<9v5t7l$c8p$1@ins22.netins.net> <3C16BF76.75560C3@mb.sympatico.ca> <3C16D48D.69452006@shaw.ca> <YhJR7.36$Tt3.107524@paloalto-snr2.gtei.net> <3C17AD84.A508D616@shaw.ca>`

```
James J. Gavan <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3C17AD84.A508D616@shaw.ca...
>
> However, the real purpose of this message is Report Writer.
>
> ... CHR$26 to set the printer to CONDENSED. Unfortunately no longer have
the source,
> but both for that LP 6 and subsequently an Epson dot matrix for a PC, I
> established the control codes for a specific printer, (from memory these
were
> COMP-6 in RM/COBOL) - allowed me to do STANDARD, CONDENSED, EXPANDED etc.,
and I
> MOVED the control characters into the beginning or end of print records.
>
> Now Report Writer - one could use the same technique, MOVING control codes
into
> the line formats in the RD Section, plus if a particular phrase needed to
be
> ITALICS that could be 'surrounded' by start and finish control codes..
>
> I'm now talking HP lasers with a PC. So Win API wise, are there routines,
> without getting overly complicated, that will let one capture the
'properties',
> (complete control set), for a specific printer, not just the default ?

Under Windows, there are no control codes like this: everything is
virtualized. You select the fonts, colors, type size, features etc and
"paint" a page. It does not matter to the programmer/painter ( L'artiste? )
what the device is: it could be a screen, a printer, a plotter, or anything
which uses what is called a "device context."

Windows - or more properly in the case of a printer, the Driver for the
printer - handles any and all control codes necessary to render the virtual
page on the physical device. No font in the size specified? No problem:
Windows has already selected  (if you let it) the "closest matching font"
supported by the driver. No color? No problem: the printer driver will make
it grey-shaded.

While more difficult to learn than text mode printing, Windows printing is
an awful lot more flexible than "pure" COBOL or other "character file"
report generation.

For printing I use a third party shareware library called DDOC, which
consists of a DLL and an EXE file, and includes a "preview" engine. There is
no reason you could not call that library from your COBOL program. The
registered version of this library will cost you all of $22.00. You can
download the shareware version at www.basicguru.com/dickinson or some link
available from Don Dickinsons' page at "The Home of the BASIC Gurus."

The header files would need modification, but all Don uses to pass data are
null-terminated strings and 32-bit signed integers, both of which you can
create in COBOL.

MCM
```

Michael Mattias wrote:

>
> Under Windows, there are no control codes like this: everything is
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> create in COBOL.

Thanks Michael,

But the 'objective' WAS to use Report Writer <G>. Currently I use PC_PRINT and
that's just fine, so at 22 bucks DDOC could be a substitute. At the current time
I'm fairly certain PC_PRINT doesn't contain a preview feature - a real 'nice to
have'.

If you think about it, regardless of which tool you use, Crystal Reports etc.
you have to produce a 'mask' of your print lines, same as for the COBOL RD
Section.

The real appeal of R/W is that it is a built-in feature of the language, (and I
could be wrong on this one), and is the *only* tool that automatically
acknowledges COBOL's OCCURS clause. Further, no messing around with moving
fields, just like Screen Section, tell the RD source where you are getting your
data from and it 'generates' moving comp-3, comp-5 or whatever, into edited
usage display automatically.

Possibly for some 80% of printing, R/W is just fine. It only fails when you want
to start including things like bit images which PC_PRINT currently does. If you
reflect on it, R/W's INITIATE, GENERATE etc. are like very early models of OO's
callbacks/iterations !

It would appear to achieve my 'possible objective' I've got to get into the guts
of the printer driver(s) - possibly that just isn't worth the effort, given the
current easy options. As a result of you mentioning 'drivers' I did check
PC_PRINT and I can use PC_PRINTER_INFO which returns a lot of data for the
specific device, but it isn't storing fonts/styles because the other routines
set these. Now,  'if only..........' I could retrieve the PC_PRINT_INFO and
store it along with font/style information - just might let me put the stored
codes into R/W line formats (?????) (Summary - open up PC_PRINT, capture
information, store to file and close - and this would be a 'once only'. Now go
into R/W and retrieve control codes from the filed information)

This is not high priority - but I'll give it a looksee in due course.

Jimmy
```

suggested, then post process it with your tool of choice for specific printing.

Ie: Flexus Form Print for Windows, something else to make it HTML or whatever
you want.  You get the generating power of Report Writier (which is quite
stunning) without embedding printer specific or platform specific print codes.

Thane Hubbell wrote:

> I still say - Use report writer to a FILE and put your own "codes" in as Bill
> suggested, then post process it with your tool of choice for specific printing.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

Oops ! Nearly missed this one ! (See re: Netscape below).

I AGREE the Report Writer feature of automatically picking up fields specified in the
RD Section is superb. Nevertheless I must say I think that is a half-assed solution,
where one is 'trying' to use R/W with a 'work around'. Either you use the tool "as
is", as was intended by the people prior to J4 - or just plain forget about it. Go
Flexus, Crystal, Word as Pete does, or PC_PRINT.

Why bugger around with makeshift solutions just to fit a Standard which doesn't 'play
the game' in the Windows world ?

And exactly where do 'your own codes' come from ? If you are producing something for
general use, you need to be able to pick up (AUTOMATICALLY) on the printer(s) that
the end user has. All the above tools referred to, do exactly that.

I can't help feeling that R/W is like attending a banquet. Last stage of the meal, up
to the dessert table. All finger foods, squares, cookies etc., with a huge
magnificent centre piece - an English trifle. Only snag is nobody has left a spoon
around to help you get at the trifle !

The same sort of deficiency applies to Screen Section. You can display and accept
whole screens, and a la DOS style, post a message at the bottom "Enter Line/Column
Number you want to change" etc., but as for changing attributes or using a Mouse -
forget it, unless you have something like the M/F Screen Attribute or Mouse routines.
Screen Section is a good tool but it's like a gelding.

Netscape : Anybody had any weirdoes (I'm using 4.7.7.) ? Loaded up comp.lang.cobol to
day and all messages, since about Monday, show up BOLD (unread). Then when I accessed
them "Error XXX - Message probably expired". Sod it ! Laboriously went through 'Mark
Thread Read' - no result. Unsubscribed and then re-Subscribed to News Group to
resolve - but even now not sure it has done the trick.

I need hardly add all my spammers managed to get through <G>

Jimmy, Calgary AB

<a id="t-b8422e80d8"></a>
## Thread: MF COBOL 3.4 and COPY REPLACING... — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### MF COBOL 3.4 and COPY REPLACING...

- **From:** graham@olympic.co.uk (graham)
- **Date:** 2001-12-12T00:49:11-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a02b0a1.0112120049.31d356d5@posting.google.com>`

```
What is wrong with COPY ... REPLACING in COBOL 3.4 ??

We have the structure:

 - COBOL source

   COPY "FREDA.ABC".

where FREDA.ABC contains
    
   01 FRED-A.
   COPY "BASEFRED.REC" REPLACING ==BASE== BY ==A==.

and BASEFRED.REC just contains various WS 03-levels etc.
       03 BASE-FIRST                PIC X.

The point of this is that BASEFRED.REC is a generic record layout and
we want to replace the leading general 'BASE' by 'A'. In other cases
we have a B version and a C version. We actually put the REPLACING
within FREDA because there are many modules that COPY FREDA and thus
we only needed to change one file...

This works fine in NetExpress but in COBOL 3.4 the REPLACING simly has
no effect, no errors are reported, the LIST output shows the
statements but there is no REPLACING performed on the contents of
BASEFRED.

I've tried a few versions, tried using REPLACE/REPLACE OFF and even
put the COPY REPLACING at the top level (i.e. without the nesting
COPY), but I never see a replacement.

The 3.2 Manual indicates that the structure is valid.

Any suggestions??

Graham
Olympic Computers
```

#### ↳ Re: MF COBOL 3.4 and COPY REPLACING...

- **From:** "Robert Sales" <Robert.Sales@microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-12T09:38:32
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9v7972$snt$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com>`
- **References:** `<8a02b0a1.0112120049.31d356d5@posting.google.com>`

```
Problem is that the REPLACING phrase works on whole, not partial words, so
in your example, BASE would only get replaced if it's a word by itself.
In current versions of the MF product (Net Express and Server Express) you
can use the new ISO200x syntax:
COPY "fred" REPLACING LEADING ==BASE== BY ==A==.
In earlier versions (I think including 3.4) you should be able to use the
'nasty trick' recently described in another thread where you have:
COPY "fred" REPLACING ==:TAG:== BY ==A==.
However in this case you will need to set up the copy file to look like:
    03 :TAG:-FIRST            PIC X.
etc...
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MF COBOL 3.4 and COPY REPLACING...

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-12-12T18:22:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C17A0B9.81F16303@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<8a02b0a1.0112120049.31d356d5@posting.google.com> <9v7972$snt$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com>`

```


Robert Sales wrote:

> Problem is that the REPLACING phrase works on whole, not partial words, so
> in your example, BASE would only get replaced if it's a word by itself.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Thanks for that Robert - however while the N/E on-line help COPY syntax
mentions "LEADING" and "TRAILING",  the on-line LRM book (18.11) doesn't. Give
the scribes at Newbury a nudge <G>.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MF COBOL 3.4 and COPY REPLACING...

- **From:** graham@olympic.co.uk (graham)
- **Date:** 2001-12-18T12:28:58-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a02b0a1.0112181228.4318637a@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<8a02b0a1.0112120049.31d356d5@posting.google.com> <9v7972$snt$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com>`

```
Thanks for the reply - you are quite right. 

I had believed that I had read the COBOL3.4 documentation and that it
was the same as NetExpress, but obviously not.

graham
```

```
Have you checked the COBOL FAQ already?
 If so, what part of the section on:

 3. Where can I get a COBOL compiler?
        and
 4. Is there a free COBOL compiler?

 didn't you understand? (This is not intended as "snide" comment but as a
serious question so that I can improve the FAQ for the next person with this
type of question.)

If not, why not check the FAQ out and tell us if it answers your question.
The FAQ can be read at:
 http://cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

#### ↳ Re: Where can I get a COBOL Compiler?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-12-13T01:46:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C18086F.D36B32F0@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<9v9045$o9l$1@neptunium.btinternet.com>`

```


Dom wrote:

> Hi
>     Does anyone know where I can get a COBOL Compiler? I need it for my Uni
> work ... any suggestions?
>     Thanx
>

This one comes up so often we could start making a buck if we charged you
enquirers $10 for each answer <G>.

1. Go to www.cobolreport.com and look at Bill Klein's COBOL FAQ - gives you
comprehensive coverage on COBOL.

2. University student - take a look see at microfocus.com  and enquire - they
have a University Edition (aimed at students of course) approx $US 100.

3. Want a freebie of Fujitsu - buy Thane Hubbell's book "Teach Yourself COBOL
in 24 Hours" - includes a CD of Fujitsu. (The book will be good back-up for you
anyway on COBOL syntax/usage).

3. This one will take you to All Things COBOL :-
    http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/ring.htm

4. Or this one, House of COBOL :-
    http://members.aol.com/CobolHouse/

5. Want a set of good examples ? University of Limerick :-
    http://www.csis.ul.ie/COBOL/examples/

Jimmy, Calgary AB






> Dom
> virtual_dom@hotmail.com
```

Our application uses Micro Focus Client-Server Binding, one off our

Gazaloo.

<a id="t-af10eba995"></a>
## Thread: Seq Access using Alt Record Key — 5 msgs

### Seq Access using Alt Record Key

- **From:** floydj@netins.net (Floyd Johnson)
- **Date:** 2001-12-13T15:39:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9vai3g$391$2@ins22.netins.net>`

```
Thane's book has an example of using sequential access using the Alternate
Record Key - it uses the start verb to set the starting point for
sequential access.  I see nothing about how to move the file pointer
associated with an Alternate Record Key to the beginning of the index.  A
couple of other texts have similar examples.

The case includes an indexed file with a primary key of
ACCOUNT-NUMBER.  The alternate key is COMPANY-NAME.  We would like to
print the file in company name order.  The work around we came up with is
to create a line sequential file and sort this new file using company name
as the sort field.  This created a temporary file that could be processed
and then deleted (see my earlier posts).  

Is there some way to use START to set the file pointer so that it is at
the beginning of the alternate record key index?

Thank you for any assistance or suggestions.

Floyd Johnson
```

#### ↳ Re: Seq Access using Alt Record Key

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-12-13T10:57:21-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Aa4S7.22959$DO.2912393@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<9vai3g$391$2@ins22.netins.net>`

```
Move low values to the key, then start as you would anywhere else. (start,
key greater than, invalid key is an empty file).  Your first read will read
the first record.

Donald

"Floyd Johnson" <floydj@netins.net> wrote in message
news:9vai3g$391$2@ins22.netins.net...

> Record Key - it uses the start verb to set the starting point for
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> +-----------------------------------------------------------+
```

## Thread: DSN access in program during runtime — 21 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### DSN access in program during runtime

- **From:** "Gonzo" <ckhamel1961@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-13T18:46:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tI6S7.1978$Sm5.1528331360@newssvr12.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Working with OS/390 COBOL on a mainframe, is there access by a CALL (or some
other way) the name of the dataset that is being read? On a possible related
note, is there a way to determine the number of the dataset being read in
the case of concatenated files?

I have a need to display some of this information on a SYSOUT listing during
runtime for tracing purposes, and I was wondering if anyone had done this
before.

Gonzo
```

#### ↳ Re: DSN access in program during runtime

- **From:** "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
- **Date:** 2001-12-13T21:19:11+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9vb5m8$l5h$1@oslo-nntp.eunet.no>`
- **References:** `<tI6S7.1978$Sm5.1528331360@newssvr12.news.prodigy.com>`

```
The short answer is no, to both questions.  Cobol has no support for this.

The reason is that there has traditionally been an intensional separation of
programs from datasets.  The link between the program's file name and the
real dataset is established at execution time with JCL (or dynamic
allocation with TSO ALLOC or SVC 99).

The longer answer is that many things are possible, it is just a bit more
difficult.  You can always use an assembler subroutine with RDJFCB macro,
that will give you the wanted information.  It is also possible to follow
the control block chains to find such information, even with Cobol, but I
will not recommend it, since it probably would include fields that are not
guaranteed to be stable in future releases.

Gunnar.


"Gonzo" <ckhamel1961@hotmail.com> wrote in message

> Working with OS/390 COBOL on a mainframe, is there access by a CALL (or some
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Gonzo

Yes it can be done. The I/O control blocks haven't changed
much (and because of downward compatibility issues, probably
won't for quite some time). So the real question is, what do
you know about MVS control blocks for task & I/O control?

Would you rather call an ALC program that gives you the
info, or do you want to call a subroutine in COBOL that does
this work?

I've done it before. The last time was in an exit in a
language to list where certain source statements had come
from. However, I've only done this in ALC.
```
