###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DSN access in program during runtime

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2001-12-18T01:31:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c18763$bd3d2e60$8dd0c943@chottel>`
- **References:** `<tI6S7.1978$Sm5.1528331360@newssvr12.news.prodigy.com> <3C191C8A.EFDF96BD@ix.netcom.com> <vJaS7.2021$TS3.1554850878@newssvr12.news.prodigy.com> <3c19c915_5@Usenet.com> <p_uT7.3509$zX4.2244796481@newssvr12.news.prodigy.com>`

```
From "Advanced Assembler Language and MVS Interfaces" by Carmine A.
Cannatello, Chapter 7, "Programming Dataset Allocation:

"In MVS, datasets may be allocated via Job Control Language (JCL) or
programmatically.  MVS provides two methods for allocating datasets via
program.  One is by modifying the Job File Control Block(JFCB) before OPEN
processing and the other is vis Dynamic Allocation (SVC 99).

Job Management creates the JFCB and stores it in the Schedular Work
Area(SWA).  The JFCB contains the contents of the JCL DD statement and
other information. A JCL DD statement must be provided for the JFCB to be
created and then modified by the program.  Datasets are allocated by
changing the dataset name field ( and volser field, if required) in the
JFCB before issuing the OPEN macro instruction.  However, only datasets
that reside on the same unit type specified in the JCL DD can be allocated
since the unit is allocated by the Initiator and cannot be change by
modifying the JFCB".

<snip>  Summary:

Typical reasons for modifying the JFCB:
  1. reading all datasets on a multifile magnetic tape volume
  2. changing the dataset name
  3. changing the dataset name and volser
  4. accessing more than one member of a PDS, using QSAM or BSAM
  5. examining the DCB attributes specified on aJCL DD statement
  6. OPENing the VTOC
  7. adding secondary allocation to a DASD dataset allocated with only
primary allocation

The RDJFCB macro instruction will cause the JFCB for the ddname of each DCB
address specified to be copied from the SWA into the area provided by the
program.

The programs from this book can be found and downloaded at: 

http://www.cbttape.org/cbtdowns.htm

See file number 069.

Gonzo <ckhamel1961@hotmail.com> wrote in article

provide

> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DSN access in program during runtime

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2001-12-18T04:53:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c1877f$dc208680$16c2c943@chottel>`
- **References:** `<tI6S7.1978$Sm5.1528331360@newssvr12.news.prodigy.com> <3C191C8A.EFDF96BD@ix.netcom.com> <vJaS7.2021$TS3.1554850878@newssvr12.news.prodigy.com> <3c19c915_5@Usenet.com> <p_uT7.3509$zX4.2244796481@newssvr12.news.prodigy.com> <01c18763$bd3d2e60$8dd0c943@chottel> <ZcyT7.10036$O7.1112687@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Bill,

I believe that the RDJFCB macro handles this for you.  I have the second
edition of the book and the program JFCB definition is:

    JFCB       DS  44F
    JFCBPTR DC  X'87'
                    DC  AL3(JFCB)

which certainly looks to me like it is for SWA=BELOW. The DCB has an
EXLST=JFCBPTR
parameter, so it looks like that pointer defintion would need to be
changed for SWA=ABOVE.  The DSECT for the JFCB is defined by:

    JFCB     DSECT
    IEFJFCBN LIST=YES 

which should pick up the latest defintion from SYS1.MACLIB.  I have not
done this recently so to be 100% certain I would definitely read the
manual for the RDJFCB macro details.



William M. Klein <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in article
<ZcyT7.10036$O7.1112687@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>...
```

```
According to:

 http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/DGT1S311/7.3.1

"2. The RDJFCB parameter list, the DCB, and the JFCB area specified in the
exit list as well as the exit list itself must reside below 16MB, although
the calling program can be above 16M. "

So perhaps that code will work.  The only way to know for certain is to try
it.  I can do that when I have the time if anyone really wants to see
assembler examples of RDJFCB.  Since this is a Cobol newsgroup I am
reluctant to post assembler code here.  

My intent was to give an overview of the JFCB for those that asked about
it.  Also I already posted a Cobol program that does some of what was
asked.  Also  IIRC the original posting mentioned concatenated datasets and
I would have to do even more research to determine how to handle that.

I would suggest that posting to ibm-main and/or one of the assembler
lists/groups might turn up some working code quicker.  Some systems
programmer is bound to have something close.  I am not begging off, but it
may be some time before I would have the time to pursue this.

Searching: http://groups.google.com/advanced_group_search for RDJFCB 
I got about 184 hits so that might be an even faster way to find some
sample code.

Charlie Hottel
chottel@cpcug.org
```

```
:>which should pick up the latest defintion from SYS1.MACLIB.  I have not
:>done this recently so to be 100% certain I would definitely read the
:>manual for the RDJFCB macro details.

This works well both with SWA below & above. The RDJFCB gets a copy of the
JFCB for the particular DDNAME, and OPEN TYPE=J will merge parts of the user
specified JFCB with the real JCFB when opening the file.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DSN access in program during runtime

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2001-12-18T23:03:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c18818$3b5e1a20$b034e641@chottel>`
- **References:** `<tI6S7.1978$Sm5.1528331360@newssvr12.news.prodigy.com> <3C191C8A.EFDF96BD@ix.netcom.com> <vJaS7.2021$TS3.1554850878@newssvr12.news.prodigy.com> <3c19c915_5@Usenet.com> <p_uT7.3509$zX4.2244796481@newssvr12.news.prodigy.com> <01c18763$bd3d2e60$8dd0c943@chottel> <ZcyT7.10036$O7.1112687@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <01c1877f$dc208680$16c2c943@chottel> <no0v1usr4c5clk0n4hvolk75muacr41rgk@4ax.com>`

```
Yes, I read some in the manual today and it copies JFCB info into your
program.  How and where it gets it is tranparent to your program. That is
one advantage of RDJFCB.  Another is that it retrieves information for
ddnames that you specify.

The Cobol program I posted retrieves information for all datasets allocated
for the step.  I tested it today with concatenated datasets and it handled
it fine.  The ddname is associated with the first dataset and the  others
have a blank ddname.  I don't know why I thought it wouldn't handle this. 
I must need more sleep.

I have four assembler examples of using RDJFCB from Cannatello's book. I
have not tried them out but I believe they will work ok.  Anyone interested
may contact me directly.

<no0v1usr4c5clk0n4hvolk75muacr41rgk@4ax.com>...

second
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DSN access in program during runtime

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompsonNOSPAM@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-14T15:47:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C1A1EEF.7BB58667@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<tI6S7.1978$Sm5.1528331360@newssvr12.news.prodigy.com> <3C191C8A.EFDF96BD@ix.netcom.com> <vJaS7.2021$TS3.1554850878@newssvr12.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Gonzo wrote:
<snip>
> Well, in all reality, I knew it could be done with ALC, but the dust in that
> part of my brain is several inches thick. The last time I wrote ALC
> subroutines was back in my 370/VSE days, and that's going back a bit.
> (Anybody remember programming the IBM 1419 sorters???)
<snip>
Read/Write Direct and the one I came across during a DOS/VS
to MVS/XA migration had a serial number less than the one in
the Smithsonian (so I was told). Problem is, unless the
machine you have has the "Direct" feature, you can't
communicate with a 1419.

I'm doing all of this off the top of my head - and it has
been a while. COBOL has pointers, so you will need to make
use of them:

You will need to look at the PSA (PSAOTCB as I recall), to
get the TCB. TCBTIOT is the pointer to the TIOT you are
using. Now you will have to break down the TIOT per the DATA
AREAS manual. This will give you the JOBNAME, STEPNAME,
PROCSTEPNAME (first 24 bytes), then you start into the
DDNAME matching. NOTE: unless you hold a lock (I've
forgotten which one) and KNOW that no other process is
happening in your address space with regards to your TCB
(task), the TIOT may be re-arranged past that first 24
bytes. There are two other control blocks you have to get
to, and I just can't remember their names. Despite what IBM
says, you can do this without having to use some I/O routine
they provide as long as you know when you are getting a 24
bit address v. a 31 bit address. I say this because all of
the code I wrote prior to the UCB moving above the line
still works (well, I think it does -- if WYLBUR is still
running somewhere...).
```

#### ↳ Re: DSN access in program during runtime

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-12-14T14:18:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9TnS7.94731$C8.6521161@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<tI6S7.1978$Sm5.1528331360@newssvr12.news.prodigy.com>`

```

On 13-Dec-2001, "Gonzo" <ckhamel1961@hotmail.com> wrote:

> Working with OS/390 COBOL on a mainframe, is there access by a CALL (or 
> some
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> the case of concatenated files?

I wish I remembered where I got this program before I started modifying it.
 But you might want to try it out and then modify it for your purposes:
000010  CBL XREF,MAP
000100 CBL NODYNAM,NOOPT,NOSSRANGE
000110*NODMLIST
000200 TITLE 'RETRIEVE JOB/STEP NAME PLUS DATASET INFO.'
000300 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000400
000500 PROGRAM-ID.  DDNAME.
000600*---------------------------------------------------------------*
000700*                                                               *
000800*  USING POINTERS, RETRIEVE OPERATING-SYSTEM INFORMATION AND    *
000900*  RETURN IT TO THE CALLER.                                     *
001000*                                                               *
001010*  IT ONLY LOOKS AT THE DDNAME ASSOCIATED WITH THIS JOB STEP    *
001020*                                                               *
001030*  IT DOES NOT YET HANDLE CONCATENATED DATA SETS.               *
001040*                                                               *
001100*---------------------------------------------------------------*
001200     EJECT
001300 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
001400 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
001500
001600 INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
001700 FILE-CONTROL.
001800
001900 DATA DIVISION.
002000 FILE SECTION.
002100
002200*---------------------------------------------------------------*
002300* BEGINNING OF WORKING STORAGE
002400*---------------------------------------------------------------*
002500 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
002600
002700*---------------------------------------------------------------*
002800* DDN-SUBSCRIPTS-POINTERS
002900*---------------------------------------------------------------*
003000
003100 01  FILLER PIC X(016) VALUE 'DDN-SUBS-PNTRS**'.
003200
003300 01  DDN-SUBSCRIPTS-POINTERS.
003400     10  DDN-TCB-ADDR-POINTER-X.
003500         15  DDN-TCB-ADDR-POINTER       POINTER.
003600
003700     10  DDN-TIOT-SEG-POINTER           POINTER.
003800     10  DDN-TIOT-BINARY  REDEFINES  DDN-TIOT-SEG-POINTER
003900                                        PIC S9(09)  COMP.
004000
004100     10  DDN-JFCB-POINTER               POINTER.
004200     10  DDN-JFCB-X       REDEFINES  DDN-JFCB-POINTER.
004300         15  FILLER                     PIC X(01).
004400         15  DDN-JFCB-LOWER-3           PIC X(03).
004500     EJECT
004600
004700*---------------------------------------------------------------*
004800* WORK-MISCELLANEOUS
004900*---------------------------------------------------------------*
005000
005100 01  FILLER PIC X(016) VALUE '**WORK-MISC*****'.
005200
005300 01  WORK-MISCELLANEOUS.
005400     10  WORK-BINARY-2               PIC S9(04) COMP VALUE ZERO.
005500     10  FILLER  REDEFINES WORK-BINARY-2.
005600         15  WORK-BINARY-2L          PIC X(01).
005700         15  WORK-BINARY-2R          PIC X(01).
005710
005800 01  SWITCHES.
005810     05  SW-MAIN-SWITCH              PIC X(01)  VALUE 'N'.
005820     88  SW-MAIN                                VALUE 'Y'.
005900     EJECT
006000*---------------------------------------------------------------*
006100*  LINKAGE AREA
006200*---------------------------------------------------------------*
006300 LINKAGE SECTION.
006400
006500 01  LINK-COMM-AREA.
006600     10  LINK-JOB-NAME           PIC X(08).
006700     10  LINK-STEP-NAME          PIC X(08).
006800     10  LINK-DD-NAME            PIC X(08).
006900     10  LINK-DS-NAME            PIC X(44).
007000     10  LINK-GDG-OR-MEMBER      PIC X(08).
007100     10  LINK-CATALOG-IND        PIC X(01).
007200         88  LINK-FILE-IS-CATALOGED  VALUE 'Y'.
007300     10  LINK-FILE-TYPE          PIC X(01).
007400         88  LINK-FILE-IS-GDG        VALUE 'G'.
007500         88  LINK-FILE-IS-JES        VALUE 'J'.
007600         88  LINK-FILE-IS-PDS        VALUE 'P'.
007700         88  LINK-FILE-IS-REGULAR    VALUE 'R'.
007800     10  LINK-VOLUME-COUNT       PIC 9(01).
007900     10  LINK-VOLUMES            PIC X(30).
007910 01  PASSED-RECORD REDEFINES LINK-COMM-AREA.
007920     05  PASSED-LENGTH     PIC 9(4)  COMP.
007930     05  PASSED-UPDATE-PARM.
007940         10  PASSED-LABEL  PIC X(14).
007950***      10  FILLER        PIC X(14) VALUE 'PASSED DDNAME='
007960         10  PASSED-DDNAME PIC X(08) VALUE 'NAME=='.
007970     05  FILLER            PIC X(99).
008000
008100 01  L02-TCB-POINTER                 POINTER.
008200
008300 01  L03-TCB.
008400     10  FILLER                      PIC X(012).
008500     10  L03-TIOT-POINTER            POINTER.
008600
008700 01  L04-TIOT.
008800     10  L04-JOB-NAME                PIC X(08).
008900     10  L04-STEP-NAME               PIC X(08).
009000
009100 01  L04-TIOT-START                  PIC X(24).
009200
009300 01  L04-TIOT-SEGMENT.
009400     10  L04-TIOT-LENGTH             PIC X(01).
009500     10  FILLER                      PIC X(03).
009600     10  L04-DD-NAME                 PIC X(08).
009700     10  L04-JFCB-ADDRESS            PIC X(03).
009800
009900 01  L05-JFCB.
010000     10  FILLER                      PIC X(16).
010100     10  L05-DS-NAME                 PIC X(44).
010200     10  L05-GDG-OR-MEMBER           PIC X(08).
010300     10  L05-DMI-BYTE                PIC X(01).
010400     10  FILLER                      PIC X(33).
010500     10  L05-DSTYPE-BYTE             PIC X(01).
010600     10  FILLER                      PIC X(30).
010700     10  L05-VOLUME-COUNT-BYTE       PIC X(01).
010800     10  L05-VOLUME-LIST             PIC X(30).
010900     EJECT
011000 PROCEDURE DIVISION USING LINK-COMM-AREA.
011100
011120     IF  PASSED-LABEL  = 'PASSED DDNAME='
011121         MOVE SPACES                 TO PASSED-LABEL
011130         SET SW-MAIN                 TO TRUE
011140     END-IF
011200     MOVE X'0000021C'                TO DDN-TCB-ADDR-POINTER-X
011300     SET ADDRESS OF L02-TCB-POINTER  TO DDN-TCB-ADDR-POINTER.
011400     SET ADDRESS OF L03-TCB          TO L02-TCB-POINTER.
011500     SET ADDRESS OF L04-TIOT         TO L03-TIOT-POINTER.
011600
011700     MOVE L04-JOB-NAME               TO LINK-JOB-NAME.
011800     MOVE L04-STEP-NAME              TO LINK-STEP-NAME.
011900     INITIALIZE LINK-DS-NAME  LINK-GDG-OR-MEMBER LINK-CATALOG-IND
012000                LINK-FILE-TYPE LINK-VOLUME-COUNT LINK-VOLUMES.
012100
012200****  IF THEY DID NOT SUPPLY A DD NAME, EXIT STAGE LEFT.
012300
012400     IF LINK-DD-NAME < 'A'
012500        GOBACK
012600     END-IF.
012700
012800     MOVE LOW-VALUES TO DDN-JFCB-X.
012900     SET ADDRESS OF L04-TIOT-START   TO L03-TIOT-POINTER.
013000     SET DDN-TIOT-SEG-POINTER        TO L03-TIOT-POINTER.
013100     ADD 24                          TO DDN-TIOT-BINARY.
013200     SET ADDRESS OF L04-TIOT-SEGMENT TO DDN-TIOT-SEG-POINTER.
013300
013400****  LOOP THROUGH ALL POINTERS TO THE DD NAMES.
013500****  WHEN WE GET A MATCH, MOVE IN ALL THE FILE DATA AND EXIT.
013600
013610****  PERFORM UNTIL DD-NAME IS BLANK
013700     PERFORM UNTIL L04-TIOT-LENGTH = LOW-VALUES
013800        MOVE L04-JFCB-ADDRESS           TO DDN-JFCB-LOWER-3
013900        SET ADDRESS OF L05-JFCB         TO DDN-JFCB-POINTER
014000        IF L04-DD-NAME = LINK-DD-NAME
014100           MOVE L04-DD-NAME             TO LINK-DD-NAME
014200           MOVE L05-DS-NAME             TO LINK-DS-NAME
014300           MOVE L05-GDG-OR-MEMBER       TO LINK-GDG-OR-MEMBER
014400           IF L05-DMI-BYTE > X'79' AND < X'90'
014500              MOVE 'Y'                  TO LINK-CATALOG-IND
014600           ELSE
014700              MOVE 'N'                  TO LINK-CATALOG-IND
014800           END-IF
014900           IF L05-DMI-BYTE > X'19' AND < X'30'
015000              MOVE 'J'                  TO LINK-FILE-TYPE
015100           ELSE
015200              IF L05-DSTYPE-BYTE = X'00'
015300                 MOVE 'R'               TO LINK-FILE-TYPE
015400              ELSE
015500                 IF L05-DSTYPE-BYTE = X'01'
015600                    MOVE 'P'            TO LINK-FILE-TYPE
015700                 ELSE
015800                    IF L05-DSTYPE-BYTE = X'02'
015900                       MOVE 'G'         TO LINK-FILE-TYPE
016000                    ELSE
016100                       MOVE '?'         TO LINK-FILE-TYPE
016200                    END-IF
016300                 END-IF
016400              END-IF
016500           END-IF
016600           MOVE L05-VOLUME-COUNT-BYTE   TO WORK-BINARY-2R
016700           MOVE WORK-BINARY-2           TO LINK-VOLUME-COUNT
016800           MOVE L05-VOLUME-LIST         TO LINK-VOLUMES
016810           IF SW-MAIN
016811               DISPLAY
016812               'JJJJJJJJSSSSSSSSDDDDDDDDNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNN'
016813               'NNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNMMMMMMMMCTCVVVVVVVVVVVVVVV'
016814               'VVVVVVVVVVVVVVV'
016815               DISPLAY LINK-COMM-AREA
016820           END-IF
016900           GOBACK
017000        ELSE
017100           ADD 20                    TO DDN-TIOT-BINARY
017200           SET ADDRESS OF L04-TIOT-SEGMENT
017300                                     TO DDN-TIOT-SEG-POINTER
017400        END-IF
017500     END-PERFORM.
017501
017510     IF SW-MAIN
017520         DISPLAY
017530         'JJJJJJJJSSSSSSSSDDDDDDDDNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNN'
017540         'NNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNMMMMMMMMCTC'
017560         DISPLAY LINK-COMM-AREA
017570     END-IF.
017600
017700     GOBACK.
017800*------------ END OF SUBROUTINE DDNAME -------------------------*
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: DSN access in program during runtime

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>

```
In private email, Gilbert Saint-Flour (who is NOT the author of this
particular program) reminded me to remind "you all" that this program ONLY
works in an environment where
   SWA=BELOW
is in effect (i.e. QSAM stuff - among others - is all "below the line").

If you don't know about your environment, check with you MVS, OS/390, or
z/OS systems programmer.
```

```
On 14-Dec-2001, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

> In private email, Gilbert Saint-Flour (who is NOT the author of this
> particular program) reminded me to remind "you all" that this program ONLY
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> z/OS systems programmer.

I once gave him false credit for this program.  It was a reasonable guess as
he is the author of several nifty programs.  Obviously, he knows enough
about the OS/390 OS to understand what this is doing.

It does work in my environment, but I suppose I need to not count on it
working forever as environments change.  Too bad, as it is useful - and I
don't know enough to convert it to a changed environment.
```

- **References:** `<tI6S7.1978$Sm5.1528331360@newssvr12.news.prodigy.com> <9TnS7.94731$C8.6521161@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
This program should help get you started:

   
Groups    
   
    � Advanced Groups Search
 � Groups Help 
 
  
Groups search result 1 for JSAB "hottel" group:comp.lang.cobol 

Search Result 1 
From: Charles Hottel (chottel@cpcug.org)
Subject: jsab rtn added to cobol ptr pgm (swareq) 
Newsgroups: comp.lang.cobol
View: (This is the only article in this thread) | Original Format 
Date: 2001-08-28 15:47:35 PST 
 

To All:

Here is the revised program.  When running in batch the component id was
'jes2'.

       ID DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.   COBPTR.
       AUTHOR.       USCS.
       DATE-WRITTEN. 08/22/01.
       DATE-COMPILED.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    VS COBOL II POINTER FEATURE EXAMPLE                        *
      *    USING MVS CONTROL BLOCKS                                   *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.
           SELECT TEST-FILE ASSIGN TO DD1.
       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       FD  TEST-FILE
           LABEL RECORDS STANDARD
           RECORDING MODE IS F
           BLOCK CONTAINS 0 RECORDS
           DATA RECORD IS TEST-REC.
       01  TEST-REC PIC X(80).
      /
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

TEST***01  WS-TEST                            PIC S9(9) COMP VALUE ZERO.
       01  WS-WORK                             PIC S9(9) COMP.
       01  WS-RIGHT-HEX-DIGIT                  PIC S9(4) COMP.
       01  WS-LEN                              PIC S9(4) COMP.
       01  WS-LENGTH REDEFINES WS-LEN          PIC X(02).
       01  WS-FLAG                             PIC S9(4) COMP.
       01  WS-FLAG1 REDEFINES WS-FLAG          PIC X(02).

       01  WS-TCB-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           05  WS-TCB-ADDR-POINTER             USAGE IS POINTER.

       01  WS-TIOT-SEG-POINT.
           05  WS-TIOT-SEG-POINTER             USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  WS-TIOT-SEG-PNT REDEFINES WS-TIOT-SEG-POINTER
                                               PIC S9(9) COMP.

       01  WS-JFCB-POINT.
           05  WS-JFCB-POINTER                 USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  WS-JFCB-PTR REDEFINES WS-JFCB-POINTER
                                               PIC S9(9) COMP.
           05  WS-JFCB-POINT-RED REDEFINES  WS-JFCB-PTR.
               07  FILLER                      PIC X.
               07  WS-JFCB-LOW-3               PIC X(3).

       01  WS-POINT.
           05  WS-POINTER                      USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  WS-PTR REDEFINES WS-POINTER
                                               PIC S9(9) COMP.
           05  WS-POINT-RED REDEFINES WS-PTR.
               07  FILLER                      PIC X.
               07  WS-LOW-3                    PIC X(3).

       01  WS-SWA-POINT.
           05  WS-SWA-POINTER                  USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  WS-SWA-PTR REDEFINES WS-SWA-POINTER
                                               PIC S9(9) COMP.
           05  WS-SWA-POINT-RED REDEFINES WS-SWA-PTR.
               07  FILLER                      PIC X.
               07  WS-SWA-LOW-3                PIC X(3).

       01  WS-QMAT-POINT.
           05  WS-QMAT-POINTER                 USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  WS-QMAT-PTR REDEFINES WS-QMAT-POINTER
                                               PIC S9(9) COMP.
      /
       01  WS-UCB-POINT.
           05  WS-UCB-POINTER                  USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  WS-UCB-POINT-RED REDEFINES WS-UCB-POINTER.
               07  FILLER                      PIC X.
               07  WS-UCB-LOW-3                PIC X(3).

       01  WS-DEB-POINT.
           05  WS-DEB-POINTER                  USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  WS-DEB-POINT-RED REDEFINES WS-DEB-POINTER.
               07  FILLER                      PIC X.
               07  WS-DEB-LOW-3                PIC X(3).

       01  WS-DCB-POINT.
           05  WS-DCB-POINTER                  USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  WS-DCB-POINT-RED REDEFINES WS-DCB-POINTER.
               07  FILLER                      PIC X.
               07  WS-DCB-LOW-3                PIC X(3).

       01  WS-CVT-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           05  WS-CVT-ADDR-POINTER             USAGE IS POINTER.

       01  WS-ASCB-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           05  WS-ASCB-ADDR-POINTER            USAGE IS POINTER.

       01  WS-ASSB-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           05  WS-ASSB-ADDR-POINTER            USAGE IS POINTER.

       01  WS-JSAB-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           05  WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER            USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      * PUT THE STATEMENTS BELOW IN AN ASSEMBLER PROGRAM TO LOCATE    *
      * THE DISPLACEMENTS OF THE SYMBOLS.  THESE LINES WERE SHIFTED   *
      * TO THE RIGHT TO MAKE COBOL COMMENTS.  SHIFT THEM BACK TO THE  *
      * LEFT SO THAT THE WORD 'TITLE' IS IN COLUMN 10.                *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *              TITLE 'PREFIX STORAGE AREA'                      *
      *              IHAPSA LIST=YES                                  *
      *              TITLE 'TASK CONTROL BLOCK'                       *
      *              IKJTCB LIST=YES                                  *
      *              TITLE 'SECONDARY TASK CONTROL BLOCK'             *
      *              IHASTCB LIST=YES                                 *
      *              TITLE 'DATA EXTENT BLOCK'                        *
      *              IEZDEB LIST=YES                                  *
      *              TITLE 'DATA CONTROL BLOCK'                       *
      *     DCBDS    DSECT                                            *
      *              DCBD  DSORG=PS                                   *
      *              TITLE 'UNIT CONTROL BLOCK'                       *
      *     UCBDS    DSECT                                            *
      *              IEFUCBOB                                         *
      *              TITLE 'TASK I/O TABLE'                           *
      *     TIOTDS   DSECT                                            *
      *              IEFTIOT1                                         *
      *              TITLE 'JOB FILE CONTROL BLOCK'                   *
      *     JFCBDS   DSECT                                            *
      *              IEFJFCBN LIST=YES                                *
      *              TITLE 'COMMUNICATION VECTOR TABLE'               *
      *              CVT DSECT=YES,LIST=YES                           *
      *              TITLE 'SUBSYSTEM COMMUNICATION VECTOR TABLE'     *
      *              IEFJSCVT                                         *
      *              TITLE 'JOB STEP CONTROL BLOCK'                   *
      *              IEZJSCB                                          *
      *              TITLE 'SWA QUEUE AREA'                           *
      *              IEFQMNGR                                         *
      *              TITLE 'JES COMMUNICATION TABLE'                  *
      *              IEFJESCT                                         *
      *              TITLE 'JSAB'                                     *
      *              IAZJSAB DSECT=YES,LIST=YES                       *
      *              TITLE 'ASCB'                                     *
      *              IHAASCB DSECT=YES,LIST=YES                       *
      *              TITLE 'ASSB'                                     *
      *              IHAASSB LIST=YES                                 *
      *              TITLE 'VSAM ACB'                                 *
      *              IFGACB DSECT=YES                                 *
      *              TITLE 'REMOTE AREAS'                             *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      /
       LINKAGE SECTION.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IHAPSA)  X'21C' = DECIMAL 540                  *
      *                         X'224' = DECIMAL 548                  *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  PSA.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(540).
           05  TCB-PTR                      USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(04).
           05  ASCB-PTR                     USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  PSA-ASM REDEFINES PSA.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(540).
           05  PSATOLD                      USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(04).
           05  PSAAOLD                      USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IKJTCB)  HAS A 32 BYTE PREFIX AREA             *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  TCB-POINTER                      USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  TCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(08).
           05  DEB-ADDR                     USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  TIOT-POINTER                 USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(164).
           05  JSCB-POINTER                 USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(128).
           05  STCB-POINTER                 USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  TCB-ASM REDEFINES TCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(08).
           05  TCBDEB                       USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  TCBTIO                       USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(164).
           05  TCBJSCB                      USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(128).
           05  TCBSTCB                      USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(CVT)                                           *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  CVT-POINTER                      USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  CVT.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(296).
           05  JESCT-POINTER                USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  CVT-ASM REDEFINES CVT.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(296).
           05  CVTJESCT                     USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IEFJESCT)                                      *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  JESCT.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(24).
           05  JESSSCVT-POINTER             USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  JESCT-ASM REDEFINES JESCT.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(24).
           05  JESSSCT                      USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IEFJSCVT)                                      *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  SSCVT.
           05  SSCVT-EYE-CATCHER  PIC X(04).
           05  NEXT-SSCVT                   USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  SUBSYSTEM-NAME     PIC X(04).
       01  SSCVT-ASM  REDEFINES SSCVT.
           05  SSCTID             PIC X(04).
           05  SSCTSCTA                     USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  SSCTSNAM           PIC X(04).
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IEFTIOT1)                                      *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  TIOT.
           05  JOB-NAME           PIC X(08).
           05  JOB-PROC           PIC X(08).
           05  JOB-STEP           PIC X(08).
       01  TIOT-ASM REDEFINES TIOT.
           05  TIOCSTPN           PIC X(08).
           05  TIOCPSTN           PIC X(08).
           05  TIOCSJSTN          PIC X(08).

       01  TIOT-SEG.
           05  TIO-LEN            PIC X.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(03).
           05  DD-NAME            PIC X(08).
           05  SWA-V-ADDR         PIC X(03).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(02).
           05  UCB-ADDR           PIC X(03).
       01  TIOENTRY REDEFINES TIOT-SEG.
           05  TIOELNGH           PIC X.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(03).
           05  TIOEDDNM           PIC X(08).
           05  TIOEJFCB           PIC X(03).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(02).
           05  TIOEFSRT           PIC X(03).
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IEFJFCBN)                                      *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  JFCB.
           05  DS-NAME            PIC X(44).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(74).
           05  VOL-SER            PIC X(06).
       01  JFCB-ASM REDEFINES JFCB.
           05  JFCBDSNM           PIC X(44).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(74).
           05  JFCBVOLS           PIC X(06).
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IEZDEB)  DEB HAS A 36 BYTE PREFIX AREA         *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  DEB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(05).
           05  NEXT-DEB-ADDR      PIC X(03).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(17).
           05  DCB-ADDR           PIC X(03).
       01  DEB-ASM REDEFINES DEB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(05).
           05  DEBDEBB            PIC X(03).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(17).
           05  DEBDCB             PIC X(03).
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(DCBD)                                          *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  DCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(17).
           05  DEVICE-TYPE        PIC X.
               88  DISK-3380-X2E  VALUE X'2E'.
               88  DISK-3390-X2F  VALUE X'2F'.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(08).
           05  DSORG              PIC X(02).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(08).
           05  RECFM              PIC X(02).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(02).
           05  DDNAME             PIC X(08).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(14).
           05  BLKSIZE            PIC S9(4) COMP.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(18).
           05  LRECL              PIC S9(4) COMP.
       01  DCB-ASM REDEFINES DCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(17).
           05  DCBDEVT            PIC X.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(08).
           05  DCBDSORG           PIC X(02).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(08).
           05  DCBRECFM           PIC X(02).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(02).
           05  DCBDDNAM           PIC X(08).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(14).
           05  DCBBLKSI           PIC S9(4) COMP.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(18).
           05  DCBLRECL           PIC S9(4) COMP.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IEZJSCB)                                       *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  JSCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(244).
           05  QMPL-POINTER       USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  JSCB-ASM REDEFINES JSCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(244).
           05  JSCBQMPI           USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IEFQMNGR)                                      *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  QMPL.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(24).
           05  QMAT-POINTER       USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  QMPL-ASM REDEFINES QMPL.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(24).
           05  QMADD              USAGE IS POINTER.

       01  QMAT.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(12).
           05  QMAT-NEXT-POINTER  USAGE IS POINTER.

       01  SWA.
           05  JFCB-ADDR          USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MODGEN(IHASTCB)                                       *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  STCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(188).
           05  JSAB-POINTER       USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  STCB-ASM REDEFINES STCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(188).
           05  STCBJSAB           USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IAZJSAB)                                       *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  JSAB.
           05  JSAB-EYE-CATCHER   PIC X(04).
           05  JSAB-NEXT-PTR      USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(05).
           05  JSAB-FLAG1         PIC X.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(02).
           05  COMPONENT          PIC X(04).
           05  JOB-ID             PIC X(08).
           05  JOB-NBR            PIC X(08).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(08).
           05  USERID             PIC X(08).
       01  JSAB-ASM REDEFINES JSAB.
           05  JSABID             PIC X(04).
           05  JSABNEXT           USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(05).
           05  JSABFLG1           PIC X.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(02).
           05  JSABSCID           PIC X(04).
           05  JSABJBID           PIC X(08).
           05  JSABJBNM           PIC X(08).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(08).
           05  JSABUSID           PIC X(08).
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IHAASCB)                                       *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  ASCB-POINTER                     USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  ASCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(336).
           05  ASSB-POINTER       USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  ASCB-ASM REDEFINES ASCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(336).
           05  ASCBASSB           USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IHAASSB)                                       *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  ASSB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(168).
           05  JSAB-POINTER       USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  ASSB-ASM REDEFINES ASSB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(168).
           05  ASSBJSAB           USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
           PERFORM JOB-STEP-NAME.
           PERFORM SUBSYSTEM-NAMES.
           PERFORM JFCB-INFO.
           PERFORM DCB-INFO.
           PERFORM JSAB-INFO.

           GOBACK.
      /
       JOB-STEP-NAME.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    JOB NAME AND STEP NAME                                     *
      *      PSA + X'21C' -> TCB -> TIOT                              *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
           MOVE X'0000021C' TO WS-TCB-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TCB-POINTER TO WS-TCB-ADDR-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TCB TO TCB-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TIOT TO TIOT-POINTER.
           DISPLAY 'JOB NAME=' JOB-NAME
                   '  JOB PROC=' JOB-PROC
                   '  JOB STEP=' JOB-STEP.
           DISPLAY '         '.
      /
       SUBSYSTEM-NAMES.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    DISPLAY SUBSYSTEM NAMES FROM SSCVT CHAIN                   *
      *    CVT -> JSECT -> SSCVT                                      *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
           MOVE X'00000010' TO WS-CVT-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF CVT-POINTER TO WS-CVT-ADDR-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF CVT TO CVT-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF JESCT TO JESCT-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF SSCVT TO JESSSCVT-POINTER.
           DISPLAY 'SUBSYSTEM NAME=' SUBSYSTEM-NAME.
           PERFORM UNTIL NEXT-SSCVT IS = NULL
               SET ADDRESS OF SSCVT TO NEXT-SSCVT
               DISPLAY 'SUBSYSTEM NAME=' SUBSYSTEM-NAME
           END-PERFORM.
           DISPLAY '         '.
      /
       JFCB-INFO.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------
      *    FIND DDNAMES AND ASSOCIATED DSNAMES
      *    PSA+X'21C' -> TCB -> TIOT -> TIOT SEG -> SWAREQ(SVA) -> JFCB
      *---------------------------------------------------------------
           MOVE X'0000021C' TO WS-TCB-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TCB-POINTER TO WS-TCB-ADDR-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TCB TO TCB-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TIOT TO TIOT-POINTER.
           SET WS-TIOT-SEG-POINTER TO TIOT-POINTER.
           ADD 24 TO WS-TIOT-SEG-PNT.
           SET ADDRESS OF TIOT-SEG TO WS-TIOT-SEG-POINTER.
           PERFORM UNTIL TIO-LEN = LOW-VALUES
               MOVE ALL LOW-VALUES TO WS-POINT
               MOVE ALL LOW-VALUES TO WS-JFCB-POINT
               MOVE ALL LOW-VALUES TO WS-SWA-POINT
               MOVE SWA-V-ADDR TO WS-SWA-LOW-3
               PERFORM SWAREQ
               SET ADDRESS OF JFCB TO  WS-POINTER
               DISPLAY 'DDNAME=' DD-NAME
               DISPLAY 'DSNAME=' DS-NAME
               DISPLAY 'VOL=SER=' VOL-SER
               DISPLAY '********************************************'
               MOVE ZERO TO WS-LEN
               MOVE TIO-LEN TO WS-LENGTH(2:1)
               ADD WS-LEN TO WS-TIOT-SEG-PNT
               SET ADDRESS OF TIOT-SEG TO WS-TIOT-SEG-POINTER
           END-PERFORM.
      /
       SWAREQ.
           DIVIDE WS-SWA-PTR BY 16
               GIVING WS-WORK
               REMAINDER WS-RIGHT-HEX-DIGIT.

           IF WS-RIGHT-HEX-DIGIT NOT = 15
               COMPUTE WS-PTR = WS-SWA-PTR + 16
           ELSE
               MOVE X'0000021C' TO WS-TCB-ADDRESS-POINTER
               SET ADDRESS OF TCB-POINTER TO WS-TCB-ADDR-POINTER
               SET ADDRESS OF TCB TO TCB-POINTER
               SET ADDRESS OF JSCB TO JSCB-POINTER
               SET ADDRESS OF QMPL TO QMPL-POINTER
               SET ADDRESS OF QMAT TO QMAT-POINTER
               SET WS-QMAT-POINTER TO QMAT-POINTER
               PERFORM UNTIL WS-SWA-PTR <= 65536
                   SET WS-QMAT-POINTER TO QMAT-NEXT-POINTER
                   SET ADDRESS OF QMAT TO QMAT-NEXT-POINTER
                   COMPUTE WS-SWA-PTR = WS-SWA-PTR - 65536
               END-PERFORM
               COMPUTE WS-PTR = WS-SWA-PTR + WS-QMAT-PTR + 1
               SET ADDRESS OF SWA TO WS-POINTER
               SET WS-POINTER TO JFCB-ADDR
               COMPUTE WS-PTR = WS-PTR + 16
            END-IF.
      /
       DCB-INFO.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    DISPLAY DCB INFORMATION                                    *
      *    PSA+X'21C' - > TCB -> DEB -> DCB                           *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
           OPEN INPUT TEST-FILE.
           MOVE ALL LOW-VALUES TO WS-DEB-POINT.
           MOVE X'0000021C' TO WS-TCB-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TCB-POINTER TO WS-TCB-ADDR-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TCB TO TCB-POINTER.
           SET WS-DEB-POINTER TO DEB-ADDR.
           PERFORM UNTIL WS-DEB-POINTER IS = NULL
               SET ADDRESS OF DEB TO WS-DEB-POINTER
               MOVE ALL LOW-VALUES TO WS-DCB-POINT
               MOVE DCB-ADDR TO WS-DCB-LOW-3
               SET ADDRESS OF DCB TO WS-DCB-POINTER
TEST***********DISPLAY 'DCB-PTR=' WS-DCB-POINTER
               DISPLAY ' DDNAME=' DDNAME
               DISPLAY '  DSORG=' DSORG
               DISPLAY '  RECFM=' RECFM
               DISPLAY 'BLKSIZE=' BLKSIZE
               DISPLAY '  LRECL=' LRECL
               IF DISK-3380-X2E
                   DISPLAY 'DEVICE-TYPE=3380'
                   DISPLAY '**************************************'
               ELSE IF DISK-3390-X2F
                       DISPLAY 'DEVICE-TYPE=3390'
                       DISPLAY '**************************************'
                    ELSE
                       DISPLAY 'DEVICE-TYPE=????'
                       DISPLAY 'DEVICE-TYPE=' DEVICE-TYPE
                       DISPLAY '**************************************'
                    END-IF
               END-IF
TEST***********DIVIDE WS-LEN BY WS-TEST GIVING WS-WORK
               MOVE NEXT-DEB-ADDR TO WS-DEB-LOW-3
           END-PERFORM.
           CLOSE TEST-FILE.
      /
       JSAB-INFO.
           SET WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER TO NULL.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    DISPLAY JSAB INFORMATION                                   *
      *    PSA+X'21C' - > TCB -> STCB -> JSAB                         *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
           MOVE X'0000021C' TO WS-TCB-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TCB-POINTER TO WS-TCB-ADDR-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TCB TO TCB-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF STCB TO STCB-POINTER.
           IF JSAB-POINTER OF STCB IS NOT = NULL
               SET ADDRESS OF JSAB TO JSAB-POINTER OF STCB
               SET WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER TO JSAB-POINTER OF STCB
           END-IF.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    PSA+X'224' - > ASCB -> ASSB -> JSAB                        *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
           IF WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER IS = NULL
               MOVE X'00000224' TO WS-ASCB-ADDRESS-POINTER
               SET ADDRESS OF ASCB-POINTER TO WS-ASCB-ADDR-POINTER
               SET ADDRESS OF ASCB TO ASCB-POINTER
               IF ASSB-POINTER IS NOT = NULL
                   SET ADDRESS OF ASSB TO ASSB-POINTER
                   SET WS-ASSB-ADDR-POINTER TO ASSB-POINTER
                   IF JSAB-POINTER OF ASSB IS NOT = NULL
                       SET ADDRESS OF JSAB TO JSAB-POINTER OF ASSB
                       SET WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER TO JSAB-POINTER OF ASSB
                   END-IF
               END-IF
           END-IF.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    IF JSAB-FLAG1 > 127 THE JSAB IS INVALID                    *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
           IF WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER IS = NULL
               CONTINUE
           ELSE
               MOVE ZERO TO WS-FLAG
               MOVE JSAB-FLAG1 TO WS-FLAG1(2:1)
               PERFORM UNTIL WS-FLAG <= 127 OR
                             WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER IS = NULL OR
                             JSAB-EYE-CATCHER NOT = 'JSAB'
                   SET WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER TO JSAB-NEXT-PTR
                   SET ADDRESS OF JSAB TO WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER
                   MOVE ZERO TO WS-FLAG
                   MOVE JSAB-FLAG1 TO WS-FLAG1(2:1)
               END-PERFORM
           END-IF.

           IF WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER IS NOT = NULL
               DISPLAY 'EYE-CATCHER=' JSAB-EYE-CATCHER
               DISPLAY '  COMPONENT=' COMPONENT
               DISPLAY '     JOB ID=' JOB-ID
               DISPLAY ' JOB NUMBER=' JOB-ID
               DISPLAY '     USERID=' USERID
           ELSE
               DISPLAY 'JSAB INFORMATION NOT FOUND'
           END-IF.
```

---

<a id="t-8754a59070"></a>
## Thread: On REDEFINES clause — 20 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### On REDEFINES clause

- **From:** "Thomas Li" <aizhongli@sprint.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-12-13T13:48:07-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6S6S7.3070$fg7.25948@newscontent-01.sprint.ca>`

```
Who can tell me how often the COBOL REDEFINES clause is used in enterprise
production level code?

This memory sharing or saving feature can cause much more trouble than
saving several bytes of memory.

I'm searching for a set of best practices as COBOL code convention/style.


Thanks in advance.


Thomas
```

#### ↳ Re: On REDEFINES clause

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-12-13T20:07:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9vb1l3$mq4$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<6S6S7.3070$fg7.25948@newscontent-01.sprint.ca>`

```

On 13-Dec-2001, "Thomas Li" <aizhongli@sprint.ca> wrote:

> Who can tell me how often the COBOL REDEFINES clause is used in enterprise
> production level code?
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks in advance.

REDEFINES isn't used for saving memory.  That was RENAMES, which I haven't
seen in decades.

REDEFINES is used all the time.  One example is with parm cards.  Here's an
example:
PGM=SIPR759,INST=BD,TERM=20017,EXTERNAL= ;
PGM=SIPR759,FAINCL=00   ,10   ,11   ;
PGM=SIPR759,FAINCL=20   ,26   ,28   ,29   ;
PGM=SIPR759,FAINCL=30   ,31   ,34   ;
PGM=SIPR759,RANGES=770000-772499

The logic reads in a record, verifies it belongs to SIPR769, and then builds
a FAININCL, RANGES, or INST table.   The descriptions of these various
records are redefines of each other.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: On REDEFINES clause

- **From:** "Thomas Li" <aizhongli@sprint.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-12-13T15:38:31-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Ct8S7.3080$fg7.26162@newscontent-01.sprint.ca>`
- **References:** `<6S6S7.3070$fg7.25948@newscontent-01.sprint.ca> <9vb1l3$mq4$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
While I'm converting  COBOL code into JAVA, the COBOL REDEFINES clause cause
some problem:

I think in COBOL, the redefined and the redefinition share the same memory
footprint at runtime.
For the following piece of code:
     05 date1.
        10 name       xx VALUE "**".
        10 month      99.
        10 day          99.
    05 date2 REDEFINES date PIC 999999.

The mempry allocated is 6 bytes in total, not 12 bytes, with considering
memory alignment..

After MOVE 50000 TO date2 , what is inside name?   "05" or "**" ?

I think redefinition has side-effect because of sharing memory.





Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:9vb1l3$mq4$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 13-Dec-2001, "Thomas Li" <aizhongli@sprint.ca> wrote:
>
> > Who can tell me how often the COBOL REDEFINES clause is used in
enterprise
> > production level code?
> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > I'm searching for a set of best practices as COBOL code
convention/style.
> >
> >
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> REDEFINES is used all the time.  One example is with parm cards.  Here's
an
> example:
> PGM=SIPR759,INST=BD,TERM=20017,EXTERNAL= ;
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> The logic reads in a record, verifies it belongs to SIPR769, and then
builds
> a FAININCL, RANGES, or INST table.   The descriptions of these various
> records are redefines of each other.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: On REDEFINES clause

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-12-13T16:04:35-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BG8S7.23298$DO.3037942@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<6S6S7.3070$fg7.25948@newscontent-01.sprint.ca> <9vb1l3$mq4$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <Ct8S7.3080$fg7.26162@newscontent-01.sprint.ca>`

```
Name becomes "05".

Donald

"Thomas Li" <aizhongli@sprint.ca> wrote in message
news:Ct8S7.3080$fg7.26162@newscontent-01.sprint.ca...
> While I'm converting  COBOL code into JAVA, the COBOL REDEFINES clause
cause
> some problem:
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> memory alignment..

Yes.  In fact, redefining it with PIC 9999999 should give you a syntax
error.


> After MOVE 50000 TO date2 , what is inside name?   "05" or "**" ?
>
> I think redefinition has side-effect because of sharing memory.
>

That is not a "side effect".  That is the purpose of the redefinition, so
that you can reference parts of the same data field in several different
ways.  The first is looking at it as 3 two byte fields, the second is
looking at the same area as a single six digit number.

a better example would be:

>      05 digit-groups.
            10 first-two-digits picture 99.
            10 middle-digits     picture 99.
            10 low-order-digits picture 99.
        05 six-digit-number redefines digit-groups.
            10 my-number        picture 9(6).

Donald
```

```
REDEFINES (COBOL)  = union (C, C++, BASIC, Java(?))  = ORG (micro assembly
languge)

01  A      PICTURE whatever.
01  B      PICTURE whatever REDEFINES A.

same as

01  A      PICTURE whatever.
01  B      PICTURE whatever.
....
SET ADDRESS OF B TO ADDRESS OF A
```

```
Not quite.  Renames AND Redefines do 'save memory'.  The Differences are:
you must redefine a specific portion of a data-area (all or part) at the
SAME LEVEL and you can redefine 'till the Cows come home'.
a RENAMES allows parts from different levels  AND MUST be IMMEDIATELY after
the 01 that contains the parts to be renamed.
01  my-rec.
    05 my names.
         10 last-name pic x(10)
         10 first-name pic x(20)
         10 middle-init pic x.
   05 my-address.
        10  number-addr  pic x(5)
        10 street-name  pic x(20)
        10  apt-number pic (5).
66 Gumbo RENAMES middle-init THRU number-addr.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: On REDEFINES clause

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-12-15T07:31:59-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZlHS7.34695$DO.4163793@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<6S6S7.3070$fg7.25948@newscontent-01.sprint.ca> <9vb1l3$mq4$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <2pyS7.39$M3.53250@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`

```
"James" <mangogrower@telocity.com> wrote in message
news:2pyS7.39$M3.53250@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net...
> Not quite.  Renames AND Redefines do 'save memory'.  The Differences are:

This is getting silly.  There is probably not a construct in the entire
language that cannot be used to save memory, and the idea that redefines
CANNOT be used to save memory is obviously wrong. That was never the claim
that I made with the original post.  The original post was in response to
the following:

> > > This memory sharing or saving feature can cause much more trouble than
> > > saving several bytes of memory.

My response was that REDEFINES is not "a memory saving feature", and that I
had never seen it used simply to save memory.  It is a way of refering to
the same data using two different descriptions.  The clause "RESERVE NO
ALTERNATE AREA" was put into the language to save memory.  The clause
REDEFINES was put into the language so that the same data could be described
in several different ways, NOT so that the same memory could be used for
several different purposes.

The exact same verbs in Cobol use data quite differently, depending on how
it is described.  If there was no way to described the same data twice, in
different ways, then it would be VERY awkward to USE the same data in
different ways.  That is the purpose of redefines, to allow that. It is not
a construct that was designed as a "memory saving feature" ... it's purpose
is quite different.

The correct use of just about any language construct, from the ADD verb to
the logical OR, can be used to save memory.  That is simply the nature of
programming.  To get back to the original statement, I will respond again.

> > > This memory sharing or saving feature can cause much more trouble than
> > > saving several bytes of memory.

So can *any* language feature, BUT that is not the purpose of the REDEFINES.
The correct purpose of the redefines is to redefine the same data several
different ways, so that you can use it several different ways.  If you think
it just a way to save memory, and that is the way you are using it, then
yes, such a usage would probably cause more problems than it solves.

Donald
```

```
> SAME LEVEL and you can redefine 'till the Cows come home'.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: On REDEFINES clause

- **From:** stephenjspiro@mail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-12-15T21:35:09-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<928495c6.0112152135.1bf2af0@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<6S6S7.3070$fg7.25948@newscontent-01.sprint.ca> <9vb1l3$mq4$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> 

> REDEFINES isn't used for saving memory.  That was RENAMES, which I haven't
> seen in decades.
> 
As mentioned elsewhere, almost anything can be manipulated to conserve
memory in some way, but that is not the purpose of RENAMES, either.
RENAMES enables one to "redefine" contiguous data across different
levels.  It was never used much, but I have seen it in many shops to
create (additional) alternate indexes to Direct Access files, when the
record description of the file is in a COPY book.  Since the RENAMES
clause comes at the end of the 01 level, it is very convenient to
place it immediately after the COPY statement.
In a technical interview, regardless of the question, the WRONG answer
is "No one uses that any more."  Almost everything is used somewhere,
even after it is obsolete.  RENAMES, however, is neither obsolete nor
archaic, but actually a very useful feature of COBOL.

Stephen J Spiro
```

#### ↳ Re: On REDEFINES clause

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-12-13T15:40:09-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Hj8S7.23276$DO.3028072@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<6S6S7.3070$fg7.25948@newscontent-01.sprint.ca>`

```
It is used all the time, but I have never seen it used in 35 years to save
memory.  That is not the purpose of it.

*Very* often in computing, one wants to look at a specific field from two
different viewpoints.  I may wish to look at a 64 bit integer as a 64 bit
integer or eight eight bit bytes.  I may wish to look at a sign separate
field as decimal integer plus sign, I may want to look at a table as several
types of array, etc., etc., etc.  None of those have anything to do with
saving memory.  They give data names to the same data from differing
perspectives, so that Cobol will know how I want to treat the data.

Donald

"Thomas Li" <aizhongli@sprint.ca> wrote in message
news:6S6S7.3070$fg7.25948@newscontent-01.sprint.ca...
> Who can tell me how often the COBOL REDEFINES clause is used in enterprise
> production level code?
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…

```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: On REDEFINES clause

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-13T21:57:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9v9S7.60590$xS6.99793@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<6S6S7.3070$fg7.25948@newscontent-01.sprint.ca> <Hj8S7.23276$DO.3028072@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
It can be, and has been used to "save memory". Take the scenerio where a file
has multiple record types; if READ INTO is used the receiving area can be
redefined to suit the various record types in the file. If READ is used,
multiple 01 levels in the FD (implied redefines) serve the same purpose: saving
memory.

Jack



In article <Hj8S7.23276$DO.3028072@news20.bellglobal.com>, Donald Tees says...
>
>It is used all the time, but I have never seen it used in 35 years to save
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: On REDEFINES clause

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-12-13T17:33:02-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xZ9S7.23401$DO.3084434@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<6S6S7.3070$fg7.25948@newscontent-01.sprint.ca> <Hj8S7.23276$DO.3028072@news20.bellglobal.com> <9v9S7.60590$xS6.99793@www.newsranger.com>`

```
How does that save memory?  It is simply describing the data buffer several
different ways. The memory used still has to be the amount used by the
largest record.

There is nothing to stop you from wasting memory by declaring four or five
buffers for one file, then only using one at a time. I can assure you,
though, that READ INTO A, B, OR C, depending on WHAT YOU ARE GOING TO READ,
does not work.  You have to read it into a buffer first, then decide to move
it somewhere else.  A single file has to have a single buffer. Period. What
you do with the data after the read is another thing altogether.

The redefines is used to describe a single area in several ways.  If you
want to create multiple copies of the data to do the same thing, then there
is nothing to stop you, but it is the making two copies of it that *wastes*
memory, not the other way around.  You are using the REDIFINE to avoid
having to make three copies of it, having already wasted one buffer by
implimenting a programming practice founded on a specific platform.

Donald
```

#### ↳ Re: On REDEFINES clause

- **From:** "Kjeld Paab���l Hansen" <paabol@12mail.dk>
- **Date:** 2001-12-14T00:54:14+01:00

```
The REDEFINES is mainly used in accessing different or varying record
layouts in a single file in file processing or in transaction processing.

Earlier, before the ability to access substrings in MOVE statements was
implemented in the Cobol language,  I have seen (and done) a lot of
accessing and manipulating parts of fields (all the way down to bit level)
using redefined storage. This was not done to conserve memory storage, but
15-20 years ago, network bandwidth where expensive and slow, compared to the
present,  and therefore you had to "pack" information in request/reply
transaction records into a maximum record size of 128 bytes!

> production level code?
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```
```

"John H Sleight Jr" <nospam@newsranger.com> wrote in message
news:8XeS7.61032$xS6.100205@www.newsranger.com...
> Yuo wouldn't READ INTO A or B or C, You'd READ INTO A, but redefine A by

…[10 more quoted lines elided]…

level)
> >using redefined storage. This was not done to conserve memory storage,
but
> >15-20 years ago, network bandwidth where expensive and slow, compared to
the
> >present,  and therefore you had to "pack" information in request/reply
> >transaction records into a maximum record size of 128 bytes!
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> Who can tell me how often the COBOL REDEFINES clause is used in
enterprise
> >> production level code?
> >>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> I'm searching for a set of best practices as COBOL code
convention/style.
> >>
> >>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >>


I wouldn't agree, especially on the READ INTO.  READ INTO implies a WS Field
is being employed, which means that for every different type of record, you
would need another matching field of matching size to move the record to.
(ie READ INTO REC-A, IF REC-A IS TYPE-1 MOVE REC-A TO REC-1 ELSE etc.).  As
a result, you end up needing X-size-of-record * Y-potential-types bytes,
rather than just the original record.  Case in point:

I work for an insurance company that keeps policy information in up to 16
different types of 300-byte records.  Not redefining would require that I
set aside 5100 bytes in working storage on the odd chance that I encounter 1
of each type ( (16 + original) * 300).  To make matters worse, some of the
main types are further broken down into sub-types of the main type, which
means potentially more records if I don't redefine the main types.  But by
redefining the original READ INTO Record, I use only 300 bytes and can
process accordingly.
```

#### ↳ Re: On REDEFINES clause

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-15T13:13:15-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C1BAEDB.39FBB3B3@sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<6S6S7.3070$fg7.25948@newscontent-01.sprint.ca>`

```
Thomas Li wrote:
> 
> Who can tell me how often the COBOL REDEFINES clause is used in enterprise
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> I'm searching for a set of best practices as COBOL code convention/style.

Yikes, my shop probably uses REDEFINES dozens of times in each program. 
Often it's used to split apart fields (dates by MM DD YY, etc), though,
not really to save space.  (Reference modification could be used now, of
course, but often REDEFINES is more readable.)  

Which isn't to say we haven't been bitten by it at times...
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: On REDEFINES clause

- **From:** "warren.simmons" <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-12-16T23:00:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0I9T7.167773$WW.10531786@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<6S6S7.3070$fg7.25948@newscontent-01.sprint.ca> <3C1BAEDB.39FBB3B3@sprynet.com>`

```
Many of you have cussed and discussed Redefines with some good
illustrations. If you put on
your thinking caps, and recall the input record layouts of the 50's and 60's
you will recall that
they were often copies of 80 col card layouts.  With the Unit Record
Generation, all data for a
record often could NOT be reduced to one card.  Consequently, some system
(perhaps many)
use one or perhaps more columns to define the CARD NUMBER which represented
a portion
of the full record.  There was a LOT of card handling to support this
variablity of record size.
So, when converting to computers, many or most of those systems used the
card procedures, but
computer speed, etc.  It was essential that RECORDS be defined in a way to
use the CARD
layouts. My guess, today, would be that more data is captured at the source,
and may be like the
cards in those manners Or have the benifit of variable length record types
that include the very long as the very short.

Warren Simmons
```

## Thread: A case for multiple inheritence. — 6 this month / 68 total

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo)_

_Thread started [2001-06](2001-06.md#t-9f50d005a9)._

#### ↳ A Kind of Thanks To Thane Hubbel

- **From:** "Jerry Skelton" <jerry_skelton@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-13T12:58:29-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<u1hujsps87up74@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<bfdfc3e8.0106220918.6e54b419@posting.google.com>`

```
I have spent years griping about other people's software.  Recently I had
the occasion to finally break down and learn to write some programs in some
language.  Of course the language was Cobol.  I had purchased "Sams Teach
Yourself Cobol in 24 Hours" By Thane Hubbell over two years ago, and had
never made it past the first few pages.

Well, In the past week I have made it through the whole book more than once,
and some sections dozens of times, and each time I pick out some small piece
that I missed before.  I was supposed to have completed a small checkbook
project by 4:30 PM today, and I may not make it, but I can honestly say that
I have learned more this week than in years.

I believe in my limited experience that Cobol is a fantastic language, and
relatively easy to learn if you spend the time working through the examples.
Its syntax is much more sensible than even Basic.  Since the company I work
for is one of those that maintains a legacy Cobol system, I can honestly say
that I'll be seeing more of it, and getting better at it as time goes on.
And with teachers/writers as good as Thane Hubbell, I am sure it won't be as
hard as some of the things I've had to learn.

Thanks Thane.


PS...   Anyone want to write this checkbook for me?

PPS..   Yes, I am ducking deep into my foxhole, expecting the incoming
rounds of indignation.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: A Kind of Thanks To Thane Hubbel

- **From:** merhottin@aol.com (Merhottin)
- **Date:** 2001-12-14T01:11:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20011213201158.09714.00000067@mb-fm.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<u1hujsps87up74@corp.supernews.com>`

```
     Without making Thanes Head too big :)  He has bailed me out of trouble a
few times.  He has went out of his way to make my job alittle easier.  
     Thanks Thane, and happy holidays.

John
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: A Kind of Thanks To Thane Hubbel

- **From:** "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net>

```
Jerry,

You couldn't find a better person to thank.  He has helped me on more than
one occasion, especially with the Windows Shellexecute API.  I also have a
young 24 year old lady who I am trying to train as a backup person.  She
read his book (also more than once) and found it "very logical and easy to
understand".  But as far as the checkbook program....that's why Intuit wrote
Quicken :~).  I'm sure you'll find much better uses for COBOL.

Denny
```

```
Of course, to my 4 year old son, she would be an "old" 24 year old.  And to
this 58 year old programmer, she's a "young" 24 year old.  In any case, next
time I promise to just say 24 year old.  That way we can all relate to the
number in our own way.  Regardless, Thane's book has been really helpful to
her.

Happy Holidays to All.

Denny
```

```
to these messages and posted it - but I have not seen it show up.  Michael, if
you happened to have seen it - please pardon the redundancy.

It's been and will remain my pleasure to help where I can.  This particular news
group has been a fantastic resource -- I have met the best and brightest here.
I hope to continue to help and to continue to learn from everyone here.
```

### Cobol - version? decompiler ?

- **From:** "Zdenek Kavalir" <kavos@artax.karlin.mff.cuni.cz>
- **Date:** 2001-12-14T08:55:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c15485$c5838940$541a71c3@flasinet>`

```
Hi,
  I have an old MS-DOS exe file which contains text "Cobol run time library
not installed - File not found" and name of library, "COBLIB.LIB". Does
anybody know what version of Cobol it is? I'd like to do some changes in
this program so I'm looking for some decompiler for this - does exist such
a tool?

thanks very very much
Zdenek Kavalir
```

```


Zdenek Kavalir

Probably is a Microfocus )micosoft  cobol Vrs  4.0 or later
there are not a  decompile .
you must have a source code  to make changes
Carlos lages
Rio de janeiro

> Hi,
>   I have an old MS-DOS exe file which contains text "Cobol run time library
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Zdenek Kavalir
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol - version? decompiler ?

- **From:** "Joseph H. Schaefer" <jhschaefer@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-12-14T18:59:40-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C1ABC9C.54E2@sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<01c15485$c5838940$541a71c3@flasinet>`

```
Microsoft used to have a cobol compiler that came out back in the 8086 days.  A source 
program was first compiled, and then linked into a ".EXE".  There is a slightly later 
version which compiles into a ".INT', which then requires the presence of COBRUN.EXE for 
it to execute.  I do not think that a decompiler exists for either of them.

Joe

Zdenek Kavalir wrote:
> 
> Hi,
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Zdenek Kavalir
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol - version? decompiler ?

- **From:** "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-12-16T01:50:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d5TS7.17298$cf4.1669191846@newssvr16.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<01c15485$c5838940$541a71c3@flasinet> <3C1ABC9C.54E2@sympatico.ca>`

```
Microfocus COBOL version 3.4.23 has a "coblib.lib" file that is used to link
programs, but as stated elsewhere, you need the source to make changes and
recompile the program.

Denny
```

## Thread: e — 13 msgs

### e

- **From:** "Stefan Krause" <Jens.Krause007@t-online.de>
- **Date:** 2001-12-14T10:17:03+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9vcg9c$vdq$07$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
e
```

#### ↳ Re: e

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-12-15T01:15:15+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c1a9807_7@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<9vcg9c$vdq$07$1@news.t-online.com>`

```

Stefan Krause <Jens.Krause007@t-online.de> wrote in message
news:9vcg9c$vdq$07$1@news.t-online.com...
> e

ej

The object of this game is to add letters to the string (in front or
appended), WITHOUT making a word. If you make a word, you lose.

BUT, before you go adding z and/or x, it must be POSSIBLE to make a word...
and it must be an ENGLISH word, no Proper Names, although plurals are
acceptable. If the string resolves to something which doesn't look like it
is possible to make a word from, any player can "challenge" and the person
who made the last move MUST state the word he believes is possible. If he
can't (he was bluffing...<G>) then he LOSES and another game starts (or
doesn't...whatever...)

Any connection to COBOL or IT is purely coincidental and unintentional and
side bets on how long this thread stays on topic can be placed with William
Hill...just like so many threads in this forum <G>.

Enjoy!

Pete.



 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services

                http://www.usenet.com
```

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

> luejack
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.

Well not on-line references, but the lady in question did hit the hard copy
dictionaries. She wouldn't go near my keyboard, that would be tantamount  to
grounds for divorce, akin to teaching your spouse to drive <G>

Originally I was thinking of ejaculate(tion). (No you dirty-minded little boys
- check the dictionary there is a 'clean' meaning for ejaculate as well as the
'smutty' one !.)

You win - Bluejacket - I would have to put the final letter in. Amazingly never
heard the word before - refers to USN and RN. Being brought up in Portsmouth
heard 'Jack Tar' or 'matelots' but NEVER Bluejackets !.

In the Christmas spirit, another 'Irish' one, (in a book published by Irishmen,
I might add)  :-

    What do you do when an Irishman throws a pin at you ?
    Run like hell. He's got a hand grenade clenched between his teeth.

    What do you do when he throws a grenade at you ?
    Take the pin out and throw it back !

And for Benyamin, down in that troubled part of the world during Hanukah
/Christmas, (hopefully it will provide a brief smile) :-

    Michael Cleary and Abe Ginzburg were stationed at a barracks in the Dublin
area. As a punishment the drill instructor made them both double, with rifles
above their heads,  around the parade ground 20 times.

Finished and exhausted Abe pantingly says, "I hate this doubling". Replies
Michael, "Come to think of it, I'm not that keen on Tel Aviv meself".

Jimmy
```

## Thread: Problem with netexpress — 6 msgs

### Problem with netexpress

- **From:** Alain Chappuis <Alain.Chappuis@medecine.unige.ch>
- **Date:** 2001-12-14T11:14:36+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C19D10C.1050203@medecine.unige.ch>`

```
Hello,
I use the NetExpress 3.0.14 and
when I compile my program I receive this message:

Starting rebuild
Rebuilding         observe.cob
C:\Src\Observe\observe.cob(3460,74): * 804-S Source limit of
2200 lines exceeded

I dont know why I have a limit of source code.
I'm bying this product with no limit? for lines sources code!

As anybody are some explanation?

Thanks in advance
Alain.
```

#### ↳ Re: Problem with netexpress

- **From:** jean.villemaire@microfocus.com (Jean Villemaire)
- **Date:** 2001-12-14T06:44:00-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b9b30ab.0112140644.3c0746f4@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3C19D10C.1050203@medecine.unige.ch>`

```
Alain Chappuis <Alain.Chappuis@medecine.unige.ch> wrote in message news:<3C19D10C.1050203@medecine.unige.ch>...
> Hello,
> I use the NetExpress 3.0.14 and
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> +----------------------+----------------------------------------+

Alain

If you are using the Net Express University Edition product you are
limited to a maximum number of lines per program.  Net Express UE is a
low-cost compiler for Academic purposes only and therefore impose a
limit on the size of a program.  If you wish to exceed this limit then
you should upgrade to the production version of Net Express v3.1.

Jean Villemaire
Micro Focus
```

```
Our leading Insurance Industry client in beautiful Madison, WI is in need of
two Sr. IT Professional as outlined below. If you are available and interested
in applying for or learning more about these exciting permanent opportunity,
please reply to this mail with your current MS-Word format resume and
detailed contact information, or feel free to contact us for more
information.  Our client is offering a strong advancement program, full
benefits, bonuses, and various other incentives.

We are also offering a $1K finders fee for those who are able to identify a
candidate who is selected by our client.

#1
Job Title: Senior Applications Engineer
Location:  Madison, WI
Job Classification:  Full-Time
Job Description:
Designs, develops, and maintains applications that are moderate to complex,
and/or critical. Will have an increasing level of business knowledge.
Analyzes problems and recommends alternatives for resolution. May take on a
lead role in the development of applications or parts of an application.
These projects will be small to medium in size. Provides guidance and
technical leadership to less experienced technologists. May work under
guidance of senior technologists on larger or complex projects. May work in
a mainframe and/or web environment.

The ideal candidate should have project experience building large-scale
applications using Java with IBM WebSphere, as well as Cobol, CICS and some
experience with DB2. Insurance industry experience would be a plus.

Primary Qualifications:

Familiarity with major tasks, processes and deliverables associated with
developing or enhancing business applications - e.g., requirements
definition, design, programming, testing, implementation and training
Knowledge of activities, tasks, practices and deliverables associated with
writing and modifying programs that comprise an application system.

Knowledge of activities, tasks, tools and techniques for testing
applications to ensure adherence to functional and quality requirements.

Knowledge of activities, tasks, practices, deliverables and techniques for
implementing new or enhanced applications into a production environment.

Ability to define, implement changes, and support a production application.

Familiarity with a variety of specific tools and toolkits for development
and support of applications. Knowledge of using Java with IBM WebSphere, as
well as Cobol, CICS and DB2.

Ability to establish, use and support integration and communications among
applications, databases and technology platforms.

Knowledge and ability to define and implement specific technical foundation
for an application - software, databases, hardware and telecommunications
platforms, security, etc.
```

### CICS Web Server programming.

- **From:** zalekbloom@hotmail.com (Zalek Bloom)
- **Date:** 2001-12-14T14:05:18-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e84e5f2f.0112141405.6997dd9b@posting.google.com>`

```
I am trying to write a small program for CICS Web Server (called also
CICS Web Interface). I wrote a command:

EXEC CICS EXTRACT TCPIP                  
     CLIENTNAME   (WS-CLIENT-NAME)       
     CLIENTADDR   (WS-CLIENT-ADDRESS)    
END-EXEC                                 

but a CICS precompiler gives me a message:
EXTRACT FUNCTION NOT COMPLETELY SPECIFIED. ATTACH OR TCT OR PROCESS OR
LOGONMSG OR ATTRIBUTES OR EXIT MUST BE SPECIFIED. COMMAND NOT
TRANSLATED.
I checked the syntax of EXTRACT TCPIP command, and there are no
options like ATTACH ot TCT.
Any idea what is wrong with this code?

Thanks,

Zalek
```
