## Thread: CICS internals on AIX — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### CICS internals on AIX

- **From:** cNOSPAMtimmerman@acsalaska.com (Curt Timmerman)
- **Date:** 2001-12-14T22:47:44
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Xns91778C5C4BAE6ctimmermanacsalaskac@10.136.100.32>`

```

I am writing an application on an AIX box that will allow me to test non-
map CICS applications in a stand alone mode. In the code following (.cbl 
generated from .cpp) does anyone have any information on the format/usage 
of the CICS-ARG-MASK?

      *     EXEC CICS
      *         WRITEQ TS
      *         QUEUE (0-TS-QUEUE)
      *         FROM (21-ADDRESS-CURSOR-RECORD)
      *         RESP (0-RESP)
      *         RESP2 (0-RESP2)
      *     END-EXEC
           MOVE 0-TS-QUEUE TO CICS-DATA-VALUE(10)
           SET CICS-DATA-AREA(5) TO ADDRESS OF 21-ADDRESS-CURSOR-RECORD
           MOVE LENGTH OF 21-ADDRESS-CURSOR-RECORD TO
                CICS-SHORT-VALUE(3)
           MOVE 536872468 TO CICS-ARG-MASK
           MOVE 107 TO CICS-FN-CODE
           MOVE 434 TO CICS-DEBUG-LINE
           >>CALLINT SYSTEM
           CALL 'CICSAPIE' USING CICS-ARGS
           >>CALLINT

Thanks in advance,
Curt
```

<a id="t-5769d584a3"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu & Install Shield — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu & Install Shield

- **From:** cobolnotdead@hotmail.com (Kate Parker)
- **Date:** 2001-12-14T15:08:58-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<49973e5b.0112141508.4c6a25ef@posting.google.com>`

```
A year ago, some of you guys asked about getting around the
requirement of using the Fujitsu's installation wrapper utility.

I was wondering if anyone has had any success yet creating an install
using anything other than the installation wrapper provided by
Fujitsu.  I need to deploy the runtime dependent files for Fujitsu
Cobol v6, Powercobol & Powerform in the same directory as my
application (in lieu of the default C:\Program Files\Common
Files\Fujitsu\COBOL\).  One of my big reasons for doing this is to get
rid of that troublesome PATH setting to AUTOEXEC.BAT.

I used a program called InstallWatch Pro v2.5c to identify the large
number of registry entries and dependent files at the point of the
runtime installations.  I copied the dependent files to my application
directory and created a .REG file that included all of the added
registry items and correct path locations.

After working on this for a couple of days, I'm still getting the
error 'RUN-TIME SYSTEM NOT INSTALLED PROPERLY.'

Any input would be greatly appreciated.
Thanks,
Kate
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu & Install Shield

- **From:** leclaire <leclaire@rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-21T14:33:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C23479E.548A1075@rr.com>`
- **References:** `<49973e5b.0112141508.4c6a25ef@posting.google.com>`

```
I ran into the same problem with the PATH, and came up with a somewhat
dirty but completely effective solution using a shareware utility named
"REGSRCH" (Registry search & replace).

After installing the app I run REGSRCH and replace that outrageously
long Common Files path with "C:\Fujitsu", then move all the files there
from Fujitsu's program files subfolder and edit the PATH in
Autoexec.bat.  
REGSRCH lets you save script files, so once I got the procedure working
properly I just saved the necessary script and utility files in the
Extra's folder with the Fuji installer.

Only problem is that the install becomes too complicated for any user,
no matter how well you document it.  The Regsrch utility is well worth
the $20 registration fee.

Personally I feel that forcing those incredibly long pathnames into the
autoexec is a dirty trick and poor programming on the part of software
vendors.  It becomes an even greater problem when there are several apps
installed that do the same thing - PC Anywhere and Pervasive SQL to name
a few.

You can (or should) also modify the config.sys to increase the
environment size, and someone else here once stated that a longer path
can be accomplished by specifying the PATH there also, though I've not
tried that myself.
```

<a id="t-94268eddf1"></a>
## Thread: OT : Humour — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT : Humour

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-12-15T03:40:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C1AC632.DA22A7FA@shaw.ca>`

```
Well bejezuz, 'tis near Chris'mas :-

The Iranians had one hell of a conflagration in their oil patch. Really
wanted to call in Red Adair, but as they weren't too friendly with the
Yanks, as a substitute contacted his Irish counterpart Michael O'Dare.

Michael duly took the details, and quoted them a price of $50,000. "When
can you arrive ?". "O'ill be there in four hours", says our Michael.

The Iranians waited nervously at the quickly prepared air strip near the
raging fire, and sure enough in comes a huge green transport plane,
lands and immediately a green fire engine zooms out 'o the back To their
utter astonishment the fire engine heads directly into the centre of the
inferno. Out jump six firefighters in green coveralls and start beating
at the flames with bits of potato hessian sacks. Incredibly in some five
hours they had the fire beat !

Michael duly accepts the cheque for $50,000. When they are safely
emplaned and  en route to the Emerald Isle, looking at the cheque,
Michael clenches his teeth and says, "Holy muther of God. Well me boyos,
the first thing we do when we'se get home is have the bloody brakes
fixed on the fire engine !".
```

<a id="t-019656ba51"></a>
## Thread: syntax.vim for cobol (Micro Focus) — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### syntax.vim for cobol (Micro Focus)

- **From:** Michael McGuire <reststop@mrmsoft.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-15T12:26:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C1B3FFF.5000209@mrmsoft.com>`

```
Does anyone have a simpler syntax.vim file for cobol? The copy 
distributed with my vim is too busy. I'd like to find something that 
comes close to the NetExpress highlighting.
```

```
I am trying to write a small COBOL program for CICS Web Server (called also
CICS Web Interface). I wrote a command:

EXEC CICS EXTRACT TCPIP                  
     CLIENTNAME   (WS-CLIENT-NAME)       
     CLIENTADDR   (WS-CLIENT-ADDRESS)

but a CICS precompiler gives me a message:
EXTRACT FUNCTION NOT COMPLETELY SPECIFIED. ATTACH OR TCT OR PROCESS OR
LOGONMSG OR ATTRIBUTES OR EXIT MUST BE SPECIFIED. COMMAND NOT
TRANSLATED.
I checked the syntax of EXTRACT TCPIP command, and there are no
options like ATTACH ot TCT.

Thanks,

Zalek
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CICS Web Server programming.

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-20T21:58:36+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<r0k42u0c4apqgb7hv0l6p117pbud1ip0jv@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<njjm1u46s6c8gnssppp1r68erusdpon8si@4ax.com> <4q432uc9lnltc05vfv3u4cic0jcgju2ire@4ax.com> <e84e5f2f.0112200831.7aaa078f@posting.google.com>`

```

Zalek,

the problem is here:


>we are running CICS/ESA COMMAND LANGUAGE TRANSLATOR VERSION 4.1), 

The translator you are using is for CICS 4.1, and is inappropriate for
CICS 5.3 (aka CICS TS 1.3)

Make sure that you are using the correct translator, and everything
works out okay

(Send me a listing of the expanded JCL, and I might be able to tell
you what to change.  I guess you are using an old canned JCL stream
which is no longer up-to-date)


No special steps are required for the EXEC CICS EXTRACT TCPIP command.
You just need to use the correct translator (See the STEPLIB in your
JCL)

                                                            
     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_
```

<a id="t-fa284cb1b7"></a>
## Thread: String Search in COBOL? — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### String Search in COBOL?

- **From:** "Fritz Sonnichsen" <sonnichs@berkshire.net>
- **Date:** 2001-12-15T21:29:03-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BCTS7.108382$lV4.16918958@e420r-atl1.usenetserver.com>`

```
It has been about 20 years since I wrote a lot of COBOL code, and I wondered
is someone can help here. I am using an IBM VSII compiler to write some
simple code, and I want to read a record, and make a conditional decision
based upon whether or not the record-buffer contains a certain substring.
  I have successfully written the code using 3  loops and 3 indexes, one
pointing to the character in the substring, one pointing to the character in
the record and one pointing to a starting point in the record, which 'walks'
across the whole record to scan it.
   Of course this works fine, but it seems somewhat verbose and inefficient.
I looked at the INSPECT and SEARCH commands, etc, but didn't really see any
simpler way.
    Did I overdo it here or is this the common acceptable technique for this
simple operation?
```

#### ↳ Re: String Search in COBOL?

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-12-16T15:59:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C1CC4D0.936164E8@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<BCTS7.108382$lV4.16918958@e420r-atl1.usenetserver.com>`

```
Fritz Sonnichsen wrote:
> 
> It has been about 20 years since I wrote a lot of COBOL code, and I wondered
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> simpler way.


	MOVE ZERO      TO Substring-Count
        INSPECT Data-Record
            TALLYING Substring-Count FOR ALL "XYZ"
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: String Search in COBOL?

- **From:** "ThatGuy" <thatguy@thatguy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-12-16T13:34:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xp1T7.17956$Z24.3571633@typhoon.jacksonville.mediaone.net>`
- **References:** `<BCTS7.108382$lV4.16918958@e420r-atl1.usenetserver.com> <3C1CC4D0.936164E8@Azonic.co.nz>`

```

"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3C1CC4D0.936164E8@Azonic.co.nz...
> Fritz Sonnichsen wrote:
> >
> > It has been about 20 years since I wrote a lot of COBOL code, and I
wondered
> > is someone can help here. I am using an IBM VSII compiler to write some
> > simple code, and I want to read a record, and make a conditional
decision
> > based upon whether or not the record-buffer contains a certain
substring.
> >   I have successfully written the code using 3  loops and 3 indexes, one
> > pointing to the character in the substring, one pointing to the
character in
> > the record and one pointing to a starting point in the record, which
'walks'
> > across the whole record to scan it.
> >    Of course this works fine, but it seems somewhat verbose and
inefficient.
> > I looked at the INSPECT and SEARCH commands, etc, but didn't really see
any
> > simpler way.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>             TALLYING Substring-Count FOR ALL "XYZ"

Or you could try this:

MOVE SPACES TO WS-VAR-1 WS-VAR-2.
UNSTRING (Record)
  DELIMITED BY (Substring)
  INTO
    WS-VAR-1
    WS-VAR-2.

IF WS-VAR-2 NOT = SPACES  (You could 88-level this if you prefer)
  DO WHATEVER.

This only works as long as the substring you are searching for is not the
last part of the record searched.  It will also parse the record for you
which comes in handy in certain situations (splitting names separated by ' &
',  ' AND ', etc.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Search in COBOL?

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-12-17T04:06:39+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c1dbef5_9@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<BCTS7.108382$lV4.16918958@e420r-atl1.usenetserver.com> <3C1CC4D0.936164E8@Azonic.co.nz> <xp1T7.17956$Z24.3571633@typhoon.jacksonville.mediaone.net>`

```
ThatGuy <thatguy@thatguy.net> wrote in message
news:xp1T7.17956$Z24.3571633@typhoon.jacksonville.mediaone.net...
>
> "Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> which comes in handy in certain situations (splitting names separated by '
&
> ',  ' AND ', etc.)
>
>

There is no comparison between doing this and the code Richard submitted.
(Of course, I WOULD say that because my code would have been identical to
Richard's...<G>).

If all you want to know is whether the record contains a given substring,
INSPECT is more appropriate than UNSTRING.

Why?

Because the UNSTRING  incurs the additional overhead of moving every
character to storage, whereas the INSPECT just looks... (and optionally
counts).

Note that BOTH these solutions will keep on scanning even after the target
string has been found.

To avoid this you COULD code:

MOVE ZERO      TO Substring-Count
         INSPECT Data-Record
            TALLYING Substring-Count FOR CHARACTERS
                                                          BEFORE "XYZ"

The disadvantage here is that you must then check the count against the
known length of the buffer in order to determine whether the target was
found...

IF Substring-Count = Buffer-Length   *> we have scanned the whole buffer
     SET NOT-found to TRUE
ELSE
     SET found-it to TRUE
END-IF

If the sub-string occurs consistently in an "early" part of the record, this
arguably less elegant solution would certainly be more efficient than either
of the above.

But I still wouldn't do it (It causes an "add" for every character looked at
before the target...this is nearly as bad as the "move" incurred with
UNSTRING, but it stops when the target is found)

Pete.




 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

- **Date:** 2001-12-17T03:41:21+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c1cb62d$1_7@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<BCTS7.108382$lV4.16918958@e420r-atl1.usenetserver.com> <3C1CC4D0.936164E8@Azonic.co.nz>`

```

>         INSPECT Data-Record

Absolutely. (Nothing missed here...<G>).
```

#### ↳ Re: String Search in COBOL?

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-12-17T15:47:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9vl3tp$l6q$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<BCTS7.108382$lV4.16918958@e420r-atl1.usenetserver.com>`

```

On 15-Dec-2001, "Fritz Sonnichsen" <sonnichs@berkshire.net> wrote:

> It has been about 20 years since I wrote a lot of COBOL code, and I
> wondered
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> simple operation?

If you don't want to use the INSPECT command, you might want to use
reference modification in your loop.  Since you last coded,  reference
modification has made this job easier:

A few new features of the language are illustrated below, but you should be
able to infer how they work:

COMPUTE STRING-LENGTH = FUNCTION LENGTH (MY-STRING)
COMPUTE START-POSITION = BUFFER-LENGTH - STRING-LENGTH.
PERFORM VARYING START-POINT FROM START-POSITION BY -1
                      UNTIL START-POINT < 1
                           OR SW-FOUND-ONE
       COMPUTE END-POSITION = START-POSITION + STRING-LENGTH
       IF MY-BUFFER (START-POSITION:END-POSITION) = MY-STRING
              SET SW-FOUND-ONE TO TRUE
       END-IF
END-PERFORM.
```

```
parties (Medicare FIs) ready to test OCTOBER 2001 (later backed off by HCFA,

I started early and got ready.

(Shameless plug for myself and others): Expert Consulting available for all
facets of HIPAA-compliance, for both the data-format changes (I do this) and

year.....
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: HIPAA

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompsonNOSPAM@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-18T20:21:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C1FA558.CB62A024@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<CiHT7.161$Hz.104713@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <20011218123705.14901.00000664@mb-fp.aol.com>`

```
S Comstock wrote:
<snip>
> What the heck are you talking about? What is HIPAA and why do I care?
> 
<snip>

HIPAA is a set of "laws" that give specifications for the
handling of data by insurance companies, hospitals and other
health care providers (even equipment vendors). I'm just in
the process of coming up to speed on this myself (I know
enough to be truly dangerous and not enough to know how to
explain what I do know).

The why you would care has to do with whether or not some
software you deal with or work on is HIPAA compliant and/or
is required to be HIPAA compliant. The other thing has to do
with how long you keep backups, what has to be archived and
for how long, physical security of data, what part of the
data is confidential and what part is not, etc.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: HIPAA

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-12-18T22:57:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zRPT7.546$Bv1.148123@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<CiHT7.161$Hz.104713@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <20011218123705.14901.00000664@mb-fp.aol.com> <3C1FA558.CB62A024@ix.netcom.com>`

```
Steve Thompson <sthompsonNOSPAM@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:3C1FA558.CB62A024@ix.netcom.com...
> S Comstock wrote:
> <snip>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> data is confidential and what part is not, etc.

Good explanation.

One modest correction: software is not and can not be certified
"HIPAA-compliant."

Only "covered entities" (health plans, health care providers and health care
clearinghouses) may be "HIPAA-compliant."

Definitions in Code of Federal Regulations # 45, Part 160, 162 August 2000.
There is a free PDF file you can get from one of the HIPAA-related web
sites; maybe it was the Washington Publishing Company site?

MCM
```

```
aka Son of Y2K for HMOs.
```

## Thread: Future drafts (and final copies) of next Standard - restricted access — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### Future drafts (and final copies) of next Standard - restricted access

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-18T17:38:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OaLT7.14105$mF.1471628@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
From a recent J4 mailing (with EDITING as required by content),

"I've placed the draft on the <link-URL removed> with a link that states for
MEMBERS ONLY.

If someone clicks on the link the user is requested to enter in a username
(<user-name removed>) and password (<password removed>).

ISO/IEC and JTC 1 have mandated that ANY document beyond FCD stage is
copywrite protected and may not be made freely available via the web.  As
participants of the US TAG - the J4 members may obtain copies for committee
review (hence it is now in a realmed area and the pw is only for members).

At such time that the formal fdis ballot is issued and there is a formal
public review -- we will work with ANSI to ensure that the draft is made
available for purchase."

  ***

Please send comments on this issue (if any) as specified below (from the
last "public" FCD)

"This ISO document is a draft International Standard and is
copyright-protected by ISO. Except as permitted under the applicable laws of
the user's country, neither this ISO draft nor any extract from it may be
reproduced, stored in a retrieval system or transmitted in any form or by
any means, electronic, photocopying, recording or otherwise, without prior
written permission being secured.

Requests for permission to reproduce should be addressed to ISO at the
address below or ISO's member body in the country of the requester:

    Copyright Manager
    ISO Central Secretariat
    1 rue de Varemb�
    1211 Geneva 20 Switzerland

        tel: +41 22 749 0111
        fax +41 22 734 0179

    e-mail central@iso.ch

Reproduction may be subject to royalty payments or a licensing agreement.

Violators may be prosecuted."

  ***

Final note, the current (restricted) draft still includes the following
wording on page xxi (and I think this should be pointed out in any
conversations with ANSI or ISO about "reproducing" this version),

"Acknowledgment

Any organization interested in reproducing the COBOL standard and
specifications in whole or in part, using ideas from this document as the
basis for an instruction manual or for any other purpose, is free to do so.
However, all such organizations are requested to reproduce the following
acknowledgment paragraphs in their entirety as part of the preface to any
such publication:

COBOL is an industry language and is not the property of any company or
group of companies, or of any organization or group of organizations.

No warranty, expressed or implied, is made by any contributor or by the
CODASYL COBOL Committee as to
the accuracy and functioning of the programming system and language.
Moreover, no responsibility is assumed by any contributor, or by the
committee, in connection therewith.

    The authors and copyright holders of the copyrighted materials used
herein

        FLOW-MATIC (trademark of Sperry Rand Corporation), Programming for
the UNIVAC (R) I and II, Data
Automation Systems copyrighted 1958, 1959, by Sperry Rand Corporation; IBM
Commercial Translator Form No. F28-8013, copyrighted 1959 by IBM; FACT, DSI
27A5260-2760, copyrighted 1960 by Minneapolis-Honeywell

    have specifically authorized the use of this material, in whole or in
part, in the COBOL specifications. Such authorization extends to the
reproduction and use of COBOL specifications in programming manuals or
similar
publications."
```

#### ↳ Re: Future drafts (and final copies) of next Standard - restricted access

- **From:** stephenjspiro@mail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-12-18T15:52:32-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<928495c6.0112181552.65f7cd0f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<OaLT7.14105$mF.1471628@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Thank you, Bill, that was fast action.

When I got the same message, I sent the following to NCITS and the
appropriate mailing lists:

---------------------------------------------
Ever since this issue of ISO/IEC "copyright" of new standards came up,
the J4 committee has resisted.  Thus far, we have always gotten drafts
available to the public, but now  the only copy is password protected.
If this restriction is not removed promptly, I intend to file criminal
charges of Theft of Intellectual Property with the United States
Department of Justice against ISO/IEC and NCITS.
This charge will allege:
1. Parts of the standard are based on already copyrighted material, as
indicated in the draft standard, itself.  This copyrighted material
has been made available to the industry. Without releases from these
copyright holders, an attempt to copyright the draft standard
infringes on these existing copyrights.
2. I, and many (all?) other current and previous members of the J4
Committee have contributed our work product with the understanding
that it was for the benefit of our profession, not for the
remuneration of the standards bodies.  Not only did we not receive
payment, but in fact we paid dues and meeting fees and documentation
fees for the "privelege" of being members.  In fact, we paid these
dues and fees, and contributed our time and expertise,  as our
contribution to the well being of our profession.  The attempt to
copyright the draft standard, and to restrict access to it from the
general public, is theft of our work product, which is intellectual
property as defined by the statute and applicable treaties.
3. The J4 Committee has never acquiesced to the plans to copyright the
COBOL standard.  We have proceeded on the hope (understanding?) that
"things would be worked out".  I, myself, have repeatedly stated my
intention to file these charges if "things did not work out".

I hope that my fellow members of the committee, both individually and
corporately, as well as former members and the general public, will
join me in this action.

Stephen J Spiro
President
Wizard Systems
Box 4451 - Brainy Boro Station
Metuchen, NJ 08840
USA
(732) 549-8965
------------------------------
```

## Thread: Come funziona MOVE? — 2 msgs

### Come funziona MOVE?

- **From:** "Giuseppe Guagliardo" <g_guagliardo@inwind.it>
- **Date:** 2001-12-18T20:34:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<JLNT7.6448$xw2.159261@twister2.libero.it>`

```
Non sono un programmatore COBOL ma ho la necessit� di modificare un
programma per un amico, in pratica devo solo fare in modo che accetti 2
cifre decimali nei campi prezzi e totali, andava tutto bene sino a quando
non ho incontrato delle istruzioni MOVE per le quali non ho trovato
spiegazioni.

Le istruzioni sono del tipo:

MOVE AC-IMPOR TO MB-IMPOR(Y).

MOVE MB-IMPOR(Y) TO IMP.

MOVE MB-IMPOR(FR)  TO OC-IMPOR(FR).

MOVE ZERO TO MB-QTA(I) MB-IMPOR(I).

Quello che vorrei sapere � come funzionano le indicazioni tra parentesi,
cosa significano quelle lettere e quali altre opzioni sono disponibili?

Spero che qualcuno mi aiuti!

Giuseppe Guagliardo
```

#### ↳ Re: Come funziona MOVE?

- **From:** "Gianni Spano" <softline2000@tin.it>
- **Date:** 2001-12-20T22:29:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7cf59ff03db1883d5ae4f911456aac3a.40368@mygate.mailgate.org>`
- **References:** `<JLNT7.6448$xw2.159261@twister2.libero.it>`

```
Ciao Giuseppe

La MOVE ï¿½ un istruzione che dice "sposta un valore da questo campo (di
partenza) a
questo campo (di arrivo).. per dirla molto spartanamente..

I valori che tu vedi tra parentesi sono un riferimento che ti informa che 
il campo ricevente ï¿½ una tabella o array di elementi indicizzati dal valore che
ï¿½
contenuto nel campo rappresentato tra le parentesi stesse..
In pratica, se conosci Visual Basic ï¿½ la stessa cosa...

se io scrivo

MOVE "CIAO"  TO MESSAGGIO (I) e il campo I ha un valore = 3

sto muovendo (assegnando) al TERZO elemento della tabella a cui appartiene
MESSAGGIO
il valore "CIAO"..

Non so se sono stato chiaro, ma spero di averti dato una semplice risposta
al tuo quesito

Gianni
```

<a id="t-752b223cf0"></a>
## Thread: *** BUSY Day - for the draft COBOL Standard — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### *** BUSY Day - for the draft COBOL Standard

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-19T04:34:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<TNUT7.725$Cw3.79099@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
As a result of the visibility of my concerns and those of another J4 member,
the latest draft of the COBOL Standard is NOW "freely available".  The PDF
version (zipped) may now be downloaded from a link on:

 http://www.ncits.org/tc_home/j4.htm

PLEASE NOTE WELL:
  This version IS intended for J4 "internal use" - in that it is NOT subject
to any "public review" (or public review period).  The FCD input period is
FINISHED (both in the US and internationally) and J4 is just "finishing" up
corrections that resulted from it.  The output of the February 2002 J4
meeting (and an "editorial review session") is expected to be the "input"
document to the next review period - which will be an FDIS (final draft
international standard).  That document will go out to an "up or down - not
for technical change" review by the countries involved in the ISO SC22
standards process.

I am UNABLE to tell you at this time, what may or may not happen with the
copyright vs "freely available" issue when (come NEXT December) this becomes
an approved Standard (as I expect it will be).

If you do download this draft and have comments/issues regarding the
"copyright statement" - then I recommend that you still contact the ISO
person/group listed within that document.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: *** BUSY Day - for the draft COBOL Standard

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-19T22:16:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gl8U7.2151$Cw3.256458@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<TNUT7.725$Cw3.79099@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <G18U7.9117$NM4.3056350@rwcrnsc53>`

```
The "1989" in the ISO COBOL Standard (current and NEXT one) is NOT a year,
but is just an ISO "identification" number - like the Unicode Standard it
"10646".  The ANSI numbers relate to X3.23 (with some additional stuff) -
but the ISO stuff all refers to "1989"

This causes REPEATED confusion (even among J4 and WG4 members).
```

```
a exclusive hold on a dataset. The exclusive hold is issued from a set
of calls from a Cobol/Assembler programs, but not coded in JCL with

whose using (browsing or sharing) the dataset currently ? Is there a
specific TSO command or Rexx I can run ?

Thanks in  advance.
JayaRam.
```

#### ↳ Re: How do I know whose using a dataset on OS/390 ?

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompsonNOSPAM@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-20T13:52:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C21ED23.E478955F@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<5cf7edb9.0112190608.5727e518@posting.google.com>`

```
JayaRam wrote:
> 
> This question pertains to OS/390 TSO. I was running a Job which wants
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> specific TSO command or Rexx I can run ?
<snip>

It occurred to me, after reading several answering posts,
that you may actually be asking a different question (or
need to).

How did your job get ABENDed? 

Does the computing environment you are running in handle
data set enqueuing for JOBs (by recognizing that JOB ???? on
a given system will conflict with another "task" on a second
system) and control JOB initiation? (I guess I'm asking if
you are in a JES3 environment?)

Let us assume that you are in a JES3 environment. If you
know in advance the name of the DSN to be dynamically
allocated, you can do the following in your JCL and stop the
enqueuing problem(s):


//xxxx JOB (whatever),your-id,.....
    .
    .
//STEP1  EXEC PGM=IEFBR14,COND=(0,GT)   <<<=== this step
will not get executed
//DD1    DD   DSN= <<<===dynamically allocated DSN 
//            DISP=   <<<=== see comments below
//DD2 .......
//* End of fake 'em out step

OK, what does this accomplish? Well, in a JES3 environment
it causes JES3 to see the DSN(s) in question for job
scheduling purposes.

AH, but if the DSN does not exist, you will fail during JCL
Parse/Scan under JES3, UNLESS you code the DD1 to give dummy
allocation information. Remember, this step will not
execute, so even if you are going to run IDCAMS and create a
VSAM D/S, there will be no conflict. The system cannot
assume this step won't execute, so during
converter/interpreter (JES3) the DSN is checked for
existence...

The idea here is to get the system to check your DISP
information and prevent what could be (or become) a deadly
embrace (two jobs running and they need the same data sets
but they ask for them in the reverse order...).

Why would I give you this idea as a solution to your
problem?  Well, as a software developer, in order to get
things to work in a JES3 environment, we had to find a way
around JES3 looking at JCL and seeing a non-creative DISP
(e.g., OLD, SHR, etc.) and failing the JOB because the DSN
does not exist (JES3 doesn't realize that the IDCAMS step(s)
may be creating the DSN). This little trick then allowed us
to submit jobs wherein we knew we would be touching certain
DSNs dynamically, BUT there is no reference in the JCL,
which can lead to the DEADLY EMBRACE problem. [And it
allowed us to write installation code without regard to JES2
or JES3 environments because this technique works for both.]

So, the non-executing STEP referencing the DSN the way we
will be using it (DISP=SHR if exists AND we won't need it
exclusive, DISP=MOD if it does/doesn't exist and we will
either create it or otherwise need it EXCLUSIVE) causes the
JOB to be handled automatically with out the submitter
having to manually check on the status of n DSNs.
```

#### ↳ Re: How do I know whose using a dataset on OS/390 ?

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-20T08:50:17-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<88g22ugeebm32kbn35q7vqb7dqnvpj11j8@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<5cf7edb9.0112190608.5727e518@posting.google.com>`

```
On 19 Dec 2001 06:08:27 -0800 jtummala@lucent.com (JayaRam) wrote:

:>This question pertains to OS/390 TSO. I was running a Job which wants
:>a exclusive hold on a dataset. The exclusive hold is issued from a set
:>of calls from a Cobol/Assembler programs, but not coded in JCL with
:>Disp=OLD. My job got abended becos of share problem. How do I know
:>whose using (browsing or sharing) the dataset currently ? Is there a
:>specific TSO command or Rexx I can run ?

There will be an ENQ outstanding with SYSDSN,dsname

You can issue GQSCAN's to get the information. There are examples on the CBT
tape.
```

<a id="t-a6897b497e"></a>
## Thread: Visual Age cobol calling Micro Focus cobol — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Visual Age cobol calling Micro Focus cobol

- **From:** rich.mchie@indianapolislife.com (Rich McHie)
- **Date:** 2001-12-19T07:28:50-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10b5e700.0112190728.4a3ab829@posting.google.com>`

```
We want to have a Visual Age cobol program call a Micro Focus cobol
DLL.  When the Visual Age cobol program is compiled and linked, it
wants to call the entry point in the Micro Focus program with an
underscore on the front (eg. CALL MFPROG in the Visual Age program
looks for "_MFPROG" in the Micro Focus DLL).  So we're trying to
compile and link the Micro Focus program so the DLL has an underscore
in the entry point.  We thought we could modify the EXPORTS statement
in the DEF file (used into the Micro Focus link step) to add the
underscore; but we can't figure out how to feed this new DEF file into
just the link step - when we try to only link, it compiles and links
and recreates a DEF file with our change wiped out.  In the Micro
Focus source code we've tried different combinations of
CALL-CONVENTIONS (74 MOSTLY).  Does anyone have ideas or a sample that
would help?  Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: Visual Age cobol calling Micro Focus cobol

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-19T22:51:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BR8U7.2280$Cw3.263280@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<10b5e700.0112190728.4a3ab829@posting.google.com>`

```
Have you tried compiling the Micro Focus COBOL program with the PROGRAM-ID
in quotes - and with the underscore in it, e.g.

  Program-Id.  "_TestThis".

I won't guarantee it, but I think that stands a chance of working.  (There
are also issues with the MAPNAME directive, but it has been too long for me
to be certain what setting would be best for that.)
```

#### ↳ Re: Visual Age cobol calling Micro Focus cobol

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gael.wilson@nospam.microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-20T10:54:31
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9vsfm1$v1s$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com>`
- **References:** `<10b5e700.0112190728.4a3ab829@posting.google.com>`

```
Rich,

If you are using the CBLLINK utility, all you need to do is specify the .DEF
file on the command line and it will pick it up automatically. Similarly, if
you are using the NetExpress development environment go into Project ->
Build Settings -> Link -> Advanced and add the .DEF file to the additional
CBLLINK directives and it should pick up your own .DEF file okay.


"Rich McHie" <rich.mchie@indianapolislife.com> wrote in message
news:10b5e700.0112190728.4a3ab829@posting.google.com...
> We want to have a Visual Age cobol program call a Micro Focus cobol
> DLL.  When the Visual Age cobol program is compiled and linked, it
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> would help?  Thanks.
```

```
Two programs were posted.  Which one are you trying?  The one I posted was
tested at work on z/OS.  I know our SWA is above the line because I had an
earlier version that was broken until I added a SWAREQ routine based upon
Gilbert St.-Flours REXX exec.

It is always possible that some control block offsets may be different on
your system.  The program I posted lists the assembler macros that I looked
at to determine my offsets.  If you can read assembler I can send you the
JCL and source to generate the DSECTS or you could extract the comments
from the posted program an with a little editing you could soon create your
own.  Contact me directly if you want
```

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2001-12-20T00:51:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c188f0$63a3eae0$b234e641@chottel>`
- **References:** `<vGPK7.2954$5R2.234981263@news1.van.metronet.ca>`

```
I know this is very late, but I just finished reading "Elements of Web
Programming with Micro Focus Net Express" by Wilson Price.  Th last chapter
has examples of embedded sql and shows how to set up the ODBC driver.  It
seemed pretty straightforward.

Dennis Edward <temp081100@ipipeline.net> wrote in article
<vGPK7.2954$5R2.234981263@news1.van.metronet.ca>...
> We just bought Acucobol's AcuSQL. It's embedded SQL, which is supposed to
be
> a standard, I guess. In any case, Acucorp doesn't include any tutorial
> material beyond a couple of very trivial sample programs. Are there any
good
> books or other resources for learning the ins and outs of embedded sql?
> 
> 
> 
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Embedded SQL tutorials?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-12-21T05:08:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C22C44B.35791245@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<vGPK7.2954$5R2.234981263@news1.van.metronet.ca> <01c188f0$63a3eae0$b234e641@chottel>`

```


Charles Hottel wrote:

> I know this is very late, but I just finished reading "Elements of Web
> Programming with Micro Focus Net Express" by Wilson Price.  Th last chapter
> has examples of embedded sql and shows how to set up the ODBC driver.  It
> seemed pretty straightforward.

True that Will does cover the topic, but only in a general tutorial sense,
aligned with the main topic, Web Programming. He doesn't cover features like
'Normalization' etc. And Will's text is backed up by the Net Express Open SQL
Assistant module which  allows you to select type of Query, generates the bare
bones, then allows you to include columns from your DB tables, query conditions
and then generates the complete  syntax between EXEC SQL ....... and.......
END-EXEC, plus picking up from your DB Table, it automatically generates a
copyfile in three parts (1) Table References INT, VARCHAR etc., (2) COBOL pic
x's and pic 9's, plus (3) NULL indicators.

All of this can be copied into your current program source. Add CONNECT etc to
the OpenSQL  'construction boxes' and you can do a test run against your DB and
view the results, without any compiling of COBOL source. It does an excellent
job, but is very specific to Net Express. (The only very MINOR criticism is
that what it generates is 'wordy; it uses Aliases and prefixes column IDs with
the Table Name - but a feature necessary when linking multi-tables with
non-unique column IDs. For a 'general' package, the verboseness is acceptable).

To Dennis Edward who was after 'tutorials' do a search on SQL using google.com
or Web Ferret. Two immediate sources :-

- http://www.fluffycat.com/sql/ - you'll see the author is nuts about movies
<G>

- http://www.highcroft.com/highcroft/sql_intro.pdf

this latter one is a good lead in for what you are after and has a whole bunch
of links to other SQL sites.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

<a id="t-f96d310a10"></a>
## Thread: Public COBOL — 1 msgs

### Public COBOL

- **From:** "warren.simmons" <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-12-20T02:54:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MpcU7.173352$WW.10899168@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
To all of you involved in the International Standards of COBOL effort

The recent scramble to keep the spec public touched my heart.

Thanks to all of you.

This same kind of thing happened with the first ANSI Standard spec. At that
time
the logic of ANSI was (as I remember it) unless they owned the content of
the specification of
the language standard, they could not recover their cost of operation.

Users have had the most cost of the development and success of the COBOL
standard,
and vendors certainly deserve a close second place.

The Goverment Printing Office published the first specification at TAX Payer
Expense.
At least one and maybe more were published by the Canadian equivalent.

By the continued publication of anything that includes COBOL language
specification
as part of any document does not remove it from public ownership.

How or why would anyone want to enforce such a restriction?

Thanks to all of you for your efforts.

Warren G. Simmons
also at:
warren@tinycobol.org
```

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2000/07/02
Version: 3.01
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Sunday, July 2, 2000

The COBOL FAQ has moved.  To view the most recent version, please check out the
web version at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send private
email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

The following is Appendix B.5 - Changes to create Version 3.01

 o Recorded new location of the FAQ
 o Updated some old (bad) web links
 o Added some new COBOL books (particularly those written or contributed to - by
frequent comp.lang.cobol submitters).
 o Added information about LegacyJ (PERCobol) and changed the company name from
"Synkronix" to "LegacyJ".
 o Updated information for changes in product and company names and addresses
where available
 o Updated the URLs for downloading the draft of the next COBOL Standard and
CobCy
 o Started the Samples/Example Section
 o Tried to get rid of most specific references to Windows/NT, Windows/95,
Windows/98, and Windows/2000.
 o Added links to Xinotech
 o Added information on Norcom and GUI ScreenIO
 o Added (back) information on Wang
 o Added (back) information on the Tiny-COBOL ('74 Standard) project
```

<a id="t-a96fe7f452"></a>
## Thread: Trying to send a PLI pointer to a COBOL fetchable — 4 msgs

### Trying to send a PLI pointer to a COBOL fetchable

- **From:** ellie.odonnell.iei8@statefarm.com (Ellie O'Donnell)
- **Date:** 2001-12-20T12:28:51-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6eb5dfdb.0112201228.36324a45@posting.google.com>`

```
Does anyone know how to call a COBOL fetchable sending it a parameter
list of PLI pointers?

The pointers are arrays of structures, and I can't send them in the
CHAR format because the length of the CHAR field exceeds 32000.

Thanks!
```

#### ↳ Re: Trying to send a PLI pointer to a COBOL fetchable

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-20T15:24:20-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a5k42ucukfrea3ahh31ilt0u6tjcri199e@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<6eb5dfdb.0112201228.36324a45@posting.google.com>`

```
On 20 Dec 2001 12:28:51 -0800 ellie.odonnell.iei8@statefarm.com (Ellie
O'Donnell) wrote:

:>Does anyone know how to call a COBOL fetchable sending it a parameter
:>list of PLI pointers?

:>The pointers are arrays of structures, and I can't send them in the
:>CHAR format because the length of the CHAR field exceeds 32000.

Why not lie and say it is shorter, and use reference to process it?

The LINKAGE SECTION does not allocate storage - it merely provides an mapping
for storage allocated by another program.
```

#### ↳ Re: Trying to send a PLI pointer to a COBOL fetchable

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2001-12-20T21:59:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20011220165924.12782.00000204@mb-mt.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<6eb5dfdb.0112201228.36324a45@posting.google.com>`

```
Ellie O'Donnell writes ...

>Does anyone know how to call a COBOL fetchable sending it a parameter
>list of PLI pointers?
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thanks!

Wait. A pointer can't 'be' an array of structures. A pointer can point to an
array of structures, on problem.

Is the problem on the PL/I side or the COBOL side?

On the PL/I side, to pass a [fixed number] of pointers, why not:

     declare cobsub entry (pointer, ..., pointer) options(asm byvalue);

then

     call cobsub (ptr1, ptr2, ,,,, ptrn);


On the COBOL side,

Linkage section.
01  ptr1 pointer.
01  ptr2 pointer.
.
.
.
01  ptrn pointer.
01  struct-01.
     02 ...

procedure division using by value ptr1, ptr2, ..., ptrn.
       .
       .
       .
       set address of struct-01 to ptr1



Something like that. There are a lot of possibilities and you haven't given us
much detail, so I hope this gets you started.


<ad>
All of the possibilities for this problem, and much more, are covered in our
course "Inter-Language Communicaiton in OS/390". For a description, objectives,
and detailed outline start at:

http://www.trainersfriend.com/m220descr.htm

</ad>

Kind regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

#### ↳ Re: Trying to send a PLI pointer to a COBOL fetchable

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-21T08:11:42+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qun52uon2ofq1cktrhkll0n82ilu265oj3@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<6eb5dfdb.0112201228.36324a45@posting.google.com>`

```
PLIPGM:
   DCL MY_POINYTER POINTER;
   RC=COBOLPGM(MY_POINTER);
   

COBOLPGM

LINKAGE SECTION.
 01 A-Pointer  POINTER.
 01 A-Structure.
    02 Field-1 ....
       ....

PROCEDURE DIVISION USING A-Pointer.
    SET ADDRESS OF A-Structure TO A-pointer
    Do whatever you want
    MOVE something TO RETURN-CODE
    GOBACK
    .

                                                            
     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_        
                            ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,     
     Volker Bandke               |,4-  ) )-,_. ,\ (  `'-'   
      (BSP GmbH)                '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)        
                                                            
      The camel never sees its own hump, but that of its brothers is always before its eyes.
      
        (Another Wisdom from my fortune cookie jar)         
______________________________________________________________________________
Posted Via Binaries.net = SPEED+RETENTION+COMPLETION = http://www.binaries.net
```

### Seminal Languages was Re: Advantages of 64-bit architecture

- **From:** J Ahlstrom <jahlstro@cisco.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-21T11:04:41-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.arch,alt.folklore.computers,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.lisp,comp.lang.prolog

```
Nick Maclaren wrote:

> to COBOL as Autocode did to Fortran?

  From:
  http://secretguide2pcs.tripod.com/Strange-tongues.htm

>    From IBMï¿½s COMMERCIAL TRANSLATOR, COBOL borrowed 
>    group items (01 and 02), PICTURE symbols, 
>    fancy IF statements, and COMPUTE formulas.


> 
> Simula and Prolog were largely derivations of Algol and LISP,
> though did have some new features.  SNOBOL had a lot of influence
> but few descendents - definitely Icon and perhaps awk, but not
> really Perl or even Python.

Simula (the original) had no OO characteristics.
Simula 67 (to lessen confusion now called Simula)
   was the first OO language AFAIK.  I think that
   deserves seminal status even tho' it had predecessors
   in other areas.

> 
> You have a good point about sh, but the seminal language was
> probably the Titan command language.  Stephen Bourne was a
> lecturer here.

Any machine readable descriptions of the Titan 
command language around?

> 
> Actually, that makes Cambridge the originator of two, which seems
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
```

#### ↳ Re: Seminal Languages was Re: Advantages of 64-bit architecture

- **From:** nmm1@cus.cam.ac.uk (Nick Maclaren)
- **Date:** 2001-12-22T10:45:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.arch,alt.folklore.computers,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.lisp,comp.lang.prolog
- **Message-ID:** `<a01o7e$l5t$1@pegasus.csx.cam.ac.uk>`
- **References:** `<4ce97a1a.0112060929.658333cb@posting.google.com> <3C236519.94EB786@cisco.com> <9vvtjr$b0f$1@pegasus.csx.cam.ac.uk> <3C2387C9.BFFEF492@cisco.com>`

```
In article <3C2387C9.BFFEF492@cisco.com>,
J Ahlstrom  <jahlstro@cisco.com> wrote:
>Nick Maclaren wrote:
>> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>   in other areas.

This is a good point.  It wasn't the first to use those techniques,
but as far as I know was the first to make them a fundamental part
of the language.  The IBM MFT/MVT/MVT Access Method macros (perhaps
dating back to PCP) were a strongly object oriented interface, for
example.  But the expansion of the technique in the 1980s can all
be traced back to Simula.


Regards,
Nick Maclaren,
University of Cambridge Computing Service,
New Museums Site, Pembroke Street, Cambridge CB2 3QH, England.
Email:  nmm1@cam.ac.uk
Tel.:  +44 1223 334761    Fax:  +44 1223 334679
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Seminal Languages was Re: Advantages of 64-bit architecture

- **From:** "Stephen Fuld" <s.fuld.pleaseremove@att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-12-22T18:34:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.arch,alt.folklore.computers,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.lisp,comp.lang.prolog
- **Message-ID:** `<Nm4V7.306865$W8.11098746@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<4ce97a1a.0112060929.658333cb@posting.google.com> <3C236519.94EB786@cisco.com> <9vvtjr$b0f$1@pegasus.csx.cam.ac.uk> <3C2387C9.BFFEF492@cisco.com> <a01o7e$l5t$1@pegasus.csx.cam.ac.uk>`

```
"Nick Maclaren" <nmm1@cus.cam.ac.uk> wrote in message
news:a01o7e$l5t$1@pegasus.csx.cam.ac.uk...

snip


> The IBM MFT/MVT/MVT Access Method macros (perhaps
> dating back to PCP) were a strongly object oriented interface, for
> example.

What characteristics make you say that?  ISTM that they are essentially
subroutine calls.  There is no data hiding - the control blocks (DCB, etc.
and of course, the data buffers) are provided by the user and certainly
visible to him.  There is no concept of inheritance or polymorphism
(different calls for for different file types).  I can't see anything about
them that are object oriented.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Seminal Languages was Re: Advantages of 64-bit architecture

- **From:** nmm1@cus.cam.ac.uk (Nick Maclaren)
- **Date:** 2001-12-23T10:51:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.arch,alt.folklore.computers,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.lisp,comp.lang.prolog
- **Message-ID:** `<a04d0e$eqf$1@pegasus.csx.cam.ac.uk>`
- **References:** `<4ce97a1a.0112060929.658333cb@posting.google.com> <3C2387C9.BFFEF492@cisco.com> <a01o7e$l5t$1@pegasus.csx.cam.ac.uk> <Nm4V7.306865$W8.11098746@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
In article <Nm4V7.306865$W8.11098746@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>,
Stephen Fuld <s.fuld.pleaseremove@att.net> wrote:
>"Nick Maclaren" <nmm1@cus.cam.ac.uk> wrote in message
>news:a01o7e$l5t$1@pegasus.csx.cam.ac.uk...
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>them that are object oriented.

Not at all.  They may look like that to a shallow inspection, but
they are actually very different.

Firstly, when you set up a DCB (essentially the class), you specify
options that select which methods will be used.  Within limitations,
those methods can be changed later.  Some are set up by the system
and inspected/called by the programmer; some the other way round.

Secondly, far more was hidden than you believe and, more importantly,
was variable according to the options.  All of the channel commands,
for a start, and much of the DEB etc.  Yes, you could get at them,
but they weren't exposed interfaces, and I have had to hack at
'hidden' interfaces in the X Toolkit, too.

All of the essentials of "object orientation" were there.  Not as
part of a language, true, but as part of an interface.


Regards,
Nick Maclaren,
University of Cambridge Computing Service,
New Museums Site, Pembroke Street, Cambridge CB2 3QH, England.
Email:  nmm1@cam.ac.uk
Tel.:  +44 1223 334761    Fax:  +44 1223 334679
```

## Thread: errors in "hello, world" — 10 this month / 11 total

### errors in "hello, world"

- **From:** dedalus@yifan.net (Duilio Foschi)
- **Date:** 2001-12-22T16:10:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c24ae3c.5786665@news.libero.it>`

```
I used the shareware cobol 650 compiler to do a very easy task: show
the words

"hello, world" 

in a dos text screen.

Alas, the compiler doesn't like what I wrote.

The LST file is attached.

Can somebody suggest a way to write something the compiler will like ?

Thank you

Duilio Foschi


 TEST    COB                                 16:57:17    22-Dec-01
PAGE   1
 Line Number Source Line   ANSI 74-85 Standard Cobol V.6.50(c)1990.

   1         IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
   2    
   3         PROGRAM-ID. HELLO.
   4    
   5         ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
   6    
   7         DATA DIVISION.
   8    
   9         PROCEDURE DIVISION.
  10              DISPLAY "HELLO, WORLD".
  11              STOP RUN.
  12    
0010:  A PARAGRAPH DECLARATION IS REQUIRED HERE.  DISPLAY
0011:  ILLEGAL CHARACTER.
```

#### ↳ Re: errors in "hello, world"

- **From:** dedalus@yifan.net (Duilio Foschi)
- **Date:** 2001-12-22T17:18:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c24be86.9957507@news.libero.it>`
- **References:** `<3c24ae3c.5786665@news.libero.it>`

```
ev is under control now :)

This version will compile w/o problems:

000010  IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000020  PROGRAM-ID.   HELLO-WORLD-PROG.
000030  AUTHOR.       TIMOTHY R P BROWN.
000040
000050  ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
000060
000070  DATA DIVISION.
000080  WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
000090  01 TEXT-OUT    PIC X(12) VALUE 'Hello World!'.
000100
000110  PROCEDURE DIVISION.
000120  MAIN-PARAGRAPH.
000130         DISPLAY TEXT-OUT
000140         STOP RUN.

Now I can even compile it with the Fujitsu 85 compiler.

Thank you

Duilio Foschi
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: errors in "hello, world"

- **From:** dedalus@yifan.net (Duilio Foschi)

```

>Stai imparando la programmazione Cobol?

ricorderai (anche perche' mi avevi offerto il tuo aiuto) che devo
convertire una quantita' di dati utilizzati da un programma scritto in
cobol Microfocus.

Sulla macchina (con s.o. Sco-Unix) c'e' un compilatore Microfocus
funzionante.

In questo NG tutti mi hanno consigliato di eseguire la conversione dei
dati sulla macchina sorgente, e allora mi sono messo di buzzo buono a
capire come funziona il cobol.

Finora ho lavorato in una quantita' di linguaggi, ma mai in cobol, che
ho sempre snobbato (confesso).

Ora di necessita' ho fatto virtu' e devo dire che lo trovo un
linguaggio estremamente potente, con un suo fascino.

>Sei partito proprio dal classico esempio.

non si fa sempre cosi' ?

>Con il Fujitsu cobol puoi utilizzare lo spezzone di codice da te prodotto e
>dovrebbe funzionare lo stesso.

si', funziona. Col Fujitsu funziona anche il tuo codice. Il cobol 650
deve essere "permalosetto".

>Hai anche il Powercobol? 

no, ne ignoravo anche l'esistenza

>Che versione hai del Fujitsu Cobol?

3.0 (scaricata ieri).

Ho perso un po' di tempo a capire come funzionavano compilatore e
linker.

Non ho in programma (no pun intended) di continuare ad utilizzare il
cobol. Dovrebbe servirmi solo questa volta.

Ciao

Duilio
```

## Thread: first conversion program: disaster — 65 this month / 66 total

### first conversion program: disaster

- **From:** dedalus@yifan.net (Duilio Foschi)
- **Date:** 2001-12-22T23:34:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c250264.895577@news.libero.it>`

```
I have studied Cobol the whole day.

My purpose is to write a conversion routine that will trasform weird
COMP-3 records in plain old ASCII data.

As I have no idea how to write COMP-3 data, I began doing the
opposite, i.e. converting ASCII numbers into their COMP-3
counterpart.

The result is disappointing to say the least.

Starting data (in 1.dat) was:

123456789
123456789
123456789
123456789
123456789

what I got is:

12345
9    
12345
9    
12345
9    
12345
9    
12345
9    
12345
9    

I assume I did something wrong... :)

Any help  ? (the code is attached...)

Thank you

Duilio Foschi


 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
 PROGRAM-ID.    CONVERT-DATA.
 AUTHOR.        DUILIO FOSCHI.

 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
 INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
 FILE-CONTROL.

        SELECT IN-FILE ASSIGN TO '1.DAT'
           ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
        SELECT OUT-FILE ASSIGN TO '2.DAT'
           ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.

 DATA DIVISION.
 FILE SECTION.

 FD  IN-FILE.

 01  IN-REC.
     03  QTA        pic 9(9).

 FD  OUT-FILE.

 01  OUT-REC.
     03  QTA        pic 9(9) COMP-3.


 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
 01 EOF-FLAG PIC X.
      88 END-OF-FILE VALUE 'Y'.


*****************************************************

 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
 MAIN-PARA.
     OPEN INPUT IN-FILE
        OUTPUT OUT-FILE
     PERFORM UNTIL END-OF-FILE
        READ IN-FILE
          AT END MOVE 'Y' TO EOF-FLAG
          NOT AT END
            MOVE IN-REC TO OUT-REC
            WRITE OUT-REC
        END-READ
     END-PERFORM
     CLOSE OUT-FILE IN-FILE
     STOP RUN.
```

#### ↳ Re: first conversion program: disaster

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <news@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-23T01:01:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<l1aV7.86001$Zd.8193582@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3c250264.895577@news.libero.it>`

```

"Duilio Foschi" <dedalus@yifan.net> wrote in message
news:3c250264.895577@news.libero.it...
> I have studied Cobol the whole day.
>
…[88 more quoted lines elided]…
>      STOP RUN.

The program is perfect except for one small detail: All group moves (MOVE
IN-REC TO OUT-REC) are alphanumeric. That is, the move is done without
conversions of any kind - as if both groups were defined as PIC X(...).

So, change that one line to read MOVE QTA OF IN-REC TO QTA OF OUT-REC and
you should be good to go.

Couple of observations:

1. My suggestion makes use of 'Qualified' data names. If you have two
different data elements of the same name, they must be 'Qualified' by using
the "OF" phrase - otherwise how would the compiler know which you mean? A
better (and MUCH more common) method of naming data is:

01  IN-REC.
   02  IN-QTA...

01  OUT-REC.
  02  OUT-QTA...

Then you MOVE IN-QTA TO OUT-QTA and don't have to fool with the "OF" stuff.

2. COMP-3 data are not that 'weird when viewed in historical perspective.
Computers were first deployed to solve commercial problems plus memory was
expensive. On the original mainframes, there existed circuitry to do decimal
arithmetic (as opposed to binary) very efficiently. COMP-3 minimizes storage
of decimal numbers (used in commercial applications) and COMP-3 numbers are
processed directly - without conversion - by decimal instructions/circuits
in the computer. This is much more efficient, both in terms of storage and
speed, and yields greater precision, than other forms of data
representation. No other common language of which I am aware can handle the
precision built into COBOL (18+ decimal digits of precision).

3. Oh, and once you perfect your program, you won't be able to view the
result with an ASCII viewer (like NOTEPAD) because the result won't be in
ASCII format. You'll have to use a hex viewer.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: first conversion program: disaster

- **From:** dedalus@yifan.net (Duilio Foschi)
- **Date:** 2001-12-23T10:47:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c25b061.3688907@news.libero.it>`
- **References:** `<3c250264.895577@news.libero.it> <l1aV7.86001$Zd.8193582@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
>The program is perfect 

:)


>except for one small detail: All group moves (MOVE
>IN-REC TO OUT-REC) are alphanumeric. 

ARGHH

I should have known it: I read this thing in the tutorial (then I
forgot it).

>2. COMP-3 data are not that 'weird when viewed in historical perspective.

ok. But why is there any sense in using that format now ? 

>So, change that one line to read MOVE QTA OF IN-REC TO QTA OF OUT-REC and
>you should be good to go.

at first there was some avoc: 

the output file was _bigger_ than the input file.

After some head-scratching I realized that the input file was 72 bytes
long, while the expected size was 66 bytes (9 bytes for '123456789' +
1 byte for 0D + 1 byte for 0A).

Further inspection revealed that I had written

1234556789

in every line, instead of 

123456789

ARGHHHH!

Now everything works as expected.

I am happy.

Thanks a lot and merry Xmas

Duilio
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: first conversion program: disaster

- **From:** dedalus@yifan.net (Duilio Foschi)
- **Date:** 2001-12-23T11:05:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c25b8b0.5816643@news.libero.it>`
- **References:** `<3c250264.895577@news.libero.it> <l1aV7.86001$Zd.8193582@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
>1. My suggestion makes use of 'Qualified' data names. 


I tried the syntax 

            MOVE CORRESPONDING IN-REC TO OUT-REC

and it works, too (in the sense that it does format convertion).

This is useful in my case, as the record is made of several fields and
this will be a quick and dirty conversion routine..

>3. Oh, and once you perfect your program, you won't be able to view the
>result with an ASCII viewer (like NOTEPAD) because the result won't be in
>ASCII format. You'll have to use a hex viewer.

I used tdump (of Borland) to see the result and I had a nice glimpse
of how COMP-3 number are written:

Turbo Dump  Version 4.1 Copyright (c) 1988, 1994 Borland International
                       Display of File 2.DAT

000000: 12 34 56 78 9F 0D 0A 12  34 56 78 9F 0D 0A 12 34 
000010: 56 78 9F 0D 0A 12 34 56  78 9F 0D 0A 12 34 56 78 
000020: 9F 0D 0A 12 34 56 78 9F  0D 0A 00 00 00 00 00 00 


do you see ?

My ASCII numbers '123456789'  where converted to 
hex 

12 34 56 78 9F

where every digit now takes a nibble.

(The last nibble F must be a filler)

A reduction of roughly 50% in space.

Very interesting

Thanks a lot

Duilio
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: first conversion program: disaster

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <news@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-24T05:11:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0OyV7.313040$uB.33224317@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3c250264.895577@news.libero.it> <l1aV7.86001$Zd.8193582@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3c25b8b0.5816643@news.libero.it>`

```

"Duilio Foschi" <dedalus@yifan.net> wrote in message
news:3c25b8b0.5816643@news.libero.it...
> >1. My suggestion makes use of 'Qualified' data names.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> and it works, too (in the sense that it does format convertion).

## Yes. I didn't mention MOVE CORRESPONDING because imbedding the record
name is more common - and for good reasons. In thirty years, I have never
seen MOVE CORRESPONDING actually used anywhere. (I know, I know, there are
those out there who've seen, or even used, MOVE CORRESPONDING. I've just
never run into such a program.)

>
> This is useful in my case, as the record is made of several fields and
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> (The last nibble F must be a filler)

## No, it's not a filler. The last nibble is the algebraic sign. It is
always a letter (A-F) and here's an easy way to remember: FACE (or CAFE) are
positive, B & D are negative.

>
> A reduction of roughly 50% in space.

## And to answer yout question about why COMP-3 is still used, this day in
age, what with electric lights and indoor plumbing, there are two answers:
1. There are still decimal arithmetic circuits in use, so COMP-3 is more
efficient than other renditions, and
2. There hundreds of billions of records and billions of lines of code that
use it every day (and I'm not exaggerating).
```

```
gulp.

I have a further question:

One of the file I want to convert (and of which I have the FD) has the

      select ITEMS-EXTENDED assign to Path-Articoli-Extended
                       organization is indexed
                       access mode is dynamic
                       lock mode is automatic
                       record key is Io001-Key
                       file status is Stato-Rec.

Can I still access it sequentially ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: first conversion program: disaster

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** leclaire <leclaire@rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-24T12:28:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C271EAA.720C8D02@rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3c250264.895577@news.libero.it> <l1aV7.86001$Zd.8193582@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3c25b8b0.5816643@news.libero.it> <0OyV7.313040$uB.33224317@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3c26f565.467666@news.libero.it>`

```
Yes, that's right.  "Access Dynamic" means that the file may be accessed
either randomly (direct read by key) or sequentially, and usually both,
within the same program.  In fact, modern file systems typically default
to dynamic access, and I think I've even encountered a compiler or two
in which the "access mode" clause is optional.

Duilio Foschi wrote:
> 
> Can I still access it sequentially ?
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
leclaire wrote:
> 
> In fact, modern file systems typically default
> to dynamic access, and I think I've even encountered a compiler or two
> in which the "access mode" clause is optional.

In the Cobol Language ('85), and thus in all conforming compilers,
'ACCESS MODE' is optional and defaults to SEQUENTIAL if not specified.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: first conversion program: disaster

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-12-25T18:37:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C28C77D.A092E8AC@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3c250264.895577@news.libero.it> <l1aV7.86001$Zd.8193582@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3c25b8b0.5816643@news.libero.it> <0OyV7.313040$uB.33224317@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3c26f565.467666@news.libero.it> <3C271EAA.720C8D02@rr.com> <3C283C35.5F2A90D1@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
> > leclaire wrote:
> > >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Which I believe is different from what leclaire was talking about. 

In what way ?

> Taken in context, 

The context is the Cobol language and its compilers, is leclaire's
context different ? 

> leclaire seems to be saying that some compilers default ACCESS MODE
> to DYNAMIC. 

Which would make them non-conforming.  _All_ compilers should have
ACCESS MODE as optional with the default as SEQUENTIAL.  If there is a
compiler that defaults to DYNAMIC then name it.

What leclaire _seems_ to be saying is that it will 'default to dynamic
access' even if the ACCESS MODE clause is not optional.  What ?

> IMO that default makes a lot more sense than defaulting to SEQUENTIAL.

If the ORGANISATION is SEQUENTIAL then it makes sense to default the
ACCESS MODE to SEQUENTIAL as that is the _only_ valid option.  If ACCESS
MODE were to have a default of DYNAMIC in _some_ cases then there must
be a rule under which it defaults sometimes to one thing and other times
to another.

What actually makes sense is having a consistent default, as it in does
in fact have.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: first conversion program: disaster

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** leclaire <leclaire@rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-25T16:08:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C28A3C5.990BC44A@rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3c250264.895577@news.libero.it> <l1aV7.86001$Zd.8193582@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3c25b8b0.5816643@news.libero.it> <0OyV7.313040$uB.33224317@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3c26f565.467666@news.libero.it> <3C271EAA.720C8D02@rr.com> <3C283C35.5F2A90D1@Azonic.co.nz> <3C28C77D.A092E8AC@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
  To clarify what was said regarding the "Access Mode" clause, let me
say that *all* of the above responses are correct.  For the sake of
*brevity* (I am, after all, a programmer) I omitted certain details.
However, I meant to imply that Access Dynamic *should* be the default. 

  That is, any modern file system should be capable of allowing (or
prepared to provide) both random and sequential access within a program
without requiring that the programmer make a determination beforehand to
*exclude* either one mode or the other. This especilly given the strong
likelihood that somewhere down the road there will be certain
modifications made to the program, and most likely be made by someone
other than the original author and who may spend agonizing hours trying
to figure out why the modifications aren't working until he/she
"happens" to review a listing which includes the copylib and notice the
offending Access Mode clause (unless the platform provides clear and
relavent error messages, but how often does THAT happen).

  Therefore, personally in practice, I *always* code "access dynamic" in
the select statement.  If the operating environment has performance
issues regarding access mode then I feel that the complier should
automatically optimize the code accordingly (please hear me out on
this).  It is my belief that specifying access mode is another largely
unnecessary throwback to legacy COBOL and represents a mere formality in
order to accomodate existing rigid (dare I say antiquated) systems, such
as being forced to honor the sacred A and B margins, or coping with
compliers which error out because there is no Label Records clause (or
error out because said clause was included).

  However, I also recognize that the widespread existence of such rigid
and unprogressive systems are largely responsible for the perpetuation
of this marvelous language, and therefore my livelihood as well.  (Try
as I may, I have not been able to develop any adequately useful systems
utilizing VB or any incarnation of C/C++/C#/u-name-it or
hobbled-together Java or Perl or whatever ... at least, not any systems
that I would want to put my name on ... rant off).  So if mainframe
vendors (ahem) and the standards groups which accomodate them determine
that access mode should default to sequential unless otherwise
specified, then so be it.

  To end this discussion, in my earlier post I meant to say this: that
at some time I may have noticed while reviewing the language reference
manual for some implementation of COBOL that the Access Mode cluase was
not required.  That particular implementation *may* have defaulted to
Dynamic mode (which I feel it should *in an ideal world*), but I would
have included an Access Mode Dynamic clause anyway, from force of
habit.  If there is in fact a standard, and that standard is to default
to sequential access, then in my view it is a good habit to maintain.

  That's my thoughts. Your thoughts and opinions are welcome.
  Merry Christmas to all and seasons greetings to everyone else!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: first conversion program: disaster

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>

```
On Tue, 25 Dec 2001 16:08:18 GMT leclaire <leclaire@rr.com> wrote:

:>  To clarify what was said regarding the "Access Mode" clause, let me
:>say that *all* of the above responses are correct.  For the sake of
:>*brevity* (I am, after all, a programmer) I omitted certain details.
:>However, I meant to imply that Access Dynamic *should* be the default. 

Certainly an argument for bloatware.

:>  That is, any modern file system should be capable of allowing (or
:>prepared to provide) both random and sequential access within a program
:>without requiring that the programmer make a determination beforehand to
:>*exclude* either one mode or the other. 

They do.

Just like a modern operating system should be capable of allowing (or prepared
to provide) a program to do pretty much about anything without requiring that
the programmer make a determination beforehand.

:>                                        This especilly given the strong
:>likelihood that somewhere down the road there will be certain
:>modifications made to the program, and most likely be made by someone
:>other than the original author and who may spend agonizing hours trying
:>to figure out why the modifications aren't working until he/she
:>"happens" to review a listing which includes the copylib and notice the
:>offending Access Mode clause (unless the platform provides clear and
:>relavent error messages, but how often does THAT happen).

If the attempted access does not match the access mode one certainly should
expect compilation errors. 

Which compiler did you find that did not do that?

:>  Therefore, personally in practice, I *always* code "access dynamic" in
:>the select statement.  If the operating environment has performance
:>issues regarding access mode then I feel that the complier should
:>automatically optimize the code accordingly (please hear me out on
:>this).  It is my belief that specifying access mode is another largely
:>unnecessary throwback to legacy COBOL and represents a mere formality in
:>order to accomodate existing rigid (dare I say antiquated) systems, such
:>as being forced to honor the sacred A and B margins, or coping with
:>compliers which error out because there is no Label Records clause (or
:>error out because said clause was included).

A bloatware argument.

The code should more appropriately be optimized to the general case (good old
80/20 rule). If you lie to the compiler or chooses an inefficient way to code
something expect poor results.

:>  However, I also recognize that the widespread existence of such rigid
:>and unprogressive systems are largely responsible for the perpetuation
:>of this marvelous language, and therefore my livelihood as well.  (Try
:>as I may, I have not been able to develop any adequately useful systems
:>utilizing VB or any incarnation of C/C++/C#/u-name-it or
:>hobbled-together Java or Perl or whatever ... at least, not any systems
:>that I would want to put my name on ... rant off).  So if mainframe
:>vendors (ahem) and the standards groups which accomodate them determine
:>that access mode should default to sequential unless otherwise
:>specified, then so be it.

Reducing program size and speeding up processing for the general case.

   [ snipped ]
```

```
"leclaire" <leclaire@rr.com> wrote in message
news:3C28A3C5.990BC44A@rr.com...

>   Therefore, personally in practice, I *always* code "access dynamic" in
> the select statement.  If the operating environment has performance
> issues regarding access mode then I feel that the complier should
> automatically optimize the code accordingly (please hear me out on

I ran a test. There are no performance issues.

The time for dynamic/sequential reads is exactly the same as it is for
sequential/sequential reads. Writing too.
```
