```
Well, Ronald, Congratulations!

This is an Organization I have never seen.

Could you please tell us WHY you would use BINARY SEQUENTIAL, which vendor
implements it, why you are so opposed to LINE SEQUENTIAL which is a PC
default for most systems (and therefore gets written by systems other than
COBOL), and just exactly how the BINARY SEQUENTIAL organization differs from
standard SEQUENTIAL organization?

I'm very interested to know.

Thanks,

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: 0A against 0D 0A

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2001-12-26T00:09:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-docistdrS0eT@thishost>`
- **References:** `<3c2906fa.56325353@news.libero.it>`

```
On Tue, 25 Dec 2001 23:14:16 UTC, dedalus@yifan.net (Duilio Foschi) 
wrote:

> is there a way to "tell" the cobol compiler that it should take 0A as
> a line separator (of a sequential data file) instead of the canonical
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Duilio Foschi

Usually the behaviour of a "line sequential" is dependant on the 
underlying file system accessed by the COBOL program. If you want to 
use some file system convention not supported by the underlying OS you
will have to code the thing yourself. If the compiler allows you 
access to "stream I/O" routines you can open the file with those and 
split the data blocks read into records based on the 0A (line feed) 
character.

PC-DOS, OS/2 and Windows/MS-DOS use 0D 0A as record terminators for 
"line sequential", *nix OSs uses 0A as a record terminator for "line 
sequential".

For PC-DOS, OS/2 and Windows/MS-DOS you could call the underlying file
routines for "binary" files and deblock the data read. The same 
applies for *nix OSs

If the writer of the compiler provides the file system routines to 
handle either line terminator for "line sequential" they usually 
provide a way to "tell" the compiler the treatment reqired for a 
particular file.
```

#### ↳ Re: 0A against 0D 0A

- **From:** "Alain.FELER" <Alain.FELER@wanadoo.fr>
- **Date:** 2001-12-28T22:20:58+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a0io4g$pv6$1@wanadoo.fr>`
- **References:** `<3c2906fa.56325353@news.libero.it>`

```
If the problem is to read Unix text files (lines ending with 0A)
with a cobol program running on a PC (lines ending with with 0D0A),
here is how I would do it:
- 1. get the free utility doseol (easily found on yahoo.com)
- 2. transform my file from Unix to DOS standard doesol unixfile dosfile
- 3. use my cobol program with LINE SEQUENTIAL

Duilio Foschi <dedalus@yifan.net> a �crit dans le message :
3c2906fa.56325353@news.libero.it...
> is there a way to "tell" the cobol compiler that it should take 0A as
> a line separator (of a sequential data file) instead of the canonical
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Duilio Foschi
```

## Thread: How to find out the size of a dataset in OS/390 ? — 6 this month / 8 total

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How to find out the size of a dataset in OS/390 ?

- **From:** jtummala@lucent.com (JayaRam)
- **Date:** 2001-12-26T09:53:50-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5cf7edb9.0112260953.15141aec@posting.google.com>`

```
I have a dataset in OS/390 with name 'my.data.set' and has some 1000
records. How to find out actual space it occupied interms of KiloBytes
or MegaBytes. Is there any TSO command that can made use of ?
Thanks in advance. JayaRam.
```

#### ↳ Re: How to find out the size of a dataset in OS/390 ?

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-26T12:42:22-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2a6k2uk2s9puk190b5p2n8kiptpf6469m5@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<5cf7edb9.0112260953.15141aec@posting.google.com>`

```
On 26 Dec 2001 09:53:50 -0800 jtummala@lucent.com (JayaRam) wrote:

:>I have a dataset in OS/390 with name 'my.data.set' and has some 1000
:>records. How to find out actual space it occupied interms of KiloBytes
:>or MegaBytes. Is there any TSO command that can made use of ?

You can use ISPF 3.2.

You can count your records and multiply by the record size.

The actual physical space depends on the device itself, blocking and
compression.

What do you really want to know? 
```

- **From:** jtummala@lucent.com (JayaRam)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
The main requirement is that... We are decommissioning the application

Thanks so much for your reply.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to find out the size of a dataset in OS/390 ?

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-29T01:10:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hK8X7.13975$XC5.13460@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<5cf7edb9.0112260953.15141aec@posting.google.com> <2a6k2uk2s9puk190b5p2n8kiptpf6469m5@4ax.com> <5cf7edb9.0112270545.2c6fb48d@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi Jaya,

It sounds like you want to know the amount of DA space required to house your
dataset. This would depend on how the recs were blocked. For example, if the
file were blocked 80X1 the IRGs (inter record gaps, in tape lingo) occupy more
DA space than the data. I don't recall how much DA space is occupied by an IRG
(they probably will differ by divice type, anyway.) If you 1/2 track block the
data that's not a big factor, however, the diff between the size of the 2 blocks
and the capacity of the track may be.

The thing to remember is that there can be a significant difference between the
# of tracks required and the # of Kbytes in the file.

HTH, Jack. 



In article <5cf7edb9.0112270545.2c6fb48d@posting.google.com>, JayaRam says...
>
>Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote in message news:<2a6k2uk2s9puk190b5p2n8kiptpf6469m5@4ax.com>...
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thanks so much for your reply.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to find out the size of a dataset in OS/390 ?

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-29T01:10:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jK8X7.13976$XC5.13403@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<5cf7edb9.0112260953.15141aec@posting.google.com> <2a6k2uk2s9puk190b5p2n8kiptpf6469m5@4ax.com> <5cf7edb9.0112270545.2c6fb48d@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi Jaya,

It sounds like you want to know the amount of DA space required to house your
dataset. This would depend on how the recs were blocked. For example, if the
file were blocked 80X1 the IRGs (inter record gaps, in tape lingo) occupy more
DA space than the data. I don't recall how much DA space is occupied by an IRG
(they probably will differ by divice type, anyway.) If you 1/2 track block the
data that's not a big factor, however, the diff between the size of the 2 blocks
and the capacity of the track may be.

The thing to remember is that there can be a significant difference between the
# of tracks required and the # of Kbytes in the file.

HTH, Jack. 



In article <5cf7edb9.0112270545.2c6fb48d@posting.google.com>, JayaRam says...
>
>Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote in message news:<2a6k2uk2s9puk190b5p2n8kiptpf6469m5@4ax.com>...
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thanks so much for your reply.
```

#### ↳ Re: How to find out the size of a dataset in OS/390 ?

- **From:** stephenjspiro@mail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-12-28T22:58:56-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<928495c6.0112282258.418bcc89@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<5cf7edb9.0112260953.15141aec@posting.google.com>`

```
jtummala@lucent.com (JayaRam) wrote in message news:<5cf7edb9.0112260953.15141aec@posting.google.com>...
> I have a dataset in OS/390 with name 'my.data.set' and has some 1000
> records. How to find out actual space it occupied interms of KiloBytes
> or MegaBytes. Is there any TSO command that can made use of ?
> Thanks in advance. JayaRam.

1000 reords --- of any size ---- is a tiny file on OS/390.  At worst a
few cylinders on a disk pack.  A single tape will be nearly empty.

Stephen J Spiro
```

## Thread: COBOL TO C STATIC CALL ON OS/390. — 6 this month / 7 total

### COBOL TO C STATIC CALL ON OS/390.

- **From:** nivas_shyam@indiatimes.com (shyam nivas)
- **Date:** 2001-12-27T05:13:25-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c02fd744.0112270513.656d6560@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,
            I would like to do static call to a c program from a cobol
program on OS/390.I am using "Pragma" option in c source code and
successfully created c
.obj from source code.When i am trying to link edit this .obj with
cobol i am getting error message:"SYMBOL SUBRTN UNRESOLVED.  MEMBER
COULD NOT BE INCLUDED FROM THE  DESIGNATED CALL LIBRARY."As i am using
simple cobol compile jcl(IGYWCL) for the same i think that some more
scripts are required in JCL for link editting c .obj with cobol.
            waiting for reply from somebody really filling the gap
between open system and close system.
       
Thanks in advance.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL TO C STATIC CALL ON OS/390.

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2001-12-27T13:58:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20011227085814.05719.00001100@mb-bd.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<c02fd744.0112270513.656d6560@posting.google.com>`

```
shyam nivas writes ...

>Hi,
>            I would like to do static call to a c program from a cobol
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>       

To do this right, then, do not used "close system" COBOL. If you use a current
(LE-enabled) COBOL compiler, such as COBOL for MVS & VM, COBOL for OS/390 & VM,
or Enterprise COBOL for z/OS & OS/390, you can code pragmaless calls between
COBOL and C.

In the COBOL program...

procedure division.

       .
       .
       .
       call 'subrtn' using arg_list

NOTE: depending on what the C progam is expecting,  you may need to pass some
or all of the arguments with a BY VALUE phrase



In the C program (which must not be a main, right?)

#include (leawi.h)
.
.
.
void SUBRTN (arg_list)
{
.
.  code for subroutine
.
}



As far as "scripts" (the UNIX way of talking, not particuarly common term in
OS/390), a standard C compile and link job and a standard COBOL compile and
link job should be sufficient.


In the OS/390 world, we don't generally keep object code (talking about "c.obj"
is unheard of); instead, we almost always compile and link into an executable
(load module or program object), since executables can always be linked
together statically or dynamically in a variety of ways. So make sure you are
doing a compile _and link_ of your C program. The link step will put the
executable into the PDS pointed at by the DD statement named SYSLMOD (that is,
//SYSLMOD  DD  DISP=SHR,DSN=executables_library).


I do not know what is in your IGYWCL JCL, but the link step (probably //LKED
EXEC PGM=IEWL or HEWL) needs to include a DD statement for SYSLIB; this DD
statement probably has several data sets concatenated under it, including your
LE linkedit library (a name something like CEE.SCEELKED or SYS1.SCEELKED) and
the library into which you compiled and linked your C subroutine.


Hope this helps.

Kind regards,



Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

- **References:** `<c02fd744.0112270513.656d6560@posting.google.com> <20011227085814.05719.00001100@mb-bd.aol.com>`


###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL TO C STATIC CALL ON OS/390.

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2001-12-28T14:17:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20011228091749.18597.00001729@mb-bk.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<c02fd744.0112280516.1557ae6f@posting.google.com>`

```
OK, here is some JCL to do a compile and link of your C routine (make changes
wherever you see lowercase letters and / or the little arrows '-->'):

//------- valid JOB statement here -----------
//C       EXEC PGM=CBCDRVR
//        PARM='FL(I),SO,LO,LANG(EXTENDED),NOLONGNAME,XREF'
//STEPLIB  DD  DISP=SHR,DSN=library_where_compiler_is  --> can omit if in
LINKIST
//               DD  DISP=SHR,DSN=LE_run_time_library           --> often
SYS1.SCEERUN
//SYSLIB   DD  DISP=SHR,DSN=HEADER_FILES                --> OFTEN SYS1.SCEEH.H
//SYSPRINT DD  SYSOUT=*
//SYSCPRT  DD  SYSOUT=*
//SYSUT1    DD  UNIT=SYSALLDA,SPACE=(CYL,(1,1))
//SYSUT4    DD  UNIT=SYSALLDA,SPACE=(CYL,(1,1))
//SYSUT7    DD  UNIT=SYSALLDA,SPACE=(CYL,(1,1))
//SYSUT8    DD  UNIT=SYSALLDA,SPACE=(CYL,(1,1))
//SYSUT9    DD  UNIT=SYSALLDA,SPACE=(CYL,(1,1))
//SYSUT10  DD  UNIT=SYSALLDA,SPACE=(CYL,(1,1))
//SYSLIN     DD  DSN=&&SYSLIN,UNIT=SYSALLDA,DISP=(MOD,PASS),
//             SPACE=(TRK,(3,3))
//SYSIN      DD  DISP=SHR,
//         DSN=your_source_library(SUBRTN)                          --> program
to compile
//*
//         IF  C.RC < 5 THEN
//LKED    EXEC PGM=HEWL,PARM='MAP,LIST'
//SYSLIN    DD  DSN=&&SYSLIN,DISP=(OLD,DELETE)
//SYSLIB    DD  DISP=SHR,DSN=subroutine_library          --> whatever libraries
you need to
//                DD  DISP=SHR,DSN=subroutine_library         --> pick up your
subroutines
//                DD  DISP=SHR,DSN=LE_link_library             --> often
SYS1.SCEELKED
//                DD  DISP=SHR,DSN=LE_link_library             --> often
SYS1.SCEELKEX
//SYSUT1   DD  UNIT=SYSALLDA,SPACE=(CYL,(1,1))
//SYSPRINT DD  SYSOUT=*                                         --> fix up
names below
//SYSLMOD  DD  DISP=SHR,DSN=load_module_library(SUBRTN)
//        ENDIF


Something like that should give you a clean compile and link of your
subroutine, placing the load module in the library called
'load_module_library'.


Now, for the COBOL main, use something like this:

//------- valid JOB statement here -----------
//COB     EXEC PGM=IGYCRCTL,
//              PARM='SIZE(MAX),FLAG(I,W),MAP,OFFSET'
//STEPLIB   DD  DISP=SHR,DSN=library_where_compiler_is --> can omit if in
LINKIST
//                DD  DISP=SHR,DSN=LE_run_time_library       --> often
SYS1.SCEERUN
//SYSLIB     DD  DISP=SHR,DSN=copy_lib                       --> for COBOL copy
books
//SYSPRINT DD  SYSOUT=*
//SYSUT1   DD  UNIT=SYSALLDA,SPACE=(CYL,(1,1))
//SYSUT2   DD  UNIT=SYSALLDA,SPACE=(CYL,(1,1))
//SYSUT3   DD  UNIT=SYSALLDA,SPACE=(CYL,(1,1))
//SYSUT4   DD  UNIT=SYSALLDA,SPACE=(CYL,(1,1))
//SYSUT5   DD  UNIT=SYSALLDA,SPACE=(CYL,(1,1))
//SYSUT6   DD  UNIT=SYSALLDA,SPACE=(CYL,(1,1))
//SYSUT7   DD  UNIT=SYSALLDA,SPACE=(CYL,(1,1))
//SYSLIN    DD  DSN=&&SYSLIN,UNIT=SYSALLDA,DISP=(MOD,PASS),
//             SPACE=(TRK,(3,3))
//SYSIN    DD  DISP=SHR,
//           DSN=your_source_library(member)                   --> program to
compile
//*
//         IF  COB.RC < 5 THEN
//LKED       EXEC PGM=HEWL,PARM='MAP,LIST'
//SYSLIN      DD  DSN=&&SYSLIN,DISP=(OLD,DELETE)
//SYSLIB      DD  DISP=SHR,DSN=subroutine_library    --> whatever libraries you
need to
//                 DD  DISP=SHR,DSN=subroutine_library    --> pick up your
subroutines
//                 DD  DISP=SHR,DSN=LE_link_library         --> often
SYS1.SCEELKED
//                 DD  DISP=SHR,DSN=LE_link_library         --> often
SYS1.SCEELKEX
//SYSUT1     DD  UNIT=SYSALLDA,SPACE=(CYL,(1,1))
//SYSPRINT DD  SYSOUT=*                          --> fix up names below
//SYSLMOD  DD  DISP=SHR,DSN=load_module_library(module_name)
//        ENDIF


In the SYSLIB concatenation above, it would be a good idea to include the
library containing your C subroutine load module.


If you can't get it to work, cut and paste your JCL and C source code for us to
get a better look at.

<ad>

Alternatively, bring us in to teach our course "InterLanguage Communication in
OS/390", which covers calls (static, dynamic, and DLL) across modules written
in the same or differing languages (course covers Assembler, C, COBOL, and
PL/I).

details at: http://www.trainersfriend.com/m220descr.htm

</ad>


Hope this helps,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL TO C STATIC CALL ON OS/390.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2001-12-31T14:56:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20011231095640.05494.00004394@mb-mg.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<c02fd744.0112302348.1eacfe8@posting.google.com>`

```
Shyam,

Well, there are still a lot of unknowns for me. First, regarding your question:

>Now is there
>any option for forcefully guide the control to COBOL program similar
>to ENTRY ....... for c programs?
>I doubt because c entry point is decided at compile time whereas cobol
>entry point is decided at run time

I don't understand the question at all.

The entry point for a C program is determined at compile and and bind time. The
entry point for a COBOL program is also determined at compile and bind time.

So let's look at the structure of your programs. I don't have the original post
here, but as I recall, you want to have a COBOL main call a C subroutine.

First, the C subroutine must not have a 'main' procedure in it, of course. The
general structure will be this:

#include statements

a prototype for the subroutine itself, to describe the format of parameters
being passed, maybe something like this:

void SUBRTN1 (char in_char[],
                        short *binary_half,
                        .
                        .
                        .
                                           )

then the body of the function...

{
 .
 .
 .
}

This program should be compiled and bound using JCL as I suggested.


Second, the COBOL main will be like any COBOL program, and it invokes the C
subroutine with a CALL, something like this:

      call 'SUBRTN1' USING char-string, 
                                        binary-half,
                                        .
                                        .
                                        .


This program should be compiled and bound using the other JCL I suggested.

If you are still having problems, perhaps you could post the important excerpts
from your programs, the JCL you are using, and any binder control statements.
If this is very long, consider emailing it to me off-list (see my signature for
the preferred email address).

Kind regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

```
Hello,

I am running a cobol program (netexpress) on a Win 2000 PC, i would
like to know the pid off the process and write it in a file.  How can
I do this ?

Greetings Peter
```

#### ↳ Re: My PID on NT2000

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-12-27T17:24:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lPIW7.504$dW6.282004@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<3c2b4342.118885507@news.eunet.be>`

```

Peter Alluyn <pa@schaubroeck.be> wrote in message
news:3c2b4342.118885507@news.eunet.be...
> I am running a cobol program (netexpress) on a Win 2000 PC, i would
> like to know the pid off the process and write it in a file.  How can
> I do this ?

Use the Windows API:

GetCurrentProcessId
The GetCurrentProcessId function retrieves the process identifier of the
calling process.

DWORD GetCurrentProcessId(VOID);
Parameters
This function has no parameters.

Return Values
The return value is the process identifier of the calling process.

Library
KERNEL32.LIB

I "think" in COBOL you would..

      CALL GetCurrentProcessId RETURNING MY-PID

(I am not a Windows OO-COBOL person).


MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: Variable Length Record

- **From:** "Robert Sales" <Robert.Sales@microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-28T09:52:37
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Trouble is the file itself does not have variable length records, but fixed
length, with this fixed length varying according to which file is actually
being opened.
There is no standard way of doing this in COBOL, but there is a trick you
can use in Micro Focus COBOL (probably in other implementations too).  Which
COBOL compiler are you using ?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Variable Length Record

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2001-12-31T22:30:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20011231173013.05958.00001056@mb-fh.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<a0hf7v$gkt$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com>`

```
In article <a0hf7v$gkt$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com>, "Robert Sales"
<Robert.Sales@microfocus.com> writes:

>
>Trouble is the file itself does not have variable length records, but fixed
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

In the OS\390 environment, it is possible to define the file as the largest
possible input record length and the system will chop off the input area based
on the length of the record specified in the DCB information of the DDname.

Granted that your program must have a means of 'getting' the record length.
I used a SYSIN parameter file that passed the characteristics of the file to be
processed.

I have not found a 'good' way of doing this same thing on the PC platform.
There does not seem to be a way of having the environment 'know' the 
LRECL/BLKSIZE of files on the PC as everything seems to be read as 
a continuous byte stream up to the internally defined LRECL/BLKSIZE.
Too bad there is not a way to issue a command like CHANGE ATTRIBUTE
MAXRECSIZE to set the internal attributes according to the particular 
file requirements.
```

#### ↳ Re: Variable Length Record

- **From:** jraben@cascinc.com (Jeff Raben)
- **Date:** 2001-12-28T21:18:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c2ce0e8.21720826@news.bullseyetelecom.net>`
- **References:** `<24df60cb.0112272007.46c45a1e@posting.google.com>`

```
sriprasu@netzero.net (sri) wrote:

>Hi,
>
>I am not able to use Variable Length Records.  
>
a record length has no meaning on INPUT files you get what is there.
Declaring a "DEPENDING" on some working storage entry has no meaning
for INPUT.
DEPENDING is used for output definition so that "WRITE" knows how much
to write.
Jeff

and stir with a Runcible spoon...
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Variable Length Record

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-28T15:49:39-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a0ipeb$pd3$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<24df60cb.0112272007.46c45a1e@posting.google.com> <3c2ce0e8.21720826@news.bullseyetelecom.net>`

```
This is either wrong - or misleading

If you use the RECORD VARYING IN SIZE DEPENDING ON ws-item

structure, then the ws-item *does* include the length of the record that is
read (on INPUT).  It is true, that it only "determines" the length of the
record on OUTPUT (WRITE or REWRITE).

If you use the VARYING IN SIZE DEPENDING ON ws-item syntax, then you CAN use
the length in ws-item to determine which "structure" as been read (assuming
each is defined as a separate 01-level under your FD)

NOTE: The RECORD CONTAINS n TO m syntax (available before the '85 Standard
introduced the RECORD VARYING IN SIZE syntax) does NOT provide any way to
(legally) determine the size of a record that is read in.
```

#### ↳ Re: Variable Length Record

- **From:** "Alain.FELER" <Alain.FELER@wanadoo.fr>
- **Date:** 2001-12-28T22:58:19+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a0iqah$9it$1@wanadoo.fr>`
- **References:** `<24df60cb.0112272007.46c45a1e@posting.google.com>`

```
This works for me:
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. VARIA.
      * ( WORKS WITH COBOL MICROSOFT (IN FACT MICROFOCUS) V 4.5 )
      * ( BUT THE ONLY NON-STANDARD ITEM IS THE LINE SEQUENTIAL CLAUSE)

       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.
       SELECT FVAR ASSIGN TO "INPUT.TXT"
           ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL
           ACCESS MODE  IS SEQUENTIAL
           FILE STATUS  IS FS-FVAR.

       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       FD  FVAR RECORD VARYING.
       01  ST-BIG.
         02  SEQ-NUM            PIC 99.
         02  FILLER                 PIC X(354).

       01  ST-SHORT             PIC X(255).

       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  FS-FVAR                PIC XX VALUE SPACES.
       88  OK-FVAR  VALUE "00".
       88  EOF-FVAR VALUE "10".

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       MAIN-PARAGRAPH.
           OPEN INPUT FVAR
           READ FVAR AT END CONTINUE END-READ
           PERFORM UNTIL EOF-FVAR
              EVALUATE SEQ-NUM
              WHEN "01"  PERFORM USE-ST-SHORT
              WHEN "02"  PERFORM USE-ST-BIG
              WHEN OTHER PERFORM SOMETHING-ELSE
              END-EVALUATE
              READ FVAR AT END CONTINUE END-READ
           END-PERFORM
           CLOSE FVAR
           STOP RUN.

       USE-ST-SHORT.
           DISPLAY ST-SHORT.
       USE-ST-BIG.
           DISPLAY ST-BIG.
       SOMETHING-ELSE.
           DISPLAY "???".

sri <sriprasu@netzero.net> a �crit dans le message :
24df60cb.0112272007.46c45a1e@posting.google.com...
> Hi,
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: Variable Length Record

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-30T20:04:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LrKX7.15440$XC5.13939@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<24df60cb.0112272007.46c45a1e@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi Sri,

A var len record is not such (recfm=V or VB) because the recs in the file vary
in length. A VLR must start w/a 4 byte record descriptor word (RDW) that
contains the # of bytes in the rec, as well as the Block). If it is variable in
length but doesn't contain a RDW, it is an Undefined Record (recfm=U). Have you
determined this?

HTH, Jack.  


In article <24df60cb.0112272007.46c45a1e@posting.google.com>, sri says...
>
>Hi,
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thanks
```

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq

                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Sunday, July 2, 2000

The COBOL FAQ has moved.  To view the most recent version, please check out the
web version at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send private
email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

The following is Appendix B.5 - Changes to create Version 3.01

 o Recorded new location of the FAQ
 o Updated some old (bad) web links
 o Added some new COBOL books (particularly those written or contributed to - by
frequent comp.lang.cobol submitters).
 o Added information about LegacyJ (PERCobol) and changed the company name from
"Synkronix" to "LegacyJ".
 o Updated information for changes in product and company names and addresses
where available
 o Updated the URLs for downloading the draft of the next COBOL Standard and
CobCy
 o Started the Samples/Example Section
 o Tried to get rid of most specific references to Windows/NT, Windows/95,
Windows/98, and Windows/2000.
 o Added links to Xinotech
 o Added information on Norcom and GUI ScreenIO
 o Added (back) information on Wang
 o Added (back) information on the Tiny-COBOL ('74 Standard) project
```

```
over 30 years - get use to it
```

<a id="t-9e83df8ffa"></a>
## Thread: COMTI, CICS, DB2, and ROLLBACK Problems — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### COMTI, CICS, DB2, and ROLLBACK Problems

- **From:** crewschuck@russellcorp.com (Chuck Crews)
- **Date:** 2001-12-31T05:24:09-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1a743e05.0112310524.2debca01@posting.google.com>`

```
Here is my structure:

COMTI
   program A
      link to program B
      link to program C
      line to program D

If I encounter an error anywhere during the processing of program A, I
issue a syncpoint rollback.    However, we have found that the
syncpoint rollback sometimes fails (i.e. gets a CICS error when
executing) resulting (generally) in an ADPL error.

2 questions:

Is this a sync level 2 problem (like maybe I am not at sync level 2)?
If the CICS rollback fails, does the actual DB2 rollback still occur
and I am just receiving a bad CICS return code?

If the rollback still does occur then I am OK and I can just make sure
my error handling is proper and proceed.

Has anyone had similar experiences.

Chuck Crews
```

---

<a id="t-22510c470f"></a>
## Thread: Way to find the trailing blanks — 3 this month / 20 total

### Way to find the trailing blanks

- **From:** bxvijay@yahoo.com (Bharath)
- **Date:** 2001-12-31T06:04:33-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7d55db4c.0112310604.6e301bd1@posting.google.com>`

```
I am working in a private company as a COBOL programmer. Right now I
am involved the conversion of assembler programs to COBOL programs. I
have some doubts in COBOL programming logic.

My doubt is, 
How to efficiently find a trailing blanks in a particular field. (the
field may contain blanks between the non-blanks)
I know one method, try to find first non-blank character from back to
front using reference modification.
Is any other way to find? 

If I want to find leading blank I can use "INSPECT". Like this any
other way to find trailing blanks?

regards and thanks 
Bharath
```

#### ↳ Re: Way to find the trailing blanks

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <news@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-12-31T16:45:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6D0Y7.104601$m05.9037176@bin5.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<7d55db4c.0112310604.6e301bd1@posting.google.com>`

```

"Bharath" <bxvijay@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:7d55db4c.0112310604.6e301bd1@posting.google.com...
> I am working in a private company as a COBOL programmer. Right now I
> am involved the conversion of assembler programs to COBOL programs. I
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bharath

1. If you're using the newest version of Fujitsu's compiler on a Win
machine, you can use:

COMPUTE NUMCHAR = FUNCTION STORED-CHARACTER-LENG(MYLINE)

2. A common way is:

INSPECT MYLINE TALLYING LEN FOR CHARACTERS BEFORE 'bb'

where 'bb' is a double space.

3. Another way:

PERFORM VARYING LEN FROM MAXLEN BY -1 UNTIL LEN < 1
    OR MYLINE(LEN:1) NOT = SPACE
   END-PERFORM.

4. Another way:
UNSTRING MYLINE INTO JUNK DELIMITED 'bb' COUNT IN LEN.

Efficiency in this case is virtually irrelevant; you can use any of these
methods at a rate of millions of fields per second. But, if you REALLY want
the MOST efficient, write a quickie program will all the above methods and
time the results through, say, one million iterations each.

If you do the above experiment, please post the results here!

At our shop, we have a callable subroutine that left-justifies a string,
squeezes out multiple blanks, and (optionally) returns the character count.
What with the linkage and all, not as efficient as it could be, but we only
have to write the routine once.
```

#### ↳ Re: Way to find the trailing blanks

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2001-12-31T11:07:25-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0112311107.1fcc593f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<7d55db4c.0112310604.6e301bd1@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello Bharath,

your idea of using reference modification seems the most appropriate,
by the time the compiler has optimised it, it should be very quick.

an example follows:

Perform varying my-counter from my-data-length by -1
   until my-data(my-counter:1) not = space
     continue
end-perform.

computer number-of-trailing-spaces =
   my-data-length - my-counter.

Happy new year, Robert

bxvijay@yahoo.com (Bharath) wrote in message news:<7d55db4c.0112310604.6e301bd1@posting.google.com>...
> I am working in a private company as a COBOL programmer. Right now I
> am involved the conversion of assembler programs to COBOL programs. I
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bharath
```
